title: Hot and toxic: Temperature regulates microcystin release from cyanobacteria
what: A paired field-monitoring and laboratory-incubation study testing whether water temperature regulates microcystin (a cyanotoxin) release from the cyanobacterium Planktothrix agardhii in a eutrophic freshwater lake, using generalized linear mixed models to quantify the temperature–toxin relationship and its use as a pos

claims.c: Lake water temperature varied seasonally from 3 °C to 27 °C during the field study period.
Cyanobacteria (Planktothrix agardhii) biomass increased with warming up to a threshold of 18 °C, then declined rapidly with further warming.
In the field, extracellular microcystin concentration was tightly coupled with water temperature and peaked between 20 and 25 °C, coinciding with the decline in cyanobacteria biomass.

A concurrent laboratory incubation experiment that replicated the lake's seasonal temperature variation reproduced the same pattern: productivity-specific microcystin release peaked between 20 and 25 °C and then declined sharply at 30 °C.

Using generalized linear mixed models relating water temperature to cyanobacteria abundance and microcystin release, the authors estimated that warming to ≥20 °C would produce a 36% increase in microcystin release when Chlorophyll a was ≤50 μg/L.

The authors conclude there is a temperature threshold for microcystin release in P. agardhii, suggesting water temperature could potentially be used to forecast bloom toxicity severity in eutrophic lakes that bloom year-round.

The study design paired seasonal field monitoring (temperature, cyanobacteria biomass, extracellular microcystin) in a single eutrophic lake dominated by P. agardhii with a concurrent laboratory incubation experiment replicating that seasonal temperature pattern, specifically to test cause-and-effect rather than rely on field correlation alone.

The paper frames its motivation around the fact that the mechanisms regulating cyanotoxin release by cyanobacteria are poorly understood, despite the global threat cyanotoxins pose to water quality and human health.

numbers: 3 to 27 °C — lake water temperature range observed over the field study period
18 °C — temperature above which cyanobacteria (P. agardhii) biomass declined rapidly (field)
20 to 25 °C — temperature range of peak extracellular microcystin concentration in the field, and peak productivity-specific microcystin release in the lab
30 °C — temperature at which laboratory microcystin release declined sharply
≥20 °C — warming threshold used in the GLMM prediction
36% — GLMM-predicted increase in microcystin release at warming ≥20 °C (point estimate; no confidence interval, p-value, or R² given in the abstract)
≤50 μg L⁻¹ — Chlorophyll a condition under which the 36% increase estimate applies
tensions: The unimodal (rise-then-fall) relationship reported here — microcystin release peaking at 20–25°C and then declining sharply by 30°C — complicates a simple monotonic "warmer water = more toxin" narrative sometimes used in HAB risk communication; it instead suggests a thermal optimum/threshold, which matters for shaping a temperature-based risk index as non-linear rather than linear. The paper also reports that peak extracellular microcystin coincided with a decline in cyanobacteria biomass, i.e., toxin release peaked as biomass was already fall

title: Identification of key feature variables and prediction of harmful algal blooms in a water diversion lake based on interpretable machine learning
what: A 2025 Environmental Research article that builds an interpretable machine-learning pipeline (CatBoost benchmarked against 9 other ML models, explained with SHAP, plus a PLS-PM path analysis) to identify the key environmental drivers of harmful algal blooms (HABs) in Yilong Lake, a Chinese lake subject to an engineered

claims.c: Among ten machine learning models benchmarked for predicting harmful algal blooms (HABs) in Yilong Lake, a CatBoost (gradient-boosted decision tree) model performed best, reaching an AUC of 0.948.

Using SHAP-based interpretation/feature selection, the authors reduced an initial pool of 24 candidate environmental variables down to 8 identified as the key predictors of HAB occurrence, including total phosphorus (TP), total nitrogen (TN), and chemical oxygen demand (CODCr).

SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) was used to interpret the CatBoost model at two levels: a global interpretation summarizing overall feature importance across the model, and a local interpretation explaining individual sample-level predictions.

A Partial Least Squares Path Model (PLS-PM) analysis indicated that the water-diversion project's mitigating effect on HABs operates indirectly, primarily by diluting nutrient concentrations, rather than through some other direct pathway.

The modeling dataset spans 2008-2022: an external-validation dataset from three monitoring sites covering a non-water-diversion period (2008-2013) and a water-diversion period (2014-2020), plus a separate internal-validation dataset from six sampling sites covering 2021-2022.

The authors frame the core methodological challenge as follows: because water diversion itself alters the receiving lake's hydrodynamics and water environment, it becomes harder to identify which environmental features actually drive HAB occurrence, motivating their interpretable-ML approach.

The validated CatBoost + SHAP model was translated into a practical, deployable application intended for use by non-research lake-management staff, specifically personnel of the Bureau of Yilong Lake Administration.

Internal validation: 6 sampling sites, period 2021-2022

title: Impact of Environmental Factors on the Regulation of Cyanotoxin Production
what: A 2014 peer-reviewed review article in Toxins (Basel) by Boopathi and Ki that synthesizes laboratory and (to a lesser extent) field literature on how environmental factors — nitrogen form/availability, phosphorus, light intensity, temperature, iron, salinity — regulate the biosynthesis of five cyanotoxin classes (micro

claims.c: The review's stated purpose is that understanding how environmental factors regulate cyanotoxin production is necessary for effective management of toxic cyanobacterial blooms, framed against a backdrop of climate-change-driven increases in bloom frequency/severity.

Field (observational, non-experimental) datasets show cyanobacterial biomass specifically (not total phytoplankton biomass) rising with water temperature, cited from a 42-year Castle Lake record and a 143-lake latitudinal transect survey; this is correlational evidence of a temperature-cyanobacteria association, not an experimental causal test.

Nitrogen limitation up-regulates microcystin (MC) biosynthesis gene transcription via the transcription factor NtcA, whose activation depends on 2-oxoglutarate (2-OG) build-up and release of PipX from the PII protein under low nitrogen.
The NtcA transcription-factor binding site is defined by a specific consensus DNA sequence and spacing relative to the promoter.

Effects of nitrate concentration on MC production are inconsistent across species: some unicellular non-nitrogen-fixing species show more MC with more nitrate, but in M. aeruginosa PCC7806 elevated nitrate only raised growth rate without changing MC production.

Light intensity regulates MC production and, per one cited study, has a larger effect on MC production than nitrogen availability does; a light intensity of 30 µmol photons m-2 s-1 was associated with initial induction of mcyD transcription.

Iron availability has contradictory, study-dependent effects on MC production and mcy transcription: iron starvation is reported in different studies to both induce MC production (despite lower mcyA transcription) and to increase mcyD transcription, while yet another study found more MC produced when more iron was available.

Nodularin (NOD) synthesis is reported to be jointly enhanced by a combination of elevated temperature, higher light, and high phosphate concentration, and inhibited by high salinity and high inorganic nitrogen.

Phosphate limitation's effect on cylindrospermopsin (CYN) production is contradictory even within the same species (Aphanizomenon ovalisporum): one study reports decreased CYN under phosphate limitation, another reports increased CYN production under phosphate limitation.

In mesocosm experiments dominated by C. raciborskii, phosphate addition (alone or with nitrate) increased CYN concentrations and increased the relative proportion of the toxic (cyrA-carrying) genotype within the population, suggesting phosphate addition may favor toxic over non-toxic strains.

CYN's response to light intensity is non-monotonic and growth-phase dependent: high light (140 µmol photons m-2 s-1) combined with moderate nitrogen deficiency raises CYN but lowers growth rate, while total CYN content peaks at an intermediate light level (10 µmol photons m-2 s-1) during exponential growth.

Saxitoxin (STX) response to temperature reverses direction between strains: in C. raciborskii C10 extracellular STX was higher at a suboptimal 19 °C than at the 25 °C optimal-growth temperature, whereas in Aphanizomenon sp. LMECYA 31 a higher temperature (28 °C) induced more STX than the usual growth temperature (22 °C).

Nitrogen-to-phosphorus (N:P) ratio and absolute nitrogen concentration have divergent effects on STX across species: high N:P raised STX about one-fold in C. raciborskii, while increased nitrogen concentration was associated with lower STX in Raphidiopsis brookii.

The biosynthetic gene clusters for the five toxin classes differ substantially in size and gene count: mcy (MC) ~55 kb/10 genes; nda (NOD) 9 ORFs; cyr (CYN) ~42 kb/15 ORFs; ana (ATX) two 29-kb clusters with a 6-kb spacer; stx/sxt (STX) 25.7-36 kb/33 genes.

Acute lethality (mouse LD50) varies by roughly an order of magnitude or more across the five toxin classes: MC-LR 25-150 µg/kg; NOD-R 50-150 µg/kg; anatoxin-a 375 µg/kg (i.p.) / >5000 µg/kg (oral); anatoxin-a(s) 20 µg/kg; saxitoxin 10 µg/kg.

Cylindrospermopsin's environmental persistence is reported inconsistently: it has a >10-day half-life in high-purity water and is 90% photodegraded within 3 days in crude algal extract under sunlight, yet a separate study found it was not degraded by natural microbial communities even after 40 days.

The 1979 Palm Island (Australia) drinking-water poisoning event, caused by CYN released after algicide treatment of a cyanobacterial bloom, hospitalized 139 children and 10 adults with hepato-enteritis.

The authors state that many of the contradictory results across the reviewed studies (e.g., iron/FurA and nitrate effects on MC) may stem from non-standardized experimental methodology rather than a single consistent biological rule, and that laboratory findings still require field validation, which faces practical constraints; they separately flag that anatoxin (ana) gene-cluster regulation and saxitoxin regulation in general remain understudied.

title: Improved algorithm for routine monitoring of cyanobacteria and eutrophication in inland and near-coastal waters
what: A short communication (Matthews & Odermatt, 2015, Remote Sensing of Environment) documenting incremental improvements to the Maximum Peak Height (MPH) satellite algorithm for MERIS/BEAM imagery — refined pixel-flagging that reduces false-positive cyanobacteria detection, a new stray-light/adjacency-effect flag, and

claims.c: The MPH algorithm estimates chlorophyll-a and detects cyanobacteria/surface scum/floating vegetation in inland and near-coastal waters by computing the position and height of a reflectance peak among MERIS red-edge bands (681, 709, 753 nm) against a 665–885 nm baseline, using Rayleigh-corrected (not fully aerosol-corrected) reflectance to avoid aerosol-correction error over optically complex water.

The revised cyanobacteria flag adds a requirement that the newly introduced BAIR (backscatter and absorption induced reflectance) peak exceed 0.002, on top of existing spectral (SICF/SIPAF) conditions, specifically to suppress false-positive cyanobacteria detections that the original 2012 flag produced in clear, oligotrophic water.

The source states that cyanobacteria-specific (phycocyanin) spectral features only become clearly resolvable above roughly chl-a > 20 mg m-3, so cyanobacteria detection from MERIS (or, as stated, any other currently available satellite imagery) is considered infeasible/unlikely at low-to-medium biomass; the improved flag is therefore restricted to eutrophic waters.

A minimum MPH1 value of 0.02 combined with an NDVI threshold of 0.2 is used to distinguish genuine floating vegetation/cyanobacterial scum (a 753 nm reflectance peak) from a spurious 753 nm peak caused by the adjacency effect (stray light from adjacent land) or, rarely, sun glint.

Adjacency effects (stray light scattered from brighter adjacent land pixels into the sensor field of view) can contribute more than 50% of the at-sensor NIR radiance in spatially constrained inland/near-coastal waters, and can be observed many kilometers offshore.

MPH chlorophyll-a estimates were matched against several thousand in-situ chl-a measurements from more than 40 lakes worldwide and were found to be more robust than several other algorithms specifically in eutrophic-to-hypertrophic waters.

In a 9-lake matchup comparison, MPH chl-a reached regression coefficients up to R > 0.6, with correlation strength statistically similar to the FLH and MCI spectral indices (MPH best in 4/9 lakes, MCI in 3/9, FLH in 2/9), but MPH's regression slope was far less variable across water types than FLH/MCI, which the authors interpret as MPH being better suited to deriving absolute chl-a concentrations across trophic states.

Unlike MPH, the FLH and MCI products used as a comparison benchmark are index values only and do not themselves provide quantitative chlorophyll-a concentration estimates, which qualifies the 'comparable correlation' finding.

Despite generally stable performance across water types, MPH tends to systematically overestimate chlorophyll-a relative to in-situ measurements, and the authors conclude the MPH coefficients need to be re-trained on a global dataset.
Within the algorithm's processing logic, a user-configurable chl-a threshold — typically 350 mg m-3 — is used to raise a 'floating matter' flag once cyanobacteria-attributed chl-a exceeds this level.

9-lake regression comparison: maximum R > 0.6 (from about 40 processing schemes tested); MPH gave the best correlation in 4/9 lakes vs. MCI in 3/9 and FLH in 2/9

title: Internal phosphorus loading in Canadian fresh waters: a critical review and data analysis
what: A peer-reviewed, invited critical literature review combined with a quantitative meta-analysis (plus narrative case studies of 7 lakes) of internal phosphorus (P) loading - the release of P from freshwater sediments back into the overlying water, a process linked to harmful algal blooms - across Canadian fresh waters.

claims.c: The review consolidated 618 estimates of internal phosphorus (P) loading from 43 publications, covering 70 Canadian water bodies (ponds, lakes, reservoirs, and coastal wetlands).
Short-term gross sediment-core P release rates (Lgross), expressed as total phosphorus, ranged from -27 to 54 mg P m^-2 d^-1 across 461 estimates.
Long-term net whole-ecosystem P loading rates (Lnet), expressed as total phosphorus, ranged from -1694 to 10,640 mg P m^-2 yr^-1 across 157 estimates.
The main environmental drivers of variation in internal P loading across the dataset were oxygen, pH, geology, and trophic state.
Geographically, the highest sediment P release rates were found in small prairie lakes (Manitoba, Saskatchewan, Alberta), and the lowest in Canadian Shield lakes (Ontario and the Maritimes).
The review included narrative case studies of seven named lakes: Lake Simcoe, Lake Winnipeg, Lake of the Woods, Lake Erie, Lake Champlain, Cootes Paradise, and Lake Diefenbaker.

The authors frame internal P loading as a process that can undercut nutrient-management efforts and slow lake recovery even after external phosphorus inputs are reduced, meaning different lakes should have different recovery expectations.

The authors/press materials identify explicit knowledge gaps: it is unclear whether internal P loading is an important process in the many thousands of Canadian reservoirs, and there are large data gaps for northern lakes undergoing rapid environmental change.

The review's companion database compiled phosphorus-flux measurements using multiple distinct methodologies (core incubation, porewater profiling, whole-system mass balance, and modeling) and multiple phosphorus fractions (SRP, TDP, TP), with a built-in quality-control flagging mechanism.
The review's geology and pH classification of study lakes drew on established external reference schemes: bedrock/rock-type data from Wheeler et al. (1996) and pH categories from Hounslow (1995).
The paper was selected by the Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences as an 'Editors' Choice' article for 2017.
UNVERIFIED (secondary source only): long-term internal P loading has been characterized elsewhere as 60%-130% of external P loading in some Canadian lakes, attributed to this paper.

title: Interplay of Nutrients, Temperature, and Competition of Native and Alien Cyanobacteria Species Growth and Cyanotoxin Production in Temperate Lakes
what: A laboratory experimental study (Toxins, 2021) using eight cyanobacterial strains isolated from four Lithuanian lakes that ran two controlled experiments — a nutrient (phosphorus concentration × N:P ratio) manipulation measuring growth rate and cyanotoxin (microcystin, saxitoxin, non-ribosomal peptide) production, and

claims.c: Under nutrient manipulation, native Aphanizomenon gracile and alien Sphaerospermopsis aphanizomenoides grew fastest of the four tested strains, while native Planktothrix agardhii grew slowest (and most variably).
P. agardhii's own maximum growth rate required both the highest phosphorus level tested and the highest N:P ratio tested.
Phosphorus (IP) and nitrogen (IN) concentration were significant GLM factors for growth rate of P. agardhii, A. gracile, and S. aphanizomenoides, but not for C. bergii.
No statistically significant relationship was found between A. gracile's growth rate, phosphorus concentration, or N:P ratio and its saxitoxin (STX) concentration.

In the toxic P. agardhii strain, phosphorus concentration was strongly negatively correlated with several microcystin/non-ribosomal-peptide variants (dmMC-RR, APs, CPs) — i.e., lower phosphorus was associated with higher toxin content per unit biomass.
A nontoxic (non-microcystin-producing) P. agardhii strain contained markedly higher concentrations of other bioactive oligopeptides than the microcystin-producing (toxic) strain.
In the interspecies competition experiment, native A. gracile achieved substantially higher biomass than native P. agardhii when co-cultured under high-nutrient, warmed (24°C) conditions.
Alien S. aphanizomenoides suppressed native P. agardhii's biomass more strongly than the reverse, especially under warmed, nutrient-enriched conditions.
Native A. gracile suppressed the biomass of both alien species (S. aphanizomenoides and C. bergii) in co-culture relative to their monoculture controls.
The authors characterize S. aphanizomenoides as a stronger invader of the two alien species tested, attributing this to broader nutrient/temperature tolerance (ecological plasticity).
Overall, native A. gracile had a suppressive (negative) effect on the biomass of both alien species and of native P. agardhii across the competition experiment.
Nutrient (IP/IN) concentration had the strongest effect on competition outcomes, followed by temperature and then species origin/identity of the competitor.

The authors state that insufficient biomass produced by P. agardhii strains at lower phosphorus concentrations limited their ability to draw firm conclusions about how nutrients affect microcystin and non-ribosomal-peptide production.

The authors state that scientific understanding of what regulates saxitoxin production in cyanobacteria, and of the mechanisms underlying interspecies competition among bloom-forming cyanobacteria, remains limited, and call for more complex, multi-stressor/multi-species experiments.

title: Laboratory-scale evaluation of algaecide effectiveness for control of microcystin-producing cyanobacteria from Lake Okeechobee, Florida (USA)
what: A laboratory toxicity study (Kinley-Baird et al. 2021, Ecotoxicology and Environmental Safety, Vol. 207, Art. 111233; DOI 10.1016/j.ecoenv.2020.111233) that exposed microcystin-producing cyanobacteria samples collected from Lake Okeechobee, Florida to seven USEPA-registered algaecide formulations in the lab, measured c

claims.c: Growth of microcystin-producing cyanobacteria in Lake Okeechobee and surrounding waters has caused adverse health impacts to humans and endangered species and significant economic losses, which motivates the search for effective management tactics.

Seven USEPA-registered algaecide formulations were evaluated against Lake Okeechobee cyanobacteria in laboratory exposure experiments: GreenClean Liquid 5.0 alone; GreenClean Liquid 5.0 + Hydrothol 191; and five copper-based products (Algimycin PWF, Argos, Captain XTR, Cutrine Ultra, SeClear); multiple formulations achieved significant and comparable control of the target cyanobacteria.

Cyanobacterial response to each algaecide was assessed 3 days after treatment (DAT) using three endpoints — cell density, chlorophyll-a, and phycocyanin — and a minimum effective exposure concentration was derived per product from those responses (exact concentration values are not given in the abstract).

'Microcystin release' was explicitly defined by the authors as the proportion of total microcystin in the aqueous (dissolved) phase rather than intracellular, and this was compared across the effective treatments at 1 DAT.

Total microcystin concentration was measured at 1, 4, and 9 days after treatment in the effective treatments specifically to test whether microcystin would persist in the water after cyanobacterial control was achieved.

The four chelated copper-based products (Algimycin PWF, Argos, Captain XTR, Cutrine Ultra) showed relatively less microcystin release at 1 DAT and lower total microcystin concentration at 4 DAT than the other effective treatments — i.e., comparable cyanobacterial control did not mean comparable short-term toxin release.

By 9 days after treatment, every evaluated treatment had significantly lower total microcystin concentration than untreated controls, i.e. no evaluated product showed elevated residual/rebounding toxin at that timepoint.

[LOW-CONFIDENCE / UNVERIFIED against directly-fetched text] A widely-repeated figure states total microcystin concentration declined from about 494 μg/L to less than 10 μg/L within 9 days in most treatments.

The authors explicitly frame this laboratory-scale study as a deliberate, preliminary first step — undertaken specifically to reduce uncertainty before larger-scale application — whose output is comparative decision-support data, not a field-validated efficacy claim.

The lead/corresponding author is affiliated with a commercial aquatic-management company (Aquatic Control, Inc.) and a second author with a commercial environmental consultancy (EA Engineering, Science, and Technology, Inc.), while the remaining co-authors are academic (University of Florida/IFAS; Clemson University Dept. of Forestry and Environmental Conservation).

title: Limnological data derived from high frequency monitoring buoys are asynchronous in a large lake
what: An open-access PLOS ONE study (Stevens et al., 2025) that used two warm seasons (May–October 2021 and 2022) of hourly sensor data from 10 static high-frequency monitoring buoys in the western basin of Lake Erie to quantify spatial synchrony/asynchrony of water temperature, dissolved oxygen, turbidity, chlorophyll-a and

claims.c: Water temperature was highly synchronous across the buoy network, whereas dissolved oxygen, turbidity, chlorophyll-a and phycocyanin were asynchronous.
The degree of asynchrony increased with increasing spatial distance between buoy pairs, significantly so for dissolved oxygen (both years), turbidity and chlorophyll (2022), and temperature (2022).

Using r=0.5 ('moderately strong') as the synchrony threshold, the predicted buoy separation needed to reach that correlation was far larger for temperature (~108 km) than for the bio-optical/chemical variables (~18–46 km).

Extrapolating the dissolved-oxygen distance-correlation model to the entire surface of Lake Erie (not just the western basin), the authors calculate at least 1,239 buoys would be needed to fully resolve its spatial complexity.

Moran's Index values were close to zero for every variable studied, including temperature, indicating no classic spatial autocorrelation even where the distance-correlation regressions were statistically significant.

The ten-buoy network did not capture the full spatial complexity of the western basin, and data from an individual buoy should not be extrapolated to conditions (including cyanobacterial blooms) outside the area it monitors.
Synchrony was generally higher near the start (May) and end (October) of the sampling season, with turbidity, chlorophyll and phycocyanin least synchronous during August-September.

The authors interpret the observed asynchrony as reflecting basin hydrodynamics (Detroit and Maumee River inflows, wind-driven mixing, weak/variable stratification, hypoxic backflow from the central basin) combined with spatially patchy biological processes such as localized cyanobacterial blooms -- an interpretation of plausible mechanism, not a causally demonstrated effect.

The authors recommend that buoy placement be tailored to specific monitoring objectives and that anchored high-frequency buoys be supplemented with other data-collection methods, since no monitoring program can feasibly deploy enough buoys to fully resolve spatial complexity in a large lake.

The buoy-spacing guidance is derived from a single region of a single lake over one warm season repeated across two years, and the authors caution against generalizing without testing on other large lakes.
The study used only surface-water sensor data, which the authors acknowledge limits its ability to investigate vertical (depth-related) processes.

Western Lake Erie is a major drinking-water source (about 11 million people) that experiences recurrent nuisance and harmful cyanobacterial blooms, which motivates the high-frequency monitoring network this study analyzes.

title: Machine Learning Approaches for Harmful Algal Bloom Prediction: Advances, Challenges, and Future Directions
what: A peer-reviewed review article (J. Environmental Engineering, Vol. 152, No. 8, article 03126007, published online 2026-05-29) by Nasrin Alamdari (Florida State University) that synthesizes 168 peer-reviewed studies (1997–2025) on machine-learning approaches to harmful algal bloom (HAB) prediction, surveying traditional

claims.c: The review frames HABs as causing an estimated $50 million in economic losses annually in the U.S. alone, in addition to public-health (toxin exposure) and ecosystem impacts, as motivation for ML-based prediction.
The paper is a literature-synthesis review covering 168 peer-reviewed studies on ML-based HAB prediction published between 1997 and 2025.
The review's scope spans traditional ML (random forest, SVM, gradient boosting) through deep learning (LSTM, CNN, transformer) and hybrid process-based/ML models.
Across the reviewed literature, deep learning models (particularly LSTM-based) showed the strongest reported performance: median R2 of 0.89 and accuracy exceeding 90% in many applications.
Hybrid process-based + ML models were reported as the highest-accuracy approach among reviewed studies, reaching a Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (NSE) of 0.991.

The review identifies persistent field-wide gaps despite ML advances: limited model transferability across water bodies, data scarcity/class imbalance, low explainable-AI (XAI) uptake (only 20% of the 168 studies), and a gap between research models and deployed operational early-warning systems.

The review sets out future research priorities: SHAP/attention-based interpretability, incorporating climate-change projections, transfer-learning frameworks for data-limited systems, and standardized benchmark datasets.

The review connects ML-based HAB prediction to regulatory/management use, specifically total maximum daily load (TMDL) development and compliance, nutrient load allocation, and scenario analysis for HAB-impaired waters.
The review frames rising HAB frequency/intensity as linked to climate change and anthropogenic nutrient loading, motivating demand for prediction systems.

title: Machine Learning-Based Early Warning Level Prediction for Cyanobacterial Blooms Using Environmental Variable Selection and Data Resampling
what: A peer-reviewed journal article (Toxics, MDPI, 2023) that builds and compares Artificial Neural Network and Random Forest classifiers to predict discrete cyanobacterial "algal alert levels" (defined by cell-density thresholds) in South Korea's Baekje Reservoir, using fused water-quality, hydrodynamic, and meteorologica

claims.c: The study converts continuous cyanobacteria cell-density measurements into discrete 'algal alert levels' with fixed numeric thresholds: L-0 (normal) <1,000 cells/mL, L-1 (caution) ≥1,000 and <10,000 cells/mL, L-2 (warning) ≥10,000 and <1,000,000 cells/mL, and L-3 (blooming) ≥1,000,000 cells/mL; only L-0/L-1/L-2 were modeled (L-3 excluded, presumably too rare/absent in the record).

The 345-record dataset used for modeling (Baekje Reservoir, Geum River, South Korea; March–November monitoring dates, 2013–2021) is heavily imbalanced toward the normal class: 269 records (78.0%) are L-0, 47 (13.6%) are L-1, and 29 (8.4%) are L-2.

Two feature-selection methods were compared over 14 candidate input variables: a linear dependence/regression test (p<0.05 threshold) that retained 11 variables (excluding water level, precipitation, and wind speed), and a non-linear Mutual Information (MI) score that retained 12 variables (excluding phosphate PO4-P and wind speed, both scoring MI=0).

Seven data-resampling techniques were evaluated to correct the training-set class imbalance: three oversampling methods (Random Oversampling, SMOTE, ADASYN), two undersampling methods (Cluster Centroid, Random Undersampling), and two hybrid oversample-then-clean methods (SMOTE–Edited Nearest Neighbor and SMOTE–Tomek Link); resampling was applied only within the training partition, not to the held-out test set.

The headline comparison in the abstract: the optimal Random Forest model trained on the original (non-resampled) data reached test-set per-class accuracies of 98.8% (L0), 50.0% (L1), and 50.0% (L2); the optimal RF model trained with SMOTE–ENN resampling instead reached 85.0% (L0), 85.7% (L1), and 100% (L2) on the same test evaluation.

For the optimal ANN configuration (non-linear/MI feature selection plus SMOTE–ENN resampling), test-step recall for the minority caution class L-1 rose from 64.3% to 71.4% relative to the pre-resampling comparison, but this came together with an overall test-accuracy decline of 9.8 percentage points in that same comparison — i.e., the ANN's gain for the rare class was not a free win.

The authors' overall conclusion is that correcting class imbalance via resampling had a bigger effect on model accuracy than changing the input-variable set via feature selection; the best RF result needed only resampling (no feature selection), while the best ANN result needed both feature selection and resampling, and RF was reported as outperforming ANN overall.

Both feature-selection methods excluded wind speed as a useful predictor in this reservoir; the authors explicitly flag that this differs from prior ocean/marine HAB studies that found wind speed a significant driver of bloom growth, transport, and diffusion, attributing the discrepancy to the much smaller spatial scale of a reservoir compared to the open ocean rather than treating their result as a general refutation.

The authors state that typical HAB water-quality monitoring frequency (weekly or monthly) is itself a limiting factor, which is why only 345 usable records across 9 years (2013–2021, winter months excluded) were available, with an average 8-day (range 4–62 day) gap between successive monitoring dates.

title: Mitigating cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms in aquatic ecosystems impacted by climate change and anthropogenic nutrients
what: A 2016 peer-reviewed review article in the journal Harmful Algae (vol. 54, pp. 213-222) by Hans W. Paerl and seven co-authors, surveying traditional and experimental strategies for mitigating cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (CyanoHABs) and arguing that climate-driven changes to watershed hydrology, water-body tempe

claims.c: Mitigating the global expansion of cyanobacterial harmful blooms (CyanoHABs) is framed as a major, ongoing challenge for both researchers and water-resource managers.

A range of traditional and experimental physical/biological control methods -- nutrient load reduction, artificial mixing and flushing, and omnivorous fish removal -- have already been used to try to reduce cyanoHAB occurrence.

Climate-driven changes to watershed hydrology/nutrient loading, lake and estuary temperature, and mixing regimes are described as favoring cyanobacteria over other phytoplankton, and as a factor that could change how well existing control measures perform.

Because climate change is said to influence virtually all mitigation strategies, the authors argue managers may need to set new (revised) nutrient input reduction targets and establish water-body-specific nutrient-bloom thresholds.

Physical-forcing control techniques specifically -- e.g., artificial flushing and mixing -- are said to require adjustment to remain effective as climate change alters the systems they are applied to.

The paper's stated purpose/scope is to review the current suite of CyanoHAB mitigation strategies and evaluate options for adapting and optimizing them for a future with greater human population pressure and climate change.

tensions: Not directly evident from the abstract text alone -- it makes no explicit comparison to, or contradiction of, other named studies. One point worth flagging for the reviewer: the paper's own keyword/MeSH tags cover both "Nitrogen" and "Phosphorus" (not just one), which touches the broader, sometimes contested question in the CyanoHAB mitigation literature of whether nitrogen-only, phosphorus-only, or dual N+P load reduction is the more defensible control target -- a debate that recurs across other Paerl-associated mitigation papers (e.g., a very

title: Modeling harmful algal blooms in a changing climate
what: A peer-reviewed review article (Ralston & Moore, Harmful Algae, published online 19 Dec 2019, journal-issue year 2020, vol. 91:101729; PMID 32057346; DOI 10.1016/j.hal.2019.101729) that surveys how harmful algal bloom (HAB) modeling is currently done and assesses whether/how those approaches can be extended to proj

claims.c: Statistical models are the most common tool for near-term HAB forecasting, but are poorly suited to longer-term (climate-scale) projection because performance degrades as forcing conditions move outside the range of the historical training data.

Process-based (mechanistic) models can, in principle, project HAB response to changing forcing conditions mechanistically, but are complex, hard to parameterize/calibrate, and can still fail if climate change causes key processes to emerge that were not represented during model development on historical data.

The review makes four explicit recommendations: (1) prioritize process-based models representing physical/biological drivers within a broader ecosystem context; (2) quantify/convey uncertainty via ensemble approaches and scenario planning; (3) use robust dynamical or statistical downscaling to bring coarse GCM output to the coastal scale; (4) evaluate HAB models against long-term observational records.

In a Puget Sound case study, a climate projection (Moore et al., 2011, as cited in this review) estimated that by the end of the 21st century the annual duration of environmental conditions favorable to Alexandrium catenella blooms would increase by about 2 weeks per year on average.

A follow-up, higher-resolution Puget Sound regional climate modeling study (Moore et al., 2015, as cited in this review) found atmospheric heating alone was projected to lengthen the favorable-growth window for A. catenella by about 30 days per year, a larger effect than projected changes in river discharge/upwelling timing on temperature and salinity.

Combining a 1982-2016 sea-surface-temperature record with laboratory-derived, temperature-dependent growth rates for Alexandrium catenella/fundyense and Dinophysis acuminata, a cited study found North Atlantic mean growth rates increased by about 0.01 per day over the period, with the duration of favorable growth conditions lengthening by 2 to 3 weeks; North Pacific-wide trends were less clear, though the Salish Sea and coastal Alaska showed increasingly favorable conditions.

A maximum-entropy species distribution model (Townhill et al., 2018, as cited in this review) projected that by 2055, on the northwest European shelf, Dinophysis acuta and Gymnodinium catenatum would show the largest poleward (northward) range shift of 200-500 km, while three other species (A. ostenfeldii, A. minutum, P. australis) were projected to shift their optimal habitat southward.

The authors identify a resolution mismatch between global climate models and coastal HAB-relevant processes: CMIP5-generation global earth-system models have nominal resolution of about 1 degree, and even a high-resolution (1/12 degree) global ocean model cannot resolve the baroclinic-Rossby-radius scale needed in more than 90% of the coastal ocean; reaching 70% coverage would require about 6 times higher resolution.

A statistical (logistic regression) near-term forecast model for toxic Pseudo-nitzschia blooms in the Monterey Bay/Santa Barbara Channel region, built on roughly 8 years of satellite ocean-color/SST and in-situ observations, detected 98% of toxic bloom events with a false-positive rate below 30%.

The 2015 'intense, widespread' Pseudo-nitzschia bloom along the U.S. West Coast co-occurred with anomalously warm water from the 2014-16 northeast Pacific marine heatwave; the review uses hedged language suggesting the bloom 'may have been fueled by' higher growth rates at warmer temperature plus upwelling-supplied nutrients, i.e. an association rather than a demonstrated causal mechanism.

Despite general acceptance that HABs are increasing globally in severity and extent, the authors state that mechanistically attributing observed trends specifically to climate change (versus other contributing anthropogenic/environmental factors) remains difficult.

The authors conclude that critically lacking long-term (multi-decade) HAB observational records — needed to distinguish genuine climate-driven trends from cyclic/natural variability and to validate downscaled projections — may be the single biggest impediment to developing HAB models that can effectively assess climate-change response.

Warming does not uniformly increase HAB risk across regions: for the ciguatera-associated genus Gambierdiscus, temperature-driven growth models project increased abundance/diversity and greater ciguatera fish poisoning (CFP) risk in the Gulf of Mexico, but an accompanying shift in species composition at higher temperatures suggests lower overall risk in the Caribbean.

The review structures current HAB modeling practice into two broad classes: statistical/empirical models that relate observed environmental forcing to HAB metrics (regression, machine learning, etc.), and process-based/mechanistic models that explicitly simulate the physical and biological processes governing bloom dynamics (e.g., temperature-dependent growth, mortality, transport).

Similarly, for Gambierdiscus, warming is projected to increase abundance/diversity and ciguatera risk in the Gulf of

title: New is not always better: Toxicity of novel copper based algaecides to Daphnia magna
what: A peer-reviewed laboratory ecotoxicology study (Kang, Mucci, Fang & Lürling, 2022, Ecotoxicology and Environmental Safety) comparing acute (48 h) and chronic (21 d) toxicity of traditional copper sulfate versus three novel/proprietary copper-based algaecide formulations (Captain XTR, SeClear, Lake Guard Blue) to th

claims.c: Acute (48 h) median-effect toxicity (EC50) was similar across all four copper-based compounds tested, falling in a narrow 0.5–0.6 mg Cu/L range, so acute EC50 alone did not clearly differentiate the novel formulations from traditional copper sulfate.
Despite similar EC50s, relative acute toxicity ranking differed by product: Lake Guard Blue and traditional copper sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O) were more acutely toxic than SeClear and Captain XTR.

In chronic (21-day) testing, the novel formulation SeClear had a lower (i.e., more potent/toxic) EC50 for reproduction and body length (0.274 mg Cu/L) than CuSO4·5H2O (0.436 mg Cu/L), Captain XTR (0.498 mg Cu/L), or Lake Guard Blue (0.295 mg Cu/L) — meaning this newer product was chronically more toxic than the traditional compound, not less.
Among the four compounds, Captain XTR had the strongest negative effect on Daphnia body weight, while CuSO4·5H2O had the least effect on body weight.

All four copper compounds significantly delayed age at first brood (a reproductive-timing endpoint), with delays of 1.8 days (Captain XTR), 2.0 days (CuSO4·5H2O), 2.3 days (Lake Guard Blue), and 3.2 days (SeClear) relative to control.
The intrinsic rate of population increase (r), a demographic/population-growth fitness metric, was lowest (0.145 per day) in the highest-dose SeClear treatment among all tested treatments.

Chemical equilibrium modelling indicated that most of the copper dosed in the experiments was chelated by EDTA present in the artificial exposure medium used for testing, meaning the measured toxicity reflects copper bioavailability under that specific lab medium's chemistry.

The study's combined acute and chronic results support the overall conclusion that the novel copper algaecide SeClear is more toxic to the non-target crustacean D. magna than the traditional copper algaecide (CuSO4·5H2O), contradicting an assumption that newer/proprietary copper formulations are inherently less harmful to non-target organisms.

The authors recommend that toxicity testing of copper compounds on target non-target organisms, accounting for local water chemistry, be conducted prior to each copper application, rather than relying on a product's general reputation or another product's data.

Here, chronic (21-day) exposure data show the novel formulation SeClear had the lowest (most potent) EC50 for reproduction and body length (0.274 mg Cu/L) of all four compounds tested — i.e., more chronically toxic to a standard non-target crustacean than traditional CuSO4·5H2O (0.436 mg Cu/L) —

title: Non-Nitrogen-Fixers or Nitrogen-Fixers? Factors Distinguishing the Dominance of Chroococcal and Diazotrophic Cyanobacterial Species
what: A 2022 field study of four small, shallow (max depth 4 m), eutrophic water bodies near Kraków, Poland, in which paired upper- and bottom-layer water chemistry (temperature, oxygen saturation, PO4^3-, NO3-, NH4+, SO4^2-, total dissolved iron) from 62 sampling events (May-October) was analyzed with decision-tree classifi

claims.c: Sulfate (SO4^2-) concentration was the single most important factor distinguishing whether non-nitrogen-fixing or nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria dominated, ranking above water temperature, phosphate, and oxygen saturation in both the upper- and bottom-layer decision-tree models.

In the upper water layer, sulfate above 69 mg/L was associated with dominance by non-nitrogen-fixing (chroococcal) taxa, while sulfate below that level was associated with nitrogen-fixing (diazotrophic) dominance.

In the bottom layer, Microcystis spp. (non-nitrogen-fixing) dominance occurred under a combination of sulfate >73 mg/L, a PO4:NH4 ratio >0.051, and oxygen saturation >7.5%, a combination present in 12% of samples.

Across the 62 sampling events, nitrogen-fixing taxa were the dominant cyanobacterium far more often than non-nitrogen-fixing taxa (57% vs. 17% of samples), with no clearly dominant species in the remaining samples (26%).

The authors' proposed mechanism is that sulfate inhibits molybdate uptake, and because molybdenum is a required cofactor for nitrogenase (N2-fixation) enzymes, elevated sulfate suppresses nitrogen fixation and thereby disadvantages diazotrophic cyanobacteria relative to non-fixers.
Total dissolved iron (TDFe), despite being one of the five candidate environmental drivers measured, was found not to meaningfully distinguish which cyanobacteria group dominated.

The authors' own sulfate-molybdenum limitation mechanism is not universally supported in the literature: they cite a contradicting study reporting no molybdenum limitation of nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria in a different set of eutrophic saline lakes, and flag their own explanation as potentially questionable as a result.
The authors explicitly characterize their causal explanation as partly speculative because they did not directly measure nitrogen-fixation rates or nutrient uptake in this study.

The statistical approach used classification (decision) trees (R package 'rpart'), fit separately on upper-layer and bottom-layer predictor sets, to classify which cyanobacterium taxon dominated at each of the 62 sampling events; tree depth was manually restricted to reduce overfitting risk.

The upper-layer decision-tree model correctly explained the dominant taxon in 74% of samples, and the bottom-layer model in 76% of samples — i.e., roughly a quarter of samples were not correctly classified by either layer's model.

The dataset comprised 62 total paired (upper 1 m + near-bottom ~10 cm above sediment) water samples collected May-October from four shallow, eutrophic water bodies near Kraków, Poland, sampled monthly outside bloom periods and weekly during blooms.

Prior laboratory culture experiments (cited from earlier literature, not this study's own field data) have shown that Aphanizomenon flos-aquae growth does not occur at or below 11°C, with a preference for higher temperatures (~20-25°C).

Within each layer's decision tree, A. flos-aquae dominance was recovered under two different combinations of environmental variables rather than one, which the authors interpret (speculatively) as possible evidence of different physiological strains of the species.

Trophic State Index (chlorophyll-a based): Piekary 64.7; Tyniec 66.1; Podkamycze 1 57.8; Podkamycze 2 65.1 (all classed 'eutrophy')
Water temperature range: upper layer 7.2-25.6 °C; bottom layer 7.0-24.1 °C
Oxygen saturation range: upper layer 11.8-236.6%; bottom layer 2.2-226.2%
PO4^3- range: upper layer 0-0.538 mg/L; bottom layer 0-0.573 mg/L
SO4^2- range: upper layer 21.2-100.1 mg/L; bottom layer 17.7-91.8 mg/L
NH4+ range: upper layer 0.02-0.78 mg/L; bottom layer 0.01-1.96 mg/L
NO3- range: upper layer 0.18-12.11 mg/L; bottom layer 0.14-13.65 mg/L

Cyanobacterial biomass range: overall 0-12.830 mg/L (Piekary 0-0.354; Tyniec 0-12.830; Podkamycze 1 0.11-5.61; Podkamycze 2 0.06-9.23 mg/L)
Dominance frequency (of 62 samples): non-nitrogen-fixing (chroococcal) 17%; nitrogen-fixing (diazotrophic) 57%; no dominant species 26%
tensions: Within the paper itself, the authors' central proposed mechanism (sulfate blocks molybdate uptake, starving the molybdenum-dependent nitrogenase enzyme and thereby suppressing N2-fixers) is directly complicated by their own cited counter-example: Evans & Prepas reportedly found nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria were NOT molybdenum-limited in a different set of eutrophic saline lakes with high sulfate/Mo ratios, leading the authors to call their own explanation "questionable." title: On the Chemistry, Toxicology and Genetics of the Cyanobacterial Toxins, Microcystin, Nodularin, Saxitoxin and Cylindrospermopsin
what: A 2010 open-access, peer-reviewed review article in Marine Drugs (Pearson, Mihali, Moffitt, Kellmann & Neilan) that synthesizes roughly a decade of genetic and biochemical research to summarize the chemistry (structural variants), toxicology (mechanism of action, potency, and health effects), and genetics/biosynthesis

claims.c: Cyanobacterial toxins fall into four toxicological classes—neurotoxins, hepatotoxins, cytotoxins, and dermatoxins (irritant toxins)—despite being structurally very diverse.
Microcystin is the largest and most chemically diverse cyanotoxin class: a monocyclic heptapeptide with roughly 90 known structural isoforms, the most common being microcystin-LR (MW 995.17).

Microcystin-LR is markedly more acutely lethal to mice than the rarer variant microcystin-RR (intraperitoneal LD50 50 µg/kg vs. 600 µg/kg), and both act by inhibiting serine/threonine protein phosphatases 1 and 2A.

The review raises a hypothesis-level (not proven-in-humans) concern that chronic, sub-lethal microcystin exposure could contribute to cancer risk, based on laboratory rodent studies showing tumor promotion.

The microcystin biosynthesis gene cluster (mcyS: 55 kb, 10 genes in two divergent operons mcyA–C and mcyD–J, sequenced from Microcystis aeruginosa PCC7806) was the first complete secondary-metabolite gene cluster ever fully sequenced from a cyanobacterium.

Microcystin gene transcription responds to light in a threshold-like way (distinct dark/low-light vs. medium/high-light regimes), and high light also shifts the toxin-variant mixture toward the more toxic DeLR isoform.

Nodularin (cyclic pentapeptide, MW 619, 7 known natural isoforms) inhibits PP1/PP2A at a similar potency to microcystin (IC50 ≈ 0.1 nM) but binds PP2A non-covalently rather than covalently, which the authors hypothesize may explain its additional carcinogenic potential relative to microcystin.

The nodularin biosynthesis gene cluster (ndaS: 48 kb, 9 ORFs, ndaA–I) was sequenced from Nodularia spumigena NSOR10 in 2004; its tailoring enzyme NdaH shares 71% sequence identity with the microcystin enzyme McyI.

Saxitoxin and its more than 30 natural derivatives are potent voltage-gated sodium-channel blockers; in mice the intraperitoneal LD50 is 10 µg/kg, the estimated human lethal dose is as little as 1 mg, and the toxin causes an estimated 2,000 paralytic shellfish poisoning (PSP) cases worldwide per year with a 15% mortality rate.

The saxitoxin (sxt) biosynthesis gene cluster spans more than 35 kb (26 proteins/30 catalytic functions) in Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii T3 and a somewhat smaller ~28 kb in Anabaena circinalis and Aphanizomenon sp.; phylogenetic analysis of the O-carbamoyltransferase gene suggests the cluster's most likely evolutionary origin is an ancestral alpha-proteobacterium, subsequently spread by horizontal gene transfer.

Whether saxitoxin-producing eukaryotes (e.g., puffer fish, the Panamanian golden frog) actually biosynthesize the toxin themselves or merely bioaccumulate it from other organisms is explicitly unresolved in the literature the review surveys.

Some published claims of bacterial (non-dinoflagellate) saxitoxin biosynthesis are considered controversial because the analytical methods used to support them were not definitive and could not be independently reproduced.

Cylindrospermopsin — a polyketide-derived alkaloid classed as hepatotoxic, cytotoxic and neurotoxic, and considered a potential carcinogen — caused a well-documented 1979 human poisoning outbreak on Palm Island, Queensland, Australia, hospitalizing 148 people with hepatoenteritis after a bloom of Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii contaminated a drinking-water reservoir.

The cylindrospermopsin biosynthesis gene cluster (cyr: 43 kb, 15 ORFs, sequenced from C. raciborskii AWT205) includes a proposed—but not fully confirmed—novel pyrimidine-biosynthesis mechanism for forming the toxin's uracil ring, a step the authors state had previously been unexplained.
The review states that research on microcystin (hepatotoxin) regulation lacks standardized experimental growth controls across studies, which it flags as a methodological weakness of that sub-field.

numbers: Microcystin: ~90 known structural isoforms
Microcystin-LR molecular weight: 995.17 (Da / g·mol⁻¹)
Microcystin-LR LD50 (mice, intraperitoneal): 50 µg/kg body weight
Microcystin-RR LD50 (mice, intraperitoneal): 600 µg/kg body weight
mcy (microcystin) gene cluster: 55 kb, 10 genes (M. aeruginosa PCC7806)
Microcystin light threshold 1 (dark→low light): 0–16 µmol photons m⁻² s⁻¹
Microcystin light threshold 2 (medium→high light): 31–68 µmol photons m⁻² s⁻¹
At high light: microcystin-DeLR content increases ~3-fold; microcystin-DeRR decreases ~2-fold
Nodularin molecular weight: 619 (Da / g·mol⁻¹)
Nodularin: 7 naturally occurring isoforms
Nodularin LD50 (mice, intraperitoneal): 50 µg/kg body weight
Microcystin/Nodularin PP2A inhibition IC50: ~0.1 nM
tensions: This review deliberately scopes itself to only four cyanotoxin classes (microcystin, nodularin, saxitoxin, cylindrospermopsin) and explicitly does not cover other cyanotoxins that appear elsewhere in the HAB literature (e.g., anatoxins); its toxin list should not be treated as exhaustive when read against broader HAB sources. Internally, the source itself surfaces two live scientific controversies relevant to any downstream HAB-toxin claim: (1) whether certain non-cyanobacterial/non-dinoflagellate organisms reported to carry saxitoxin actually

title: Perceived global increase in algal blooms is attributable to intensified monitoring and emerging bloom impacts
what: A global statistical meta-analysis (Hallegraeff et al. 2021, Communications Earth & Environment) that tests whether reported harmful algal bloom (HAB) events actually increased worldwide from 1985–2018, by combining the Harmful Algae Event Database (HAEDAT) with Ocean Biodiversity Information System (OBIS) occurrence r

claims.c: There is no statistically significant uniform global increasing trend in HAB events over 1985–2018 once data are adjusted for regional differences in monitoring/sampling effort.
The raw (unadjusted) HAEDAT event count does show a statistically significant increase globally over the study period.
After adjusting for OBIS-derived sampling effort, only a subset of regions show real increases or decreases; the pattern is not globally uniform.

Adjusting for sampling effort actually flips the direction (sign) of the trend in several regions compared to the raw/unadjusted data, demonstrating that monitoring effort is a real, non-trivial confounder rather than a minor correction.
Global aquaculture production grew roughly 16-fold between 1985 and 2018, and this growth correlates strongly and significantly with the number of reported HAB events.
The authors conclude there is no empirical support for broad, undifferentiated statements that HABs are increasing globally; any trend claims must be qualified by region and species.
The perceived global increase in HAB events is attributed largely to intensified observational/monitoring effort rather than a demonstrated uniform ecological increase.
Intensified monitoring tied to aquaculture growth, not necessarily climate change or nutrient pollution, is presented as the best-supported explanation for the perceived increase.

Data quality and monitoring coverage were themselves highly uneven over the study window: consistent phytoplankton/seafood-toxin monitoring only began in most regions in 1985–1990s, and in North Asia only from 2000, meaning the analyzed period partly captures monitoring-system build-out rather than a stable observation baseline.
Some regional datasets were too sparse to support trend analysis at all and were excluded from the formal statistics.

The dataset comprises 9,503 total HAEDAT events (as of the data cutoff), and OBIS sampling-effort proxy data comprise 5,944,392 microalgal records including 289,668 harmful-species distribution records.

Data prior to 1985 are too sparse and unreliable to determine whether a real HAB increase might already have been underway before the analyzed period, so the paper's 'no global trend' finding is explicitly bounded to 1985–2018 and cannot be extended backward.

numbers: Study period analyzed: 1985–2018
Total HAEDAT events logged: 9,503 (as of 10 December 2019)
OBIS microalgal records extracted: 5,944,392 total; 289,668 distribution records of IOC-UNESCO-listed harmful species
Unadjusted global trend (HAEDAT events vs. year): r=0.37, z=2.97, p=0.003 (significant)
Effort-adjusted global trend (HAEDAT events standardized by OBIS sampling effort vs. year): r=0.35, z=1.33, p=0.18 (not significant) — this is the paper's headline null result
Regions with significant increase after adjustment: 5 (Caribbean/Central America [CCA], South America [SAM], Mediterranean [MED], North Asia [NAS], Europe [EUR])
Regions with significant decrease after adjustment: 2 (West Coast North America [WCA], Australia/New Zealand [ANZ])
Regions with no significant change after adjustment: 2 (East Coast North America [ECA], Southeast Asia [SEA])
Regions whose trend direction reversed after adjustment vs. raw data: 4 (SAM, WCA, ANZ, NAS)
Global aquaculture production growth: 11.35 million tonnes (1985) to 178.5 million tonnes (2018) = ~16-fold increase
Correlation of aquaculture production with HAB event counts: r=0.43, z=3.59, p=0.0003

Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP): 3,800 human poisonings reported 1985–2018 globally; Philippines subset ~2,555 cases with 165 fatalities (single-fetch figure, not independently cross-verified in a second pass)
tensions: This source directly complicates any HAB-literature claim of the form "HABs are increasing globally due to climate change/nutrient pollution" — a framing common elsewhere in HAB basic-science literature and in public/regulatory messaging — by showing that a naive (unadjusted) global increase in reported HAEDAT events becomes statistically non-significant once monitoring/sampling effort (proxied via OBIS records) is controlled for, and that effort adjustment can even reverse the sign of regional trends. It is an important methodological caution

title: Phosphorus losses from monitored fields with conservation practices in the Lake Erie Basin, USA
what: A multi-year (2004–2013 surface runoff; 2008–2013 tile discharge), field-scale, before-after/control-impact monitoring study of four privately owned fields in the St. Joseph River watershed (headwaters of the Maumee River, Lake Erie basin, NE Indiana) that quantifies how four USDA Farm Bill-funded conservation practice

claims.c: A conservation corn–soybean–wheat–oat crop rotation cut phosphorus losses in surface runoff far more than any tillage change, reducing soluble P (SP) loads by 85% and total P (TP) loads by 83% versus a standard corn–soybean rotation.

No-till reduced total phosphorus loss in surface runoff relative to rotational tillage but nearly doubled soluble phosphorus loss, i.e., the two P fractions responded in opposite directions to the same practice.
Installing grassed waterways (intended to trap sediment) was followed by a significant increase in soluble P loss in surface runoff, while total P loss did not significantly change.

The novel "blind inlet" (a buried gravel/geotextile inlet replacing a standard tile riser on closed-depression fields) significantly reduced both soluble P and total P losses in surface runoff compared with the conventional tile riser.

None of the four conservation practices produced a statistically significant change in phosphorus loads moving through subsurface tile drainage (the one near-exception, conservation crop rotation's SP tile load, only reached p<0.15, short of the study's own 0.10 significance threshold).

At this study site, a large share (20–80%) of phosphorus loss occurs via the subsurface tile network rather than surface runoff, yet most of the conservation practices tested were originally designed to control surface erosion, not tile transport.

None of the conservation practices changed the volume of surface runoff or tile discharge produced by the fields, so the observed concentration/load differences reflect phosphorus dynamics rather than changes in water quantity.

On the actual acreage where conservation practices were adopted in the watershed (2005–2013), the practices are estimated to have cut soluble P loads by 17% and total P loads by 58% relative to an untreated baseline for that same land.

Aggregated across the whole St. Joseph River watershed, the conservation practices adopted 2005–2013 are estimated to have kept roughly 340 kg of soluble P and 8,800 kg of total P out of surface water per growing season (combining surface and tile pathways).

As of the 2005–2013 adoption period, conservation practices had been applied on only about 12.8% of the St. Joseph River watershed's land area, meaning the estimated benefit above reflects a small fraction of the total watershed.

A large share of annual total phosphorus loss from the Maumee River basin occurs outside the April–November growing season that this study monitored, so the study's growing-season-only field data likely understate the full annual loss (and potential annual benefit of practices).

The authors conclude that the Farm Bill conservation practices studied could plausibly achieve the region's total-phosphorus reduction target for Lake Erie, but will not by themselves achieve the region's soluble-phosphorus reduction target, so additional strategies beyond these four practices are needed.

The authors explicitly caution that a documented rise in soluble P concentration in the Maumee River coinciding with conservation-tillage adoption should not be read as proof that conservation tillage caused the rise.

The study's results (no-till cutting sediment/TP while increasing SP) directly contradict the older working assumption in conservation planning that controlling sediment loss automatically controls phosphorus loss.

No-till growing-season surface SP load: 22.9 g/ha; rotational tillage: 11.6 g/ha; conventional tillage: 5.1 g/ha (p<0.10)
No-till growing-season surface TP load: 98 g/ha; rotational tillage: 321 g/ha (p<0.001); conventional tillage: 51.7 g/ha

Conservation rotation surface TP FWM: 0.36 mg/L vs corn-soybean 0.80 mg/L (p<0.001)
Conservation rotation surface SP load: 4.6 g/ha vs corn-soybean 31 g/ha (p<0.05)
tensions: (1) This source directly complicates a simple "reduce erosion/sediment → protect the lake" narrative that could otherwise be assumed in HAB-driver literature: the authors state plainly that the old planning assumption — "if you stop the sediment you will stop the P" — "has been disproven," because no-till cut TP and sediment (sediment reduced "as much as 82%" per the Discussion) while nearly doubling soluble P (SP) loss, the bioavailable P fraction most implicated in Lake Erie cyanobacterial blooms. title: Phycocyanin concentration retrieval in inland waters: A comparative review of the remote sensing techniques and algorithms
what: A 2018 peer-reviewed review article in the Journal of Great Lakes Research (Yan, Bao & Shao) that surveys remote-sensing techniques and retrieval algorithms used to estimate phycocyanin (PC) concentration — the pigment signature of cyanobacteria — in inland waters, comparing empirical and semi-analytical inversion appr

claims.c: Phycocyanin is framed as a more suitable pigment than chlorophyll-a for monitoring cyanobacterial blooms and toxic cyanobacteria.
Phycocyanin's absorption peak is at approximately 620 nm, which is why the 615-630 nm wavelength band is the target range for PC-specific remote-sensing inversion algorithms.
The field has produced both empirical and semi-analytical algorithms that use remotely sensed PC concentration as a proxy for cyanobacterial blooms.
Unlike chlorophyll-a, large-scale remote-sensing estimation of PC concentration remains constrained by a scarcity of data with sufficient spatial and spectral resolution.

The review's explicit purpose is to give a comprehensive overview of remote-sensing techniques and retrieval algorithms for PC monitoring, with emphasis on inversion algorithms and their realization on current and prospective sensors.

The review concludes with a detailed discussion of the overall challenges and potential of remote-sensing-based cyanobacterial PC pigment retrieval, built on its analysis of state-of-the-art techniques and algorithms.

numbers: ~620 nm — approximate wavelength of phycocyanin's absorption peak, per the abstract (stated as a physical/spectral property; no uncertainty or baseline given)
615–630 nm — wavelength range described in the abstract as attractive for PC-targeted inversion algorithms (no uncertainty or baseline given)
tensions: By the source's own framing, PC-specific retrieval is more data- and resolution-constrained at large scale than Chl-a retrieval. This is a scope caveat worth weighing against any HAB analysis (including this project's) that leans on phycocyanin-band products versus chlorophyll-a or composite cyanobacteria-index products (e.g., EPA CyAN's cyanobacteria index) for broad-coverage or near-real-time screening: the source implies PC-band retrieval may currently be harder to scale than Chl-a-based approaches. This is stated as a scope/data-availabilit

title: Predicting Cyanobacteria dominance in lakes
what: A short (~4-page) peer-reviewed paper in the Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences (2001) that compiles data from 99 well-studied temperate-zone lakes to test whether the nitrogen-to-phosphorus (N:P) ratio, versus absolute total phosphorus, total nitrogen, or standing algal biomass, better predicts cyanoba

claims.c: The paper explicitly frames itself as challenging an established ecological precept: that low N:P ratios cause noxious/toxic cyanobacteria blooms, a precept it says rests on prior observational and lab/field-experimental evidence.
Using a compiled dataset of 99 well-studied temperate-zone lakes, the authors conclude the N:P-ratio model of cyanobacteria dominance is flawed.

Water-quality degradation risk from cyanobacteria blooms correlates more strongly with variation in total phosphorus (TP), total nitrogen (TN), or standing algal biomass than with the N:P ratio itself.
The authors conclude cyanobacteria-associated risk is better explained by simple increases in nutrient concentration and algal biomass than by the N:P ratio.

The paper motivates its topic by framing cyanobacteria dominance as a major human and ecosystem health risk, which is why stoichiometric (N:P) control had become central to freshwater resource management prior to this study.

numbers: n = 99 lakes ("of the temperate zone's most studied lakes") — the dataset size underlying the cross-lake statistical comparison; no confidence interval/uncertainty is given in the abstract itself.
Bibliographic locator: Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences, Vol. 58, Issue 10, pp. 1905–1908 (2001) — from OpenAlex structured metadata.
Cited-by count as of fetch (July 2026): 749 (OpenAlex) vs. 807 (Semantic Scholar) — the two bibliometric indexes disagree; included only as bibliometric context, not a scientific finding.

UNVERIFIED / excluded from claims: a figure of "269 observations" (purportedly from the 99 lakes) appeared identically in two independent WebSearch summaries, but I could not match it to a directly fetched, verbatim prim
tensions: This paper is itself framed as a direct challenge to an earlier, established N:P-ratio paradigm in aquatic ecology (the abstract calls it a "controversial precept... based on... lab and field experiments"), so its conclusion — that absolute nutrient/biomass levels outperform the N:P ratio as correlates of bloom risk — is a known point of contention rather than settled consensus in the HAB literature. It is also worth flagging (though NOT verified as part of this source, and explicitly excluded from key_claims/data_numbers above) that other resu

title: Quantifying sample biases of inland lake sampling programs in relation to lake surface area and land use/cover
what: A statistical/methodological study that quantifies systematic sampling bias in non-probability-based inland lake monitoring programs run by six U.S. state agencies (MI, WI, IA, OH, ME, NH), by comparing which lakes get monitored against the full census of lakes in each state (from the National Hydrography Dataset) with

claims.c: Across all six study states, larger lakes have a consistently higher probability of being included in state-agency lake monitoring programs than smaller lakes.

Sampling probability is related to surrounding land use/land cover (LULC), but a significant lake-size × LULC statistical interaction prevents the authors from isolating a clean 'main effect' of LULC; the overall qualitative pattern is that lakes most likely to be sampled tend to have high urban land use, high agricultural land use, high forest cover, or low wetland cover.

Study design: state-monitored ('non-probability based') lakes from six state agencies were compared against a census population of all lakes (derived from the National Hydrography Dataset), and the probability of a lake being sampled was modeled using generalized linear mixed models.

The authors conclude that data from non-probability-based lake surveys must be used cautiously when generalizing to the full population of lakes, and that probability-based survey designs are needed for unbiased, accurate estimates of lake status and trends at regional-to-national scales.

The study specifically examined LULC-related bias because surrounding land use is already an established driver of lake water quality, so biased selection of lakes by LULC context could distort water-quality conclusions drawn from monitoring-network data.

The publicly accessible text of this source (the PubMed abstract) contains no quantitative statistical results — no percentages, odds ratios/effect sizes, per-state sample sizes (numbers of lakes), or p-values are reported; only qualitative/directional findings are stated. No open-access full text or PDF of the paper (which would contain such numbers, plus Methods/Results tables and a Discussion/Limitations section) could be located.

numbers: Six (6) U.S. state monitoring agencies analyzed: Michigan, Wisconsin, Iowa, Ohio, Maine, New Hampshire (count of jurisdictions, not a measured statistic)
Citation numbers only: Environmental Monitoring and Assessment, Volume 141, Issue 1-3, pages 131-147, published June 2008 (ePub 28 Aug 2007); DOI 10.1007/s10661-007-9883-z; PMID 17724567

No quantitative results (percentages, odds ratios/effect sizes, per-state lake counts/sample sizes, confidence intervals, or p-values) for the sampling-probability GLMMs are present in the publicly accessible abstract te
tensions: This is not a HAB-specific ecological finding but a monitoring-design/statistics paper, so it does not directly contradict any HAB causal claim. However, it creates an important tension for HAB analyses (including this project's) that draw 'in-situ aquatic data' from state DEQ lake-monitoring programs or aggregators like the Water Quality Portal as though those records represent a random/unbiased sample of lakes: this source shows such agency-run networks are systematically skewed toward larger lakes and toward particular surrounding land-use c

title: Rapid flotation of Microcystis wesenbergii mediated by high light exposure: implications for surface scum formation and cyanobacterial species succession
what: A laboratory experimental study comparing two single-cell Microcystis strains (M. wesenbergii FACHB-908 and M. aeruginosa FACHB-905) under high light to identify a non-buoyancy-driven, hours-scale flotation mechanism (photosynthetic micro-bubble trapping plus EPS-mediated cell aggregation) that produces rapid surface s

claims.c: Under high light (150 μmol photons s⁻¹ m⁻²), M. wesenbergii migrated upward rapidly and formed visible surface scum within 4 hours, whereas M. aeruginosa did not form visible scum for the duration of the experiment.

The rapid flotation is associated with photosynthesis-driven dissolved-oxygen (DO) supersaturation and formation of micro-bubbles (50–200 µm diameter) that become trapped in/on cell aggregates, providing lift to the surface.
DO rose far faster under light in M. wesenbergii (5 mg L⁻¹ h⁻¹) than in M. aeruginosa (2 mg L⁻¹ h⁻¹), with M. wesenbergii reaching the DO probe's detection limit (42 mg/L) within about 300 minutes.

M. wesenbergii secreted significantly more extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) - both dissolved and bound polysaccharides and proteins - than M. aeruginosa, and this EPS is proposed to mediate cell-cell adhesion via a 'bridging' network of functional groups.

Zeta potential of M. wesenbergii cell suspensions became markedly less negative over the light exposure (from below −30 mV toward about −5 mV), consistent with reduced electrostatic repulsion enabling aggregation, while M. aeruginosa showed no significant zeta-potential change.

M. wesenbergii's surface-layer samples had much higher polysaccharide concentrations (dissolved 41.8 mg/L, bound 72.2 mg/L) than its mixed/water-column samples (dissolved 10.7 mg/L, bound 6.6 mg/L), whereas M. aeruginosa showed no significant difference in polysaccharide content between surface and water-column samples.

The observed hours-scale scum formation is faster than the days-to-weeks timescale typically described for conventional buoyancy (gas-vesicle)-driven Microcystis bloom/scum formation, implying an additional mechanism operates under strong light.

The authors conclude the mechanism is species-specific and propose it has implications for surface scum formation and succession among cyanobacterial species (i.e., which Microcystis species/morphotypes come to dominate a bloom's surface layer).

The authors' three-part conclusion is that (a) EPS from M. wesenbergii contributes to large aggregate formation, (b) aggregate plus micro-bubble formation together drive surface scum formation, and (c) light and EPS jointly contribute to formation of the large algal aggregate.

The authors explicitly caveat that their experiments used laboratory strains under simplified indoor conditions and that the mechanism still needs to be tested in natural samples/populations, which may behave differently (e.g., environmental M. wesenbergii forms large colonies with complex vertical-migration dynamics).

numbers: Experimental light intensity: 150 μmol photons s⁻¹ m⁻²
Culture maintenance light: 32 μmol photons s⁻¹ m⁻², 16 h light / 8 h dark cycle
Room/incubation temperature: 25°C
Initial chlorophyll-a concentration (both strains): ~800 μg/L
M. wesenbergii surface scum formation time: within 4 hours
M. aeruginosa: no visible scum formed throughout the experiment
Bubble onset: ~1 hour after experiment start; stable/numerous bubbles by ~2 hours
Micro-bubble diameter: 50–200 µm
Initial DO concentration (both strains): ~10.8 mg/L
DO increase rate under light, M. wesenbergii: 5 mg L⁻¹ h⁻¹
DO increase rate under light, M. aeruginosa: 2 mg L⁻¹ h⁻¹
DO probe detection limit reached (M. wesenbergii): 42 mg/L at ~300 minutes

title: Recent Advances in the Ecology of Bloom-Forming Raphidiopsis (Cylindrospermopsis) raciborskii: Expansion in China, Intraspecific Heterogeneity and Critical Factors for Invasion
what: A 2023 open-access narrative literature review (Int. J. Environ. Res. Public Health 20(3):1984, DOI 10.3390/ijerph20031984) that synthesizes published research on the ecology and invasion of the bloom-forming, toxin-producing cyanobacterium Raphidiopsis (Cylindrospermopsis) raciborskii in China. It focuses on the speci

claims.c: R. raciborskii has expanded from tropical/subtropical China into temperate zones over recent decades and is now documented across a wide latitudinal/longitudinal range spanning eight major river basins.
The first reported R. raciborskii bloom in China occurred in 2002 in the Pearl River (Zhujiang) delta area.
In Liangzi Lake (Hubei), R. raciborskii biomass rose from a low background level first noted in 2006 to a bloom level of 52.03 mg/L during 2013–2017.
The Haihe River basin currently marks the northernmost documented limit of R. raciborskii presence in China.

R. raciborskii dominance and blooms have been observed in relatively cool water (10-15 °C), and there is marked strain-to-strain variation in cold tolerance: some/several strains sustain high growth at 15 °C while others are completely growth-inhibited at similarly low temperatures.

Competition outcomes between R. raciborskii and Microcystis aeruginosa are highly strain-dependent and vary with experimental conditions (temperature, phosphorus source, light limitation, pH/inorganic carbon), with no single consistent winner across studies.

R. raciborskii is a major producer of the hepatotoxin cylindrospermopsin (CYN); Chinese isolates show genetic variation in the CYN-synthesis genes cyrI (4 main sequence types) and cyrJ (3 main sequence types), with lower-latitude reservoirs harboring more sequence types, consistent with a southeast-China diversity/origin center.

Toxigenic strains producing paralytic shellfish poison (PSP, i.e., saxitoxin-group toxins) have also been isolated and identified from Chinese R. raciborskii populations, and these are phylogenetically close to strains from North and South America.

R. raciborskii can dominate under both phosphorus-limited and nitrogen-limited/fluctuating conditions via a physiological toolkit: strong P storage plus alkaline-phosphatase-mediated use of dissolved organic phosphorus under P limitation, and N2 fixation plus cyanophycin N storage under N depletion; the review states it can dominate at both very low and high N:P ratios, but gives no specific concentration or ratio thresholds.

Selective grazing pressure affects R. raciborskii establishment: Daphnia grazing on R. raciborskii can suppress its population, whereas copepods that preferentially graze on co-occurring non-Raphidiopsis taxa can indirectly favor R. raciborskii persistence.

The authors argue that a 'microevolution' framework (ongoing fine-grained genetic/phenotypic diversification) explains the coexistence of different R. raciborskii phenotypes/genotypes within a single environment better than the traditional 'ecotype' concept, and propose this intraspecific heterogeneity as a driving force behind the species' geographic expansion.

R. raciborskii has spread among China's disconnected water bodies via multiple physical dispersal pathways, including engineered water-diversion projects — e.g., a project linking five lakes in the Huaihe River basin with three reservoirs — plus river flow, flood-linked floodplain lakes, ship ballast water, aquatic-product transport water, and migratory birds carrying akinetes.
