title: A Review of Harmful Algal Blooms: Causes, Effects, Monitoring, and Prevention Methods
what: A 2025 open-access narrative literature review (Water journal, MDPI) by Brenckman et al. (NJIT) that synthesizes existing literature and case studies on the causes, ecological/health/economic effects, monitoring and detection technologies, and prevention/mitigation strategies for harmful algal blooms (HABs). It is a te

claims.c: HABs are framed as a growing environmental concern with adverse impacts spanning aquatic ecosystems, human health, and economic activity.
Bloom formation is attributed to a combination of nutrient enrichment, environmental factors, and hydrological conditions.
HABs produce toxins that threaten aquatic biodiversity, contaminate drinking water, and cause economic losses in fisheries and tourism.
Agricultural runoff, wastewater discharge, and industrial pollution are identified as the specific anthropogenic sources introducing excess nitrogen and phosphorus that fuel bloom formation.
Climate change is presented as an amplifying factor for HABs via altered precipitation patterns, rising water temperatures, and intensified coastal upwelling.
The review identifies satellite remote sensing, molecular biotechnology, and AI-driven predictive models as the effective monitoring/detection methods offering promise for early intervention.
Nutrient load reductions, bioremediation, and regulatory policy are named as sustainable management strategies that can help mitigate HAB effects.

Public awareness and community involvement (responsible agricultural practices, reduced waste discharge, conservation support) are described as playing a crucial role in preventing and managing HAB events.

The paper self-describes as a review of existing literature and case studies (not original data collection or modeling), concluding that comprehensive, interdisciplinary approaches are urgently needed to regulate HABs.
The publication's own keyword metadata confirms the review's scope spans HAB causes, impacts, monitoring/detection, mitigation, and public awareness/education.

tensions: Not directly assessable from the abstract alone; no explicit disagreement with other HAB literature is stated in the accessible text. Flag for reviewers: the abstract uses causal-sounding language ("driven by," "fueling... formation," "exacerbates," "create favorable conditions") to link nutrients, hydrology, and climate change to bloom occurrence, without itself flagging correlation-vs-causation caveats in the visible text. Given this project's requirement to phrase driver/effect claims carefully, any causal statements drawn from this source f

title: Advancing CyanoHAB monitoring with hyperspectral data from NASA PACE: First results and validation
what: A 2026 journal article reporting the first validation of NASA's newly launched PACE mission hyperspectral Ocean Color Instrument (OCI) for cyanobacterial harmful algal bloom (cyanoHAB) monitoring, benchmarking OCI-derived Cyanobacteria Index / cell-density and chlorophyll-a retrievals against the operational multispect

claims.c: The study is presented as the first assessment of PACE OCI hyperspectral imagery for cyanoHAB monitoring, directly comparing it against Sentinel-3 OLCI multispectral imagery and the operational CyAN product using summer-2024 bloom events in three water bodies.

Benchmarked against the operational CyAN product, OCI-derived cyanobacterial cell density (CCD) showed strong agreement overall (R²=0.84, NRMSE=8.95%), but with an approximate 11% negative bias specifically at extreme/highest-density bloom pixels.
Validated against in-situ chlorophyll-a measurements, PACE OCI produced substantially more accurate biomass retrievals than the CyAN/OLCI operational product (NRMSE roughly half: 21.57% vs. 38.67%).
PACE OCI reproduced spatial bloom patterns comparable to Sentinel-3 OLCI across the three study lakes during the summer-2024 bloom season.

The authors frame the results as demonstrating that hyperspectral PACE OCI can extend/improve on the existing CyAN operational monitoring framework, offering better biomass estimates and potential for cyanobacteria taxonomic discrimination in optically complex inland waters.

title: An Overview of the Global Historical Climatology Network-Daily Database

Oceanic Technol.) that formally describes GHCN-Daily, NOAA/NCDC's global compilation of daily in-situ land-station weather observations (temperature, precipitation, snow) assembled from dozens of source networks, quality-screened, and distributed a

claims.c: GHCN-Daily was purpose-built to meet the need for daily-resolution climate data over global land areas, to support applications such as analyzing heavy-rainfall frequency and heat-wave duration.

As of the 2012 publication, GHCN-Daily contained records from over 80,000 stations in 180 countries and territories, and its processing system produced the official archive of U.S. daily station data.

Commonly reported variables are maximum/minimum temperature, total daily precipitation, snowfall, and snow depth, but roughly two-thirds of all stations report precipitation only (i.e., no temperature).

Quality-assurance checks are applied to the entire dataset, but GHCN-Daily is explicitly NOT homogenized -- station-level artifacts from different reporting eras (i.e., changes in systematic bias over time) are left uncorrected.

GHCN-Daily is compiled from 20+ constituent source datasets and is fully reconstructed on roughly a weekly cadence so that it stays synchronized with its growing list of sources; each update/reprocessing is assigned a unique version number, the latest version is freely published, and every version is archived in perpetuity at NOAA's National Climatic Data Center.

title: Application of a eutrophication model for assessing water quality in Lake Winnipeg
year: 2012 (per Crossref issue date, Vol. 38 Issue S3); ScienceDirect PII/one indexed DOI carry a 2011 stamp, consistent with online-first posting
what: A peer-reviewed application study that calibrates and validates a mechanistic eutrophication model (WASP, the Water [Quality] Analysis Simulation Program) for Lake Winnipeg, Canada, simulating N and P cycling and three phytoplankton functional groups (non-cyanobacteria, N-fixing cyanobacteria, non-N-fixing cyanobacteri

claims.c: The WASP eutrophication model was applied to Lake Winnipeg for 2002–2007, simulating N and P cycling through three phytoplankton functional groups (non-cyanobacteria, N-fixing cyanobacteria, non-N-fixing cyanobacteria) and representing the South and North Basins as distinct units with different morphology, hydrology and climate.
The calibrated and validated model showed good agreement with observed data for TN, NO3, NH4, TP, PO4, DO, and total chlorophyll-a.

The model reproduced the qualitative seasonal/spatial pattern of phytoplankton community shifts: cyanobacteria dominant in the North Basin in late summer, non-cyanobacteria dominant in the South Basin in spring.
In one scenario, a 10% reduction in phosphorus load lowered the cyanobacteria percentage in both basins and reduced late-summer chlorophyll-a peaks in the North Basin.

A separately worded rendering of what appears to be the same, or a closely related, 10% total-phosphorus-load-reduction scenario reports a more mixed outcome: decreased abundance of total cyanobacteria overall, but an increase in the biomass of the non-N-fixing cyanobacteria functional group specifically.

The paper's headline management conclusion is that raising the N:P loading ratio — phosphorus-load reduction exceeding 12% combined with nitrogen-load reduction under 7% — is projected (based on modeled chlorophyll-a and phytoplankton composition) to be effective for improving Lake Winnipeg water quality.
A separate scenario simulating a simultaneous 10% reduction in both nitrogen and phosphorus loading is reported to restrict (limit) the non-N-fixing cyanobacteria functional group.
The model's spatial structure reportedly represented Lake Winnipeg as two segments — a combined South Basin/Narrows segment and a North Basin segment.

title: Assessment of Atmospheric Correction Algorithms for Landsat-8/9 Operational Land Imager over Inland and Coastal Waters
what: A peer-reviewed remote-sensing methods paper (Remote Sensing, MDPI, 2025) that benchmarks six atmospheric correction (AC) algorithms — ACOLITE, C2RCC, iCOR, L2GEN, OC-SMART, and POLYMER — for Landsat-8/9 OLI imagery against 440 in-situ radiometric matchups spanning inland lakes in China and globally distributed coastal

claims.c: The study systematically benchmarked six state-of-the-art atmospheric correction (AC) algorithms — ACOLITE, C2RCC, iCOR, L2GEN, OC-SMART, and POLYMER — applied to Landsat-8/9 OLI satellite imagery.

The evaluation benchmark comprised 440 high-quality in-situ radiometric matchups, combining inland-lake data from China's Satellite-Ground Synchronous Campaign with coastal-water data from the globally distributed GLORIA dataset.

A single, unified Optical Water Type (OWT) classification framework was applied across the combined inland+coastal dataset so that AC performance could be compared consistently across a wide range of environmental/turbidity conditions.
AC algorithm performance is not uniform — it varies significantly depending on water type, meaning no single algorithm is universally best.
In coastal waters, L2GEN produced the lowest errors in the visible bands, whereas in inland waters OC-SMART achieved the best overall accuracy.
ACOLITE specifically outperformed the other five algorithms in the blue spectral region (443 nm and 482 nm bands) for inland waters.

Algorithm accuracy also depends on turbidity class (Optical Water Type): OC-SMART stayed robust across the whole turbidity gradient; ACOLITE and iCOR performed best in highly turbid waters (OWT 5-6); L2GEN, C2RCC, and POLYMER performed comparatively better in clearer waters (OWT 3-4).

The paper also discusses each algorithm's practical applicability and offers recommendations for mitigating 'adjacency effects' (AE) — a known contamination source near land-water boundaries — to further improve AC accuracy.

title: Comment on "Early warning of harmful cyanobacteria blooms based on high frequency in situ monitoring and intelligible machine learning modelling: The case study of Lake Müggelsee (Germany)" by Recknagel et al. (Water Research 287 2025 124,514)
what: A short peer-reviewed "Comment" (Water Research, Vol. 293, article 125406, epub 15 Jan 2026) by Feilong Shen and Zhongbing Chen that critiques the methodology of Recknagel et al. (2025, Water Research 287, article 124514), a study forecasting cyanobacterial blooms in Lake Müggelsee (Germany) 5 days ahead using high-fre

claims.c: The commenters characterize the paper under comment as using high-frequency in-situ data and three fundamentally different machine learning algorithms to forecast cyanobacterial blooms in Lake Müggelsee at a 5-day horizon.

The commenters argue that cross-year preprocessing and interpolation of weekly biovolume measurements up to hourly/daily resolution can introduce temporal (look-ahead-style) leakage and can artificially smooth the bloom dynamics the model is meant to predict.

The commenters argue the original study's evaluation emphasized R2/RMSE rather than threshold-exceedance skill and lead-time distributions at the operationally relevant 4 mm3/L biovolume hazard level, and communicated uncertainty only in a limited way.

The commenters argue that the original model's strong reliance on the phycocyanin (PHYCO) sensor variable, while operationally reasonable, needs PHYCO-persistence baselines and no-PHYCO ablation tests to quantify how much of the forecasting skill is incremental value from the model versus simple persistence/dependence on that one sensor.

The commenters argue that mixed data-aggregation choices, in which single-station driver (predictor) variables were paired with multi-station or depth-integrated target variables, can bias the relationships the model learns.

The commenters conclude that adopting leakage-robust validation practices, sensitivity analyses, event-based metrics, uncertainty reporting, and strong baselines would further strengthen the original study's operational credibility, while explicitly stating this would not change its overall contribution as a pragmatic demonstration.

numbers: 5-day forecast horizon — lead time of the original Recknagel et al. (2025) early-warning models, as characterized by the commenters
4 mm3/L — cyanobacteria biovolume 'hazard level' the commenters say should anchor threshold-exceedance skill/lead-time evaluation, instead of relying only on R2/RMSE
three (3) — number of 'fundamentally different' machine learning algorithms used in the original study, per the commenters' description
Water Research, vol. 287 (2025), article no. 124,514 — citation of the original Recknagel et al. paper under comment
Water Research, vol. 293 (2026), article no. 125406; DOI 10.1016/j.watres.2026.125406; PMID 41619426; Epub 15 Jan 2026, print/issue date 1 Apr 2026 — bibliographic identifiers for this comment itself

(2025)'s Water Research paper on ML-based early warning of cyanobacteria blooms in Lake Müggelsee: it argues the original paper's reported forecasting skill may be inflated by (i) temporal leakage from interpolating weekly biovolume to hourly/daily resolution, (ii) reliance on R2/RMSE instead of threshold/event-based metrics at the operational hazard level, (iii) unquantified dependence on a single sensor variable (phycocyanin) absent a persistence baselin

title: Cyanobacterial blooms. Ecology, prevention, mitigation and control: Editorial to a CYANOCOST Special Issue
what: This is the editorial introduction (Ibelings, Fastner, Bormans & Visser, 2016, Aquatic Ecology, Vol. 50, pp. 327-331, DOI 10.1007/s10452-016-9595-y) to a CYANOCOST (European COST Action) special issue on cyanobacterial bloom management. It frames the issue's roughly 13-14 contributed papers around a three-tier taxonomy

claims.c: The editors define three distinct, mutually exclusive management strategies for cyanobacterial blooms - prevention, control, and mitigation - distinguished by when in the bloom life-cycle the intervention acts.

The editorial argues sustainable bloom management must be organized around controlling nutrient availability at three spatial levels, and states the special issue's contributions are structured this way.

The editorial frames eutrophication (nutrient pollution) as the primary driver of cyanobacterial blooms, quoting Schindler that nutrient pollution is the greatest threat to global water quality, and describes blooms as the most visible symptom of eutrophication.

The editorial acknowledges climate warming is widely thought to raise cyanobacterial bloom risk (citing the 'Blooms like it hot' framing) but explicitly cautions that evidence for a synergistic nutrient-times-temperature interaction driving blooms is weak and the nutrient-climate relationship remains an open scientific question.

CYANOCOST, the COST Action that produced the special issue, has the explicit aim of coordinating and networking cyanobacteria/cyanotoxin risk-management efforts across Europe, spanning academia, authorities, industry, and citizens.

The special issue compiles roughly 13-14 peer-reviewed contributions spanning cyanobacterial ecological traits, nitrogen/phosphorus load-reduction strategies, physical and chemical in-lake control methods (artificial mixing, cyanocidal compounds, biomanipulation, water-level management, evaluated 'end-of-pipe' measures), long-term case studies (the Dutch Borderlakes), and a socio-ecological stakeholder-engagement approach, closing with a dedicated synthesis/critical-review paper.

The editorial states that some popularly marketed 'exotic' bloom-control methods (e.g., ultrasound, effective microorganisms) lack adequate scientific validation and should not be considered supported treatments.

numbers: None: the editorial itself reports no quantitative thresholds, concentrations, percentages, or effect-size statistics for cyanobacteria/cyanotoxins - it is a qualitative framing/synthesis piece, not a data paper.
Bibliographic locators only: Aquatic Ecology, Volume 50, pages 327-331 (2016); DOI 10.1007/s10452-016-9595-y.
tensions: The editors' explicit caution that nutrient-times-temperature synergy evidence is only 'scant' (citing Rigosi et al. 2014) sits in tension with more deterministic 'warming causes/worsens blooms' framings elsewhere in the HAB literature - including the very 'Blooms like it hot' catchphrase (Paerl & Huisman 2008) the editorial also cites approvingly. The editors treat climate as a plausible but not firmly established amplifier of the primary, nutrient-driven mechanism rather than a co-equal or independent driver; other sources in a HAB review tha

title: Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii dominates under very low and high nitrogen-to-phosphorus ratios
what: A field mesocosm (2500-L limnocorral) manipulation experiment in a eutrophic lake that imposed three total-nitrogen:total-phosphorus (N:P) treatments — ambient (40:1), low (7:1), and high (122:1) — to test how nutrient stoichiometry shapes phytoplankton community composition and cyanotoxin (saxitoxin) production, cente

claims.c: The study manipulated nitrogen-to-phosphorus (N:P) ratios in field mesocosms to three treatment levels: ambient (40:1), low (7:1), and high (122:1), using 2500-L limnocorrals.
Initial nutrient concentrations in the mesocosms were approximately TN ~1360 µg/L and TP ~75 µg/L.

By the end of the experiment, the phytoplankton community had become dominated almost entirely by the nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii — the title finding is that this dominance occurred under BOTH the very low (7:1) and very high (122:1) N:P treatments, not only under classic N-limiting (low N:P) conditions.
Concentrations of the cyanobacterial neurotoxin saxitoxin were higher ("enhanced") under the two highest N:P treatments tested.

The initial phytoplankton community was diverse (reported as 18 genera, dominated by chlorophytes and cyanobacteria including Cylindrospermopsis) before converging to near-monodominance by C. raciborskii, and this convergence under both low and high N:P was described as contrary to predictions from ecological stoichiometry theory.

The authors' interpretation (per the search-tool summary) is that because C. raciborskii can dominate across such an extreme range of N:P ratios, short-term nutrient-ratio management (e.g., via fertilization to shift N:P) is unlikely to effectively control blooms of this invasive species.

numbers: N:P treatments: ambient 40:1, low 7:1, high 122:1
Mesocosm (limnocorral) volume: 2500 L
Initial total nitrogen (TN): ~1360 µg L⁻¹
Initial total phosphorus (TP): ~75 µg L⁻¹
Initial phytoplankton diversity (per WebSearch-tool summary of the record, not independently re-verified verbatim by direct fetch): 18 genera

No standard deviation/standard error, confidence interval, or replicate (n) count for any of the above values was present in the abstract text retrieved — only the '~' (approximate) qualifier on TN and TP
tensions: This finding complicates a simple "manage N:P ratio to control cyanobacteria" narrative sometimes invoked in HAB literature. Classic ecological-stoichiometry theory predicts N-fixing cyanobacteria (like C. raciborskii) should be favored specifically under N-limited, LOW N:P conditions (where fixing atmospheric N2 confers an advantage), and disadvantaged at high N:P. This study instead found near-total dominance by C. raciborskii under the ambient (40:1), low (7:1), AND high (122:1) treatments alike — i.e., across the full extreme range tested —

title: Cyanobacterial blooms. Ecology, prevention, mitigation and control - Editorial to a CYANOCOST Special Issue
what: The editorial introducing a special issue of *Aquatic Ecology* (2016, 50(3)) produced by the European COST Action **CYANOCOST**, framing a collection of review/method papers on the ecology, prevention, mitigation, and control of cyanobacterial blooms in lakes.

claims.c: The special issue is a product of the European COST Action CYANOCOST and compiles contributions describing methods currently available for managing cyanobacterial blooms, framed around blooms threatening the ecological functioning of lakes and the ecosystem services they provide.

The issue is organized around two management arms: (1) **prevention** via nutrient restriction at three levels — in the catchment, at the inflow to the lake, and in-lake methods; and (2) **control**, where treatments restrict cyanobacterial growth through methods such as artificial mixing, flushing, or biomanipulation.

numbers: Bibliographic: Aquatic Ecology, Vol. 50, Issue 3 (2016); DOI 10.1007/s10452-016-9595-y
No quantitative findings extracted (full text inaccessible; editorial is a framing/overview piece rather than a data study)
tensions: No new tensions can be drawn at metadata level. The prevention-vs-control framing it introduces is consistent with the treatment-and-management literature captured elsewhere in this review (nutrient-load reduction and in-lake interventions such as mixing, flushing, and biomanipulation). Any specific efficacy claims must come from the underlying primary sources (e.g., [[ACAD-016]] biomanipulation, [[ACAD-038]] alum longevity), not from this editorial.

title: Development of an algal bloom satellite and in situ metadata hub with case studies in Canada
year: 2024 (published online 28 Dec 2023; print Vol. 79, March 2024)
what: A peer-reviewed methods/data-infrastructure paper (Beaulne & Fotopoulos, Ecological Informatics, 2024) that presents the "Algal Bloom Metadata Hub," a free ArcGIS Online web application cataloging metadata (not raw imagery) from seven public optical and SAR satellite sensors to help users find, filter, and inspect sate

claims.c: The paper introduces the Algal Bloom Metadata Hub, a web application built in ArcGIS Online that hosts and lets users query/filter/sort metadata from contemporary, publicly accessible optical and SAR satellite sensors, to streamline finding and inspecting imagery for detecting, characterizing, and monitoring algal blooms.

The Hub hosts metadata for four optical satellite sensors (Sentinel-3 OLCI, Sentinel-2 MSI, Landsat-8 OLI, Landsat-9 OLI-2) and three SAR sensors (Sentinel-1, RADARSAT-2, RADARSAT Constellation Mission) -- seven sensors in total.

Each metadata record integrated into the Hub is downloaded from that sensor's own data provider, reduced/processed, and includes at minimum the acquisition time, the scene footprint, and a unique file ID.

Over small water bodies in Ontario, accounting for cloud cover substantially lengthens the median satellite revisit period relative to each sensor's nominal orbital revisit: Landsat-8/9 OLI/OLI-2 lengthens from 4.8 days to 23 days, and the Sentinel-2 A/B constellation from 2.5 days to 12 days; combining Landsat-8/9 with Sentinel-2 A/B imagery brings the cloud-adjusted median revisit period down to 8.6 days.

In the Ramsey Lake, Ontario case study, combining scenes from multiple optical sensors with SAR (which is largely unaffected by cloud cover) is shown to improve the effective temporal resolution of bloom monitoring on cloudy days without sacrificing spatial resolution.
The Hub's utility is demonstrated through two Canadian case studies: the Western Basin of Lake Erie and Ramsey Lake, Ontario.

The Hub includes a citizen-science reporting feature letting members of the public submit bloom observations, including images, to a centralized database; the authors frame this as a way to address limited in-situ data availability for under-studied and remote water bodies.

The authors note that medium-resolution optical sensors' usefulness for algal-bloom applications is constrained by each sensor's spectral characteristics (centre band wavelength, bandwidth, and whether bands relevant to bloom-detection algorithms are present at all), and separately that medium-resolution imagery struggles on irregularly-shaped lakes or where blooms concentrate along shorelines and in bays.

numbers: 7 total satellite sensors hosted by the Hub: 4 optical (Sentinel-3 OLCI, Sentinel-2 MSI, Landsat-8 OLI, Landsat-9 OLI-2) + 3 SAR (Sentinel-1, RADARSAT-2, RADARSAT Constellation Mission)
Landsat-8/9 OLI/OLI-2 median revisit period over small Ontario water bodies: 4.8 days (nominal) -> 23 days (cloud-cover-adjusted)
Sentinel-2 A/B constellation median revisit period over small Ontario water bodies: 2.5 days (nominal) -> 12 days (cloud-cover-adjusted)
Combined Landsat-8/9 + Sentinel-2 A/B median revisit period, cloud-cover-adjusted: 8.6 days
Journal: Ecological Informatics, Volume 79, Article 102447; online 28 Dec 2023, print March 2024; DOI 10.1016/j.ecoinf.2023.102447
License: CC BY-NC-ND (Gold Open Access, per Crossref/Unpaywall/Semantic Scholar metadata)
Cited-by count: 12 (per OpenAlex/Semantic Scholar citation metadata as of this fetch -- bibliometric metadata, not a finding from the paper's own text)

title: Early Seasonal Increases and Persistence in Relative Abundance of Potentially Toxic Cyanobacteria: Concerning Impacts of Extended Ice-Free Periods in Northern Temperate Lakes
what: A bioRxiv preprint (posted 2022-12-21, version 1; no peer-reviewed journal version on record) by Cameron, Müller, Stone, Buttle, Leach, Webster and Emelko (University of Waterloo) reporting an 18-month (July 2018-January 2020), 10-event field survey of cyanobacterial community composition via 16S rRNA (V4 region) ampli

claims.c: The study observed proliferation of cyanobacteria across all seasons in three oligotrophic lakes, without the visible biomass accumulations conventionally associated with cyanobacterial blooms in eutrophic summer systems.

Warmer water temperature and high nutrient availability are described as the key characteristics generally associated with cyanobacteria occurrence, with blooms typically expected in summer in eutrophic systems.

Analysis of almost 40 years of lake ice-phenology records for TLW showed rising temperatures have led to significantly longer ice-free periods and aquatic growing seasons, with warming most pronounced in autumn via a significantly later onset of ice-on.

Cyanobacterial community composition in three interconnected TLW lakes was characterized using 16S rRNA (V4 region) amplicon sequencing across an 18-month field campaign (July 2018-January 2020) comprising 10 synoptic sampling events.

During winter under ice cover, photosynthetic cyanobacteria were low in abundance or occasionally absent, but a non-photosynthetic basal cyanobacterial lineage, Melainabacteria, was detected persisting through the ice-covered period.

Photosynthetic cyanobacterial populations reappeared in the water column immediately after ice-off, and abundance was especially elevated in lakes whose surficial geology and morphometry favor greater fine-sediment (and associated nutrient) supply.

The authors interpret their combined ice-phenology and community-sequencing results as showing that climate forcing of hydrological/biogeochemical processes, together with intrinsic landscape features, is associated with increases in the relative abundance of potentially toxic cyanobacteria over an increasingly longer part of the year in this temperate Canadian forest biome.

numbers: 3 interconnected oligotrophic lakes studied (Turkey Lakes Watershed, Ontario, Canada)
"Almost 40 years" of ice phenology data analyzed
18-month field sampling period: July 2018 to January 2020
10 synoptic sampling events across the 18-month period
16S rRNA gene, V4 hypervariable region used as the amplicon-sequencing target (community characterization marker, not a magnitude)
tensions: The abstract itself frames a tension with the conventional cyanoHAB paradigm it states as typical -- "concern of cyanobacteria blooms (e.g., visible biomass accumulations) occurring in the summer season of eutrophic systems" -- by reporting season-spanning proliferation of potentially toxic cyanobacteria (including winter persistence of a basal lineage, Melainabacteria, and immediate post-ice-off recolonization) in oligotrophic, non-bloom-visible lakes. For any HAB early-warning approach built on visible/satellite bloom signals and/or warm-seas

title: Elimination of cyanobacteria and microcystins in irrigation water—effects of hydrogen peroxide treatment
what: A field-mesocosm study (Lake Köyliönjärvi, SW Finland, 2014) testing whether dosing cyanobacteria-laden lake water with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) can eliminate cyanobacterial cells and their microcystin (MC) toxins so the water is safe for spray-irrigating a food crop. Four seasonal trials applied 0, 2, 5, and 20 mg L⁻¹

claims.c: Hydrogen peroxide at all three tested doses (2, 5, 20 mg L⁻¹) effectively lysed a majority of the MC-producing cyanobacterial cells in lake water, with the fraction lysed varying by dose and by experiment/season.

The study's central/primary hypothesis was only partially confirmed: H2O2 lysed cyanobacterial cells (moving MC from cell-bound to dissolved form) but did not destroy/degrade the released microcystins.

Even at the highest dose tested (20 mg L⁻¹), residual cell-bound microcystin remained, which the authors attribute to either a H2O2-resistant cyanobacterial subpopulation or regrowth/re-establishment of a population.
No microcystins (MC-RR or MC-LR) were detected in any spinach samples irrigated with H2O2-treated, MC-containing water, and no phytotoxic effects were observed.
H2O2 doses of 10 mg L⁻¹ and higher were detrimental to non-target moving plankton (zooplankton and moving phytoplankton), with effects already visible at 5 mg L⁻¹.
The authors recommend doses close to 2 mg L⁻¹ as a safer trade-off between cyanobacterial lysis and harm to non-target organisms, at least in the short term.
The authors explicitly caution that H2O2-treated irrigation water should not be used for critical purposes without first testing its microcystin concentration, because released MCs may persist in it.
Baseline (pre-treatment) cell-bound microcystin concentrations in the study lake were relatively low and seasonally variable across the 2014 sampling season, rising toward autumn.

The authors state that H2O2's effectiveness against cyanobacteria/microcystins is strongly dependent on the specific water's chemical matrix, making dose recommendations difficult to generalize from one water body to another.

numbers: H2O2 doses tested: 0 (control), 2, 5, and 20 mg L⁻¹
Cyanobacterial lysis: 70–88% of MC-producing cyanobacteria lysed across doses/experiments overall; 80–96% lysed specifically at 5 mg L⁻¹ H2O2

Cell-bound (intracellular) MC in lake water before treatment: June 0.97 µg L⁻¹; July 0.037 µg L⁻¹ (season description gives 0.04 µg L⁻¹ for July); August 1.27 µg L⁻¹; September 2.4 µg L⁻¹ — overall 2014 season range '0.0
Historical (2013) cell-bound MC peaks in same lake: 3.6 µg L⁻¹ (19 Aug 2013) and 6.2 µg L⁻¹ (26 Sep 2013)
Comparator cited from elsewhere in Finland: cell-bound MC as high as 42 µg L⁻¹ recorded on Åland Islands
Extracellular (dissolved) MC before treatment: usually <0.1 µg L⁻¹; up to 0.12–0.28 µg L⁻¹ in August 2014
Extracellular MC after H2O2 treatment (September experiment, highest dose): approx. 3.8 µg L⁻¹
MC:chlorophyll a ratio in lake water: below 10% (0.0043–0.081), versus 10–100% typical in cyanobacteria-dominated samples per cited literature
Chlorophyll a in source water at experiment start: June 38.8 µg L⁻¹; July 8.7 µg L⁻¹; August 46.9 µg L⁻¹; September 29.8 µg L⁻¹
Chlorophyll a in tank water at experiment end: June 24.3 µg L⁻¹; September 26.8 µg L⁻¹
Water temperature (noon readings): lake surface 16.3–21.2 °C; tank water 14.3–22.3 °C
Lake morphometry: mean depth 3 m, max depth 12.8 m, surface area 12.5 km², catchment area 123.7 km², fed by 26 agricultural ditches
tensions: The source's central tension is with treatment guidance drawn from other H2O2 field trials: it cites a prior whole-lake application of 2 mg L⁻¹ H2O2 (different study/water body) that reportedly destroyed ~99% of microcystins within days, whereas here even 20 mg L⁻¹ (10x that dose) failed to destroy released microcystins in Lake Köyliönjärvi water. The authors attribute this discrepancy to water-matrix-dependent H2O2 consumption (organic matter, radical scavengers), which argues against transferring a single "effective dose" across water bodies

title: Evaluating the applicability of machine learning and deep learning models for predicting cyanobacterial alert levels in a drinking water reservoir
what: A 2025 Journal of Water Process Engineering paper (Byeon, Lee, Shin, Baek, Hwang, Kim & Park) that builds and compares three predictive model families — 1D-CNN, ANN, and an ensemble (random forest + boosting) — to make short-term/early predictions of cyanobacterial ("algal") alert levels in a South Korean drinking-

claims.c: The study constructs and compares three model types — 1D-CNN, ANN, and an ensemble of random forest + boosting — to predict short-term cyanobacterial alert levels in a drinking-water reservoir from a small-scale dataset of meteorological, hydrodynamic, and water-quality variables.
The ensemble model (random forest + boosting) achieved the highest accuracy of the three approaches: 84.7% (training) and 78.7% (testing), versus 72.6%/77.0% for 1D-CNN and 79.0%/73.8% for ANN.
The ensemble model outperformed both deep-learning models (1D-CNN, ANN) overall and was selected by the authors as the optimal model for predicting algal alert levels.

Water temperature was identified as the most influential predictor of algal alert level in the model(s), with nitrogen-related water-quality variables also ranking as relatively important contributors (an association/importance finding from model explainability analysis, not a claim of causation).
The models were trained on roughly nine years of meteorological, hydrodynamic, and water-quality monitoring data from the studied reservoir (a 'small-scale' dataset by the authors' own framing).

The authors frame their central research question as whether ML/DL models remain feasible and useful predictive tools when the available input data are limited in scope, rather than assuming an ideal, large dataset.
The authors propose that the resulting model can be used operationally within a cyanobacterial early-warning system for the reservoir.

numbers: 72.6% — 1D-CNN training-step alert-level prediction accuracy
79.0% — ANN training-step alert-level prediction accuracy
84.7% — ensemble (random forest + boosting) training-step alert-level prediction accuracy (best of the three)
77.0% — 1D-CNN testing-step alert-level prediction accuracy
73.8% — ANN testing-step alert-level prediction accuracy
78.7% — ensemble model testing-step alert-level prediction accuracy (best of the three)
~9 years — length of meteorological/hydrodynamic/water-quality dataset used (one lower-confidence retrieval said 10 years; unresolved discrepancy)
Journal of Water Process Engineering, Volume 73, Article 107685 (2025); DOI 10.1016/j.jwpe.2025.107685
No stated baseline model or uncertainty interval (e.g., CI) found for any of the above accuracies in the retrieved material
tensions: (1) The finding that a simpler ensemble of random forest + boosting matched or beat both deep-learning architectures (1D-CNN, ANN) on a small real-world ecological dataset is a concrete external data point supporting the "explainability/simplicity over sophistication" stance in this project's own operating principles. (2) Anomaly I noticed in the reconstructed numbers (not addressed in any retrieved source text): the 1D-CNN's reported test accuracy (77.0%) is higher than its training accuracy (72.6%), which is unusual for a well-behaved supervi

title: Evaluating the treatment effectiveness of copper-based algaecides on toxic algae Microcystis aeruginosa using single cell-inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry
what: A 2019 laboratory (analytical chemistry) study that develops a single cell-inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (SC-ICP-MS) method to measure copper and magnesium mass in individual Microcystis aeruginosa cells, then uses that method (plus flow cytometry and LC-MS/MS) to evaluate how two copper-based algaecides

claims.c: The novel SC-ICP-MS method is highly sensitive and reproducible: detection limits of 65 ag/cell for intracellular copper and 98 ag/cell for intracellular magnesium, with relative standard deviation within 12%.

Both copper-based algaecides (cupric sulfate and EarthTec®), tested across 0–200 μg/L copper, successfully diminished M. aeruginosa populations at the 60, 100, and 200 μg/L doses, with higher copper concentrations producing faster copper uptake and faster cell lysis.

At the lowest tested dose (30 μg/L copper), cell integrity was largely preserved: more than 70% of cells remained intact after 18 h of EarthTec® treatment, while cupric sulfate at 30 μg/L showed no significant change in the percentage of cells with detectable magnesium.
Cupric sulfate acted faster than EarthTec®: extracellular magnesium (the lysis marker) rose sooner after cupric sulfate treatment than after EarthTec® treatment at comparable copper doses.

Individual M. aeruginosa cells show marked heterogeneity in copper uptake: cells that take up copper quickly lyse at a lower intracellular copper content, while slower-accumulating cells survive longer and reach a higher intracellular copper mass before lysing.

Only a small fraction of the nominal dosed copper was ever recovered as intracellular copper across all treatments (roughly 3.64%–8.13% depending on dose and formulation), implying most of the applied copper stayed outside the cells or was otherwise unaccounted for.

Killing the algae does not remove the toxin: cell lysis released intracellular microcystin-LR into the water in a dose-dependent way, while total (intra+extracellular) MC-LR concentration stayed unchanged, showing the toxin was redistributed, not degraded, by algaecide treatment.

The two copper formulations differed in the cell states they produced: EarthTec® treatment left a mix of intact-viable and intact-nonviable cells, whereas cupric sulfate treatment produced primarily intact-viable cells (prior to lysis), consistent with the two products' different uptake/lysis kinetics.

Detection limit, intracellular magnesium: 98 ± 12 ag/cell
Detection limit, extracellular copper: 1 μg/L
Detection limit, extracellular magnesium: 0.2 μg/L

Method reproducibility: relative standard deviation within 12%
Copper treatment concentrations tested: 0, 30, 60, 100, 200 μg/L copper
Exposure/sampling timepoints: 1, 2, 4, 8, and up to 18 h
Control mean intracellular Cu, cupric sulfate batch: 237 ± 15 ag/cell, present in 31% ± 3% of cells
Control mean intracellular Cu, EarthTec® batch: 207 ± 14 ag/cell, present in 49% ± 5% of cells
Cupric sulfate: % of nominal dosed Cu recovered intracellularly — 8.13% (30 μg/L), 5.99% (60 μg/L), 5.83% (100 μg/L), 5.72% (200 μg/L)
EarthTec®: % of nominal dosed Cu recovered intracellularly — 6.44% (30 μg/L), 7.69% (60 μg/L), 5.29% (100 μg/L), 3.64% (200 μg/L)
tensions: This source cuts against any treatment framing that equates algaecide-driven cell death (cell-count or chlorophyll decline) with a resolved bloom risk: it shows mechanistically that copper-induced lysis is precisely what releases stored microcystin-LR into the water, and that total toxin mass is not reduced by treatment — only redistributed from intracellular to extracellular (dissolved) form. Any HAB tool or product claim that recommends copper algaecide dosing purely to 'clear the bloom' would need this caveat: a successful kill (by cell-dens

title: Extracellular polysaccharide synthesis in a bloom-forming strain of Microcystis aeruginosa: implications for colonization and buoyancy
what: A peer-reviewed primary-research paper (Scientific Reports, 2019) comparing a colony-forming, bloom-derived Microcystis aeruginosa strain (CHAOHU 1326, isolated from Chaohu Lake, China) against three unicellular M. aeruginosa reference strains, using morphology (light/SEM/TEM microscopy), EPS quantification and size-fr

claims.c: Under identical axenic laboratory culture conditions, bloom-derived strain Microcystis aeruginosa CHAOHU 1326 forms visible colonies (aggregating as thin layers on the medium surface), whereas three unicellular reference strains (FACHB-925, FACHB-940, FACHB-975) remain single-celled and well dispersed.

CHAOHU 1326 cells have a significantly larger mean diameter (4.14 ± 0.4 μm) than each of the three unicellular strains (1.83 ± 0.2, 2.36 ± 0.2, and 1.76 ± 0.2 μm for FACHB-925, -940, -975 respectively), p < 0.05.

Massive gas vesicles are regularly observed between the thylakoids of CHAOHU 1326 by electron microscopy; this ultrastructural feature was not detected in any of the three unicellular reference strains examined.
After 4 days of incubation, CHAOHU 1326 produces significantly more extracellular polysaccharide (EPS) than each of the three unicellular strains.
Within CHAOHU 1326's soluble EPS pool, low-molecular-weight carbohydrate (<1 kDa) dominates at 62.2% of total soluble EPS, while high-molecular-weight carbohydrate (>5 kDa) makes up less than 10%.

Comparative genomics shows the CHAOHU 1326 genome carries more genes encoding glycosyl transferases, isomerases, epimerases, and polysaccharide-biosynthesis proteins than three reference Microcystis genomes (NIES-843, PCC7806SL, TAIHU98), including six isomerase genes and five epimerase genes.

The CHAOHU 1326 genome contains a cluster of 12 genes predicted to encode gas-vesicle synthesis machinery, including three identical-sequence copies of gvpA (the major structural gas-vesicle protein), which the authors interpret as enabling rapid, high-volume synthesis of GvpA.

All 18 EPS-biosynthesis-related genes selected for testing, and all predicted gvp (gas-vesicle) genes, show detectable transcriptional activity in CHAOHU 1326 during late exponential growth phase, as determined by reverse-transcription PCR.

The authors interpret their combined morphological, biochemical, and genomic results as indicating that EPS synthesis and gas-vesicle-mediated buoyancy are held in a fine balance in CHAOHU 1326, proposing EPS synthesis itself (via density regulation) as an additional buoyancy-control strategy alongside gas vesicles.

The authors note an association between high EPS production and reduced growth rate in CHAOHU 1326, consistent with prior literature linking high EPS content/colony formation to lower specific growth rate.

The paper frames colony formation and floating colonies reaching the water surface as ecologically advantageous for bloom formation, associating larger colony size with faster migration/floating to the surface and greater resistance to heavy-metal stress, high-light inhibition, and zooplankton predation.

numbers: CHAOHU 1326 cell diameter: 4.14 ± 0.4 μm (mean ± uncertainty given)
FACHB-925 cell diameter: 1.83 ± 0.2 μm
FACHB-940 cell diameter: 2.36 ± 0.2 μm
FACHB-975 cell diameter: 1.76 ± 0.2 μm
Significance of cell-diameter differences: p < 0.05
Soluble EPS <1 kDa fraction in CHAOHU 1326: 62.2% of total soluble EPS
Soluble EPS >5 kDa fraction in CHAOHU 1326: <10% of total soluble EPS
Time point at which EPS yield differences became significant: 4 days of incubation
Isomerase genes detected in CHAOHU 1326 genome: 6
Epimerase genes detected in CHAOHU 1326 genome: 5
Gas-vesicle (gvp) gene cluster size: 12 genes
gvpA gene copies (identical sequence): 3
tensions: The study's causal framing in places ("high EPS production negatively influenced the growth rate") is not matched by a manipulative experiment in this paper — no EPS-synthesis gene knockout/induction was performed, so the EPS–growth-rate and EPS–buoyancy relationships are correlational/associative despite some causal language in the source's own discussion; this dossier treats those as correlations, not demonstrated causes. There is also a domestication-artifact tension: the three "unicellular" comparison strains may have lost colonial/gas-vesi

title: Extreme rainfall drives early onset cyanobacterial bloom
what: A field case study documenting how a single extreme-rainfall/flood event in a eutrophic Ontario reservoir (Conestogo Lake) triggered an atypically early, toxin-producing cyanobacterial bloom, tracked through paired in-situ water-chemistry, phytoplankton-community, and cyanotoxin sampling before and after the storm. It

claims.c: Following a 2017 extreme rainfall/flood event, Conestogo Lake (a eutrophic Ontario reservoir) developed an atypically early, toxin-producing cyanobacterial bloom rather than its usual late-summer bloom.

Bioavailable phosphorus in surface waters rose more than 27-fold after the flood, preceding a marked Aphanizomenon flos-aquae increase about 2 weeks post-event — roughly 5 weeks earlier than the reservoir's typical bloom onset.

The storm was hydrologically extreme: record daily rainfall (78 mm at Conestogo Dam; watershed-wide range 40-120 mm) on 23 June 2017 sharply raised inflow (from a baseline mean of 4.56 m3/s to 219 m3/s, peaking at 521 m3/s), added 3.5 Mm3 to reservoir volume, replaced ~80% of reservoir volume, and cut residence time to 6.04 days versus a normal range of 26-119 days (mean 43 days).

A. flos-aquae dominated cyanobacterial biomass (95-97% of the cyanobacterial fraction at all sites) and peaked at 10,900 mg/m3 (transitional zone, 19 July) and 4,710 mg/m3 (lacustrine zone, ~25 July), yet total cyanobacterial biomass overall (mean 1550 ± 2920 mg/m3) showed no significant relationship with sampling time or site.

Cyanotoxins (anabaenopeptin-A, microcystin-LR/-YR/-RR) were present through the 3-5 week bloom period, detectable in 87% of samples (n=13) and quantifiable in 73% (n=11); MC-LR was the dominant congener (highest zone mean 74.46 ± 156.5 ng/L, transitional zone), and total microcystin never exceeded the cited 20,000 ng/L recreational guideline.

The authors interpret the result as evidence that extreme rainfall can elongate the cyanobacterial bloom/toxicity season by shifting onset earlier, while stating explicitly that the magnitude of this effect is contingent on storm timing and reservoir physical structure.

The authors draw a forward-looking management implication: because extreme-rainfall events are projected to increase faster than total precipitation under climate change, managing blooms using historical/average conditions may become inadequate.

numbers: Bioavailable phosphorus (surface waters): >27-fold increase post-flood (headline figure; no confidence interval found in retrieved text)
Bloom timing: ~2 weeks after the flood event for the A. flos-aquae rise
Bloom timing vs. normal: ~5 weeks earlier than the reservoir's typical onset
Bloom duration: 3-5 weeks (toxin-producing period)

Rainfall: 78 mm in one day at Conestogo Dam, 23 June 2017 (DOY 174); watershed daily totals reported in range 40-120 mm; called the largest single rainfall event in the Grand River Watershed since records began in 1950
Secondary rain event: ~25 mm on 24 July 2017 (DOY 205)
Inflow (Conestogo River): baseline mean 4.56 m3/s rose to 219 m3/s during the event, peak 521 m3/s
Reservoir volume increase during flood pulse: 3.5 Mm3
Reservoir flushing: ~80% of total reservoir volume replaced
Hydraulic residence time: 6.04 days during the event vs normal range 26-119 days (mean 43 days)
Surface water temperature (0-2 m): mean range 21.2-25.5 degC across the reservoir
A. flos-aquae dominance: 95-97% of cyanobacterial biomass (by site) across the sampling campaign
tensions: (1) Correlation vs. causation: despite the causal-sounding title verb ("drives"), the core evidence is a single storm, single reservoir, before/after case study — phosphorus rose and A. flos-aquae biomass rose ~2 weeks later — which is a strong temporal association plus a plausible bioavailable-nutrient mechanism, not a controlled/manipulated causal experiment; this entry treats "extreme rainfall → earlier bloom" as a well-evidenced driver hypothesis from one event, not a proven general causal law. (2) It complicates simple temperature- or cale

title: Global Applications of the CE-QUAL-W2 Model in Reservoir Eutrophication: A Systematic Review and Perspectives for Brazil
what: A systematic review, published in Water (MDPI, open access CC-BY, publication date 2024-12-10), of the global peer-reviewed literature applying the CE-QUAL-W2 hydrodynamic/water-quality model to reservoir eutrophication. From 151 papers initially identified, the authors selected 38 for detailed ("rigorous") analysis, a

claims.c: The review's literature search identified 151 candidate papers on CE-QUAL-W2 applications, of which 38 were retained after a screening/selection process the authors describe as 'rigorous analysis'.
Within the reviewed literature, 2021 was the peak year for number of publications (six papers), and 2022 was the peak year for number of citations (113 citations).
Among countries represented in the reviewed literature, Iran had the most CE-QUAL-W2 publications, followed by China, then Brazil.
Coupling CE-QUAL-W2 with the SWAT watershed model is characterized as a standard combination in the reviewed literature, effective for complex watershed studies.

Across the reviewed applications, CE-QUAL-W2 is reported as able to predict future environmental conditions, diagnose environmental extremes, and calculate multiple hydrodynamic and water-quality parameters.

The authors interpret the model's increasing use in high-impact journals as evidence of global relevance and as showing particular promise specifically for Brazilian aquatic-environment studies, attributed to the model's efficiency and accessibility.

numbers: 151 papers - total relevant papers identified by the literature search (count; no baseline or uncertainty reported)
38 papers - subset selected for detailed/'rigorous' analysis after screening (count; no baseline or uncertainty reported; screening criteria not stated in the accessible text)
6 papers - highest number of publications recorded in a single year among the reviewed literature, occurring in 2021 (count/year)
113 citations - highest number of citations recorded in a single year among the reviewed literature, occurring in 2022 (count/year)
Country ranking (ordinal only, no per-country counts given): Iran (1st by publication count) > China (2nd) > Brazil (3rd)
tensions: (1) Scope tension for a HAB-focused dossier: the abstract never mentions algae, cyanobacteria, blooms, or toxins -- it frames the topic strictly as "eutrophication" and general "water quality." CE-QUAL-W2 is a mechanistic, calibration-intensive simulation model applied per water body, which is methodologically distinct from the empirical/statistical or remote-sensing-driven detection and early-warning approaches (e.g., satellite cyanobacteria indices fused with in-situ data) that a HAB early-warning tool would typically rely on. Eutrophication

title: GlobalHAB: A New Program to Promote International Research, Observations, and Modeling of Harmful Algal Blooms in Aquatic Systems
what: A short (12-page) 2017 Oceanography journal program-overview/perspective article by Berdalet, Kudela, Urban and 13 co-authors that announces GlobalHAB, a new IOC(UNESCO)/SCOR-cosponsored international program for harmful algal bloom (HAB) research, observations, and modeling — presented as the successor to the 1998–201

claims.c: From 1998 to 2013, international HAB research in marine systems was coordinated by scientists working through two intergovernmental bodies — the Intergovernmental Oceanographic Commission (IOC) of UNESCO and the Scientific Committee on Oceanic Research (SCOR) — with the goal of better understanding the ecological and oceanographic controls on HABs.

During that same 1998–2013 period, IOC and SCOR specifically cosponsored a program called GEOHAB (Global Ecology and Oceanography of Harmful Algal Blooms) to advance HAB research, observations, and modeling.

In 2016, IOC and SCOR launched a successor program (GlobalHAB) that builds on GEOHAB but explicitly broadens scope to include freshwater HAB systems and the effects of HABs on human society amid ongoing environmental change.

The abstract's own framing implies that the preceding GEOHAB program (1998–2013) was scoped to marine systems and had not made freshwater HABs or societal/environmental-change impacts a central mandate — that gap is what GlobalHAB was created to close.

2016 — year IOC and SCOR launched the new GlobalHAB project design, per the abstract
2017 (March) — publication year/date of this article
Oceanography, vol. 30, no. 1, pp. 70–81 — journal citation (12 pages); DOI 10.5670/oceanog.2017.111

33 citations (Scopus) — as displayed on the UHI Pure repository record for this article at the time of this research session; this is a dynamic third-party metric about the record, not a value stated within the article's
tensions: None discernible from the accessible text. This is a program-announcement/perspective piece rather than an empirical or modeling study, so its abstract makes no quantitative or predictive claims that could be checked against, or could conflict with, other HAB literature. The one notable framing choice — implicitly positioning marine-only HAB coordination (GEOHAB, 1998–2013) as insufficient on its own and needing extension to freshwater systems — is a programmatic self-assessment by the same scientific community (IOC/SCOR) rather than a contradi

title: Harmful Algal Bloom–Associated Illnesses in Humans and Dogs Identified Through a Pilot Surveillance System — New York, 2015
what: A CDC Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report (MMWR) surveillance summary (Figgatt et al., MMWR 2017;66:1182–1184, DOI 10.15585/mmwr.mm6643a5) reporting the first season (June–September 2015) of a One Health pilot illness-surveillance system run by the New York State Department of Health in 16 counties to actively find,

claims.c: New York's 2015 pilot surveillance system logged far more HAB-associated illness reports in one 4-month season than the state's routine reporting had ever logged statewide in a full year, which the authors interpret as evidence of prior underreporting.
Of 51 HAB-associated illness reports collected by the pilot, 35 (68.8%) met CDC's case definitions: 32 human cases (1 confirmed, 20 probable, 11 suspected) and 3 canine cases (all probable).

Statewide across all of New York in 2015 (not limited to the pilot counties/season), 139 HABs were identified, 97 (70%) of them laboratory-confirmed, resulting in 77 separate beach closures at 37 beaches on 20 distinct water bodies.

Beaches with a visually identified HAB stayed closed to water contact until the bloom was invisible for at least one day and a commercial test strip showed cyanobacterial toxin concentration below a 10 microgram-per-liter guidance value.

Cases were classified using a three-tier CDC definition in which 'suspected' requires exposure plus symptoms plus a public-health plausibility judgment, 'probable' adds a medical diagnosis or observational/environmental lab data, and only 'confirmed' requires clinical HAB laboratory data.
Among the 32 human cases, skin problems (rash) were the most common symptom (22 patients, 69%), no human was hospitalized or died, and swimming was the dominant exposure route (28 patients, 88%).
All three canine cases had gastrointestinal symptoms; two of the three dogs were hospitalized and the third recovered without treatment, and none died.
The authors frame the higher case count in the pilot year as evidence of underreporting in prior (passive) surveillance rather than evidence that HAB illness incidence actually increased in 2015.

The authors state that HAB illness surveillance is inherently low-specificity because the associated signs and symptoms are nonspecific and there is often no data causally linking a given illness to HAB exposure.

The 16 pilot counties were not a representative sample of New York: all had already experienced at least one HAB in the prior season, so reported illness burden may reflect more HAB-aware communities rather than the state overall.

(2) Only 1 of 32 human cases (3%) reached the clinically "confirmed" tier; the rest rested on nonspecific s

title: Health-Based Cyanotoxin Guideline Values Allow for Cyanotoxin-Based Monitoring and Efficient Public Health Response to Cyanobacterial Blooms
what: A peer-reviewed paper (Toxins, 2015, 7(2):457-477) in which the Oregon Health Authority (OHA) describes how, absent U.S. federal recreational-water criteria, it derived its own health-based guideline values (for drinking water, human recreational exposure, and dog recreational exposure) for four cyanotoxins — anatoxin-

claims.c: In the absence of U.S. federal cyanotoxin criteria for recreational water, OHA derived its own health-based guideline values for the four most common cyanotoxins in Oregon's fresh waters (anatoxin-a, cylindrospermopsin, microcystins, saxitoxins), with three separate values per toxin for drinking water, human recreational exposure, and dog recreational exposure.

Monitoring that uses cyanobacteria genus/species identification and cell enumeration as a surrogate for actual cyanotoxin presence can overestimate health risk and lead to unnecessary health advisories, because not all cyanobacteria produce toxins and toxin-producers do not always do so at harmful levels.

Guideline concentrations (µg/L) were derived from a toxicological Tolerable Daily Intake (TDI, µg/kg-day) using the formula Guideline Value = TDI x BW x RSC / IR, with drinking-water exposure assumed at 60 kg body weight and 2 L/day intake, and recreational exposure assumed at 20 kg body weight (representing a 4-6-year-old child) and 0.1 L/day intake, both with a Relative Source Contribution (RSC) of 1 (i.e., 100% of exposure assumed to occur via water).

For microcystin and saxitoxin, applying the formula with the derived human TDI (0.05 µg/kg-day for both) and the drinking-water exposure factors produced a calculated value of 1.5 µg/L, which OHA then rounded down to a final published guideline of 1.0 µg/L for extra public-health protection.

Between 2009 and 2014, Oregon issued 88 recreational HAB advisories in total; 78 were triggered by cyanobacteria cell counts exceeding a threshold, and only 10 were triggered by measured cyanotoxin concentrations exceeding a toxin guideline value.

TBM demonstrably averted at least 13 advisories over the study period — cases where cyanobacteria cell counts exceeded the advisory threshold but measured toxin concentrations stayed below the toxin guideline value — with additional advisories likely averted in individual years (12 in 2012, 14 in 2013, 13 in 2014) that OHA could not fully verify without complete cell-count data.

Adding toxin-based criteria alongside cell-count criteria did not meaningfully change how long the public was kept under advisory: mean advisory duration was 8.0 weeks for cell-count-based advisories versus 7.8 weeks for toxin-based advisories.
A 2009 incident in which dogs died along the South Umpqua River in Douglas County, Oregon was confirmed as cyanotoxin-related; the deceased dog's stomach contents contained 10 µg/L anatoxin-a.

Even during blooms severe enough that multiple cyanobacteria genera (Microcystis, Dolichospermum, Aphanizomenon, and in one case Oscillatoria) simultaneously exceeded cell-count advisory thresholds, measured total microcystin concentrations reached as high as 705 µg/L and 310 µg/L in two separate documented cases — tens to hundreds of times above the state's 10 µg/L human recreational guideline.

Despite high concentrations detected in raw/source water (peak of 5.24 µg/L microcystin-LW), no Oregon finished (treated) drinking water sample exceeded the drinking water guideline values during the study period; the highest cyanotoxin level ever detected in finished drinking water was 0.3 µg/L anatoxin-a.

The four-toxin guideline framework does not cover other cyanobacterial metabolites of concern, including BMAA, which is produced by most cyanobacteria and has been implicated by human epidemiological studies in elevated risk of neurodegenerative diseases such as ALS and Parkinson's disease, but for which no established dose-response relationship exists to allow a guideline to be set — leading the authors to caution that toxin-only monitoring programs could underestimate overall public health risk.

numbers: Human TDI: anatoxin-a 0.1 µg/kg-day; cylindrospermopsin 0.03 µg/kg-day; microcystin 0.05 µg/kg-day; saxitoxin 0.05 µg/kg-day (human), 0.005 µg/kg-day (dog)
Drinking water guideline: anatoxin-a 3.0 µg/L; cylindrospermopsin 1.0 µg/L; microcystin 1.0 µg/L (calculated 1.5 µg/L, rounded down); saxitoxin 1.0 µg/L (calculated 1.5 µg/L, rounded down)
Human recreational guideline: anatoxin-a 20.0 µg/L; cylindrospermopsin 6.0 µg/L; microcystin 10.0 µg/L; saxitoxin 10.0 µg/L
Dog-specific recreational guideline: anatoxin-a 0.4 µg/L; cylindrospermopsin 0.1 µg/L; microcystin 0.2 µg/L; saxitoxin 0.02 µg/L
Exposure factors, drinking water: body weight 60 kg; intake rate 2 L/day; RSC = 1
Exposure factors, recreational water: body weight 20 kg (4-6 yr-old child); intake rate 0.1 L/day; RSC = 1
Dog body-weight-normalized water intake rate (CalEPA OEHHA, exercising dogs): 0.255 L/kg-day

Cylindrospermopsin critical study (11-week mouse, doses 0-240 µg/kg-day): NOAEL 30 µg/kg-day; LOAEL 60 µg/kg-day; EPA benchmark dose 33.1 µg/kg-day; total uncertainty factor 1000
Microcystin-LR critical study (28-day rat, drinking water): LOAEL 50 µg/kg-day; estimated NOAEL 5 µg/kg-day; total uncertainty factor 1000
Saxitoxin (EFSA human-intoxication data): acute reference dose 0.5 µg STX-eq/kg-day; human uncertainty factor 10; additional dog interspecies uncertainty factor 10
Pre-2012 cyanobacteria cell-count advisory thresholds: 100,000 cells/mL (general potentially toxigenic genera); 40,000 cells/mL (Microcystis or Planktothrix specifically)
tensions: This source's central caution — that using cyanobacteria cell counts/genus identification as a proxy for toxin risk can overestimate risk (Oregon's own data show cell-count-based advisories outnumbered toxin-confirmed exceedances 78-to-10, with at least 13, and possibly up to ~39 more across 2012-2014, retrospectively judged unnecessary) — is in tension with any HAB early-warning approach, including satellite/remote-sensing-derived biomass, chlorophyll-a, or phycocyanin indices, that treats bloom presence or magnitude alone as a proxy for publi

title: Increasingly severe cyanobacterial blooms and deep water hypoxia coincide with warming water temperatures in reservoirs
what: A peer-reviewed observational/statistical analysis of 32 years (1987–2018) of routine in-situ monitoring data from 20 temperate USACE reservoirs in the USA, testing whether cyanobacterial cell densities, water-column temperature, dissolved oxygen, and nutrients changed over time, and identifying which environmental var

claims.c: Cyanobacteria cell densities across 20 US reservoirs remained mostly low-to-moderate human-health risk until 2003–2005, then rose rapidly; since 2009, high-risk densities (>100,000 cells mL⁻¹) became frequent (75% of yearly observations), including 53% of observations from mostly-forested-watershed reservoirs, and by 2018 all 20 reservoirs had recorded blooms above that threshold.

Human-health risk was operationalized using WHO cell-density thresholds (low <20,000; moderate 20,000–100,000; high >100,000 cells mL⁻¹) rather than measured toxin concentrations, because cyanotoxins themselves were not monitored.

The rise in bloom severity coincided with progressively earlier and longer summer warming of surface waters, earlier onset of stratification, lengthening deep-water hypoxia, and warming of deep waters in non-stratifying reservoirs — a temporal coincidence rather than a manipulated causal test.

Deep-water dissolved oxygen declined across nearly all months and reservoir types between 1989 and 2018 (roughly 0.0 to about -2.1 mg/L depending on month/type per Table 1), and summer hypoxia now persists about two months longer in agricultural reservoirs than earlier in the record.

In stratifying reservoirs, three variables (summer precipitation z-scores, June surface water temperature, and surface-water total Kjeldahl nitrogen/TKN) jointly explained 56.3% of deviance in a GAM of cyanobacteria cell density (adjusted R2 = 0.534 per the associated figure caption) — an association among years, not proof of causation.

In weakly/non-stratifying reservoirs, the best available predictors (August deep-water temperature and June dissolved oxygen) explained far less variance — only 22% of GAM deviance — showing the statistical account of bloom drivers is markedly weaker for that reservoir subgroup.

Total nitrogen and phosphorus concentrations and watershed land cover were largely stable over the study period even as cyanobacteria densities rose sharply, suggesting the bloom increase in these reservoirs is not simply explained by increased bulk nutrient loading or land-use change.

Cyanobacteria are physiologically favored by warming because their growth rates peak at higher temperatures (25-35 degrees C) than other phytoplankton groups such as diatoms and cryptomonads, whose growth declines above roughly 15-25 degrees C — offered as a mechanistic rationale drawn from phytoplankton physiology, not a new experiment in this study.

Potentially toxin-producing taxa dominate these reservoirs' cyanobacterial communities: 9 of the 11 most abundant taxa recorded across all reservoirs and sampling events are possible toxin producers, and their densities increased since the early-to-mid 2000s.

Findings rest on a substantial long-term monitoring record: 20 USACE reservoirs, 1987-2018, with 775 sampling events at the deepest station and 23,043 cyanobacteria cell-density records spanning four depths (surface, 1.52, 3.05, 6.10 m).

The authors argue that reduced within-year sampling frequency after 2005 should, if anything, have made blooms and increasing trends harder to detect, implying the true increase is unlikely to be an artifact of more frequent recent sampling.

Data from targeted monitoring conducted since 2012 in response to publicly reported HAB events were deliberately excluded from the trend analysis to avoid bias from non-random, bloom-triggered sampling; only routine ambient monitoring data were analyzed.
Nutrient data were sparse, especially in 2000-2006, which limits confidence in the nutrient-trend conclusions relative to the temperature/dissolved-oxygen trend conclusions.

numbers: 20 temperate reservoirs studied (USA), built by USACE 1953-1983; surface areas 1-43 km^2; watershed forest cover 1-84%

Reservoir grouping: 5 forested (>65% watershed forest cover, strongly stratifying); 7 agricultural (>30% agricultural cover, strongly stratifying); 7 agricultural + 1 urban (74% developed), weakly/non-stratifying
Monitoring period: 1987-2018 (32 years); temperature/DO depth profiles since 1989 at 1.524 m intervals
775 sampling events at deepest reservoir location; 23,043 cyanobacteria cell-density records across surface, 1.52 m, 3.05 m, 6.10 m depths
WHO-based risk thresholds used: low <20,000 cells mL^-1; moderate 20,000-100,000 cells mL^-1; high >100,000 cells mL^-1
Pre-2003: 83% of observations (n=132) <20,000 cells/mL; 16% (n=26) 20,000-99,999 cells/mL; 1 observation >100,000 cells/mL
Since 2009: 75% of yearly observations (n=110) >100,000 cells/mL (high risk); 21% (n=30) moderate; 4% (n=6) low; 53% of forested-reservoir observations were high-risk

GAM for stratifying reservoirs: 56.3% deviance explained; adjusted R^2 = 0.534 (per figure caption), predictors = summer precipitation z-scores, June surface water temperature, surface-water TKN; n=88 complete observatio
GAM for weakly/non-stratifying reservoirs: only 22% deviance explained; predictors = August deep-water temperature, June dissolved oxygen
Surface-water warming, 1989 vs 2018 monthly means (Table 1): approximately +0.5 degC (July, forested stratifying) to +3.6 degC (October, forested & agricultural stratifying)
Deep-water warming, 1989 vs 2018 monthly means (Table 1): approximately +1.5 degC (June, forested stratifying) to +4.5 degC (October, non-stratifying agricultural)
Deep-water dissolved oxygen change, 1989 vs 2018 monthly means (Table 1): approximately 0.0 mg/L (August, forested stratifying) to about -2.1 mg/L (May, agricultural stratifying)
tensions: This paper attributes much of the observed rise in bloom severity to warming, precipitation patterns, and surface-water TKN, while explicitly stating that "no clear and consistent changes in watershed land cover, precipitation, and total nitrogen or phosphorus concentrations" occurred over the same period - i.e., it documents intensifying blooms without a corresponding rise in bulk total-N/total-P loading. This sits in some tension with HAB literature that centers total (especially total phosphorus) nutrient-loading reduction as the primary dri

title: Integrated explainable deep learning prediction of harmful algal blooms
what: A 2022 peer-reviewed journal article (Technological Forecasting and Social Change, Elsevier, vol. 185, article 122046) presenting an "integrated" convolutional neural network (CNN) model that predicts chlorophyll-a concentration (used as the harmful-algal-bloom proxy) across four major Korean rivers from water-quality

claims.c: The study built an integrated convolutional neural network (CNN) model, using eight water-quality variables and four weather variables as inputs, to predict chlorophyll-a concentration across four major Korean rivers with a single model (rather than one model per site).

Deep SHAP (a Shapley-value-based explainability method adapted for deep networks) was applied to the CNN to identify which input variables drive the chlorophyll-a predictions, explicitly framed as support for policy/decision-making.
The integrated CNN model reduced RMSE by 38.01% and improved R² by 36.16% relative to an LSTM (long short-term memory) baseline model.

The integrated model trained 394 times faster than LSTM-based models, even though it simultaneously predicts at all monitoring stations (whereas the comparison implies LSTM models were trained per station).

The authors frame the model's practical value as decision-support: forecasting HAB spread, surfacing variable influence for decision-makers, and enabling preemptive management responses to reduce economic loss and protect aquatic ecosystems.

36.16% improvement in R² (coefficient of determination) for the integrated CNN model vs. the LSTM baseline (no confidence interval given)

title: Integrative monitoring strategy for marine and freshwater harmful algal blooms and toxins across the freshwater-to-marine continuum
year: 2023 (Integrated Environmental Assessment and Management, Vol. 19, Issue 3, pp. 586–604; first published online/EPA manuscript dated 4 Aug 2022, per Wiley/Oxford Academic listing)
what: A peer-reviewed synthesis/recommendations paper (Howard et al., Integrated Environmental Assessment and Management, 2023) arguing that HAB and cyanotoxin monitoring must be redesigned around the hydrological "freshwater-to-marine continuum" rather than isolated waterbodies. It proposes three recommendations — cross-jur

claims.c: Estuaries and other downstream receiving waters face a 'dual risk' of HAB impacts: blooms originating in the coastal ocean itself, and cyanotoxins produced inland and transported downstream — yet most HAB monitoring programs are not designed to track these hydrological connections.

The paper's core proposal is a three-part integrative monitoring strategy: (1) coordinated monitoring across hydrologically interconnected waterbodies and across organizational/political/jurisdictional boundaries; (2) combining multiple sampling modalities (grab samples, passive samplers, tissue, benthic mats) rather than relying on discrete grab sampling alone; and (3) routine monitoring for toxin mixtures (both marine algal toxins and cyanotoxins) at the land-sea interface.

A prior California statewide stream assessment found more than 90% of stream kilometers supported potential toxin-producing cyanobacteria genera and 23% supported toxin-producing species, with microcystins detected at 33% of sites, lyngbyatoxin at 21%, and saxitoxin or anatoxin-a at 7% of sites.
A separate systematic study of wadeable streams across the southeastern USA (Loftin, Clark, et al. 2016, as cited) detected microcystins in 39% of the streams sampled.

Microcystins bioaccumulate in marine bivalves to concentrations reportedly more than 100-fold greater than in the surrounding water (15–415 µg/kg); California's OEHHA fish-tissue guidance level for MCs is 10 µg/kg and WHO's provisional tolerable daily intake for chronic MC-LR exposure is 0.04 µg/kg body weight — yet no federal or state regulations address cyanotoxins in commercially harvested marine shellfish.

Cyanobacteria salinity tolerance varies widely by species/strain — Florida strains of Microcystis aeruginosa tolerate salinities up to 18 psu (surviving 0.1–18 psu for extended periods, and up to 48 hours in Monterey Bay seawater), while Dolichospermum circinale does not tolerate salinities above 7.5 psu — offered as the physiological basis for why freshwater-origin cyanobacteria and their toxins can persist as they move into brackish/marine water.

Case study (Klamath River/estuary): toxic Microcystis aeruginosa blooms originating in upstream headwater reservoirs (Copco and Iron Gate) were transported more than 300 km downriver, exceeding public health thresholds in downstream waters annually from 2005–2016; molecular methods confirmed Iron Gate Reservoir as the source of the downriver assemblages, and the authors conclude downstream-only mitigation would be ineffective without addressing the upstream reservoir source.

Case study (San Francisco Bay): over the same multi-year monitoring period (Nov 2011–June 2016), passive samplers (SPATT) detected toxins far more often than discrete/grab particulate water samples — microcystins in 76% of SPATT samples vs. 50% of particulate water samples, and domoic acid in 97% of SPATT samples vs. 11% of particulate water samples, with both toxins co-occurring in 73% of SPATT vs. 3% of grab samples.

Case study (San Francisco Bay): mussels showed chronic, multi-toxin contamination — in 2012, microcystins were detected in 82% of mussel samples (up to 18.9 µg/kg) and domoic acid in all mussels across both 2012 and 2014 (20.5–565 µg/kg), with paralytic shellfish toxins in 45%/83% and Dinophysis shellfish toxins in 91%/100% of samples (2012/2014); in endemic Central Bay mussels sampled in 2015, MCs reached up to 416 µg/kg (61% of samples), while DA, PSTs, and DSTs were detected in 98%, 59%, and 71% of samples respectively.

Case study (Eel and Russian Rivers): benthic cyanobacterial mats were themselves a microcystin source at nearly all sampled sites in both rivers (most toxin-producing when present in August–September), and the spatial/temporal patterns of microcystins differed across water, SPATT, and mat sample types — meaning no single sampling modality alone captured the full toxin picture.

Case study (California coastwide survey, Jan–Dec 2017): multiple toxins (≥2) co-occurred simultaneously via SPATT at all monitoring locations during some part of the year; domoic acid was detected in 95% of SPATT samples statewide, anatoxin-a was detected at all locations at least once and consistently at all sites July–September, and a toxin diversity index (Shannon-Wiener based) rose significantly in the second half of the year, peaking in autumn at the Los Angeles River, Santa Cruz Wharf, and San Francisco Bay stations.

Case study (Santa Clara River Estuary): this intermittently closed estuary accumulated cyanotoxins while not flowing to the ocean, then delivered a pulse of substantially higher toxin concentrations to coastal waters after a berm-breaching flushing event (a king tide in December 2017); mussels at coastal sites contained microcystins both the week before and for three weeks after the breach.

Passive samplers (chiefly SPATT), despite rapidly growing use (peer-reviewed SPATT citations increased 14-fold over the past decade), remain limited for regulatory use because it is difficult to relate the toxin concentrations they measure back to numeric health thresholds, and the devices are also vulnerable to vandalism, storm debris, and biofouling.

The authors state that modeling capability lags the monitoring need: most current HAB-relevant models represent land and ocean separately, mechanistic models of coastal biogeochemical processes are not well defined, and there are 'few if any examples of coupled model systems' spanning the freshwater-to-marine continuum.

It reports that dissolved domoic acid is "chronically present, even when blooms are not evident," that benthic cyanobacterial mats (which lack a strong water-surface spectral signature) were a toxin source at nearly all sampled river sites, and that toxin transport can carry contamination more than 300 km downstream of — and weeks aft

title: Machine learning-based prediction of harmful algal blooms in water supply reservoirs
what: A 2022 peer-reviewed journal study that applies random forest (RF) and extreme gradient boosting (XGB) classifiers, combined with SMOTE oversampling for class imbalance and SHAP post-hoc explainability, to predict harmful algal bloom (cyanobacterial) occurrence in eight water-supply reservoirs in South Korea using in-s

claims.c: The study applied random forest (RF) and extreme gradient boosting (XGB) machine learning classifiers to predict HAB occurrence in eight water-supply reservoirs in South Korea.
Applying SMOTE (synthetic minority oversampling technique) to correct imbalanced HAB-occurrence data improved the ML models' classification performance.
RF and XGB performed similarly overall, but XGB was more stable than RF specifically under data imbalance.
Without SMOTE correction, both models showed high overall accuracy (RF 0.71-0.93; XGB 0.71-0.95) but weak performance on the minority bloom-occurrence class (AUC 0.63, recall 0.34, F-measure 0.39).
After SMOTE, the largest performance gain occurred in the Unmun reservoir.
SHAP (Shapley additive explanation), a post-hoc explainability technique, was used to estimate the relative importance of input features to the models' predictions.
Water temperature and the nutrient concentrations total nitrogen (TN) and total phosphorus (TP) were the features found most important to predicting HAB occurrence.
The authors conclude that pairing ML prediction with explanation methods increases the practical usefulness of such models as a decision-support tool for water-quality management.
Predictions were framed as short-horizon (roughly 1-week-ahead) forecasts of bloom occurrence.
