(2) The reported pre-SMOTE contrast between high overall accuracy (0.71-0.95) and weak minority-class metrics (AUC 0.63, recall 0.34, F-measure 0.39) is a

title: New capabilities of Sentinel-2A/B satellites combined with in situ data for monitoring small harmful algal blooms in complex coastal waters
what: A single-event case study (Guadiana estuary, SW Iberian Peninsula, Spain-Portugal border, late June-July 2019) that fuses Sentinel-2 MSI (10 m) satellite imagery with weekly in-situ phytoplankton cell counts to map a small, patchy bloom of the toxic dinoflagellate Lingulodinium polyedra, benchmarking Sentinel-2's detec

claims.c: Sentinel-2's 10 m spatial resolution allowed detection and mapping of a small, patchy, elongated bloom slick that Sentinel-3's 300 m OLCI resolution could not resolve.

Bloom presence/extent was mapped using a red-edge-based Normalized Difference Chlorophyll Index (NDCI) computed from ~665 nm and ~708 nm reflectance bands (only possible for Sentinel-2/3), with NDCI values above zero used as the bloom-presence flag; observed NDCI over bloom pixels ranged 0 to 0.7.

At three spot-check points on the 16 July scene, NDCI was 0.62 and 0.38 inside the bloom versus -0.1 outside it, and the ~704 nm red-edge reflectance peak was identified as the most informative band for bloom mapping.

In-situ phytoplankton counts peaked at 8x10^5 cells/L (monitoring Area 102) during the July 2019 event, far below the up-to-2x10^7 cells/L concentrations the authors cite from the literature for this species elsewhere (Adriatic Sea).
The satellite record shows the bloom first appearing (via Landsat-8) on 30 June 2019, reaching its largest mapped extent on 11 July, and contracting to two confined patches by 16 July.

Several Sentinel-2 scenes (4, 6, 9, 14 and 19 July 2019) were degraded by severe sun-glint on the eastern side of the satellite swath and required masking of affected pixels, a data-quality limitation the authors say could be mitigated by preferring western-swath tiles in this region during summer.

Landsat-8 cannot compute the NDCI at all because it lacks red-edge bands (700-720 nm), and its fallback OC3 chlorophyll algorithm is acknowledged to lose accuracy in optically complex coastal waters containing non-covarying CDOM and suspended matter.

The authors conclude ACOLITE-plus-NDCI is a robust way to map this bloom's extent from Sentinel-2 imagery, but explicitly call for validation across more bloom events and note that shellfish yessotoxin levels linked to this species have never been measured in this region.

numbers: Sentinel-2 MSI spatial resolution: 10 m (bands also at 10-20-60 m depending on band)
Sentinel-3 OLCI spatial resolution: 300 m
Landsat-8 OLI spatial resolution: 30 m

Sentinel-3 revisit frequency: 1-2 days
Landsat-8 repeat cycle: 16 days
Sentinel-2 radiometric resolution: 12 bits
Sentinel-3 OLCI: 21 bands, 400-1020 nm, 14-bit radiometric resolution, ~1270 km swath width
Landsat-8: 12-bit radiometric resolution, tile size 170 km x 185 km
NDCI bands: Rrs at 665 nm (red) and 708 nm (red-edge); bloom-flag threshold NDCI > 0
Observed NDCI range across bloom pixels: 0 to 0.7 (Figure 4)
Spot-check NDCI values, 16 July scene: P1 in-bloom = 0.62; P2 in-bloom = 0.38; P3 outside bloom = -0.1 (Figure 7)
tensions: This is a marine/estuarine dinoflagellate (Lingulodinium polyedra) case, not a freshwater cyanobacterial bloom, so its NDCI thresholds and ACOLITE settings are not validated by this study for the freshwater cyanoHAB context (e.g., EPA CyAN's Sentinel-3-based cyanobacteria index) that is this project's focus - any transfer is methodological/analogical, not a direct application. More substantively, the paper's central finding - that a 300 m sensor (Sentinel-3 OLCI) and coarser sensors generally can entirely miss small, thin, patchy blooms that 10

title: Nitrogen Limitation of Intense and Toxic Cyanobacteria Blooms in Lakes within Two of the Most Visited Parks in the USA: The Lake in Central Park and Prospect Park Lake
what: A five-year (2015–2019) nearshore water-quality/toxin monitoring record plus a detailed 2020 open-water sampling campaign and manipulative nutrient-addition/dilution bioassay experiments on two small, artificial, heavily-visited urban lakes in New York City parks (The Lake in Central Park [LCP] and Prospect Park Lake [

claims.c: Both lakes exhibited extremely elevated cyanobacterial biomass and microcystin over five years of nearshore monitoring, with cyanobacteria dominating phytoplankton biomass.
Cyanobacteria made up more than 99% of total phytoplankton biomass across both lakes from 2015 to 2019.
Nutrient-addition and dilution bioassay experiments showed that nitrogen, not phosphorus, consistently limited cyanobacterial growth, whereas green algae were rarely nutrient-limited.

DIN:DIP molar ratios in both lakes stayed well below the Redfield ratio of 5 all season and fell below 1 during the July–August bloom peak, indicating N was scarce relative to P exactly when blooms were most intense.

Absolute dissolved inorganic nitrogen concentrations, not just the N:P ratio, were low enough (below ~2 µM for most of the summer) to be growth-limiting on their own, since this is below the reported half-saturation constant for most bloom-forming cyanobacteria.

In manipulative bioassays, phosphorus addition alone never stimulated cyanobacterial or green-algal growth in LCP, while nitrogen addition (5 or 15 µM nitrate) significantly increased cyanobacterial growth in both lakes.

Public drinking water used to replenish these lakes during summer evaporation is comparatively rich in phosphorus and poor in nitrogen, and experimental addition of that drinking water to lake water significantly enhanced cyanobacterial growth rates.

Measured microcystin concentrations routinely and vastly exceeded recreational and health-based guideline levels (EPA 8 µg/L; WHO 20 µg/L 'moderate probability of adverse effects'; NY DEC >20 µg/L notification threshold), framed by the authors as a potential public-health concern given these parks' heavy visitation.
The microcystin-synthetase gene marker mcyE was significantly correlated with measured microcystin toxin concentrations in the 2020 dataset across both lakes.

The saxitoxin-synthesis gene sxtA was detected at high copy numbers (up to ~10^6 copies/mL), especially in PPL and in early summer, but actual saxitoxin toxin concentrations were not measured, leaving open whether saxitoxin itself reached concerning levels.

The very high nearshore microcystin/biomass values reported for 2015–2019 are not representative of open/offshore lake conditions: 2020 offshore sampling found levels generally orders of magnitude lower even though blooms still persisted all summer.
The specific sources supplying nitrogen to these urban lakes (sediment release, drinking-water supplementation, watershed/runoff loading) were not apportioned or quantified in this study.

Cyanobacterial chlorophyll a, 5-yr nearshore average: 1.0 × 10^5 µg cyano-chl a L⁻¹ (abstract)
Cyanobacteria = >99% of total phytoplankton biomass, 2015–2019

2020 open-water PPL microcystin: 0.28 µg L⁻¹ (June) rising to peak 0.47 µg L⁻¹ (September)

It also surfaces a counterintuitive, system-specific mechanism: topping up these artificial, tributary-less urban lakes with P-rich, comparatively N-poor municipal drinking water (N:P <0.5) to offset su

title: pH-dependent gas vesicle formation in Microcystis
what: A laboratory molecular-physiology study showing that raising extracellular pH increases gas-vesicle abundance and cell buoyancy in cultured Microcystis, via reduced decay (increased stability) of gas-vesicle-protein (gvp) gene transcripts over a roughly 48-hour timescale. The primary URL (Wiley/FEBS Letters) is paywall

claims.c: Gas vesicle abundance and cell buoyancy in Microcystis both increase in response to elevation of extracellular pH.

Within 48 hours of a pH upshift, gas vesicle protein (gvp) genes are upregulated at both the mRNA and protein level, and the abstract attributes this specifically to reduced decay of gvp transcripts (i.e., a post-transcriptional/mRNA-stability mechanism) rather than describing it as increased transcription.
The pH effect on GvpC (gas vesicle protein C) level is essentially independent of inorganic carbon (Ci) availability, i.e., Ci does not appear to explain or confound the pH effect.

The authors frame this as the first report that long-term (sustained) pH range, specifically, controls gas vesicle formation in certain Microcystis species — an explicit novelty claim that is also self-bounded to 'certain' species, not Microcystis or cyanobacteria in general.
The environmental motivation for the study is that lakes with seasonal cyanobacterial blooms show pH fluctuating from slightly above 7 to around 10.

(Secondary, unverified) The pH-upshift experiment used a Microcystis culture referred to as 'Microcystis 930', grown in BG11 medium buffered with TES to a final concentration of 8 mM, comparing pH 7.0 versus pH 9.0; gvpA1 and gvpC transcripts were measured by qRT-PCR with gene-specific primers, GvpC protein by Western blot, and gas vesicles by electron microscopy.

(Secondary, unverified) Because full induction of GvpC took about 48 h, the authors reasoned that gas-vesicle abundance would not track short-term diurnal pH fluctuations in lakes, but instead responds to the lake's longer-term/seasonal pH regime — used to argue that long-term pH (not daily swings) is what is mechanistically linked to surface Microcystis bloom formation.

(Secondary, unverified, single-mention) Microcystis 930 reportedly carries three tandem gvpA gene copies that encode an identical protein but differ at the nucleotide (silent-substitution) level, which is given as the reason gene-specific primers were needed to isolate gvpA1 expression.

numbers: PRIMARY (verbatim abstract): pH in lakes with seasonal cyanobacterial blooms fluctuates from 'slightly above 7 to around 10' (qualitative range, no decimal bounds stated)
PRIMARY (verbatim abstract): gvp gene upregulation (mRNA + protein) occurs 'within 48 h' after pH upshift
PRIMARY (citation metadata): FEBS Letters, Volume 590, Issue 18, pages 3195-3201, published September 2016
PRIMARY (identifiers): PMID 27543911; DOI 10.1002/1873-3468.12370
SECONDARY/unverified (WebSearch-derived, not in the fetched abstract): experimental pH upshift compared pH 7.0 to pH 9.0
SECONDARY/unverified: TES buffer added to BG11 medium to a final concentration of 8 mM
SECONDARY/unverified: culture/strain referred to as 'Microcystis 930'; one single-source mention of a culture temperature of 28 degrees C
SECONDARY/unverified (but consistent with the primary 48 h figure): 'full induction of gvpC took about 48 h' after the pH-7.0-to-9.0 transfer
tensions: (1) Causality/confounding: in real lakes, elevated pH is itself often a downstream consequence of intense photosynthesis by dense algal/cyanobacterial populations (CO2 drawdown raises pH), so rising pH and bloom development can be mutually reinforcing rather than pH acting as a clean, one-directional upstream driver of buoyancy/scum formation. The abstract available to me frames pH as inducing gas-vesicle formation but does not address (in the text I have) whether bloom biomass itself elevates ambient pH; treating pH as an 'independent' predict

title: PRISM High-Resolution Spatial Climate Data for the United States (max/min temp, dewpoint, precipitation)
year: Not clearly stated as a single publication year; page metadata lists dataset "Years of Record" as 1895-01 to 2023-04, implying the page/content was last current around 2023
what: A data-guide article (NCAR/UCAR Climate Data Guide) describing PRISM, a set of gridded (statistically interpolated, not raw point) monthly, yearly, and daily climate data products -- mean/max/min temperature, precipitation, and dewpoint -- for the conterminous United States, produced by the PRISM Climate Group from sta

claims.c: PRISM produces gridded (interpolated, not raw point) climate data products -- mean/max/min temperature, precipitation, and dewpoint -- primarily for the United States, generated by a statistical mapping system that accounts for terrain effects such as orography, rain shadows, temperature inversions, slope aspect, and coastal proximity.
Climatological normals are distributed at 30 arcsec (~800 m) resolution, while monthly (and daily) operational grids are distributed at 2.5 arcmin (~4 km) resolution.

PRISM's daily record begins 1 January 1981 and its monthly record begins January 1895, both continuing to the page's stated 'present'; a metadata line on the page lists the record as running through April 2023.
Each day, PRISM's estimates are built from data collected from roughly 20,000 stations reporting precipitation, 10,000 reporting temperature, and 4,000 reporting dew point/vapor pressure deficit.

PRISM precipitation grids (the 'AN81' product) incorporate radar-aided analyses east of the Rockies (NEXRAD-based Stage II/Stage IV AHPS radar/rain-gauge analyses, beginning in 2002), which the source states is important for capturing convective precipitation.

Published regional validation (attributed by the page to Daly et al. 2008) found mean absolute errors in PRISM's annual precipitation normals of 5% in the eastern US versus 10% in the western US, and temperature errors of 0.5 degC (east) versus 1.0 degC (west).
In a comparison against the dense Coweeta (NC) rain-gauge network, PRISM's monthly precipitation grids matched within 5%.
In a Walker Basin (CA-NV) validation study, individual-site mean absolute errors for max/min temperature ranged from 1.1 to 3.7 degC, with better performance in summer than in winter.
The source explicitly warns that PRISM data are not homogenized and therefore should not be used for century-long trend analysis.

Monthly PRISM temperature grids can disagree with the average of that month's daily grids because the two products draw on different station subsets: all available stations feed the monthlies, while only 'PRISM Day' morning observers and hourly stations feed the dailies.

High-resolution (800 m) PRISM timeseries data now require a fee and are no longer distributed in netCDF format; only the coarser 4-km products and the free 800-m 30-year normals remain freely downloadable.

Annual precipitation normals mean absolute error: 10% in the western US (cites Daly et al. 2008)

Two points are worth flagging for a HAB dossier that emphasizes leakage/overfitting rigor: (1) PRISM is itself an interpolated/modeled product built from station data (plus radar for precipitation), not raw in-

title: Quantifying the Human Health Benefits of Using Satellite Information to Detect Cyanobacterial Harmful Algal Blooms and Manage Recreational Advisories in U.S. Lakes
what: A peer-reviewed "impact assessment" paper that builds an economic (cost-of-illness) framework to monetize the socioeconomic/human-health benefit of using the multiagency CyAN satellite system (EPA/NASA/USGS/NOAA) to detect cyanobacterial HABs and trigger recreational advisories, then applies that framework to a real 20

claims.c: Satellite (CyAN) availability during the 2017 Utah Lake cyanoHAB event is estimated to have produced a central socioeconomic (human-health) benefit of approximately $370,000 (2017 USD), with a sensitivity-analysis range of $55,000–$1,057,000.
The counterfactual (no-satellite-data) scenario assumes a ~7-day detection/advisory-posting delay, during which an estimated 8,000 people were exposed to the bloom at Utah Lake access points.

Applying a 5% gastrointestinal (GI) symptom rate to the 8,000 exposed people yields roughly 200 mild GI cases and roughly 198–200 moderate-or-severe cases; of the moderate/severe group, about 30 are estimated to be severe.

Cost per case of cyanoHAB-associated illness, adjusted to 2017 USD from DeFlorio-Barker et al. (2018), is $11.23 for mild cases, $264.38 for moderate cases, and $10,719.02 for severe cases (excluding mortality).

CyAN estimates cyanobacterial cell density (cells/mL) via the Cyanobacteria Index algorithm using MERIS (historically, on Envisat) and, from 2016 onward, Sentinel-3 OLCI data — it is a proxy for bloom presence, not a direct toxin measurement.
National-scale cyanotoxin prevalence appears to be increasing: EPA's National Lakes Assessment detected microcystin in 39% of U.S. lakes in 2012, a 9.5 percentage-point increase from 2007.

The authors estimate the CyAN program's ongoing annual operating cost at roughly $141,000/year, covering data storage/processing (~200 GB/year at $0.40/GB) and two GS-12-equivalent scientist salaries (~$70,000/year each) — but this excludes satellite launch and sensor manufacturing costs.

The authors state several explicit limitations of their own estimate: full advisory compliance was (unrealistically) assumed, pet/livestock harms were excluded, lost recreational-economy/tourism revenue was excluded, and respiratory-symptom costs were excluded as not cleanly comparable in the literature.

The authors explicitly caution that the Utah Lake dollar estimate is not generalizable: Utah Lake's unusually high visitor numbers likely make its benefit estimate higher than a 'typical' U.S. lake, and cross-lake variation in bloom/use characteristics prevents national extrapolation.

As of 2019, Utah shifted to permanent, year-round posted cyanoHAB warnings at Utah Lake regardless of detection timing, which the authors say reduces the future decision-relevant value of satellite-based early detection specifically at that lake.

title: Reducing the water residence time is inadequate to limit the algal proliferation in eutrophic lakes
what: A single-lake field-monitoring plus laboratory nutrient-enrichment bioassay case study testing whether shortening a lake's water residence time (WRT) — a hydrodynamic control strategy some cities use to protect drinking-water-source lakes/reservoirs from algal blooms — is by itself adequate to prevent bloom risk when t

claims.c: Even when water residence time (WRT) is reduced to about 3 days in a lake whose inflow is already eutrophic, the risk of algal bloom occurrence still exists — shortening WRT alone is not adequate to prevent blooms.
Despite generally meeting surface-water environmental quality standards, the studied lake had a high average dissolved inorganic nitrogen (DIN) concentration.

Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) concentration increased substantially along the inlet-to-outlet flow path; in July the outlet-site Chl-a was 8.1 times the inlet-site value, and cyanobacteria dominated phytoplankton cell density.
In nutrient-enrichment growth bioassays, algae grew rapidly within just 3 days under baseline/ambient conditions.
Adding nitrogen and phosphorus further increased algal growth rates in the bioassays, with phosphorus-addition treatments producing the highest rates.

The authors conclude hydrodynamic control measures (e.g., raising flow rate / shortening WRT) may not fully protect a drinking-water-source lake from algal blooms, particularly when its inflow is already eutrophic.

It argues nutrient loading remains the binding constraint (DIN stayed high despite the short 3-day WRT, and N/P-addition bioassays raised growth rates further), which would complicate any HAB mitigation narrative built primarily around hydrodynamic/flushing c

title: Remote sensing to detect harmful algal blooms in inland waterbodies
what: A peer-reviewed primary-research journal article (Liu, Glamore, Tamburic, Morrow & Johnson, 2022, Science of the Total Environment, 851(Pt 1):158096) that empirically compares three satellite platforms (Planetscope, Sentinel-2, Landsat-8) and 20 existing spectral-index algorithms plus a Self-Organizing Map (SOM) method

claims.c: Satellite remote sensing is already a well-established HAB monitoring tool for large lakes, but reliably detecting HABs in small-to-medium waterbodies via satellite data is still an unsolved problem.

The abstract enumerates three specific current barriers to satellite-based HAB detection in small/medium waterbodies: waterbody size itself, scarcity of freely available high-resolution imagery, and lack of field (in-situ) calibration data.

The study empirically compared three satellite platforms — Planetscope, Sentinel-2, and Landsat-8 — specifically to isolate how spatial resolution, spectral-band availability, and waterbody size each affect HAB detection skill.
Among 20 existing HAB-detection spectral-index algorithms tested (alongside a non-parametric Self-Organizing Map method), the indices 'Curvature Around Red' and 'NIR minus Red' performed best.
Landsat-8 and Sentinel-2 outperformed Planetscope for HAB detection in small-to-medium waterbodies, despite Planetscope's finer native spatial resolution.

The single most important satellite attribute for HAB detection is which spectral bands are available on the sensor, because band availability constrains which detection algorithms can even be applied — outweighing spatial resolution as a driver of performance.

Across the satellites and algorithms tested, detection-algorithm performance showed little relationship to the physical size of the waterbody being monitored (an associational finding from this comparison, not a demonstrated causal mechanism).

Even where a good detection algorithm/satellite pairing exists, practical barriers remain: algae dynamics, macrophyte (aquatic plant) cover within the waterbody, weather effects, and imperfect satellite data correction models, plus a need to time-match satellite overpasses with field sampling for calibration.

The authors' operational recommendation is to combine regular in-situ/field sampling with satellite remote sensing, rather than relying on satellite data alone, for monitoring and managing small-to-medium waterbodies.

20 existing HAB-detection algorithms tested, plus 1 additional non-parametric method (Self-Organizing Map, SOM)

Publication date: 10 Dec 2022 (Epub 18 Aug 2022)

title: Repeated low-dose copper algaecide treatments promote beneficial phytoplankton and zooplankton communities
what: A 2025 peer-reviewed field study (Journal of Environmental Management) reporting a 42-day field mesocosm experiment in a hypereutrophic aquaculture pond that compares repeated, lower "MLR" (pH- and dissolved-organic-carbon-based) copper sulfate dosing against conventional, higher alkalinity-based copper dosing for cyan

claims.c: Conventional copper sulfate treatment using alkalinity-based dosing commonly prescribes higher copper doses than are actually necessary to achieve HAB control in many systems.

The experiment compared three copper dosing regimens — alkalinity-based (350 µg/L Cu), full MLR-based (80 µg/L Cu), and half-MLR (40 µg/L Cu) — each reapplied every 14 days for 3 applications across a 42-day field mesocosm trial in a hypereutrophic aquaculture pond, designed to mimic real repeated-application practice.

All three copper doses — including the two much lower MLR-based doses — produced an identical, strong initial reduction in cyanobacteria, indicating the lower doses controlled the bloom just as effectively at the outset as the higher conventional dose.
Over the full 42-day course of the experiment, the lower MLR-based doses caused less harm than the higher alkalinity-based dose to beneficial chlorophytes (green algae) and to zooplankton.
With repeated applications, the higher alkalinity-based dose became progressively less effective at controlling harmful phytoplankton, whereas the lower doses did not show this decline in efficacy.

The authors conclude that a repeated, low, MLR-based copper dosing strategy gives better sustained (long-term) HAB control with less ecological impact than the conventional higher, alkalinity-based dosing approach.

The MLR-based dosing approach tested here computes the copper dose from water pH and dissolved organic carbon (DOC) rather than from alkalinity, and was developed in prior work; this paper's contribution is testing that method under repeated, real-world-style dosing rather than introducing the method itself.

title: Response of cyanobacteria and phytoplankton abundance to warming, extreme rainfall events and nutrient enrichment
what: A 13-month (July 2014-August 2015) full-factorial mesocosm experiment at the UK Centre for Ecology & Hydrology's Aquatic Mesocosm Facility (32 outdoor 3,000-L mesocosms, 54°1'N 2°46'W) testing the individual and combined effects of warming (+4°C), simulated extreme-rainfall 'flushing' events, and nutrient enrichment (e

claims.c: Warming and nutrient enrichment, applied alone, each significantly increased cyanobacteria, but when combined the two stressors produced a weak antagonistic (sub-additive, negative-interaction) effect rather than reinforcing one another.
Occurrence of the toxin-forming genus Microcystis was overwhelmingly concentrated in the heated (warmed) mesocosms.
Dolichospermum spp. biovolume increased significantly under the warming treatment.
Aphanizomenon spp. biovolume increased significantly under nutrient enrichment.
Pseudanabaena spp. presence and biovolume both increased significantly under nutrient enrichment.
Nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria genera dominated the overall cyanobacterial community by biovolume across the experiment.
In winter, nutrient-enriched and flushed mesocosms showed disproportionate cyanobacterial dominance compared to all other treatment combinations.
The +4°C warming treatment used in the experiment sits within the upper range of IPCC-projected warming under the RCP8.5 scenario.
The primary cyanobacteria measurement tool (a submersible phycocyanin fluorometer) was cross-validated against, and correlated well with, independent microscope-based biovolume counts.

A fluorometer calibration/interface error meant that cyanobacteria chlorophyll-a data prior to May 2015 were compromised and were explicitly excluded from statistical modeling, even though they are shown and discussed descriptively.
Cyanobacterial cell densities reached under the study's warmed/nutrient-enriched conditions exceeded World Health Organization guideline thresholds for drinking and bathing water safety.

Treatment effects (warming, nutrients, flushing and their interactions) explained only a modest share of variance in cyanobacteria chlorophyll-a; a further, larger share of variance was attributable to differences between individual mesocosms rather than to the manipulated treatments.

The authors explicitly bound the applicability of their findings to enriched, shallow lakes, stating that climate-change responses are expected to differ in deep lakes and that food-web factors not manipulated here (grazing, macrophytes) also shape real-world outcomes.

The authors hypothesize that carbon (dissolved inorganic carbon) limitation may explain the observed warming x nutrient antagonism, having ruled out nitrogen and light limitation as mechanisms, but state this hypothesis could not be directly tested.

The authors recommend that nutrient loads may need to be substantially reduced in shallow lakes to counteract both the direct effect of warming on bloom-forming cyanobacteria growth rates and its indirect effect via enhanced nutrient cycling.

The authors caveat that their simulated flushing (extreme-rainfall) events were likely too infrequent and too short in duration to fully capture real extreme-rainfall impacts on lake phytoplankton, which may explain the limited long-term flushing effect observed.

Nutrient-enriched (hypertrophic) mesocosms achieved markedly higher total phosphorus and total nitrogen concentrations than ambient-nutrient mesocosms, which sat near the eutrophic-hypertrophic boundary.

title: Response of cyanobacterial blooms to climate warming: evidence from satellite observations and long-term trends in Lake Taihu in China
what: A peer-reviewed, open-access study (Scientific Reports, 2025) that fuses 20 years (2003–2022) of MODIS/Aqua and MODIS/Terra satellite imagery (NDVI-based cyanobacterial bloom detection, ~14,600 images) with in-situ hourly meteorological station data (5 ground stations run by the Jiangsu Climate Center) to quantify how

claims.c: Lake Taihu's annual average air temperature rose over 2003–2022 (mean 17.1°C), increasing by about 0.4°C per decade; linear trend Tyear = 0.04t + 16.86 (R = 0.49, p < 0.05).

The headline dose–response finding: for every 1°C rise in annual average temperature, annual cumulative satellite-detected bloom area is estimated to increase by about 5,377 km² (S = 5377.1T − 78931; R = 0.44, p < 0.05); cumulative bloom area itself trended upward by about 3,566 km²/decade on average.

The first observed bloom date (FOD) each year shifted earlier over the record — about 39 days/decade earlier on average (41 days earlier comparing the second half of the record to the first) — and is negatively correlated with winter–spring (Dec–May) mean temperature (FOD = −0.02·TWin-Spr + 12.55; R = −0.73), a temperature series that itself rose ~0.7°C/decade.

The last observed bloom date (LOD) each year shifted later — about 18 days/decade later on average (23 days later in the second half of the record vs. the first) — and is positively correlated with November–December mean temperature (LOD = 0.02·TNov-Dec + 2.34; R = 0.6); the Nov–Dec temperature trend itself was weak (TNov-Dec = 0.02t − 21.49, R = 0.11).
The annual cyanobacterial bloom season lengthened, from 238 days in the first decade of the record to 302 days in the second decade — a 27% increase in duration.

The relationship between temperature and bloom extent/frequency follows an empirical S-shaped curve: blooms are rarely observed below 5°C; area and frequency both exceed 10% of their annual totals at 15°C, rising above 20% at 20°C; both peak at 30°C (32% of area, 28% of frequency); and both decline sharply above 30°C.
The annual frequency (count) of satellite-detected bloom events shows a continuous increasing trend over 2003–2022 (Frequencyyear = 5.69t + 38.86).

2006–2007, 2017 and 2019–2020 were the most severe bloom periods in the 20-year record, with annual bloom areas roughly 30% above the long-term average; 2003, 2004, 2009 and 2014 had areas roughly 30% below average.

Bloom extent was mapped from 14,600 MODIS/Aqua and MODIS/Terra images (2003–2022, cloud-masked and 6S-atmospherically-corrected) using an NDVI-based threshold of −0.02, calibrated from 100 paired MODIS RGB/NDVI images (candidate thresholds ranged −0.0086 to −0.0325); 1,973 images retained a detectable bloom area ≥1 km².

The authors themselves caution that temperature alone does not fully explain interannual bloom variability — e.g., 2004 and 2016–2020 had similar average annual temperatures but markedly different cumulative bloom areas — attributing residual variation to other climatic factors, nutrient levels and human intervention, and stating no single climatic factor can comprehensively describe bloom dynamics.

numbers: Study period: 2003–2022 (20 years)
Lake Taihu area: ≈2338 km²; average depth: 1.9 m (described as third-largest freshwater lake in China)
Reported coordinates: 30°50′–32°80′N, 119°80′–121°55′E (minute values >59 are internally inconsistent — likely a transcription/OCR artifact in the source extraction; reported as-is, not corrected)
Satellite images used: 14,600 MODIS/Aqua + MODIS/Terra images (2003–2022)
Images retained with detectable bloom (≥1 km²): 1,973 images
NDVI bloom threshold: −0.02 (candidate range −0.0086 to −0.0325, calibrated from 100 image pairs)
Meteorological stations: 5 (Dongshan, Wujiang, Suzhou, Wuxi, Yixing), hourly data, 2003–2022, per standard GB/T 35226−2017
Annual mean temperature: 17.1°C; trend +0.4°C/decade; Tyear = 0.04t + 16.86, R = 0.49, p < 0.05
Seasonal warming rate: 0.2–0.7°C/decade; spring/winter ≈0.6°C/decade vs. summer/autumn ≈0.2°C/decade
Winter–spring (Dec–May) temperature trend: +0.7°C/decade
Nov–Dec temperature trend: TNov-Dec = 0.02t − 21.49, R = 0.11
Bloom-area sensitivity to warming: +5,377 km² per +1°C annual mean temperature; S = 5377.1T − 78931, R = 0.44, p < 0.05
tensions: The paper's own limitations section already undercuts a simple "warming alone drives blooms" reading: it explicitly notes that years with near-identical mean temperature had very different bloom extents and attributes the gap to nutrients and human intervention, and that no single climatic factor can fully explain bloom dynamics. Despite this, the abstract/conclusion use causal-sounding language ("climate warming promotes/drives the expansion of cyanobacterial blooms") that is stronger than what a purely correlational satellite-vs-meteorology r

title: Retention and Internal Loading of Phosphorus in Shallow, Eutrophic Lakes
what: A review/synthesis article — "a general overview of the nature and important mechanisms behind internal loading of phosphorus (P)... in shallow, eutrophic lakes upon a reduction of the external loading" (The Scientific World Journal, Vol. 1, pp. 427–442, published 23 Aug 2001; DOI 10.1100/tsw.2001.72). It explains why

claims.c: Internal P loading is a common phenomenon in shallow, eutrophic lakes following reductions in external P loading, such that lake water quality is not improved as expected.
After external load reduction, summer total-P concentrations can rise and whole-lake P retention can turn negative during most of the summer.
The internal P release originates from a phosphorus pool that accumulated in the sediment during the earlier period of high external loading.

In most lake sediments, P bound to redox-sensitive iron compounds and P fixed in labile organic forms are the major sediment-P fractions that are potentially mobile and can be released to the water column.
The recovery period after a reduction in external P loading depends on the lake's loading history and can last for decades where sediment P accumulation is high.
During the recovery phase, both the duration and the net P release rate from the sediment tend to decline progressively over time.
Internal P loading is strongly influenced by a lake's biological (food-web) structure, illustrated by lakes shifting from a turbid to a clearwater state, e.g. via biomanipulation.
In lakes that shift to a clearwater state, phosphorus concentrations may fall to about 50% of pre-biomanipulation levels, shortening the period of negative summer P retention.

Restoration methods such as sediment dredging (to remove accumulated P) or addition of iron or alum (to raise sediment P-sorption capacity) can significantly shorten the duration of internal P loading.

Long-term water-quality benefits from these restoration methods require a sufficient reduction in external P loading as a prerequisite; internal-loading treatments alone are implied to be insufficient without it.

numbers: 50% — total phosphorus (TP) concentration in lakes that have shifted from a turbid to a clearwater state (e.g., via biomanipulation), relative to the pre-biomanipulation TP level; stated as "may be reduced to," i.e., a q

"Decades" — qualitative, non-numeric order-of-magnitude estimate for how long the sediment-driven internal-loading/recovery period can last in lakes with high sediment P accumulation; no specific year range, mean duratio

Volume 1, pp. 427–442 — bibliographic locator (The Scientific World Journal / "The Scientific World"), not a scientific data point, confirmed via PMC metadata fetch and the Aarhus University (pure.au.dk) publication reco
tensions: This review's central point — that shallow eutrophic lakes can show negative P retention and rising summer TP for years to decades after external load cuts, driven by legacy sediment P release — complicates simpler steady-state/Vollenweider-type mass-balance framings (common elsewhere in eutrophication literature) that treat in-lake P as tracking external loading with only a short lag. It implies that a remote-sensing or in-situ nutrient/chlorophyll signal measured soon after a watershed nutrient-management intervention could still reflect inte

title: Retrieval of phycocyanin concentration from remote-sensing reflectance using a semi-analytic model in eutrophic lakes
what: A 2013 peer-reviewed methods paper (Lyu, Wang, Wu, Zhu, Yin, Li & Huang; Ecological Informatics, Vol. 18, pp. 178-187) that evaluates the semi-analytic phycocyanin (PC) retrieval algorithm of Simis et al. (2005) and proposes a modified version -- replacing a constant specific-absorption coefficient with a concentration

claims.c: Phycocyanin (PC) is framed as a pigment unique to freshwater cyanobacteria ('blue-green algae') and is commonly used as a quantitative indicator of cyanobacteria abundance in eutrophic inland waters -- the motivating rationale for retrieving it remotely.
The study's stated purpose was to evaluate the existing semi-analytic PC retrieval algorithm developed by Simis et al. and to test whether it could be improved for eutrophic lakes such as Taihu Lake.

The authors recalibrated the Simis-model correction coefficients (γ and δ) -- which scale the absorption of chlorophyll-a at 665 nm and of PC at 620 nm -- from their own in-situ measurements rather than reusing the originally published coefficients.

The specific absorption coefficient of PC at 620 nm, a*pc(620), was found to decrease exponentially as PC concentration increases rather than being constant as Simis et al. assumed; the authors replaced the constant with a non-linear power-function of a*pc(620) in their revised algorithm.
In Taihu Lake, the improved retrieval algorithm achieved R² = 0.55 and RMSE = 58.89 µg/L for phycocyanin concentration.

The improved algorithm is reported to have 'generally superior performance' relative to the original Simis et al. algorithm, but the source text as retrieved does not give the original algorithm's own R2/RMSE figures on the same Taihu data, so no side-by-side numeric baseline could be confirmed.

The improved algorithm was validated against an independent dataset from Dianchi Lake, and an accompanying error analysis is reported to show it generalizes ('universality') better than the original algorithm, particularly at higher PC concentrations.

Retrieval error is reported to be primarily associated with the ratio of total suspended solids to phycocyanin (TSS:PC), rather than the ratio of chlorophyll-a to phycocyanin (Chl-a:PC); both the original and improved models are indicated to perform poorly when PC concentration is low and TSS is high.

The recalibrated correction coefficients (γ, δ) are described as differing from values used/reported elsewhere, attributed to the coefficients being site-dependent because of differing bio-optical properties among lakes.

numbers: R² = 0.55 (improved phycocyanin retrieval algorithm, calibrated/tested in Taihu Lake)
RMSE = 58.89 µg/L (improved phycocyanin retrieval algorithm, Taihu Lake; no confidence interval reported in retrievable text)
Journal: Ecological Informatics, Volume 18, pp. 178–187 (2013)
Key absorption wavelengths used in the underlying semi-analytic (Simis-type) model: 620 nm (phycocyanin absorption) and 665 nm (chlorophyll-a absorption)
tensions: The paper's central move -- replacing a constant specific-absorption coefficient a*pc(620) (as used in the original Simis et al. 2005 algorithm, calibrated elsewhere) with a lake/concentration-dependent power-function to fit Taihu Lake, plus finding the gamma/delta coefficients themselves to be "site dependent" -- indicates that semi-analytic phycocyanin retrieval algorithms do not transfer cleanly across water bodies without local recalibration. For a HAB early-warning or multi-lake product, this complicates any assumption that a single reflec

title: Retrieving Inland Water Quality Parameters via Satellite Remote Sensing: Sensor Evaluation, Atmospheric Correction, and Machine Learning Approaches
what: A 2025 peer-reviewed literature review and qualitative evaluation (not a new empirical retrieval study) examining the three components required to retrieve inland-water quality parameters — chlorophyll-a, colored dissolved organic matter (CDOM), and non-algal particles (NAP) — from optical satellite imagery: satellite

claims.c: Effective retrieval of water quality parameters from optical satellite imagery requires three components: (1) a sensor sensitive to water-quality-driven optical variation, (2) accurate atmospheric correction to recover water-leaving radiance/reflectance, and (3) a bio-optical model linking the optical signal to the water quality parameter.

The paper reviews decommissioned, active, and upcoming satellite sensors and introduces a ranking method for selecting sensors suited to retrieving chlorophyll-a, CDOM, and non-algal particles specifically in inland (not open-ocean) waters, intended to aid sensor selection for future studies.

No single atmospheric correction (AC) algorithm evaluated performs consistently well across all inland-water conditions; algorithm choice should instead be matched to the specific use case based on each algorithm's documented strengths and weaknesses.

Machine-learning-based bio-optical retrieval models for inland water quality have four specific, named limitations — low generalizability, low dimensionality, spatial/temporal autocorrelation, and information leakage — which motivate locally trained models, rigorous cross-validation, and integration of auxiliary data to increase dimensionality as mitigations.
The paper concludes with recommendations for promising future research directions spanning the three reviewed components (sensors, atmospheric correction, ML-based bio-optical modeling).

numbers: No quantitative retrieval-accuracy metrics (R2, RMSE, MAE, bias, SNR, etc.) are stated in the abstract; such values, if present, would be in the inaccessible full-text tables/figures (e.g., the described sensor-ranking t
171 references cited (Crossref 'reference-count' metadata field — bibliometric, not a scientific finding).
Published online 15 May 2025; Remote Sensing (MDPI), ISSN 2072-4292, Volume 17, Issue 10, Article No. 1734 (Crossref/DOI metadata).
13 citing works recorded by Crossref ('is-referenced-by-count') as of the Crossref index snapshot dated 2026-06-20 — a growing bibliometric count, not a fixed statistic.
tensions: This review's own abstract flags exactly the validation pitfalls (spatial/temporal autocorrelation and "information leakage" inflating apparent skill, plus low cross-site generalizability) that many single-lake or single-study ML-based chlorophyll-a/CDOM/NAP retrieval papers may not adequately guard against — this source is independent, review-level (171-reference) corroboration that reported retrieval accuracies elsewhere in the HAB remote-sensing literature should be read cautiously unless those studies specifically test spatial/temporal gene

title: Sediment Resuspension as a System-Wide Driver of Legacy and Bioavailable Phosphorus Release in Lake Erie
what: A 2026 peer-reviewed primary research article (Dhiman et al., Environmental Science & Technology) that combines in-situ sediment phosphorus fractionation, Beryllium-7 radiotracer analysis, and Sentinel-3 satellite-derived suspended-particulate-matter (SPM) retrievals to produce what the authors call "the first even

claims.c: During the observed resuspension event, sediments released bioavailable phosphorus (BioP) at 2.3–11 × 10⁻² g m⁻², which the authors calculate is 22–256× greater than previously reported aerobic diffusive P fluxes, establishing resuspension as a major episodic internal P source in western Lake Erie.

Scaling in-situ sediment P data to the whole basin via satellite-derived ΔSPM, the authors estimate the May 18–26, 2023 event mobilized 40,314 kg of total phosphorus (TP) basin-wide and released 12,665 kg of bioavailable phosphorus (BioP) as dissolved P to the water column, with resuspended sediment accounting for 89.2% of total resuspended material across the lake.

The single event's mobilized P equaled roughly 1.6% and 3.3% of the 2017–2021 mean Maumee River spring TP and SRP loads respectively, or roughly 4.7% (Results) / 4.8% (Abstract) and 7.0% of the current, reduced spring target loads for TP and SRP respectively.

Beryllium-7 (⁷Be) depth profiles show that a spring 2023 resuspension episode increased the sediment mixing rate (Db) roughly 5-fold at the near-river station WLE1 (14.5 to 70.51 cm² yr⁻¹) and roughly 12-fold at nearshore station WLE13 (5.43 to 69.6 cm² yr⁻¹), consistent with resuspension reworking multi-year sediment deposits and remobilizing legacy P.

The satellite SPM algorithm used to detect resuspension carries a validated uncertainty of NRMSE = 13.9%, and only pixel-level SPM changes exceeding this threshold were classified as resuspension events.

Satellite-derived erosion depth during the May 2023 event varied spatially: 1.39 cm at the shallow nearshore station WLE13 (the highest observed value), about 0.3 cm each at WLE14 and WLE16, with a basin-wide range of 0.2 to 0.8 cm across western Lake Erie.

Despite comparable satellite-estimated erosion depths at WLE14 and WLE16 (~0.3 cm each), only WLE14 showed a corresponding rise in dissolved SRP, showing that resuspension magnitude alone does not linearly predict local bioavailable-P response — the authors attribute WLE16's muted response to its peripheral position receiving water already depleted of BioP.

The authors state that most existing ecological and HAB-forecast models for systems like Lake Erie rely on external nutrient loading or constant diffusive-flux terms and do not capture this episodic, resuspension-driven internal P source, which they suggest is a gap relevant to explaining interannual bloom variability.

The paper operationally defines 'bioavailable phosphorus' (BioP) as the labile fraction of total sediment P obtained via sequential extraction: the loosely-bound, redox-sensitive, and Al/Fe-oxide-bound fractions (excluding Ca-bound and residual-organic fractions).

numbers: BioP release during the May 2023 resuspension event: 2.3–11 × 10⁻² g m⁻² (event⁻¹)
Baseline comparator — previously reported aerobic diffusive P flux: 0.43–0.91 × 10⁻³ g m⁻² day⁻¹; event release was 22–256× greater than this baseline
Basin-wide TP mobilized during the May 18–26, 2023 event: 40,314 kg
Basin-wide BioP released as dissolved P to the water column during the event: 12,665 kg
Resuspended sediment share of total: 89.2% of total resuspended sediment across the lake
Event contribution to 2017–2021 mean Maumee River spring loads: ~1.6% of TP and ~3.3% of SRP

Event contribution to current Maumee River spring target loads (TP target = 86 × 10⁴ kg; SRP target = 18.6 × 10⁴ kg): ~4.7% TP and ~7.0% SRP per Results text; Abstract rounds TP figure to ~4.8% (internal inconsistency of
Historical Maumee River loading (2017–2021 mean): sediment load 1.2 × 10⁹ kg/year; spring TP 1.1–2.0 × 10⁶ kg; spring SRP 2.2–4.0 × 10⁵ kg (~20% of TP)
Maumee River watershed: ~17,000 km², 72% agricultural land use, >70% of cropland under tile drainage
Erosion depth from satellite ΔSPM (May 2023 event): WLE13 = 1.39 cm (highest observed); WLE14 and WLE16 ≈ 0.3 cm each; basin-wide range 0.2–0.8 cm
TP:SPM ratios during event: 1.25 mg g⁻¹ (WLE13), 0.86 mg g⁻¹ (WLE14), 0.29 mg g⁻¹ (WLE16)
SRP:SPM ratios during event: 0.43 mg g⁻¹ (WLE13), 0.32 mg g⁻¹ (WLE14), <0.002 mg g⁻¹ (WLE16, below detection)
tensions: This paper's central quantitative claim — that a single, moderate resuspension event mobilizes internal legacy P equivalent to roughly 4.7-4.8% of TP and ~7.0% of SRP of the current Maumee River spring target loads, at a rate 22-256x the previously assumed aerobic diffusive background flux — sits in tension with nutrient-management frameworks built around controlling external (agricultural) P loading, including the very "spring target loads" the paper itself uses as its comparison baseline. The authors state directly that "most ecological and b

title: The ecology of the invasive cyanobacterium Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii (Nostocales, Cyanophyta) in two hypereutrophic lakes dominated by Planktothrix agardhii (Oscillatoriales, Cyanophyta)
what: A peer-reviewed field-ecology study (Kokociński et al., 2010, European Journal of Phycology 45(4):365-374) comparing the invasive cyanobacterium Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii with the established bloom-forming cyanobacterium Planktothrix agardhii across two hypereutrophic, shallow, polymictic lakes (Bnińskie and Bytyń

claims.c: The study's stated purpose was to examine the ecology of the invasive C. raciborskii during blooms of the established bloom-former P. agardhii in two shallow lakes in western Poland, and to determine whether the two species have different environmental preferences (i.e., different ecological niches).

C. raciborskii is framed in the paper as a planktonic cyanobacterium originally reported as exclusively tropical, whose range has expanded over recent decades into temperate-zone lakes, where it colonizes shallow, highly eutrophic lakes previously dominated by other cyanobacteria.

The two study lakes -- Lake Bnińskie (BN) and Lake Bytyńskie (BY), in the Wielkopolska region of western Poland near Poznań -- are shallow, polymictic, hypereutrophic water bodies in which P. agardhii forms blooms and C. raciborskii also occurs.

In Lake Bnińskie specifically, P. agardhii biomass was statistically significantly and negatively related to Secchi depth (water transparency) -- i.e., higher P. agardhii biomass co-occurred with lower transparency / more turbid water in that lake.

In Lake Bytyńskie specifically, P. agardhii biomass was statistically significantly and positively related to chlorophyll a and total phosphorus concentrations, and negatively related to Secchi depth and total nitrogen -- a different combination of significant relationships than in Lake Bnińskie.

A third-party automated summary of this paper (not the paper's own wording) characterizes its overall conclusion as: light availability is an important driver of phytoplankton community structure, with a shift from communities dominated by P. agardhii in very turbid water toward more diverse communities -- potentially including the invasive C. raciborskii -- in clearer water.

numbers: No specific numeric results (nutrient/chlorophyll concentrations, biomass values, correlation coefficients, sample sizes, or sampling-date ranges) could be confidently attributed to THIS paper. Candidate numbers surfaced

title: The role of surface water waves on cyanobacterial blooms in lakes
what: A literature-synthesis (review) article, not a primary data study, that examines how wind-driven surface waves physically and biologically interact with cyanobacterial bloom formation, transport, aggregation/disaggregation, aerosolization, and termination in lakes, and proposes a set of testable hypotheses to guide fut

claims.c: The paper's motivating premise is that the biophysical mechanisms by which wind-driven surface waves affect cyanobacterial bloom formation, transport, aerosolization, and termination are a major unresolved gap in bloom-dynamics science.

The paper's method is a synthesis of existing literature (not new field or laboratory data) covering four specific wave–bloom interaction pathways: cyanobacterial recruitment/inoculation, sediment nutrient resuspension, transport/aggregation/disaggregation of bloom biomass, and aerosolization of bloom biomass and cyanotoxins.
The transport/aggregation/disaggregation of bloom biomass is specifically linked to named wave-driven physical mechanisms: Stokes drift and Langmuir circulation.

The paper's deliverable is a set of testable hypotheses and concepts meant to direct future research, i.e., the wave-bloom mechanisms it discusses are proposed, not yet empirically validated in a unified framework.

The authors argue that because wave- and bloom-relevant processes act at different spatial and temporal scales, mechanistic wave-bloom research must be scaled up to whole-ecosystem relevance in order to usefully inform bloom management strategies.

numbers: No quantitative results, thresholds, effect sizes, or statistics appear in the abstract text itself — this is a qualitative literature synthesis / hypothesis paper, and the body text (where such numbers might appear, e.g
tensions: Not stated explicitly by the source (the abstract names no other specific HAB study or method to contrast against), but one tension is reasonably inferable from the abstract's own scope: because the synthesis explicitly covers physical, non-biological "aggregation, and disaggregation of bloom biomass" driven by wind/waves (e.g., via Stokes drift and Langmuir circulation), it implies that the visible extent, density, or patchiness of a surface cyanobacterial bloom — as might be captured in a single satellite overpass, grab sample, or shoreline o

title: The Use of Sentinel-3 Imagery to Monitor Cyanobacterial Blooms
what: A short, single-author open-access journal article (Ogashawara, 2019, Environments 6(6):60, MDPI) that case-studies whether the Ocean and Land Colour Instrument (OLCI) aboard the Sentinel-3 satellite can identify the two pigments used to remotely detect cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (CHABs) — phycocyanin (PC) and

claims.c: The study's explicit goal was to evaluate whether Sentinel-3/OLCI can identify phycocyanin and chlorophyll-a.
Remote monitoring of CHABs is explicitly grounded in the optical properties of two specific pigments, phycocyanin and chlorophyll-a.
Data consisted of OLCI images over the western part of Lake Erie, USA, collected during three summer bloom seasons: 2016, 2017 and 2018.
None of the traditional remote-sensing algorithms tested could accurately estimate both PC and chl-a together.
Simpler empirical approaches (single bands and band ratios) gave stronger correlations for estimating the pigments than the traditional algorithms did.
The authors' recommendation is that OLCI spectral band selection be re-examined to build new, sensor-specific retrieval algorithms.

Overall conclusion is a hedged, qualified endorsement: Sentinel-3/OLCI has potential for PC/chl-a identification but is not yet operationally validated because dedicated algorithm development is still required.
The paper frames CHABs as a concern specifically for water bodies used for drinking-water supply and recreation, motivating why CHAB monitoring matters for water-governance policy.

numbers: Imagery/study period: summers of 2016, 2017, and 2018 (three bloom seasons) over the western basin of Lake Erie, U.S.A. — no baseline or uncertainty stated in the abstract.

Bibliographic: Environments 2019, Volume 6, Issue 6, article/page no. 60 (MDPI); published 2019-06-03; DOI 10.3390/environments6060060; Crossref reference-count (length of the paper's own bibliography) = 40.

This is worth flagging against HAB literature (e.g., material built on EPA CyAN's operational Sentinel-3-based cyanobacteria index) that may present s

title: Toxic Effects Produced by Anatoxin-a under Laboratory Conditions: A Review
what: A 2022 peer-reviewed narrative literature review, published in the open-access journal Toxins (MDPI) and indexed on PubMed Central, that compiles laboratory in vitro and in vivo toxicological studies of the cyanobacterial neurotoxin anatoxin-a (ATX-a) in order to characterize its mechanisms of toxicity and dose–respons

claims.c: The review's explicit aim is to compile all in vitro and in vivo laboratory toxicological studies of anatoxin-a (ATX-a) performed to date and to identify data gaps, motivated by WHO's assessment that the existing database is inadequate for a formal guideline value.

WHO has issued only provisional (not formal) numeric reference values for ATX-a — 30 µg/L for acute/short-term drinking-water exposure and 60 µg/L for recreational water exposure — while explicitly stating the underlying toxicological database is inadequate to support a formal guideline.

ATX-a's mechanism of action is as a nicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist that causes membrane depolarization via the receptor channel, blocking neuromuscular transmission (with the introduction also describing acetylcholinesterase inhibition as part of the toxic mechanism).
ATX-a has been detected in a meaningful minority of algae-based dietary supplement samples, indicating a human dietary/supplement exposure route distinct from drinking water.
Among the water-body types surveyed in the literature the review draws on, reservoirs show the highest reported frequency of ATX-a occurrence, which the authors flag as a specific concern.

The authors conclude that ATX-a toxicological research remains far more limited than for other major cyanotoxins (e.g., microcystins, cylindrospermopsin), and specifically call for more standardized in vitro neuronal work.
The authors identify mutagenicity, genotoxicity, immunotoxicity, and hormonal-balance alteration as specific ATX-a toxicological endpoints that remain understudied and need deeper investigation.
Toxicokinetic data (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion) for ATX-a are described by the authors as scarce, limiting confident extrapolation from lab dosing to real-world exposure.
The authors state that very few of the existing in vivo studies reflect real-world/natural exposure conditions, and call specifically for longer-duration oral exposure studies.

Reported acute lethal-dose values for ATX-a vary by roughly an order of magnitude or more across species and administration routes (e.g., oral LD90 ~120 mg/kg in goldfish vs. ~1800 mg/kg in mice; intraperitoneal LD50 ~260–315 µg/kg in mice vs. ~60 µg/kg in rats), illustrating substantial interspecies and route-dependent variability in measured potency.

In vitro pharmacology data reviewed indicate ATX-a is consistently more potent than nicotine at nicotinic receptors across multiple neuronal/tissue preparations, with wide-ranging potency ratios reported between studies.

numbers: Molecular weight of anatoxin-a (ATX-a): 165.237 g/mol
Chemical formula: C10H15NO
pKa of (+)ATX-a: 9.36

Thermal/pH stability: reported unstable at 100 °C and at basic pH 9.5
(+) enantiomer reported ~160-fold more potent than the (−) enantiomer (as extracted by fetch tool; not independently re-verified by verbatim quote)
Fish bioaccumulation: 96-hour exposure to 132–524 µg/L ATX-a produced bioconcentration factors (BCF) of 30–47
Fish muscle accumulation reported up to 30 ng/g fresh weight
Carp tissue levels after exposure: 0.031–0.768 µg/g dry weight
In vitro potency vs. nicotine: (+)ATX-a 3–50x more potent in fetal rat hippocampal neurons; 7–136-fold more potent than nicotine across various tissue preparations
EC50 for ion-channel blocking effects: 0.14–0.28 µM
Rat thymocyte apoptosis observed at 1–10 µg/mL after 3–24 hours
tensions: Three tensions are worth flagging for a HAB literature synthesis, stated cautiously since this source does not itself make comparative claims about other data streams: (1) Most operational HAB monitoring and remote-sensing products (e.g., EPA CyAN's satellite cyanobacteria index, chlorophyll-a/phycocyanin proxies) detect bloom biomass or pigment signal, not toxin identity or concentration; this review's own emphasis that ATX-a toxicokinetic and bioaccumulation data are scarce, and that guideline values remain provisional, underscores that a sat

title: Transcriptionally active nitrogen fixation and biosynthesis of diverse secondary metabolites by Dolichospermum and Aphanizomenon-like Cyanobacteria in western Lake Erie Microcystis blooms
what: A 2023 peer-reviewed genome-resolved metagenomics/metatranscriptomics study (Yancey, Mathiesen & Dick; Harmful Algae, Vol. 124, Art. 102408; preprinted on bioRxiv in Oct 2022) that used field metagenomic and metatranscriptomic data from the 2014 western Lake Erie cyanobacterial harmful algal bloom to reconstruct genome

claims.c: Western Lake Erie cyanoHABs are dominated by microcystin-producing Microcystis spp., but other coexisting cyanobacterial taxa may play important roles in toxin production and in shaping bloom dynamics and community function.

The study applied metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing to field samples from the 2014 western Lake Erie cyanoHAB specifically to probe the genetic diversity and biosynthetic potential of the Anabaena/Dolichospermum/Aphanizomenon ("ADA") clade.

Two near-complete metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) were reconstructed, one each from two distinct ADA-clade species, and each genome carried biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) encoding novel and known secondary metabolites (some with toxic and/or taste-and-odor properties) that were shown to be transcriptionally active (expressed), not merely present.

Neither of the two reconstructed ADA genomes contained genes for guanitoxin, anatoxin, or saxitoxin biosynthesis, even though ADA-clade organisms are known (elsewhere in the literature) to produce these toxins — i.e., a genome-specific negative finding for these particular toxin classes.

ADA-clade cyanobacteria accounted for most of the metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing reads mapping to nitrogen-fixation genes, implying ADA (rather than the non-diazotrophic Microcystis) were the dominant nitrogen-fixers at the sampled times and stations.

Despite being relatively low in abundance within the bloom community, ADA taxa could plausibly influence water quality and the ecology of Microcystis blooms, but the authors explicitly state this potential influence has not been quantified.

In the earlier bioRxiv preprint version of this work, the authors additionally framed ADA's nitrogen-fixation ability and varying nutrient-acquisition strategies as potentially supporting synthesis of N-rich metabolites and bloom persistence, and described ADA as having "the potential to produce unmonitored toxins" — framing that was not carried into the final published abstract (which instead reports the specific negative finding that guanitoxin/anatoxin/saxitoxin genes were absent from these two MAGs).

numbers: 2 near-complete metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) reconstructed, representing 2 distinct ADA-clade (Anabaena/Dolichospermum/Aphanizomenon) species (abstract; no genome size in Mbp, %completeness, or %contamination figu
Field metagenomic + metatranscriptomic data drawn from the 2014 western Lake Erie cyanoHAB (single bloom-year dataset per the abstract, not a multi-year time series)

Bibliographic record (Crossref/Europe PMC/Semantic Scholar): journal Harmful Algae, Volume 124, Article no. 102408, published May 2023; DOI 10.1016/j.hal.2023.102408; PMID 37164563; preprint DOI 10.1101/2022.09.30.510322

No percentages, concentrations, gene/read counts, p-values, or confidence intervals appear anywhere in the abstract text itself (see fetch_notes/stated_limitations) — this dossier cannot report quantitative Results-secti
tensions: (1) Correlation vs. causation: the paper's own headline "influence" claim is explicitly framed as a possibility inferred from co-occurrence and relative sequencing-read abundance ("suggesting," "highlight the possibility," "remains to be quantified") rather than a measured causal or rate-based effect (e.g., no nitrogenase activity assay or manipulative experiment is mentioned in the abstract) — any use of this source as a "driver" in a HAB risk/treatment narrative must preserve that hedge and not upgrade it to a causal mechanism. title: Using convolutional neural network for predicting cyanobacteria concentrations in river water
what: A 2020 Water Research journal article (Pyo et al.) that trains and stress-tests a convolutional neural network (CNN) to nowcast and short-term-forecast Microcystis (cyanobacteria) biomass from gridded, spatio-temporal water-quality data generated SYNTHETICALLY by a 3D hydrodynamic-water-quality model of a river reach (

claims.c: The study is a proof-of-concept that trains/tests a CNN to predict cyanobacteria (Microcystis) biomass using SYNTHETIC spatio-temporal water-quality data generated by a 3D hydrodynamic-water-quality model of a river reach, rather than directly observed field or satellite cyanobacteria data.
In nowcasting (same-time prediction), the CNN achieved a Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (NSE) of 0.87 for Microcystis biomass.
Prediction skill degrades substantially as forecast lead time increases: NSE falls from 0.87 (nowcast) to 0.58 at the longer lead time(s) tested.

Model skill depends strongly on how densely the input grid is populated with observations: as spatial observation density rose from 20% to 100% of the input image grid cells, CNN prediction NSE rose from 0.70 to 0.84.
The CNN retains 'acceptable' performance under a moderate amount of injected input noise: at 10% noise amplitude, NSE = 0.76, though noise generally degrades accuracy.

The authors conclude the CNN, run on high-temporal-frequency gridded imagery, successfully demonstrates capability for cyanobacterial bloom prediction, with performance that varies spatially across the studied river reach.
The rationale for using model-simulated rather than field-observed training data was that CNNs need a data volume that current field/monitoring sampling cannot supply.

Lower-confidence/secondary detail: the case-study site was roughly a 29-km reach of the Nakdong River in South Korea, and the CNN was evaluated at nowcast plus 3-day and 7-day forecast horizons, with Microcystis concentrations of interest described in relation to a ~1000 cells/mL level.

NSE falls from 0.87 to 0.58 as forecast lead time increases (abstract; exact lead-time values not given in abstract)

That is an important distinction for any literature review that also includes models validated against real monitoring data (e.g., EPA CyAN satellite-derived cyanobacteria products, or Water Quality Portal in-situ chlorophyll-a/nutrient records): the NSE values here measure agreement with a hydrodynamic-water-quality model's own

title: A national harmonized dataset of discrete chlorophyll from lakes and streams (2005-2022)
year: 2023 (data release); companion peer-reviewed paper 2024 in Scientific Data
what: A U.S. Geological Survey public data release (DOI 10.5066/P9J0ZIOF) providing a harmonized, national compilation of discrete (field-collected, lab-analyzed) chlorophyll and pheophytin measurements from inland U.S. waters spanning 2005-2022, compiled by merging the Water Quality Portal (WQP) with USGS National Water Qua

claims.c: The dataset spans 2005-2022 (an 18-year span inclusive, described by the authors as a "17-year record") and covers nearly 84,000 unique sites and over 1,374,000 pigment measurements across the U.S. and territories.
The exact unique site count after removing suspected in-situ sensor records is 83,829.

Measurement counts differ sharply by pigment type and by phytoplankton vs. periphyton, with corrected chlorophyll a (phytoplankton) the largest single category and essentially no uncorrected chlorophyll a periphyton records.

The three chlorophyll/pheophytin parameters are not interchangeable: subtracting pheophytin from uncorrected chlorophyll a does not equal corrected chlorophyll a, and the WQP label "corrected chlorophyll a by EPA 445" is ambiguous between two different lab procedures (acidification vs. modified narrow-band-pass fluorometry) that the authors could not distinguish from WQP metadata alone.

A depth cutoff of 10 meters was applied and records deeper than that were dropped because deep chlorophyll maxima could mislead remote-sensing and surface-oriented HAB modeling; records with missing depth were kept under the assumption most are near-surface.

Roughly 0.48% of phytoplankton records (about 6,000 records) exceed 2,000 microgram/L chlorophyll, an extreme-value tail the authors could only partially verify against data providers and which they recommend users independently confirm before use.

Approximately 40% of the chlorophyll records sourced from USGS NWIS were still "preliminary" (not reviewed by a data steward) at time of release, and these were retained in the dataset rather than excluded.

Co-located monitoring sites (e.g., multiple site numbers on the same lake or river reach) were deliberately not aggregated into a single physical location, which the authors note would otherwise increase apparent sample density at a given place.

Chlorophyll a concentration is explicitly characterized by the authors as only a rough proxy for algal biomass, since cellular chlorophyll content varies with species, nutrients, light, temperature, and cell condition, and high benthic (periphyton) chlorophyll does not necessarily indicate high growth rate.

The dataset is intended to support HAB-relevant modeling in rivers in particular, an environment the authors describe as historically understudied for HABs relative to lakes, and the largest number of chlorophyll records in the dataset actually comes from rivers/streams rather than lakes.

title: A national harmonized dataset of discrete chlorophyll from lakes and streams (2005-2022) [USGS data release]; companion peer-reviewed paper: "Chlorophyll a in lakes and streams of the United States (2005-2022)" (Scientific Data, 2024). Note: the task's title field also names a second, distinct USGS product, "AquaMatch Chlorophyll a Data from Water Quality Portal: ~1970-2024" — see tensions.
year: 2023 (USGS ScienceBase data release, published/updated May 2023); 2024 (companion Scientific Data paper, published 12 June 2024)
what: A USGS data release (ScienceBase, DOI 10.5066/P9J0ZIOF) and its companion open-access Scientific Data paper that harmonize discrete, field-collected/lab-analyzed chlorophyll a and pheophytin measurements from roughly 84,000 U.S. monitoring sites (lakes, streams, rivers, reservoirs, canals, estuaries, 2005-2022), pooled

claims.c: The dataset compiles nearly 84,000 monitoring sites and over 1,374,000 discrete chlorophyll/pheophytin measurements from U.S. inland and coastal waters (2005-2022); the authors describe it as the largest such harmonized compilation for the US.

The dataset's explicit purpose is to support development of process-based, machine-learning, and remote-sensing models for predicting harmful algal blooms, and the USGS data-release page frames the same purpose independently.

Data were harmonized from two source pipelines: the multi-agency Water Quality Portal (WQP) and previously unpublished USGS National Water Quality Laboratory (NWQL) records, retrieved programmatically with USGS's dataRetrieval R package.

Three chlorophyll-related pigment metrics are reported (uncorrected chlorophyll a, corrected chlorophyll a, and pheophytin), harmonized to two units depending on sample type: micrograms per liter (µg/L) for phytoplankton (suspended algae) and milligrams per square meter (mg/m2) for periphyton (benthic algae).

The dataset retains extreme values rather than removing them: about 6,000 phytoplankton records (0.48% of all records) exceed 2,000 µg/L, and users are told to confirm apparent outliers with the original data provider rather than assume they are errors, since some were verified as real surface algal scums.

A large share (about 40%) of the chlorophyll records sourced from USGS NWIS were still flagged 'preliminary' (not yet reviewed by a data steward) at compilation time, and these were retained in the released dataset rather than excluded.

Only 437 records across the entire ~1.37-million-record dataset carry a hydro_event_cd value of 'algal bloom'; explicit bloom-event tagging is present for a tiny fraction of records, so this is a general chlorophyll compilation, not a labeled bloom/no-bloom occurrence dataset.

A key methodological caution raised by the authors: subtracting pheophytin from uncorrected chlorophyll a is NOT equivalent to corrected chlorophyll a, and WQP metadata cannot reliably distinguish two different lab procedures both reported under 'corrected chlorophyll a' (acidification-based EPA 445 vs. a fluorometric 'modified' method) — the authors, citing prior literature, recommend using uncorrected chlorophyll a or fluorometry-based values instead of corrected chlorophyll a going forward.

Co-located monitoring sites were not aggregated in the released dataset; site numbers are assigned independently by each contributing organization and are not necessarily stable over time or across sampling campaigns for the same physical location, so raw site counts likely overstate true independent spatial coverage.

As part of technical validation, the authors performed source-specific manual corrections beyond generic automated rules: removing records from one contributing organization due to a known unit-conversion error, removing a site located in Canada that fell outside the intended US/territories scope, and deleting apparent in-situ sensor readings that had been mislabeled under a discrete-sample method code.

The data release and its production code are both openly published with no stated use restrictions: the harmonized data are archived on USGS ScienceBase (DOI 10.5066/P9J0ZIOF, CC0 1.0 Universal / public domain) and the R-based processing pipeline is archived on Zenodo (DOI 10.5281/zenodo.7879199), which together allow the harmonization to be reproduced end-to-end.

Geographic coverage spans the continental US plus Hawaii, Alaska, American Samoa, Guam, the Northern Mariana Islands, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands, but sampling frequency, spatial distribution, and analytical methods vary greatly by state/jurisdiction — coverage is opportunistic/administrative (aggregated from many independent monitoring programs), not a designed statistical survey.

A separate, differently-scoped USGS product with a similar name — 'AquaMatch Chlorophyll a Data from Water Quality Portal ~1970-2024' (DOI 10.6073/pasta/4c795593504d927ac546a36e5dc9fdb0, contact Michael F. Meyer) — also harmonizes WQP chlorophyll data but spans a different date range, uses a three-tier (restrictive/narrowed/inclusive) data-quality scheme, and is described as a component of a forthcoming AquaSat V2 pipeline; it is a distinct dataset with a distinct DOI and lead contact from the Platt/Spaulding 2005-2022 release this entry's primary URL points to, and the two should not be conflated even though FED-002's title field names both.

title: About the Nutrients Annex (Annex 4)
year: Undated EPA program webpage; most recent milestone referenced is DAP renewal "up for renewal in 2023"; targets discussed were adopted February 2016
what: An EPA program webpage explaining Annex 4 (Nutrients) of the binational U.S.-Canada Great Lakes Water Quality Agreement (GLWQA), under which both countries committed to Domestic Action Plans and formally adopted (Feb 2016) numeric phosphorus load-reduction targets and concentration objectives for Lake Erie to address e

claims.c: Lake Erie's nearshore waters are described as experiencing the most severe eutrophication among Great Lakes nearshore waters, with 67% rated in fair or poor condition in 2015.
Under Annex 4 of the Great Lakes Water Quality Agreement (GLWQA), the U.S. and Canada each committed to develop a Domestic Action Plan (DAP) for phosphorus reduction in their respective country.

In February 2016 the U.S. and Canada formally adopted a target of a 40% reduction in total phosphorus loads entering the western and central basins of Lake Erie, intended to bring the central basin to an annual load of 6,000 metric tons.

A parallel 40% reduction target was set for spring total and soluble reactive phosphorus (SRP) loads specifically from the Maumee River (US), plus matching 40% targets for other priority tributaries: Thames River and Leamington-area tributaries in Canada, and River Raisin, Portage River, Toussaint Creek, Sandusky River, and the Huron River (Ohio) in the US.
No numeric phosphorus load-reduction target was set for Lake Erie's eastern basin, because the nuisance-algae (Cladophora) problem there is attributed to multiple factors beyond phosphorus loading.

Annex 4 set basin-specific total phosphorus concentration objectives for Lake Erie: western basin interim objective 15 µg/L (expected to reach 12 µg/L once load reductions are implemented); central basin interim objective 10 µg/L (expected 6 µg/L); eastern basin interim objective 10 µg/L (expected 6 µg/L).

The phosphorus load and concentration targets were derived using nine different computer simulation models relating phosphorus loading levels to algal growth outcomes, referenced against 2008 as the baseline year, chosen for its data quality and because it was considered a representative 'average' year for Lake Erie conditions.

Implementation has proceeded on a defined timeline: the GLWQA (which contains Annex 4) was renewed in 2012; a target review/evaluation process began around 2013; the phosphorus targets were formally adopted in February 2016; the first five-year Domestic Action Plans were issued in 2018 and were up for renewal in 2023; and the binational Lake Erie Phosphorus Reduction Strategy was published in 2019.

The Annex 4 targets are meant to address three distinct eutrophication-related problems that differ by Lake Erie basin: western basin cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) blooms and associated toxins, central basin seasonal hypoxia (low dissolved oxygen), and eastern basin nuisance growth of the alga Cladophora.

The 2019 Lake Erie Binational Phosphorus Reduction Strategy is structured around four components: an updated environmental-condition assessment for lake-wide nutrient management, a summary of the target-development process and load allocation between the two countries, binational priorities for phosphorus management (including watershed prioritization and research/monitoring priorities), and a progress-tracking methodology using an adaptive-management approach.

The source states an explicit ecological trade-off limitation on the phosphorus-reduction strategy: reducing phosphorus too far is itself framed as a risk, since insufficient nutrients could threaten fisheries.

6,000 metric tons: target annual total phosphorus load to the central basin after reductions
40% reduction target: spring total and soluble reactive phosphorus (SRP) loads, Maumee River (US)

40% reduction target: spring TP/SRP loads, River Raisin, Portage River, Toussaint Creek, Sandusky River, and Huron River-Ohio (US)

Central basin total phosphorus: interim objective 10 µg/L; expected outcome after load reduction 6 µg/L
Eastern basin total phosphorus: interim objective 10 µg/L; expected outcome after load reduction 6 µg/L
No numeric load-reduction target set for the eastern basin (0 formal % target)
Baseline year used for phosphorus/algal-growth modeling: 2008
Nine (9) computer simulation models used to relate phosphorus loading to algal growth
tensions: This source frames Lake Erie eutrophication/HAB risk primarily through a single-lever, phosphorus-load-reduction management model, using simulation models to correlate phosphorus loads with algal growth outcomes -- a correlation the page does not claim to validate as strictly causal, and the page's own eastern-basin carve-out (Cladophora driven by light, substrate, and invasive mussels rather than phosphorus alone) is itself evidence that non-nutrient covariates can dominate bloom/algae dynamics in parts of the same lake.
