title: About the One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System (OHHABS)
year: Page last reviewed Feb. 23, 2024; OHHABS system itself launched in 2016 (per page text)
what: OHHABS is a CDC-run, voluntary national public health surveillance system, launched in 2016, through which U.S. state and territorial health departments report harmful algal bloom (HAB) occurrences and the associated human and animal illnesses (including foodborne HAB-toxin exposures). CDC frames it explicitly as a 'On

claims.c: OHHABS is CDC's system for collecting data on harmful algal blooms and the human and animal illnesses they cause.
CDC launched OHHABS in 2016.
OHHABS is described by CDC as the only national public health system that collects information on both HAB occurrence and the human/animal illnesses HABs cause.
OHHABS operates under a 'One Health' framework, i.e., it is designed on the premise that human, animal, and environmental health are interconnected.
CDC states OHHABS data are meant to help define patterns of HAB occurrence, protect water and food supplies, and inform public communication to prevent future illness.

OHHABS collects reports in four data categories: harmful algal bloom occurrence/description (fresh, salt, or brackish water), food-source exposure (for HAB-toxin foodborne illness without an observed bloom, e.g. seafood harvest location), human illness cases, and animal illness cases (pets, livestock, wildlife).
A bloom can be logged in OHHABS on its own, without any associated illness report, if health departments have not identified any illnesses linked to it.
Reporting to OHHABS is done voluntarily by state and territorial public health departments, not directly by individual clinicians, veterinarians, or the public.
Health departments may designate environmental-health or animal-health partners to help with OHHABS reporting duties.
Healthcare providers and veterinarians are instructed to report HAB-associated illness cases to their local or state health department, not directly to CDC/OHHABS.

Public health, environmental-health, or animal-health professionals seeking to report to or access OHHABS should contact their state's OHHABS Reporting Site Administrator (RSA), reachable via a CDC email contact.

This page does not name any specific causative organisms (e.g., particular cyanobacteria genera or algal species) or specific toxins (e.g., microcystin, domoic acid); it refers only generically to 'harmful algal blooms' and 'HAB toxins.'
This page provides no quantitative statistics whatsoever — no bloom counts, no illness case counts, no number of participating states/territories, and no time-series or trend data.
The page's metadata shows it was last reviewed/updated on Feb. 23, 2024.

numbers: 2016 — year CDC states it launched OHHABS (no baseline or uncertainty given; a program-history fact, not a measured quantity)
Feb. 23, 2024 — page 'last reviewed'/updated date shown in page metadata (citation/currency date, not a study statistic)
tensions: As a voluntary, jurisdiction-mediated reporting system with no stated completeness or validation metrics, OHHABS records (if pulled from elsewhere, e.g., a dashboard) would need to be treated as under-ascertained/non-random surveillance data rather than a representative ground truth of true bloom or illness prevalence — a caveat this 'about' page itself does not spell out but that follows from the reporting design it describes, and that matters if OHHABS-derived illness/bloom locations were ever used to validate a satellite- or in-situ-driven H

claims.c: The dataset is presented as a component of a forthcoming update to the original AquaSat resource, called 'AquaSat version 2 (v2),' whose overarching purpose is to expose the individual pipeline components used to create satellite-to-in-situ water-quality matchups.
The release classifies records into a 'restrictive' quality tier defined as verifiably self-similar across organizations and time periods and thus highly reliable.
The release classifies a second, 'narrowed' quality tier as data believed self-similar but whose compatibility across data providers cannot be fully verified.
The release classifies a third, 'inclusive' quality tier as data assumed reliable and harmonized to the best ability given the information each contributing organization provided.
The dataset adds 'flag columns' intended to help users understand complexities in the available sampling depth and field-sampling metadata.
The release states it incorporates additional recent in-situ data that was not available at the time the original AquaSat resource was published.
The underlying records were downloaded from the Water Quality Portal (WQP) and span January 6, 1970 to June 20, 2024, covering the United States and US Territories.

This particular release explicitly does NOT contain satellite/remote-sensing matchups; a user who wants to pair it with satellite reflectance must separately apply the Ross et al. (2019) pipeline to add Landsat surface reflectances.
The data release is placed in the public domain under a CC0 1.0 Universal dedication.

The dataset's creators, per the USGS Science Data Catalog mirror, are Matthew R. Brousil, Michael F. Meyer, Kathryn ('Katie') Willi, B. G. Steele, Juan De La Torre, and Matthew R.V. Ross, with Michael F. Meyer as both data and metadata point of contact.
The release's foundational/larger-work citation is Ross et al. (2019), the original AquaSat paper published in Water Resources Research.

The source's own metadata contains an internal inconsistency about whether the item has an assigned DOI: one part of the Science Data Catalog mirror points users to a doi.org link for 'Get Data and Metadata,' while another part of the same mirror states no DOI is currently registered for the item.

The fetched landing-page text does not report any quantitative dataset-size metrics (e.g., number of records, monitoring sites, states, organizations, or characteristics/parameters), nor any file/entity list or spatial bounding box.

title: CAPTAIN LIQUID Pesticide Product Label
what: An EPA-accepted FIFRA registration-review decision letter and stamped product label (dated March 23, 2022) for CAPTAIN® LIQUID COPPER ALGAECIDE (EPA Reg. No. 67690-9, Decision No. 559224), a copper-ethanolamine-complex algaecide/herbicide made by SePRO Corporation, labeled for controlling algae (including cyanobacteria

claims.c: Captain is a chelated copper (copper ethanolamine complex) formulation labeled as effective against a broad range of green algae and cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) — filamentous, planktonic, and macrophytic forms — and as an herbicide on copper-susceptible submersed weeds.
The active ingredient is a copper ethanolamine complex at 28.2% concentration, equivalent to 9.1% metallic copper.
The label imposes a blanket ceiling of 1.0 ppm metallic copper for any single application to any water, with an effective-control range of 0.2-1.0 ppm.

Copper does not break down in the environment (unlike most organic pesticides) and therefore accumulates in treated waters with repeated applications, which the label ties to its toxicity to fish and aquatic organisms.

Killing algae/plant biomass with this product can itself deplete dissolved oxygen as the dead biomass decomposes, risking fish and invertebrate suffocation; the label's mitigation is to cap any single pass at half the water body and require at least 14 days between treatments.

Applying algaecide to high-density cyanobacterial blooms can rupture cells and release intracellular toxins (hepatotoxins and neurotoxins) into the water, so the label directs applicators to avoid treating high-density toxic-cyanobacteria blooms rather than to use treatment as a mitigation for them.

The label sets explicit water-chemistry thresholds below which non-target acute toxicity risk (e.g., to koi and trout) increases: pH ≤ 6.5, dissolved organic carbon (DOC) ≤ 3.0 mg/L, and alkalinity < 50 mg/L ("soft" water); the approved rates assume water above all three thresholds.

For whole-waterbody treatment, the label caps cumulative annual dosing at 21.9 lbs metallic copper per acre-foot per year, built from up to 8 applications/year at 1 ppm each, staggering treatment of each half of the water body every 14 days across an 8-month (244-day) season.

For partial-waterbody treatment (discrete sections summing to no more than half the waterbody within the 14-day retreatment window), the annual cap roughly doubles to 46.6 lbs metallic copper per acre-foot per year, from up to 17 applications/year.

For aquaculture ponds with fish present, the label imposes a stricter single-application ceiling of 0.4 ppm metallic copper when targeting nuisance algae, below the general 1.0 ppm cap used elsewhere.
Treatment with Captain does not by itself make water potable; drinking-water sources require additional, separate potable-water treatment after algaecide application.

numbers: Active ingredient: Copper Ethanolamine Complex 28.2% (w/w); metallic copper equivalent 9.1% (front-label declaration, not an experimental measurement)
General single-application ceiling: 1.0 ppm as metallic copper, in any water, any single application
Labeled effective-control range: 0.2-1.0 ppm copper (Table 1), corresponding to 0.6-3.0 gallons of product per acre-foot for planktonic/filamentous algae
Aquaculture with fish present: single-application ceiling lowered to 0.4 ppm metallic copper when targeting nuisance algae
Whole-waterbody maximum annual rate: 21.9 lbs metallic copper/acre-foot/year (8 applications/yr at up to 1 ppm; half-body staggered every 14 days over 244 days / ~8 months)
Partial-waterbody maximum annual rate: 46.6 lbs metallic copper/acre-foot/year (17 applications/yr at up to 1 ppm; 14-day minimum retreatment interval over 244 days)
Minimum retreatment interval: 14 days between treatments, with no single pass exceeding half the water body
Non-target-toxicity risk thresholds (labeled rates not validated below these): pH ≤ 6.5; DOC ≤ 3.0 mg/L; alkalinity < 50 mg/L

Pulse (irrigation-conveyance) application: max 13 lbs metallic copper per year per 5 miles of conveyance per cfs; up to 0.5 lbs metallic copper (0.55 gal product) per cfs per 5-30 mile treatment segment
Chemigation dosing: 0.91-9.1 gallons Captain per 1,000,000 gallons water (0.3-3.0 gal/acre-foot) yields 0.1-1.0 ppm copper; hard cap 1.0 ppm or 0.91 gal per 100,000 gallons
Flow-rate estimation formula for flowing-water dosing: cfs = average width (ft) × average depth (ft) × average velocity (ft/s) × 0.9
Maintenance retreatment cadence for flowing-water/rotational programs: every 2-6 weeks
tensions: The label's own hazard language complicates a simple 'detect bloom via remote sensing/satellite -> dispatch algaecide' decision rule that a HAB early-warning tool might otherwise imply: it explicitly warns that treating a high-density cyanobacterial bloom with this algaecide can rupture cells and acutely release intracellular hepato- and neurotoxins into the water, meaning fast action on a detected high-biomass bloom is not unambiguously protective and may transiently increase toxin exposure — a nuance any 'risk readout -> action window' framin

title: Climate Change and Freshwater Harmful Algal Blooms
year: Not stated as an original publication year on the page. Page footer gives "Last updated on February 6, 2026" (confirmed verbatim across two independent fetches); no earlier publication/first-posted date is given in the fetched text.
what: An EPA HABs-portal web page that explains, in plain language for a general audience, the mechanisms by which climate change is expected to make freshwater (and adjoining estuarine/marine) cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (HABs) more frequent and severe when combined with nutrient pollution. It is a synthesis/explain

claims.c: Climate change effects on freshwaters, estuaries, and marine environments are already being documented by scientists and are predicted to worsen; combined with nutrient pollution, this may make algal blooms more severe and more frequent across more waterbodies.

HABs (blooms with potential to harm human health, pets, livestock, or aquatic ecosystems) can also have wide-ranging economic impacts; in freshwater, cyanobacteria are described as the most common HAB producers, and some cyanobacterial HABs produce toxins that cause illness in humans and other animals.

Cyanobacteria that form HABs thrive in warm, slow-moving water and typically bloom when water temperatures are warmer; climate-driven warming is expected to increase both the magnitude and the duration of cyanoHABs.

CyanoHABs gain a competitive advantage over other algae in warming water because they can grow faster, can migrate vertically to access nutrients from cool bottom layers while photosynthesizing in the warmer, light-rich upper layer (a trait other algae lack), and absorb sunlight/release heat in ways that further warm the surface water.

In some regions, climate change is predicted to reduce freshwater runoff via drought and increased evaporation, raising salinity in inland waters; this has allowed salt-tolerant "golden algae" HABs to recur regularly, expand into lagoons, and kill fish in freshwater lakes of the southwestern and south-central U.S. since 2000.

In other regions, more intense rainfall may increase freshwater flows to coastal areas; because freshwater is lighter and layers atop saltwater, this can prevent mixing between oxygen-rich surface water and oxygen-depleted deeper water (oxygen depleted by decomposition of dead HABs), producing hypoxic zones that harm or kill oxygen-dependent animals, including commercially important species.

Because algae require CO2 to survive, higher atmospheric and aquatic CO2 levels can drive rapid growth of algae, especially cyanoHABs that float to the surface; higher CO2 also raises water acidity, which affects competition among algal species and affects the organisms that graze on algae.

Climate change is increasing both rainfall intensity and drought duration; increased rainfall causes higher nutrient runoff into waterbodies, which fueled HABs observed in Lake Erie in 2011 and 2015, and subsequent drought lets waterbodies retain those nutrients longer, favoring species that compete well under high-nutrient conditions.

Increases in extreme rainfall and the resulting increases in freshwater flows may flush large loads of nutrients, freshwater HABs, and their associated toxins into estuaries and marine areas, raising the risk that those waters develop new HABs or see existing HABs worsen.
Scientists predict that sea level could rise one meter by the year 2100, which would increase the area of shallow, stable coastal waters that provide favorable conditions for HABs.

Climate change is expected to alter the timing and intensity of coastal upwelling; along the U.S. west coast, excess nutrients delivered by upwelling might lead to more algal blooms, including HABs, especially when combined with increased runoff of land-based nutrient pollution.
The page illustrates the topic with a real satellite image of a cyanobacterial HAB in the western basin of Lake Erie, captured October 8, 2022, credited to the European Space Agency.
The page cites no specific peer-reviewed study, government report, dataset, or statistical/mechanistic model as the evidentiary basis for any of its six climate-mechanism claims.

numbers: Sea level rise projection: "one meter by the year 2100" — a single-point estimate with no stated confidence interval, emissions scenario, or citation on this page.

"since 2000" — start year given for recurring "golden algae" (salt-tolerant HAB) fish-kill events in freshwater lakes of the southwestern and south-central U.S.; no end date, frequency count, or fish-kill magnitude given
"2011 and 2015" — the two specific years the page names for rainfall/nutrient-runoff-driven Lake Erie HAB events; no bloom-size, toxin-concentration, or loading figures accompany these years.
"October 8, 2022" — capture date of the one satellite image on the page (ESA-credited), showing a cyanoHAB in Lake Erie's western basin; illustrative only, not a quantified variable.
"Last updated on February 6, 2026" — page/footer metadata (site maintenance date), not a scientific data point; included here only for source-currency traceability.
tensions: This page attributes rising HAB frequency/severity jointly to climate change AND nutrient pollution, which is broadly consistent with (not contradictory to) the wider HAB literature's emphasis on nutrient loading as a primary driver. However, because the page gives no dose-response thresholds, effect sizes, or relative-contribution estimates, it cannot itself be used to apportion how much of any specific bloom (e.g., the 2011 and 2015 Lake Erie events it names) is attributable to climate-driven rainfall intensification versus agricultural/point

title: Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN)
what: CyAN (Cyanobacteria Assessment Network) is a multi-agency (EPA, NASA, NOAA, USGS, USACE) U.S. federal project that uses satellite ocean-color remote sensing (chiefly Sentinel-3 OLCI, plus historical Envisat MERIS, and since 2024 Sentinel-2 MSI and a Landsat surface-water-temperature product) to detect and quantify cyan

claims.c: CyAN is a multi-agency project to build an early-warning indicator system for detecting algal blooms in U.S. freshwater systems.
The CyAN project officially started October 1, 2015.
The project used the historical Envisat MERIS satellite archive (2002-2012) to provide continental U.S. coverage, applied in Fiscal Year 2017.
Daily and weekly satellite coverage using the Sentinel-3 OLCI sensor has been publicly available from 2016 to the present.

The original (Sentinel-3-based) CyAN project concluded in 2023 and the program transitioned to applying higher-resolution Sentinel-2 Multi-Spectral Imager data starting in 2024; Sentinel-3 data will keep being published and reprocessed annually even as that portion of the project winds down.
In July 2024, EPA researchers began beta-testing an experimental cyanoHAB forecasting model that produces weekly forecasts for over 2,000 U.S. lakes.
Program goals explicitly scope the detection approach to 'the contiguous U.S.' using ocean satellites.
Satellite data/results are disseminated to the public through four specific interfaces: a web-based application (the CyAN app), EnviroAtlas, Report on the Environment, and NASA's Ocean Color Web.
The program's standard, uniform bloom-identification approach is built on the Ocean Land Colour Instrument (OLCI) aboard the Sentinel-3 satellite.

The program's outcomes include an information-dissemination system built for rapid public-health advisory postings, linking cyanobacterial satellite detections to health, economic, and environmental conditions.

A peer-reviewed 2020 journal article tied to this program evaluated a satellite-based cyanobacteria bloom detection algorithm against field-measured microcystin data, but the overview page itself reproduces only the title, not the accuracy results.
The page gives an operational contact-transition detail: the CyAN project mailbox address is scheduled to change after September 30, 2027.
The page was last updated on June 9, 2026, meaning the timeline and forecasting details above reflect that update, not the original 2015 launch content.

Named agency leads/points of contact for the project include Blake Schaeffer (EPA), Bridget Seegers (NASA), Richard Stumpf (NOAA), Keith Loftin and Tyler King (USGS, including CyAN Sentinel-2 work), and Molly Reif (USACE, CyAN Sentinel-2 work), with USACE described as having joined the project in 2023 for Sentinel-2 applications.

title: Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN)
year: Continuously updated project page (not a dated publication); current data processing "Version 6," release notes dated August 2025
what: CyAN is a multi-agency (EPA, USGS, NOAA, hosted by NASA's OB.DAAC) project that produces satellite remote-sensing data products — built on a Cyanobacteria Index (CI) algorithm applied to ESA ocean-color sensor data (MERIS, then Sentinel-3 OLCI) — to detect and quantify cyanobacterial algal blooms in U.S. lakes and estu

claims.c: CyAN is a multi-agency project (EPA, USGS, NOAA, with NASA hosting the data at OB.DAAC) providing satellite-based detection and quantification of cyanobacteria algal blooms in U.S. lakes and estuaries.
The project's core output is a satellite-derived Cyanobacteria Index (CI) product, expressed as CI_cyano, covering lakes across the contiguous U.S. and Alaska (CONUS).

CI data derive from three sequential ESA ocean-color sensor records: Envisat MERIS (2002-2012), then Sentinel-3A OLCI (2016-present), then Sentinel-3B OLCI (2018-present); delivered as daily GeoTIFFs and 7-day maximum-value composites.
The best temporal coverage is achieved from 2018 onward, once two Sentinel-3 satellites (3A and 3B) were both providing OLCI imagery.
The sensor's native spatial resolution is 300 m; the land mask separating water from land is coarser for the Alaska product (500 m) than for CONUS (50 m).
Digital Number (DN) pixel values (0-255) are converted to a CI_cyano cell-density estimate via a stated exponential formula, with a reported dynamic range of about 10,000 to 7,000,000 cells/mL.
Within the 0-255 DN scale, 0 flags pixels below the CI detection limit, 1-253 are valid data, 254 marks land, and 255 marks no-data (e.g., cloud-covered) pixels.

Currently distributed CI data are processed to 'Version 6,' with release notes (dated August 2025, per the linked filename) covering production, interpretation, limitations, and version changes; the merged Sentinel-3A/3B OLCI CI product carries a specific citation and DOI.
The landing page does not itself list limitations; it directs users to the release notes for interpretation, limitations, and known issues.
A supplementary look at the linked Version 6 release notes found an explicit statement that CI product accuracy is reduced by atmospheric correction uncertainty and adjacency effects near coastlines.

The project distributes data and tools through a CyAN File Search API, SeaDAS analysis software (with a training presentation), an RS_Tools ArcGIS toolbox (versioned for ArcGIS 10 and ArcGIS Pro) for computing composites and extracting point/polygon time series, and downloadable CONUS/lake shapefiles.

EPA separately built a public-facing mobile application (the CyAN app) that consumes this satellite data product, distributed via the Google Play store; this NASA page links out to it rather than describing it technically.
The page names EPA, USGS, and NOAA as partner agencies collaborating on CyAN, with NASA hosting the data at its Ocean Biology Distributed Active Archive Center (OB.DAAC).

500 m — Alaska land-mask resolution (coarser than CONUS)

CI_cyano = 10^(DN x 0.011714 - 4.1870866) — DN-to-cell-density conversion formula
DN 0-255 scale: 0 = below CI detection threshold; 1-253 = valid data; 254 = land; 255 = no data/cloud
2002-2012 — Envisat MERIS data record (~10 years)
2016-present — Sentinel-3A OLCI data record
2018-present — Sentinel-3B OLCI data record (dual-satellite 'best' coverage begins)

DOI 10.5067/MERGED-S3/OLCI/L3M/CYAN/CI/6.0 — citation identifier for merged Sentinel-3A/3B OLCI CI product v6.0 (as reported on page; DOI not independently resolved)
RS_Toolbox v2.3.1 (ArcGIS 10) and v3.1.4 (ArcGIS Pro) — named ArcGIS plugin versions

what: EPA's Cyanobacteria Assessment Network application family — currently the browser-based "CyANWeb" tool (successor to the now-deprecated "CyAN Android" app) — which gives water-quality managers a no-programming-required interface to satellite-derived cyanobacteria bloom estimates for over 2,000 of the largest U.S. lakes

claims.c: CyANWeb provides satellite-derived cyanobacteria bloom data for over 2,000 of the largest lakes and reservoirs across the United States.
The app was built specifically so local and state water quality managers could make faster, better-informed HAB management decisions.
CyANWeb is a browser-based interface that is OS- and device-agnostic, unlike the deprecated CyAN Android app.
The tool is designed for fast, initial-assessment use and is constrained to water bodies of roughly 1 km² or larger.
The tool does not impose a single EPA-defined cyanobacteria concentration threshold; users set their own, because jurisdictions handle HABs differently.
The sole satellite sensor named as the data source is ESA's Copernicus Sentinel-3 Ocean and Land Colour Instrument (OLCI).
EPA explicitly labels the app and its underlying satellite measures as experimental, provisional, and potentially erroneous.
EPA began beta-testing an experimental predictive cyanoHAB forecasting model in July 2024 that produces weekly forecasts for over 2,000 lakes.

Both CyAN app versions were independently tested for over a year, with functionality and satellite data validated and published in multiple peer-reviewed papers, developed jointly with NASA, NOAA, and USGS.
The original CyAN Android app was publicly released in 2018.
CyANWeb launched in 2021 with additional features and became a full functional superset of the CyAN Android app.
EPA officially ended support for the CyAN Android app on December 31, 2024.
Despite the Android app's discontinuation, the CyAN REST API providing processed cyanobacteria index (CI) data continues unchanged with no planned changes.
The CyANWeb Fact Sheet PDF is 245.67 KB (document EPA/600/F-19/061b, updated Dec 31 2024) and the Palm Card PDF is 1.67 MB (dated Dec 31 2024).

title: Cyanobacterial HAB Monitoring using Satellite Imagery for the United States Great Lakes region created by the NOAA Harmful Algal Bloom Forecasting Branch (HAB-F) from 2000 to 2024 (NCEI Accession 0312614)
what: An NOAA NCCOS/HAB-F data report and NCEI archive accession (0312614; DOI 10.25921/wzk1-r208) presenting a 25-year (2000–2024), continuous, cross-sensor-harmonized satellite time series of the Cyanobacteria Index (CIcyano) for priority U.S. Great Lakes waterbodies — Lake Erie, Sandusky Bay, Saginaw Bay (Lake Huron), Gre

claims.c: The dataset provides a 25-year, continuous, cross-sensor-consistent satellite-based time series (2000–2024) of cyanobacterial HAB observations for the U.S. Great Lakes region.
The monitoring effort specifically targets Lake Erie, Sandusky Bay, Saginaw Bay (Lake Huron), Green Bay (Lake Michigan), and Lake Winnebago as the priority CyanoHAB-affected waterbodies.
The time series was built by fusing three satellite ocean-color sensor missions: Envisat-MERIS, Sentinel-3A/B OLCI, and MODIS-Terra.
To bridge the observational gap between the MERIS and OLCI sensor eras, the Cyanobacteria Index (CIcyano) was derived from MODIS-Terra using a purpose-built deep-learning framework called CyanNet.
The stated purpose of the long time series is to characterize bloom phenology, assess trends in phenological changes, and understand the key drivers that cause and exacerbate blooms.
CyanoHABs are framed by the source as an ongoing threat to water quality, ecosystems, economies, and public health in the Great Lakes region (motivational framing, not a new empirical finding).

The dataset is formally archived with DOI 10.25921/wzk1-r208, spatial bounding box roughly 40.941°N–49.141°N by -92.4° to -74.528° longitude, temporal extent 2000-06-01 to 2024-11-15, distributed as CSV and GeoTIFF files via HTTPS/FTP under a CC0 1.0 public-domain license.
NOAA/NCEI applies a standard no-warranty use constraint to the dataset, disclaiming responsibility for data suitability.
The report page carries a general website accessibility disclaimer stating that some linked scientific publications may not conform to Section 508 accessibility standards.

title: Cyanobacterial Harmful Algal Blooms Forecasting Research
year: 2024 (forecasting model developed/launched; underlying workflow published as Schaeffer et al. 2024); forecasts resumed 2025; page itself "Last updated on April 3, 2026"
what: An EPA Office of Research and Development web page describing EPA's experimental cyanobacterial harmful algal bloom (cyanoHAB) forecasting research, conducted as part of the multi-agency Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN) project. It describes a model, launched in 2024, that issues a weekly 7-day-ahead probability

claims.c: In 2024 EPA developed and launched an experimental cyanoHAB forecasting model that issues a 7-day probability-of-bloom forecast for the population of lakes EPA already monitors via near-real-time satellite data.
The forecast model's coverage is capped at 2,192 lakes in the contiguous United States, specifically those large enough to be resolved by the Sentinel-3 satellite's 300 x 300 meter pixel resolution.

A cyanoHAB "bloom" is operationally defined by the model as median lake chlorophyll-a of 12 ug/L or greater together with cyanobacteria dominance, based on the surface layer only (typically the upper two meters or less).

Each weekly forecast is valid for a fixed 7-day window running Sunday through Saturday, and forecasts are typically only produced from April through November because bloom probability is generally lower in colder months.

The chlorophyll-a input feeding the model is computed by masking out unusable satellite pixels (cloud, cloud shadow, glint, straylight, snow, ice, land cover) and taking the median chlorophyll-a of the retained pixels per lake, following a workflow detailed in a separate 2024 methods publication (Schaeffer et al. 2024).
EPA states the model currently overpredicts positive bloom events (a false-positive bias) and recommends this be offset with complementary field sampling or additional remote sensing.

EPA characterizes the model as having "low false omission," i.e., rarely missing a true bloom, which it frames as a deliberately conservative, health-protective design trade-off, while providing no quantitative accuracy, precision, recall, or AUC statistic anywhere on the page.
EPA explicitly states the forecasting model must not substitute for direct field sampling or observation, and that it may contain errors and will be refined over time.
Weekly forecasts, after being paused, resumed for the 2025 season and are now hosted on a separate EPA "Harmful Algal Bloom Forecasting" webpage rather than on this research description page.

upper two meters or less — surface depth represented by the satellite chlorophyll-a value ("the median lake chlorophyll-a in the surface (typically upper two meters or less)")

April through November — seasonal window when forecasts are typically generated
2024 — year the experimental forecasting model was developed/launched
2025 — year weekly forecasts "resumed" and were relocated to a separate EPA forecasting webpage
Page "Last updated on April 3, 2026"

This is directly relevant to (and in tension with) any other source in this review that treats CyAN/satellite chlorophyll-a detections as equivalent to a confirmed toxic bloom, or that argues remote sensing alone is sufficient w

title: Drinking Water Health Advisory for the Cyanobacterial Microcystin Toxins (EPA 820R15100)
what: A non-regulatory EPA Drinking Water Health Advisory (Office of Water, Health and Ecological Criteria Division; EPA 820R15100, dated June 15, 2015) that derives Ten-day Health-Advisory concentration levels for microcystin toxins in drinking water from animal dose-response/toxicological data, and surveys microcystin chem

claims.c: EPA set non-enforceable Ten-day Health Advisory levels for microcystins in drinking water: 0.3 µg/L for bottle-fed infants and young children of pre-school age, and 1.6 µg/L for school-age children through adults.

The advisory's toxicity basis is a single critical study — Heinze (1999), a 28-day rat drinking-water study — identifying a LOAEL of 50 µg/kg/day for microcystin-LR based on increased liver weight, liver lesions with hemorrhage, and elevated serum liver enzymes.
EPA applied a composite (total) uncertainty factor of 1000 to the LOAEL to derive the Reference Dose.
EPA uses microcystin-LR as a surrogate toxicant for total microcystins (roughly 100 known congeners) in setting this advisory, since MC-LR is the most potent and most-studied congener.
EPA states there is inadequate information to assess the carcinogenic potential of microcystins under its 2005 cancer-risk-assessment guidelines.

Microcystins in surface water are reported to have a half-life of roughly 4 to 14 days, and cyanobacterial blooms are typically seasonal (May through October), so EPA does not expect chronic (year-round) drinking-water exposure.

As of this document's publication, no U.S. federal water-quality criteria or enforceable regulations existed for cyanobacteria/cyanotoxins under the Safe Drinking Water Act or the Clean Water Act; microcystin-LR (with anatoxin-a and cylindrospermopsin) was on EPA's Contaminant Candidate List 3 (2009) and draft CCL4 (April 2015) as a candidate for future regulatory determination.

International and state drinking-water guideline values for microcystins (lifetime-exposure basis) cluster between 1.0 and 1.5 µg/L, anchored to a WHO provisional guideline of 1.0 µg/L microcystin-LR (TDI 0.04 µg/kg) adopted as a national-standard basis by 16 countries; among U.S. states, values found in the document range from 0.04 µg/L (Minnesota) to 1 µg/L (Ohio, Oregon).

The main historical human health-effects evidence base for microcystins is acute recreational-exposure symptom reporting, plus a documented case series of intravenous exposure via microcystin-contaminated dialysate that caused acute liver failure and death in a large number of exposed patients; oral (ingestion) toxicity data in humans are limited and confounded.
This Health Advisory is explicitly non-regulatory: it is not a legally enforceable standard and confers no legal rights or obligations.

numbers: LOAEL = 50 µg/kg/day (microcystin-LR, oral, rat, Heinze 1999 28-day study) — critical-effect basis for the RfD
Composite uncertainty factor = 1000 (10x intraspecies x 10x interspecies x ~3x LOAEL-to-NOAEL x ~3x database-deficiency)

title: Drinking Water Health Advisory for the Cyanobacterial Toxin Cylindrospermopsin
what: A June 2015 U.S. EPA non-regulatory Drinking Water Health Advisory (Doc. No. 820R15101) that establishes Ten-day Health Advisory concentrations for the cyanobacterial toxin cylindrospermopsin in drinking water, derived from an animal dose-response study, together with background on the toxin's chemistry, cyanobacterial

claims.c: EPA's Ten-day Health Advisory for cylindrospermopsin in drinking water is 0.7 µg/L for bottle-fed infants and pre-school-age children, and 3 µg/L for school-age children through adults.

The critical toxicological endpoint underlying the advisory is kidney toxicity (increased kidney weight, decreased urinary protein) identified in an 11-week (77-day) oral study in mice by Humpage and Falconer (2002, 2003), yielding a No-Observed-Adverse-Effect-Level (NOAEL) of 30 µg/kg/day.

EPA applied a composite (total) uncertainty factor of 300 to the NOAEL to derive the reference dose: 10x for intraspecies variability, 10x for interspecies variability, and roughly 3x (10^0.5) for database deficiencies.

EPA used a 77-day critical study even though it states 7-to-30-day studies are 'typically used' for Ten-day Health Advisories, justifying this because the only available shorter-duration studies (Shaw et al., 2001; Reisner et al., 2004) could not be used for quantitative dose-response derivation, while showing similar effects at similar doses to the longer study.

There are no U.S. federal regulatory standards for cyanobacteria or cyanotoxins (including cylindrospermopsin) in drinking water under the Safe Drinking Water Act or in ambient waters under the Clean Water Act, and this Health Advisory itself is explicitly non-binding.

Existing international and U.S. state drinking-water guideline values for cylindrospermopsin mostly cluster at 1 µg/L (Australia, New Zealand, Ohio, Oregon), while Brazil's recommended value is 15 µg/L — an order of magnitude higher.

Cylindrospermopsin-producing cyanobacteria 'do not tend to form visible surface scums,' with the highest cell concentrations occurring below the water surface, and the toxin is mostly extracellular (dissolved in water or attached to particulates) rather than retained inside cells.

Cylindrospermopsin degrades photochemically within hours (half-life 1.5-3 hours) when algal pigments are present but shows little degradation without pigments; in natural surface waters, reported biodegradation half-lives range from 11-15 days up to 8 weeks.

EPA states there is inadequate information to assess the carcinogenic potential of cylindrospermopsin because no epidemiological studies linking it to cancer and no chronic animal cancer bioassays of the purified toxin were identified.

Cylindrospermopsin is a zwitterionic tricyclic alkaloid with a molecular weight of 415.43 g/mole, and two naturally occurring congeners have been identified (7-epicylindrospermopsin and 7-deoxycylindrospermopsin).

numbers: NOAEL = 30 µg/kg/day (kidney toxicity endpoint: increased kidney weight, decreased urinary protein), from the Humpage and Falconer 2002/2003 11-week [77-day] oral mouse study — EPA's critical study for RfD derivation.

Composite uncertainty factor = 300 (10x intraspecies x 10x interspecies x ~3x [10^0.5] database-deficiency factor) applied to the NOAEL — a ~300-fold built-in safety margin, not a point estimate of human harm.
Ten-day Health Advisory = 0.7 µg/L for bottle-fed infants and pre-school-age children.
Ten-day Health Advisory = 3 µg/L for school-age children through adults.
International guideline values: Australia 1 µg/L (NHMRC/NRMMC 2011); New Zealand 1 µg/L (Ministry of Health 2008); Brazil 15 µg/L, labeled 'recommended' (Brasil 2009).
U.S. state guideline values: Ohio 1 µg/L (OHEPA 2014); Oregon 1 µg/L (OHA 2015).

Molecular weight = 415.43 g/mole; molecular formula given in the source as 'C H N O S' with subscripts '15 21 5 7' split onto a separate line by extraction (i.e., C15H21N5O7S per Ohtani et al., 1992) — reconstructed here
Photochemical degradation half-life = 1.5 to 3 hours in the presence of algal pigments; no half-life value given for the no-pigment case, only 'little degradation.'
Biodegradation half-life in natural surface waters = 11 to 15 days, with up to 8 weeks also reported — a range, not a single point estimate with confidence interval.
Critical study duration = 11 weeks (77 days), versus the 7-30 day duration EPA states is 'typically used' for Ten-day HA derivation — an explicitly flagged mismatch, bridged only qualitatively.
Context (not toxin-specific): EPA's HA Program (est. 1978) lists Health Advisories for 213 contaminants in total.

Cyanotoxins (anatoxin-a, cylindrospermopsin, microcystin-LR) were added to EPA's Contaminant Candidate List on CCL 3 (2009) and draft CCL 4 (April 2015); cyanotoxins as a category first appeared on CCL 1 (1998) and CCL 2

It summarizes the 2015 10-day exposure advisory levels for two cyanotoxins (cylindrospermopsin and microcystins), broken out by age group, and points to the un

These cyanotoxin Health Advisories are explicitly not legally enforceable federal standards, and are subject to change as new science becomes available.

title: EPA Researchers Develop Forecasting Approach to Predict Harmful Cyanobacterial Blooms for U.S. Lakes
year: 2024 (published January 23, 2024; updated July 23, 2024)
what: An EPA "Science Matters" news/feature article (science-communication piece, not the peer-reviewed methods paper itself) announcing a Bayesian statistical model that fuses EPA CyAN satellite-derived cyanobacteria detections with environmental predictors (lake morphology, temperature, precipitation) to produce weekly cya

claims.c: The forecasting approach is framed by EPA as the first of its kind to successfully forecast high-risk cyanobacterial bloom events at broad (multi-lake, CONUS) scale, in contrast to prior small-scale, single-lake HCB forecasting research.
The model's overall prediction accuracy, assessed by comparing forecasts to actual satellite-observed cyanobacteria presence, was 90%.

The method is a Bayesian (probabilistic) statistical model combining CyAN satellite cyanobacteria-monitoring data with simple environmental predictors (lake morphology, lake temperature, precipitation) to estimate the weekly probability of a bloom.
The public could access weekly forecast probabilities for the 2,192 lakes through a beta version of the tool posted on an EPA webpage, available from July through November 2024.
EPA research scientist Blake Schaeffer frames the work as analogous to weather forecasting applied to water quality.
EPA environmental scientist Wilson Salls states that the novelty of the approach relative to existing HCB forecasting research is its broad spatial scale, not the underlying forecasting concept.
EPA risk assessor Mark Myer characterizes the tool as using historical satellite-derived HCB data plus lake morphology/temperature/precipitation to produce a useful prediction of future HCB events.
Michael Paul, EPA's National HABs Program lead, credits the CyAN satellite monitoring program as the backbone that state and Tribal partners rely on for HAB monitoring.

The article frames HCB toxins as posing multi-pathway risks (recreational exposure, drinking water, irrigation, livestock, fisheries) and additional water-quality harms (taste/odor issues, hypoxia, biodiversity loss, socio-economic costs).
The article states a forward-looking concern, explicitly hedged rather than presented as a firm projection, that climate and land-use pressures could increase HCB frequency, extent, and magnitude.
This news article summarizes a separate peer-reviewed methods paper that is referenced only by title, with no journal name, volume, year, or DOI given anywhere in this article's own text.
Blake Schaeffer publicly presented this forecasting work on January 31, 2024, at the first 'HABs, Hypoxia, and Nutrients Research Webinar.'

title: Forecasting freshwater cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms for Sentinel-3 satellite resolved U.S. lakes and reservoirs
year: 2024 (published online 7 Nov 2023; journal issue dated Jan 2024, Vol. 349, Article 119518)
what: A peer-reviewed methods paper (Schaeffer et al., 2024, Journal of Environmental Management) by U.S. EPA researchers that builds and validates a near-term (one-week-ahead) probabilistic forecasting model for cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms across 2,192 Sentinel-3-resolvable U.S. lakes and reservoirs, fusing a weekly

claims.c: The paper develops a Bayesian hierarchical spatiotemporal (INLA) model that forecasts, one week ahead, whether a lake will exceed the WHO Recreational Guidance Alert Level 1 cyanoHAB threshold, and evaluates it across 2,192 Sentinel-3 OLCI-resolvable U.S. lakes and reservoirs.
On an independent, out-of-sample 2021 test year, the INLA model achieved 90% overall accuracy, 88% sensitivity, 91% specificity, 49% precision, and AUC 0.95.

The INLA model outperformed six machine-learning/neural-network comparison models (Support Vector Classifier, Random Forest, Dense Neural Network, LSTM, RNN, and a 'Gneural Network'/GRU); the best-performing comparison models (SVC and Random Forest) reached only about 0.84-0.85 accuracy.
Model performance varied substantially by week across the 2021 forecast year: weekly accuracy ranged 80.1%-98.1%, precision 26.1%-64.0%, sensitivity 59.7%-97.5%, and specificity 75.8%-98.4%.

Across the full multi-year study record, 10.1% of lake-weeks (43,482 of 432,030) were classified as bloom weeks (exceeding WHO Alert Level 1), with a median cyanobacterial concentration of 33.1 ug/L during those bloom weeks.

The model's four environmental predictors (beyond the satellite signal) - weekly water-surface temperature, weekly precipitation, and static lake mean depth and surface area - were associated with bloom log-odds in the fitted model: warmer water was associated with higher bloom log-odds, while higher precipitation, larger lake area, and greater mean depth were associated with lower bloom log-odds. These are model-fitted statistical associations, not demonstrated causal effects.

The bloom 'ground truth' signal is itself a derived satellite product: the Sentinel-3 OLCI cyanobacteria index (CIcyano) is converted to a chlorophyll-a proxy via a fixed linear equation, and this conversion carries roughly the same magnitude of error as typical in-situ chlorophyll-a measurement.

A Monte Carlo uncertainty analysis found the forecast was robust to satellite retrieval noise up to a standard deviation of 2.65 ug/L (reclassifying under 0.69% of observations), with acceptable performance maintained up to a standard deviation of 5.30 ug/L (44% of the threshold value), before larger noise levels began to degrade performance.

The authors state that the model currently overpredicts positive (bloom) events, which they connect to its comparatively low precision (49%) given the low true base-rate prevalence of blooms (about 9-10%).

The model is explicitly scoped to near-surface (within about 2 m depth) biomass/chlorophyll detection and does not forecast cyanotoxins, and it does not incorporate longer-term climate change, nutrient dynamics, or anthropogenic-scenario effects.

The comparison machine-learning and neural-network models underperformed the INLA model partly because the dataset was imbalanced (most lake-weeks are 'static' bloom-or-no-bloom states rather than transition weeks) and because those models could not handle missing satellite data without additional preprocessing.
The authors frame the weekly forecast as providing 7 days of advance notice, which they compare favorably to a stated United Nations recommendation of 2-3 days advance notice for this kind of hazard.

Training split reported as n=308,204 lake-week observations (2017-2020); cross-check: 308,204 + 132,688 = 440,892, i.e. ~70%/30% train/validation split of the 2017-2020 pool
432,030 total lake-week observations reported across the full study record (261 weeks, 2192 lakes, 2017-2021)
43,482 lake-weeks (10.1%) classified as exceeding the WHO Alert Level 1 bloom threshold across the full study record
Median cyanobacterial concentration during bloom weeks: 33.1 μg L⁻¹ (chlorophyll-a proxy)
2021 weekly performance range: accuracy 80.1%-98.1%; precision 26.1%-64.0%; sensitivity 59.7%-97.5%; specificity 75.8%-98.4%
Average annual cyanoHAB prevalence across 2021: 9.1%
Comparison ML models (SVC, Random Forest) reached ~0.84-0.85 accuracy, below INLA's 0.90
Bloom threshold definition: >12 μg L⁻¹ chlorophyll-a with cyanobacterial dominance (WHO Alert Level 1)
Chlorophyll-a conversion from satellite index: Chl = 6620×CIcyano − 3.07; ~60% validation error (vs. 30-60% typical in-situ chl-a measurement error)
Spatial correlation range: 88.02 km (SD 3.31 km); reported spatial variance 83.05 (units unclear in extracted text - flagged)
tensions: The source defines "bloom" via a chlorophyll-a/biomass-based threshold (WHO Alert Level 1, >12 ug/L chlorophyll-a with cyanobacterial dominance) rather than a cyanotoxin-concentration threshold, and it explicitly states it cannot forecast toxins directly - this could complicate comparison with other HAB sources that key their risk/harm definition to measured cyanotoxin (e.g., microcystin) concentrations, since biomass exceedance and toxin presence do not always co-occur. Second, the paper's headline "90% accuracy" coexists with only 49% precisi

title: GitHub - DOI-USGS/dataretrieval-python: Python package for retrieving water data from USGS or the multi-agency Water Quality Portal
year: 2023 (suggested-citation software release); repository actively maintained through latest release v1.2.0 (Jun 24, 2026)
what: dataretrieval-python is a USGS-maintained (DOI-USGS) open-source Python package, the Python counterpart to USGS's R `dataRetrieval` package, that provides a programmatic interface for discovering and retrieving major U.S. hydrologic and water-quality data types from federal web services: the modern USGS Water Data API,

claims.c: The package's stated purpose is to simplify loading hydrologic data into Python by retrieving major USGS hydrology data types plus data from the Water Quality Portal (WQP), the National Ground-Water Monitoring Network (NGWMN), and the Network Linked Data Index (NLDI).

The Water Quality Portal module (dataretrieval.wqp) retrieves analytical results, monitoring locations, organizations, and project details, and the WQP itself aggregates contributions from USGS, EPA, and other agencies.
The legacy `dataretrieval.nwis` module (functions get_dv, get_iv, get_info, get_stats, get_discharge_peaks) is explicitly marked deprecated in favor of the newer `dataretrieval.waterdata` module.
Users are advised to obtain a USGS API key for higher rate limits and to break continuous (high-frequency) data requests into smaller time windows to avoid timeouts.

The software carries the standard USGS provisional-software disclaimer: it is preliminary/provisional, has not received final USGS approval, and neither USGS nor the U.S. Government accepts liability for its use.
Development of the package was partially funded by a National Science Foundation award.
The official suggested citation is Hodson, Hariharan, Black, and Horsburgh (2023), published as a USGS software release with DOI 10.5066/P94I5TX3.

As of the fetch date, the repository listed 252 stars, 59 forks, 11 watchers, 7 open issues, 8 open pull requests, 23 releases (latest v1.2.0, dated Jun 24, 2026), 421 total commits, and a language composition of 100% Python.
dataretrieval-python is presented as the Python counterpart to / mirror of USGS's original R package `dataRetrieval`.

Query functions return a two-item result of a pandas DataFrame plus a metadata object, e.g., `df, metadata = waterdata.get_daily(monitoring_location_id='USGS-01646500', parameter_code='00060', time='2024-10-01/2025-09-30')`.

title: Global Historical Climatology Network daily (GHCNd)
year: Ongoing (continuously updated archive with records back to 1832); foundational overview paper cited on the page is Menne et al. (2012)
what: GHCNd is a NOAA/NCEI-maintained, quality-controlled, integrated database of daily climate summaries (maximum/minimum temperature, total precipitation, snowfall, and snow depth) from more than 100,000 land surface weather stations in 180 countries and territories worldwide, assembled from more than 25 underlying data-so

claims.c: GHCNd is an integrated, quality-assured database of daily climate summaries compiled from land surface weather stations across the globe.
GHCNd contains records from more than 100,000 stations located in 180 countries and territories.
Roughly 20,000 stations are updated with new observations within any 30-day period, while daily maximum/minimum temperature specifically is available from over 25,000 sites.
Individual station records range in length from under one year to more than 175 years, with an average record length exceeding four decades.
The earliest observation in GHCNd is a precipitation reading from January 1, 1832 at Parramatta, Australia; the earliest temperature observations are from January 2, 1833 at Uccle, Belgium.
The core measured variables in GHCNd are maximum and minimum temperature, total daily precipitation, snowfall, and snow depth, and roughly half of all stations report precipitation only.

Data are integrated through a three-step process: screening source stations of unknown/questionable identity, classifying stations as new versus already-represented, and merging (mingling) data across sources, with station matching relying primarily on network ID/affiliation and a minimum 50% match-rate threshold for new sources within 40 km of existing stations.

GHCNd draws on four broad source categories: a U.S. Collection (about a dozen constituent U.S. datasets), an International Collection (~20,000 locations, >100 countries), Government (bilateral/GCOS) Exchange Data, and Global Summary of the Day SYNOP reports; Government Exchange Data alone includes more than 7,500 Canadian and more than 17,000 Australian station records.

The dataset receives daily updates and the full archive is reconstructed each weekend from more than 25 data source components; real-time feeds are normally superseded by archive-quality data 45-60 days after a data month closes.
An automated QA system flags approximately 0.3% of nearly 2 billion data values, and NOAA's own estimate is that 98-99% of flagged values are true data errors (1-2% false positives).

The QA pipeline runs at least 20 distinct automated statistical tests with explicit numeric thresholds, e.g., a spike/dip check flags a daily max or min temperature that differs from the preceding/following day by more than 25°C, and a spatial regression check flags station temperatures differing more than 8°C from neighbor-predicted values with a standardized residual exceeding 4 standard deviations.

Unlike its monthly counterpart GHCNm, GHCNd contains no adjustment for biases from historical changes in instrumentation or observing practice, and GHCNd/GHCNm were not internally consistent with one another until GHCNm version 4.

Station density is geographically uneven, with dense networks in North America, Eurasia, the U.S., Canada, and Australia, and comparatively sparse coverage in Africa, Antarctica, and South America; many International Collection records are historical and no longer updated (e.g., the source states Brazil and South Africa precipitation records ended in the late 1990s and India's records ended in 1970).

The date-based climatological outlier check applied to snowfall and snow depth has a substantially higher false-positive rate (about 50% for snowfall, about 75% for snow depth) than the standard rate of less than 20% for other QA checks.

numbers: More than 100,000 stations in 180 countries and territories
Approximately 20,000 stations report new observations during any 30-day period
Daily maximum/minimum temperature available from more than 25,000 sites

Earliest temperature observation: January 2, 1833, Uccle, Belgium
About half (~50%) of all stations report precipitation only (no temperature)
International Collection: ~20,000 locations from >100 countries
Government Exchange Data - Canada: more than 7,500 station records
Government Exchange Data - Australia: more than 17,000 station records
New-source station matching threshold: at least 50% match rate for stations within 40 km of existing sites
tensions: GHCNd itself contains no content about harmful algal blooms, cyanobacteria, nutrients, or water quality — it is purely a meteorological/climate station archive, so it cannot directly corroborate or contradict any HAB-specific finding from other sources in this review. The one methodologically relevant caution the source itself raises is that GHCNd is explicitly NOT bias-adjusted or homogenized (unlike monthly GHCNm), so raw long-term temperature trends computed directly from GHCNd station series could partly reflect instrument or station-networ

title: Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) Forecasting
year: 1999–present (ongoing project; cited internal dataset/documentation records dated 2017, 2018, 2021)
what: A NOAA National Centers for Coastal Ocean Science (NCCOS) project page (ongoing since January 1999) describing NOAA's harmful algal bloom forecasting systems, which fuse satellite ocean-color imagery, NOAA meteorological station data, and state/university field-monitoring data into predictive models that notify coastal

claims.c: The NOAA NCCOS HAB Forecasting project began in January 1999 and is an ongoing initiative to notify coastal managers of harmful algal blooms before they reach shore by identifying bloom species, location, size, and trajectory.

NOAA has designated seven regions nationally as critical HAB problem areas: Florida/eastern Gulf and western Gulf (Karenia brevis), Lake Erie (cyanobacteria), Gulf of Maine (Alexandrium fundyense), Washington Coast (Pseudo-nitzschia), California Coast (multiple species), and Chesapeake Bay (multiple species).

Of the seven designated regions, only the Florida/Texas Gulf coast has a mature operational forecast; Lake Erie's cyanobacteria forecast is shown by linked dataset records to have transitioned from an 'Experimental' product to an operational HAB-OFS product, while the remaining regions are described only as developmental forecast projects.

Forecast products are generated by fusing satellite (ocean-color) imagery, NOAA meteorological station data, and field data from state/university monitoring programs into predictive models of bloom trajectory over the following days.
The Florida/Texas HAB Bulletin is emailed to coastal managers bi-weekly during an active bloom and weekly during non-bloom periods, supplemented by a public-facing Operational Conditions Report.
The project explicitly characterizes itself as not yet at national forecast capacity, describing several regions as operational or expected to become operational only over the next decade.

A stated resource constraint underlies the forecasting approach: the program lacks funds to sample (ground-truth) every HAB event, part of the rationale for relying on remote sensing plus modeling rather than exhaustive field sampling.
The satellite ocean-color component of the forecasting pipeline follows a specific, named, publicly documented processing methodology.

The project page links three distinct archived datasets of forecast products plus associated satellite imagery: two for Lake Erie (2008-09-04 to 2016-11-01 under an 'Experimental' NCCOS-labeled product, and 2017-06-25 to 2020-10-20 under the operational HAB-OFS system) and one for the Gulf of Mexico (2004-01-12 to 2021-07-23, HAB-OFS).
Named points of contact for the project include NOAA scientists Michelle Tomlinson and Richard Stumpf.
Forecasts and their underlying data draw on partner support beyond NOAA itself, including NASA, CDC, NOAA's CO-OPS office, state/university monitoring programs, and Michigan Sea Grant.

7 regions designated nationally as critical HAB problem areas
Florida/Texas Gulf HAB Bulletin cadence: bi-weekly during an active bloom; weekly during non-bloom periods
Dataset (NCCOS, 2021): Experimental HAB bulletin forecast products + satellite imagery, Lake Erie, 2008-09-04 to 2016-11-01
Dataset (CO-OPS, 2018): HAB-OFS forecast products + satellite imagery, Lake Erie, 2017-06-25 to 2020-10-20
Dataset (CO-OPS, 2017): HAB-OFS forecast products + satellite imagery, Gulf of Mexico, 2004-01-12 to 2021-07-23
Referenced methods document: NOAA Technical Memorandum NOS NCCOS 252 (2018), Ocean Color Satellite Imagery Processing Guidelines
Forecast horizon stated only qualitatively as 'the next few days' — no numeric lead-time, spatial resolution, or accuracy/skill statistic is given anywhere on the page
tensions: As a program-overview webpage rather than a peer-reviewed validation study, this source cannot itself support a specific forecast-skill number (e.g., % correct classification, verified lead-time accuracy) — it documents scope, inputs, and delivery cadence only, so it should be paired with underlying peer-reviewed CyAN/HAB-OFS validation literature before any accuracy claim is used in a product/tool. It also shows that "Lake Erie HAB forecasting" is not one static capability: the page's own linked dataset metadata records a shift from an NCCOS-l

title: Impacts of Climate Change on the Occurrence of Harmful Algal Blooms
what: A May 2013 U.S. EPA Office of Water fact sheet (820-S-13-001) that synthesizes existing science on the mechanisms by which climate change — warming, salinity shifts, rising atmospheric CO2, altered rainfall patterns, sea level rise, and changing coastal upwelling — may promote the growth, dominance, and geographic spre

claims.c: EPA defines harmful algal blooms (HABs) as algal blooms that impair aquatic ecosystems or have the potential to affect human health, distinguishing them from ordinary (non-harmful) algal blooms.

The fact sheet states that scientists have observed an increase in the frequency, severity, and geographic distribution of HABs worldwide in recent decades, and frames climate change as a plausible contributor via six listed mechanisms (warmer water temperatures, changes in salinity, increased atmospheric CO2, changes in rainfall patterns, intensifying coastal upwelling, sea level rise).

Certain harmful cyanobacteria, exemplified by Microcystis, are described as growing faster than non-harmful algae at relatively high water temperatures, with some outcompeting above 77°F, which the source says increases the likelihood of HAB events.

Warming is said to increase the frequency, strength, and duration of thermal stratification, and stratified conditions favor cyanobacteria/dinoflagellates that can regulate buoyancy (e.g., via gas vesicles) to migrate and form dense surface blooms, gaining light/nutrient advantage over algae unable to reach the surface.
The fact sheet describes a positive feedback loop in which HABs absorb sunlight and warm surface water, which in turn further favors their own growth and competitive advantage.

Warmer water is said to have lower viscosity, which increases the speed at which small cyanobacteria can migrate to the surface (aiding bloom formation) while promoting sinking of larger, non-motile algae/organisms, increasing cyanobacteria's competitive advantage.

Climate-change-driven increases in drought intensity and duration are described as reducing lake/reservoir inflow and increasing evaporation, lowering water levels and raising salinity; this can both stress algal cells into leaking/releasing toxins and create conditions favorable to marine HAB species invading freshwater systems.

As a concrete example of marine-to-freshwater HAB invasion under elevated salinity, the source states that the marine golden alga Prymnesium parvum has been increasing in southwestern and south-central U.S. lakes since 2000, causing significant fish kills in inland waters.

Rising atmospheric CO2 is said to increase dissolved CO2 in aquatic systems, favoring algae that grow faster under elevated CO2, with surface-floating cyanobacteria singled out as having a distinct advantage because they can access atmospheric CO2 directly rather than relying only on dissolved CO2.

Decomposition of algal biomass (including bloom-derived organic matter) is described as consuming oxygen and adding CO2 in poorly circulated waters, contributing a local/ecological acidification effect on top of ocean acidification from atmospheric CO2; the source states these water-chemistry shifts can alter competitive relationships between HABs and other algae and can change zooplankton grazing control of HABs.

The source states that intensifying extreme rainfall (more concentrated bursts) followed by longer dry/drought periods, as projected under climate change, could increase nutrient runoff into water bodies and, if followed by drought, allow those nutrients to be retained longer, increasing the potential for HAB development.

The source cites projections that global sea level could rise 18 cm to one meter by 2100, which would expand continental shelf area and create shallow, stable coastal waters that may favor HAB growth or expand HAB habitat further inland.

The source notes that HAB response to coastal upwelling is variable (depending on atmospheric oscillations, water-column stratification, nutrient availability, and upwelling intensity/persistence), and that along ocean boundaries such as the U.S. west coast, climate-driven intensification of upwelling may deliver more nutrients to coastal waters, favoring growth of both harmful algal blooms and benign species, not HABs specifically.

The document itself states that although much of its presented evidence suggests HAB problems may worsen under future climate scenarios, further research is needed to better understand the climate-HAB association.

numbers: 77°F — water temperature above which certain harmful cyanobacteria (e.g., Microcystis) are said to grow faster than non-harmful algae (illustrative example; no baseline, error bar, or underlying-study citation given in t
18 cm to 1 meter — projected global sea level rise by year 2100, attributed only to unnamed 'scientific models' (range only; no point estimate, confidence interval, or emissions scenario specified)

since 2000 — period over which Prymnesium parvum (golden algae) is said to have been increasing in southwestern/south-central U.S. lakes (qualitative trend statement; no magnitude, rate, or count given)
EPA 820-S-13-001 (MC 4304T), May 2013 — document identifier and publication date, not a scientific measurement
tensions: Within the document itself, the coastal-upwelling mechanism is described as benefiting "both harmful algal blooms and benign species" — an explicitly non-selective effect that sits in tension with the fact sheet's broader narrative (temperature, viscosity, and CO2 mechanisms) that climate change gives HABs a specific competitive edge over non-harmful algae. More generally, because this is a 2013 qualitative mechanism/plausibility review that itself states the climate-HAB association "needs further research," it should be weighed in a literature

title: Impaired Waters and Nutrients (TMDL Program)
year: 2026 (page states "Last updated on January 5, 2026"; the key cited guidance document within it, the "Protocol for Developing Nutrient TMDLs," is dated November 1999)
what: An EPA "Impaired Waters and Nutrients" program webpage, part of the Total Maximum Daily Load (TMDL) program under Clean Water Act Section 303(d), that explains why nitrogen/phosphorus (nutrient) pollution is difficult to list and remediate through the 303(d)/TMDL process, and links to EPA nutrient-TMDL guidance documen

claims.c: Excess nitrogen and/or phosphorus in a waterbody is described as producing several harmful conditions, including increased harmful algal blooms, reduced light penetration, and reduced dissolved oxygen.
EPA frames addressing nutrient pollution in U.S. waters as a top agency priority.
Thousands of U.S. waterbodies are impaired by nitrogen and phosphorus, though no specific count or percentage is given.
Most U.S. states rely on narrative (not numeric) water quality standards for nitrogen and phosphorus, which complicates listing nutrient-impaired waters under CWA Section 303(d).
Nutrient pollution has diffuse, mixed sources; a significant portion of nitrogen loading can come from agricultural or atmospheric sources rather than a single point source.
EPA has published a specific technical protocol for developing nutrient TMDLs, dated November 1999.
EPA convened a three-day workshop in February 2011 specifically on 303(d) listing, TMDL development, and TMDL implementation for nutrient-impaired waters.
EPA lists specific named waterbodies as real-world nutrient TMDL case examples.

Protocol for Developing Nutrient TMDLs (First Edition): 137 pp, 1.9 MB, published November 1999, EPA Doc #841-B-99-007
