2014 CWA Sections 303(d)/305(b)/314 Integrated Reporting Memo: 369.89 KB
2016 CWA Integrated Reporting Memo: 19 pp, 227 K
Nutrient TMDL Workshop: 3-day event, February 15-17, 2011 (New Orleans)
Page 'last updated' date: January 5, 2026
tensions: This source is a policy/administrative overview, not an empirical or dose-response study: it asserts that excess nitrogen/phosphorus causes "increased harmful algal blooms" but supplies no concentration thresholds, load-response relationships, monitoring data, or citations to underlying science within the fetched text — so it cannot by itself support a quantitative nutrient-to-HAB-risk claim (only a qualitative, non-causal association) that a HAB analysis would need to parameterize. It also implicitly complicates any single-driver framing of HA

title: Introduction to the dataRetrieval package (USGS)
what: dataRetrieval is a USGS-maintained R package (documented as version 2.7.25) that provides functions to query and download U.S. Geological Survey hydrologic data — streamflow/discharge, gage height, water temperature, groundwater levels, peak flows, rating curves, and site metadata — from the USGS Water Data API and USG

claims.c: dataRetrieval is an R package created to simplify loading hydrologic data into the R environment, covering major USGS hydrologic data types available on the web plus discrete water-quality data from the multi-agency Water Quality Portal (WQP).
WQP, one of the package's two main data backends, currently houses water-quality data contributed by EPA, USDA, and USGS.

The package exposes a family of read_waterdata_* functions, each mapped to a specific USGS Water Data API product (daily values, continuous/instantaneous values, rating tables, field measurements, peak flows, monitoring-location/site metadata, parameter-code lookups, period-of-record and date-range statistics), plus separate readWQP*/whatWQP* functions for Water Quality Portal queries and read_waterdata_samples/summarize_waterdata_samples for the USGS Samples Data service.

A worked example retrieves daily discharge (parameter code 00060) for USGS monitoring location 01491000, the Choptank River near Greensboro, MD, over 1980-01-01 to 2010-01-01 using read_waterdata_daily.

Documented USGS parameter codes include 00060 (Discharge, ft3/s), 00065 (Gage height, ft), 00010 (Temperature, C), 00045 (Precipitation, in), 00400 (pH), and 00631 (Nitrate plus nitrite, water, filtered, milligrams per liter as nitrogen).
Documented statistic codes for daily-value aggregation include 00001 (Maximum), 00002 (Minimum), 00003 (Mean), and 00008 (Median).

USGS's water data APIs impose query rate limits keyed to the requesting IP address per hour, and users are directed to register for a personal API token (stored as an .Renviron variable) to use with the package.

Data retrieved by the package is time-stamped in UTC; the documentation illustrates this by noting that a local U.S. Eastern Standard Time midnight appears five hours offset in the returned dateTime column.

USGS surface-water monitoring sites are typically identified with 8-digit numbers (e.g., 01491000) while groundwater sites typically use 15-digit numbers (e.g., 434400121275801), both prefixed 'USGS-' in the newer API functions.
Left unspecified, the startDate/endDate arguments default to requesting the maximum available data (full period of record) for a site/parameter.
The documentation states that legacy water-use data services have been retired, with replacement functionality not yet available at the time of writing.

USGS attaches a standing disclaimer to this information, describing it as preliminary and subject to revision, provided to meet the need for timely best science, with no liability accepted by USGS or the U.S. Government for damages arising from its use.

The documented package version is 2.7.25, citable as a USGS software release (De Cicco, Hirsch, Lorenz, Watkins, Johnson, Blodgett, Hinman, Zemmels, 2026), alongside separate recommended citation templates for the underlying NWIS and WQP data themselves.

Water Quality Portal query examples in the documentation filter by state/county FIPS-style codes and characteristic-name strings, e.g., specific conductance at site WIDNR_WQX-10032762, chloride sites in state code US:34, and pH records in state code US:55 / county code US:55:025.

numbers: Documented package version: v.2.7.25 (DOI 10.5066/P9X4L3GE)
Parameter code 00060 = Discharge [ft3/s]
Parameter code 00065 = Gage height [ft]
Parameter code 00010 = Temperature [C]
Parameter code 00045 = Precipitation [in]
Parameter code 00400 = pH (unitless)
Parameter code 00631 = Nitrate plus nitrite, water, filtered [mg/L as nitrogen]
Statistic code 00001 = Maximum
Statistic code 00002 = Minimum
Statistic code 00003 = Mean
Statistic code 00008 = Median
Surface-water monitoring-location IDs: 8 digits (example: USGS-01491000)
tensions: This is a data-access software tool, not a HAB-analytic or ecological study, so it does not itself make findings that contradict other HAB literature. Two analyst-level (not source-stated) observations relevant to fusing this source with the rest of the HAB dossier: (a) dataRetrieval only reaches in-situ/discrete hydrologic and water-quality time series (e.g., discharge, gage height, temperature, nutrients, pH) and the separate USGS Samples service — it does not provide the remote-sensing/satellite HAB signal (e.g., EPA CyAN cyanobacteria index

title: Lake Erie Harmful Algal Bloom Forecast
what: NOAA NCCOS's operational Lake Erie page bundling several linked cyanobacterial-HAB decision products for western Lake Erie: a seasonal (July–October) bloom-severity outlook and Severity Index, pre-season weekly severity projections, and near-real-time (≥96-hour) bloom position and vertical-mixing forecasts, built by fu

claims.c: NOAA issues seasonal forecasts for cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) blooms in Lake Erie, framed around the July–October window when warm water favors blooms.

The forecast's Severity Index (SI) is defined as bloom biomass integrated over a sustained 30-day period, normalized to the historically lowest (2005) and highest (2011, 'to date') observed bloom-biomass years.

A bloom position/extent forecast product provides a minimum 96-hour forecast lead time, generated from hydrodynamic modeled currents combined with satellite imagery used to fix the initial bloom location.
A companion vertical-mixing forecast, over the same minimum ~96-hour window, estimates the likelihood the bloom is at the surface versus subsurface.
Ahead of the full seasonal severity forecast, NOAA issues weekly 'early season' bloom-severity projections (starting in May) that are driven by Maumee River discharge and modeled phosphorus loads.
The 2026 western Lake Erie seasonal HAB forecast was announced June 25, and the immediately preceding (2025) forecast season's operational window ran through November 19, 2025.

The program is run by NOAA NCCOS, with Ohio Sea Grant named as the partner that co-hosts the live seasonal-forecast announcement webinar; Ohio EPA, Ohio State University, and Heidelberg University's National Center for Water Quality Research are also named in connection with the program.

The forecast system fuses multiple data streams: satellite imagery (OLCI and true-color) for bloom location/extent, hydrodynamic/mixing model output, Maumee River discharge and modeled phosphorus loads, and field-collected toxicity measurements.

No explicit statement of forecast accuracy, uncertainty, skill score, or an itemized list of what the forecast cannot predict was recoverable from the page text, despite a fetch pass specifically targeted at finding such wording.

One extraction pass (not corroborated by the other two) reported that satellite data underlying the severity time series was reprocessed after the 2024 bloom with new calibrations, and separately described a 20-meter-resolution 'Demonstration Great Lakes HAB Monitoring' weekly imagery product for Maumee Bay.

numbers: 96 hours — minimum forecast lead time for both the bloom position/extent forecast and the vertical-mixing forecast
30-day period — sustained window over which bloom biomass is integrated to compute the Severity Index
2005 — calibration year used as the Severity Index scale's minimum observed bloom biomass
2011 — calibration year used as the Severity Index scale's maximum observed bloom biomass 'to date' (per source wording)
June 25 — announcement date of the 2026 western Lake Erie seasonal HAB forecast
11/19/2025 — final day of the 2025 Lake Erie HAB forecast season
20 m — resolution cited for a 'Demonstration Great Lakes HAB Monitoring' weekly imagery product (single fetch-pass only, not corroborated — low confidence)
tensions: Not directly evidenced against other sources within this task (only this one source was reviewed here). Two points are worth flagging for cross-source reconciliation later: (1) the Severity Index is normalized to a fixed historical calibration window (floor = 2005, ceiling = 2011 "to date" per the source's own wording) — the fetched text does not clarify how, or whether, the scale has been revised to accommodate any bloom years whose biomass may have exceeded the 2011 reference after that year, which matters for comparing SI values across the f

title: Lake Erie HAB Tracker Archive (NOAA GLERL) — successor to the Lake Erie Harmful Algal Bloom Forecast bulletin
year: Not stated on page (archived seasons run 2017–2019; page reflects a post-discontinuation status with no explicit publication/update date in the fetched text)
what: The page documents the (now-discontinued) Experimental Lake Erie Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) Tracker, a NOAA GLERL/NOS 5-day forecasting tool that combined satellite-derived cyanobacterial chlorophyll-a imagery, wind/wave hydrodynamic forecasts, and weekly in-situ microcystin sampling to depict likely surface bloom exten

claims.c: The Experimental Lake Erie HAB Tracker model has stopped running entirely; its functionality was transitioned into an operational successor product, the NOAA Lake Erie HAB Forecast.
The Tracker's core product was a 5-day-ahead forecast of surface HAB concentration expressed as cyanobacterial chlorophyll-a, alongside supporting wind speed, wave height, and microcystin data.

A companion vertical-distribution component modeled how wind-driven mixing and thermal stratification move Microcystis through the water column, built on the assumption that colonies float — while explicitly noting some colonies instead sink and settle on the bottom under the same calm conditions that produce surface scums.
Satellite inputs come from NASA MODIS Aqua/Terra and Copernicus Sentinel-3, processed through a named NOAA system and converted from a cyanobacterial index into a cyanobacterial chlorophyll-a scale.

Wind/wave conditions used by the model come from the Great Lakes Coastal Forecasting System (GLCFS), using observed lake-station winds for the nowcast and the National Weather Service's National Digital Forecast Database for the forecast period.

The Tracker does not itself forecast toxin (microcystin) levels — toxin data comes from separate weekly in-situ sampling, and values exceeding Ohio's Elevated Recreational Public Health Advisory level are flagged in red on the monitoring maps.
In-situ water-quality/toxin samples are collected weekly at monitoring stations, with surface samples drawn from approximately 0.75 m depth.
Dates for which satellite coverage was unusable due to clouds are flagged with an asterisk and backfilled using the most recent relatively cloud-free imagery rather than left blank.

NOAA GLERL explicitly disclaims operational-center status for this program and labels all of its forecasts/products, including the Tracker, as experimental with no guarantee of availability or accuracy.
Archived full-season Tracker animations and archived forecasts are retained on the page for the 2017–2019 period only.

The operational successor, the NOAA Lake Erie HAB Forecast, only begins issuing bulletins after in-situ sampling confirms Microcystis presence, runs during the bloom season typically from July to October, and is updated daily with current bloom extent and trajectory.

numbers: 5-day forecast horizon for the HAB Tracker's surface bloom prediction
~0.75 m depth for in-situ surface water toxin/quality samples
Weekly (approximately 7-day) in-situ sampling frequency for microcystin monitoring
Bloom/forecast season described only qualitatively as 'typically July to October' (no exact calendar dates given)
Archived seasons span 2017-2019 (full-season animations for 2019, 2018, 2017; individually selectable archived forecasts noted for 2017 and 2018 in one extraction pass)

No numeric microcystin concentration threshold (e.g., in µg/L) for the 'Elevated Recreational Public Health Advisory' was present in the fetched text -- the page references the Ohio EPA advisory level by name only, witho
No satellite pixel resolution (m/km), model grid resolution, or exact tracker start/discontinuation calendar dates were present in the fetched text despite a targeted extraction attempt
tensions: Two tensions relevant to the HAB_PoC project: (1) The source explicitly and repeatedly separates satellite-derived cyanobacterial chlorophyll-a signal from actual toxin (microcystin) risk -- "The HAB Tracker doesn't predict toxin concentrations" -- which cautions against treating a satellite/chlorophyll-based "bloom" signal as equivalent to a public-health/toxin risk signal; any product claim in this PoC that frames a satellite-driven risk score as a toxin-exposure risk should be bounded accordingly, consistent with the project's own claim-gate

title: Lake Erie Harmful Algal Bloom Forecast: 2020 Improvements
what: A short NOAA National Ocean Service web story (page metadata shows "last updated 06/16/24"; content and a corroborating companion NOAA Research post are dated 2020) announcing that NOAA's operational Lake Erie Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) Forecast, run by CO-OPS since 2016, was upgraded for the 2020 season with (1) a 3D h

claims.c: The Lake Erie HAB Forecast now incorporates a 3D hydrodynamic model to represent what is happening beneath the lake surface, because many drinking-water intakes and fishing spots sit below the surface, out of view of aerial/satellite imagery.
The 3D hydrodynamic model was developed by research partners within NOAA's Office of Oceanic & Atmospheric Research (OAR), not by CO-OPS itself.
NOAA's Center for Operational Oceanographic Products & Services (CO-OPS) has issued operational Lake Erie HAB forecasts since 2016.
The (upgraded) forecast product pairs a daily satellite-derived estimate of current surface Microcystis bloom location with a five-day forward forecast of bloom movement.
Before/alongside the 3D-model upgrade, users relied on a twice-weekly cycle of forecast analysis of bloom position, movement/mixing potential, forecast water currents, and recent satellite imagery.
The forecast website was redesigned, adding a bloom-movement animation and plots of vertical Microcystis movement at select sampling locations.

Not all algal blooms are harmful; the term HAB specifically covers toxin-producing varieties such as the blue-green alga Microcystis, which harms people, fish, marine mammals, birds, and local economies.
NOAA states the forecast's purpose is to help those responding to HAB impacts, framed around safe drinking water and lake recreation.

The page links to a separate, related NOAA item about a 'moderate' bloom severity outlook for western Lake Erie dated July 2020, but this article's own body text does not explain that seasonal-outlook method.

numbers: 2016 — year CO-OPS began issuing operational Lake Erie HAB forecasts (no uncertainty given)
five-day (5-day) — forecast horizon for Microcystis bloom-movement predictions
daily — update frequency of the satellite-derived Microcystis surface bloom-location estimate
twice-weekly — frequency of (legacy/bulletin-level) forecast analysis and of the optional email forecast notifications
2020 — forecast season / year of the upgrades described in the title
August 2019 — date of the illustrative aerial photo shown in the article (image context, not a forecast data point)
July 2020 — publication month/year of a separately linked, related NOAA item on a 'moderate' bloom outlook (not detailed in this article's body)
06/16/24 — page's own 'last updated' metadata timestamp (site maintenance date, not the article's original publication date)
No accuracy, skill score, RMSE, percent-correct, or other quantitative performance/uncertainty metric is stated anywhere in the fetched text for either the legacy or the 3D-upgraded forecast.
tensions: This is a promotional/descriptive NOAA communications page, not a validated technical study — it documents that a capability changed (3D model added, site redesigned) starting with the 2020 season, but provides no quantitative evidence that forecast accuracy or lead time actually improved. It should not be cited as evidence of measured forecast-skill improvement; any such claim needs a technical/validation source instead. Separately, the page's repeated use of "twice-weekly" (for forecast-analysis bulletins and email notifications) sits alongsi

title: National Lakes Assessment 2022 Key Findings
what: The National Lakes Assessment (NLA) 2022 Key Findings page is EPA's summary of "The Fourth Collaborative Survey of Lakes in the United States" — a statistically-based (probability-sample) survey of lake condition across the conterminous U.S., run by EPA with states, tribes, and other partners as part of the National Aq

claims.c: Half of U.S. lakes are in poor condition for phosphorus and nearly half for nitrogen.
Nearly half of lakes have excessive (poor-rated) chlorophyll a, and 30% are hypereutrophic.
Lakes with elevated phosphorus show a statistical association with degraded benthic invertebrate communities, stronger in natural lakes than reservoirs.
Roughly a quarter to a third of U.S. lakes have degraded biological communities nationally.
Lakeshore disturbance and physical habitat degradation are widespread, and are linked to worse biological condition.
The cyanotoxin microcystin is detected in half of U.S. lakes, though only a small fraction exceed the recreational health criterion.
The cyanotoxin cylindrospermopsin is less prevalent than microcystin and did not exceed its health criterion anywhere sampled in 2022.
Microcystin detection increased sharply since the last survey cycle even though nutrient levels changed little.
The herbicide atrazine is widely detected at low levels, with benchmark exceedance rare but ticking up since 2017.
Mercury and PCB contamination in lake fish tissue is essentially universal nationally.
PFAS ('forever chemicals'), especially PFOS, are nearly ubiquitous in lake fish tissue nationally.
The 2022 cycle is the fourth in EPA's repeating national lake probability-survey series, and this page's trend analysis only goes back to 2017.

numbers: 50% of lakes: poor condition, elevated total phosphorus
47% of lakes: poor condition, elevated total nitrogen
30% of lakes: hypereutrophic conditions
49% of lakes: chlorophyll a rated poor (excess)
1.7x: relative likelihood of poor benthic-macroinvertebrate condition in high-phosphorus lakes, nationally
2.2x: same comparison restricted to natural lakes excluding reservoirs
29% of lakes: poor condition (benthic macroinvertebrates); 26% fair
25% of lakes: poor condition (zooplankton); 25% fair
16% of lakes: good condition, lakeshore disturbance measure; 84% moderate-to-high human activity/shoreline alteration
5% of lakes (an estimated 13,400 lakes): poor drawdown rating
55% of lakes: good shallow-water habitat (down 9 percentage points from 2017)
52% of lakes: good riparian vegetation cover
tensions: (1) Nutrient impairment (phosphorus/nitrogen) and biological indicators showed "little change" nationally between 2017 and 2022, yet microcystin detection rose sharply ("increased by almost 30 percentage points, to 50%") over the same interval while its health-threshold exceedance rate (2%) did not change significantly — the source does not explain this divergence, so it should not be read as evidence that nutrient loading alone tracks cyanotoxin prevalence; other factors (e.g., temperature, weather in the sampled year, analytical sensitivity)

title: National Lakes Assessment 2022: The Fourth Collaborative Survey of Lakes in the United States (interactive webreport)
what: The National Lakes Assessment (NLA) 2022 webreport is EPA's public, interactive summary of the fourth in a series (2007, 2012, 2017, 2022) of nationally representative, probability-based (stratified random) field surveys of lake condition in the conterminous United States. It reports 18 in-situ trophic-state, biologica

claims.c: NLA 2022 uses a probability-based, state-stratified random sampling design so that the 981 sampled lakes can be statistically extrapolated to the full national lake population with a quantified margin of error.
NLA 2022 sampled 981 lakes via 80 field crews, representing a target population of 268,020 U.S. lakes (≥1 hectare), of which about 31% are natural lakes and 69% are reservoirs.

Microcystins (a cyanotoxin) were detectable in an estimated 50% of U.S. lake extent in 2022 — up roughly 30 percentage points from 21% in 2017 — while 2% of lakes (about 5,360 lakes) exceeded EPA's 8 ppb recreational health criterion.
Cylindrospermopsin, the second cyanotoxin NLA tracks, was detected in 12% of lakes in 2022 but did not exceed EPA's 15 ppb recreational criterion in any lake, with no significant change since 2017.

NLA classifies lakes into trophic-state classes using fixed chlorophyll-a thresholds (oligotrophic ≤2 ppb; mesotrophic >2–≤7 ppb; eutrophic >7–≤30 ppb; hypereutrophic >30 ppb), and found 30% of U.S. lakes hypereutrophic and 43% eutrophic in 2022.

As a standalone biological indicator (scored good/fair/poor against ecoregional reference lakes), chlorophyll a was rated poor for 49% of lakes nationally in 2022, versus 31% good and 20% fair — the most degraded rating among the three biological indicators.
Nutrient over-enrichment is the most widespread chemical stressor found: 50% of lakes rated poor for phosphorus and 47% poor for nitrogen in 2022, versus only 37% and 34% rated good, respectively.

The report presents a statistical association (not a demonstrated causal effect) between elevated phosphorus and poor benthic-macroinvertebrate condition: lakes with high phosphorus were 1.7 times (all lakes) to 2.2 times (natural lakes) more likely to be rated poor, and eliminating excess phosphorus is estimated to be associated with a 27–30% reduction in poor-condition prevalence, conditioned on causal, reversibility, and independence assumptions the report itself flags as limitations.

In a fish-tissue sub-study covering 413 of a targeted 636 lakes, mercury was detected in 100% of composite fillet samples, and an estimated 51% of the resulting 'sampled population' of 58,747 lakes exceeded EPA's 300 ppb human-health screening criterion — but the report states these fish-tissue results cannot be extrapolated to the full 268,020-lake national target population.

PFAS ('forever chemicals') were found in 93% of the 413 fish-tissue composite samples analyzed, with PFOS alone estimated present in 95% of the sampled lake population, though 16 of the 40 PFAS compounds tested were not detected in any sample.

NLA's good/fair/poor condition benchmarks are explicitly non-regulatory: the report states they have no legal effect, do not equal individual state water-quality standards, and a 'poor' NLA rating does not necessarily mean a site is legally 'impaired' under the Clean Water Act.

The survey is designed for national/regional statistical estimation, not site-specific management: it explicitly states it does not measure all stressors and has limited usefulness for inferring local lake condition, recommending in-depth local monitoring for restoration or protection decisions at any individual lake.

NLA monitors only two cyanotoxins (microcystins and cylindrospermopsin); the report itself notes other algal/cyanobacterial toxins exist that are not captured by the survey, and advises checking with state, Tribal, or local authorities for lake-specific cyanotoxin information before recreating.

Some indicators shifted substantially between the 2017 and 2022 surveys (e.g., good-condition shallow water habitat fell 9 percentage points, from 64.6% to 55.2%), but the report cautions that change over one survey cycle cannot yet be distinguished from natural variability, and that earlier (2012, 2017) condition estimates were themselves revised due to benchmark and statistical methodology changes.

numbers: 981 lakes sampled (spring/summer 2022) by 80 field crews
268,020 lakes in target population (≥1 hectare, conterminous U.S.): 31% natural lakes (83,560) / 69% reservoirs (184,460)
Margin of error ≈ ±5% at 95% confidence, national scale
Microcystins detected in 50% of lakes (2022) vs 21% (2017); ~30 percentage-point increase; regional range 29%–71%
Microcystins exceeded EPA recreational criterion of 8 ppb in 2% of lakes (~5,360 lakes)
Cylindrospermopsin detected in 12% of lakes (2022); recreational criterion 15 ppb; 0% of lakes exceeded
Trophic-state chlorophyll-a thresholds: oligotrophic ≤2 ppb; mesotrophic >2–≤7 ppb; eutrophic >7–≤30 ppb; hypereutrophic >30 ppb
Trophic state 2022: 30% hypereutrophic, 43% eutrophic, 7% oligotrophic; oligotrophic regional range <1% (Southern Plains) to 39% (Western Mountains)
Chlorophyll a condition 2022: 31% good / 20% fair / 49% poor
Phosphorus condition 2022: 37% good / 50% poor (regional range 9%–66%)
Nitrogen condition 2022: 34% good / 47% poor (Southern Plains: 5% good / 83% poor)
Benthic macroinvertebrates 2022: 42% good / 26% fair / 29% poor / 3% not assessed
tensions: This source is a national in-situ monitoring/status survey, not a remote-sensing product or a predictive/early-warning model — it complements rather than conflicts with satellite-based cyanoHAB detection efforts (e.g., EPA CyAN) by supplying nationally representative, probability-based ground-truth prevalence statistics for cyanotoxins and trophic state, but it reports point-in-time condition/prevalence rather than bloom timing, within-lake spatial extent, or forecasted risk, so it cannot by itself validate a remote-sensing early-warning or for

title: NCCOS Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) Operational Forecasting System
year: Undated on the page itself; the cited processing-methodology reference is dated 2020 (Wynne et al.), and the archive listings span bloom seasons 2021-2024
what: HABOFS (NCCOS Harmful Algal Bloom Operational Forecast System) is a NOAA operational program -- catalogued and archived at NCEI -- that issues regionally-specific HAB nowcasts plus short-term (roughly weekly to twice-weekly) and seasonal forecasts by fusing processed satellite ocean-color imagery with meteorological da

claims.c: HABOFS is a national network of NOAA HAB forecast products spanning current-condition nowcasts, short-term (once/twice-weekly) forecasts, and seasonal forecasts.
The stated purpose of HABOFS is to give advance warning of HABs affecting coastal natural resources, people, communities, and economies.
NCEI currently archives HABOFS output for two specific regions: Lake Erie (bloom seasons 2021-2023) and the Florida & Texas Gulf of Mexico system (2022-2024).
For Lake Erie, the archived products are daily-frequency processed satellite imagery inputs and a daily HAB Bulletin.
For the Gulf of Mexico, the Florida & Texas Respiratory Irritation Forecast runs on a 3-hour model cycle, alongside daily 8-day satellite composite images for each state.
HAB forecast bulletins produced by the system look up to five days into the future.
HABOFS archived imagery/model-output products are retained at NCEI for one year after processing.
Forecasts are issued on a routine, pre-set schedule that is tailored per region and end-user need rather than a single fixed national cadence.

The satellite imagery feeding HABOFS is processed through a separate NCCOS product, the "Harmful Algal Bloom Monitoring System," and archived copies of that imagery reflect whichever processing version was used for the forecast at the time -- not necessarily the most current reprocessing.
The satellite ocean-color processing methodology underlying HABOFS is documented in a specific, citable NOAA technical report with its own DOI.

Beyond the two regions NCEI itself archives, NOAA NCCOS's live operational program runs its own forecasts for three regions (Florida & Texas, Gulf of Maine, Lake Erie) and additionally lists two externally-run partner forecasts (Pacific Northwest via NANOOS, California via SCCOOS).

The forecasting effort targets different HAB-causing organisms by region: Karenia brevis in Florida/eastern and western Gulf waters, cyanobacteria in Lake Erie, Alexandrium fundyense in the Gulf of Maine, Pseudo-nitzschia off the Washington coast, and multiple species along the California coast and in Chesapeake Bay.

Forecasts are produced by fusing multiple observing streams -- satellite imagery, NOAA-station meteorological data, state/university field monitoring data, and bloom-trajectory models -- rather than from satellite data alone.
For Lake Erie specifically, forecast issuance steps up from weekly during non-bloom periods to twice-weekly ("bi-weekly") once a bloom is active.
NOAA's own program page states an explicit resource constraint and an acknowledged shortfall from a true national forecasting capability.
None of the fetched pages (the primary NCEI page or the two linked NCCOS companion pages) publish a quantitative accuracy, skill, or validation statistic for the forecasts.

numbers: Forecast bulletin horizon: "up to five days in the future"
Archive retention: processed imagery/model output "archived within one year"
Florida & Texas Respiratory Irritation Forecast model cycle: "3 hour"
Lake Erie archived seasons at NCEI: 2021, 2022, and 2023
Florida & Texas (Gulf of Mexico) archived seasons at NCEI: 2022, 2023, and 2024
System-wide short-term forecast frequency: "once or twice weekly"
Lake Erie forecast frequency: weekly during non-bloom periods, stepping up to "bi-weekly" (twice weekly) "during an active bloom"
Methodology citation DOI for satellite-imagery processing: https://doi.org/10.25923/606t-m243 (Wynne, Meredith, Stumpf, Briggs & Litaker, 2020)
No quantitative accuracy/skill/validation percentage is stated anywhere in the fetched pages (explicitly noted as absent by two independent extractions)
tensions: This source complicates a satellite-only framing of HAB early warning: NOAA's own operational system explicitly fuses satellite imagery with meteorological data, field/in-situ monitoring, and predictive models rather than issuing forecasts from remote sensing alone -- implying that a model built solely on a satellite signal (e.g., an EPA CyAN-type product) is, by NOAA's own operational design choice, a partial input rather than a full analog of an operational "forecast." It also complicates any assumption that operational HAB forecasting is a m

title: NOAA Global Historical Climatology Network Daily (GHCN-D) — Registry of Open Data on AWS
what: An AWS "Registry of Open Data" catalog entry describing NOAA's Global Historical Climatology Network Daily (GHCN-D) dataset — daily land-station weather/climate observations (temperature, precipitation, snow) distributed as public CSV files in an Amazon S3 bucket under NOAA's Open Data Dissemination (NODD) program. It

claims.c: GHCN-D contains daily observations over global land areas from land-based station measurements, and about two-thirds of those stations are precipitation-only stations.

The dataset is a composite of climate records merged from numerous source networks and subjected to a common suite of quality-assurance reviews (specific QA methodology/metrics are not detailed on this page).
Beyond precipitation, the other meteorological elements the dataset records include daily maximum and minimum temperature, temperature at time of observation, snowfall, and snow depth.
Some station records extend more than 175 years, and the dataset is organized into one CSV file per calendar year covering 1763 through the present.
The dataset's listed Update Frequency is Daily.

The data are released under the Creative Commons CC0-1.0 Public Domain Dedication, with no stated restrictions on use, though NOAA requests attribution be retained on unaltered data and that modified/adapted data not be represented as official NOAA content or imply NOAA endorsement.

The dataset's cloud resource is the public S3 bucket 'noaa-ghcn-pds' (ARN arn:aws:s3:::noaa-ghcn-pds) in AWS region us-east-1, paired with an SNS notification topic (ARN arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123901341784:NewGHCNObject), and is accessible without AWS credentials.

The listing is managed by NOAA, tagged with the keywords agriculture, climate, meteorological, weather (plus an internal 'aws-pds' program tag in the underlying source file), and points to a separate GitHub technical-documentation repository rather than embedding technical schema docs on the landing page itself.

The page lists example downstream uses: AWS tutorials on calculating agricultural growing degree days and on visualizing 200+ years of global temperature (via Amazon Athena/QuickSight and via Apache Spark/BigQuery), plus a cited peer-reviewed publication relating natural/socioeconomic conditions to tick-borne encephalitis case patterns in Russia.

(From the page's own linked 'Documentation,' not the registry landing page itself) The yearly CSV files encode each observation as one station-day row with five core elements — PRCP (precipitation, in tenths of mm), SNOW (snowfall, in mm), SNWD (snow depth, in mm), TMAX (max temperature, in tenths of °C), and TMIN (min temperature, in tenths of °C) — alongside station ID, date, element code, data value, and M-/Q-/S-flag quality-control columns.

No baseline, error bar, accuracy percentage, or uncertainty estimate for any measurement is given anywhere in the fetched text — this is a data-access listing, not a validation/accuracy study

title: NWQP Research on Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) | U.S. Geological Survey
year: 2019 (page's own "Active" status date); linked sub-content and publications referenced range from 2008 to 2021
what: A USGS National Water Quality Program (NWQP) science-topic portal page summarizing the agency's ongoing research portfolio on harmful algal blooms (HABs), especially cyanobacterial HABs (cyanoHABs) — covering health/economic impacts, monitoring networks, laboratory and molecular methods, a predictive model case study,

claims.c: Toxic freshwater cyanobacterial HABs (cyanoHABs) have been implicated in human and animal illness and death in at least 43 U.S. states.
As of August 2016, at least 19 U.S. states had public health advisories in place because of cyanoHABs.
Cyanobacteria that cause HABs occur in large rivers, not only in lakes and reservoirs, based on a USGS study that sampled eleven large river sites in June–September 2017.
Microcystins (a cyanotoxin) were detected in 39 percent of small streams assessed across the southeastern United States, per a linked USGS feature story.

A multiple linear regression model (built using Virtual Beach software v3.0.6) using readily available real-time measurements can predict when cyanotoxin-mixture concentrations at Kabetogama Lake, Voyageurs National Park, will exceed drinking-water guideline levels.
Before the 2021 Kabetogama Lake study, there were reportedly no known models that predicted a cyanotoxin mixture (as opposed to a single toxin).

The USGS real-time water-quality tracking network monitors nitrate using optical sensor technology at more than 140 sites nationwide via the "Tracking Water Quality of the Nation's Rivers and Streams" website.
USGS built a dedicated 2,500-square-foot Algal and Environmental Toxins Research Laboratory in Lawrence, Kansas, constructed in 2019, to support this research.

Cyanobacteria can produce taste-and-odor compounds detectable at parts-per-trillion concentrations and cyanotoxins (e.g., anatoxin-a, microcystin, saxitoxin) that can cause human health effects including allergic reactions, dermatitis, gastroenteritis, and seizures.

HABs arise from a complex combination of physical, chemical, biological, hydrological, and meteorological conditions, and USGS states that many questions remain about their occurrence, environmental triggers for toxicity, and the ability to predict timing, duration, and toxicity.

The program identifies nutrient enrichment from agricultural and urban sources, transported downstream, as a key focus of its HAB research, linked to USGS models of nutrient concentrations and loads used to identify source areas contributing nutrients to downstream water bodies, estuaries, and the Great Lakes.
USGS states that advanced warning of cyanoHABs at management-relevant time scales (hours to days) remains an unmet capability despite scientific advances.

numbers: At least 43 states — states where toxic freshwater cyanobacterial HABs have been implicated in human/animal illness and death (cumulative, undated baseline)
At least 19 states — states with public health advisories due to cyanoHABs, as of August 2016

Eleven (11) — large river sites sampled for cyanotoxin occurrence, June–September 2017 (basis for the 'Cyanotoxin occurrence in large rivers of the United States' study/publication, Jan. 29, 2020)
More than 140 sites nationwide — real-time nitrate monitoring sites using optical sensor technology (via nrtwq.usgs.gov)
2,500 square feet — size of the Algal and Environmental Toxins Research Laboratory (Lawrence, Kansas), constructed 2019
Three (3) recurring bloom monitoring sites at Kabetogama Lake, Voyageurs National Park, Minnesota, with data collected in 2016 and 2017, underlying the cyanotoxin-mixture prediction model
Virtual Beach software version 3.0.6 — modeling tool used to build the multiple linear regression cyanotoxin-mixture model

No stated baselines, error bars, confidence intervals, or uncertainty ranges accompany any of the above figures on this page — they are presented as descriptive/programmatic statistics, not as validated model-performance

title: Recommendations for Cyanobacteria and Cyanotoxin Monitoring in Recreational Waters, Revision 1 (EPA 823-R-19-001)
what: A September 2019 EPA Office of Water guidance document (EPA 823-R-19-001, Revision 1) that lays out a voluntary, non-binding four-step framework — assess vulnerability/prioritize, observe for blooms, monitor for cyanotoxins, follow up — to help recreational-water managers and public health officials decide whether a bl

claims.c: EPA frames the document around a voluntary four-step conceptual monitoring program: (1) assess vulnerability and prioritize waters, (2) observe for blooms throughout the season, (3) monitor for cyanotoxins, (4) follow up.
EPA explicitly states the document's recommendations are not legally binding, do not create legal rights, and do not substitute for Clean Water Act provisions or EPA regulations.
EPA (citing Loftin et al., 2008) characterizes 'extremely high' cyanobacterial bloom cell densities as being above roughly 20,000 to 100,000 cells/mL.

The source associates exposure to elevated cyanobacterial cell densities with dermal effects (skin rash), ear/eye infections, and gastrointestinal distress, stated as an association rather than a described causal mechanism.

Table 1 sets EPA's 2019 recommended recreational-water cyanotoxin criteria at two magnitude thresholds (8 µg/L and 15 µg/L, one for microcystins and one for total cylindrospermopsin), assessed over 10-day periods, with a frequency trigger of more than 3 'excursions' per recreational season recurring across more than one year indicating the water is not supporting recreational use.

WHO (2003) guidance (Table 2), used by several U.S. states for swimming advisories, defines four relative-probability tiers from 'Low' (<20,000 cyanobacteria cells/mL, <10 µg/L chlorophyll a, <10 µg/L microcystin) to 'Very High' (>10,000,000 cells/mL, >5,000 µg/L chlorophyll a, >2,000 µg/L microcystin); the microcystin values in this table were derived from the cell-density values rather than measured independently.
Visual/field observation cannot confirm whether cyanotoxins are present: unsafe toxin levels can occur with no visible bloom, so only laboratory testing of the water confirms cyanotoxin presence.
Cyanotoxin production is not a fixed or predictable property of a bloom — it is highly variable and depends on environmental conditions and which cyanobacterial species is present.

NOAA satellite imagery is already used operationally to help predict blooms in western Lake Erie and the Gulf of Mexico, and the NOAA/EPA/USGS/NASA CyAN partnership aims to build a nationwide early-warning cyanobacteria indicator from satellite data — but the source states this approach is unsuitable for all waterbodies because typical satellite resolution and revisit frequency are limited.

Across the reviewed cyanotoxin detection methods (ELISA, HPLC with MS/UV-PDA, PPIA, LC/MS, qPCR/microarrays, microscopy), the source states no single method is best: chromatographic methods are accurate but congener-limited (may underestimate total risk), PCR-based methods are rapid but may overestimate risk if toxin genes are present without toxin release, and ELISA kits (the most commonly used field method) lack congener specificity and can cross-react.

As an example of risk-based monitoring prioritization, Utah's environmental agency identified 16 'sensitive' water bodies for close swim-season monitoring, selected using designated use, proximity to population, number of recreational users, and history of blooms.

numbers: "Extremely high" cyanobacterial bloom density (Step 1, citing Loftin et al., 2008): >20,000 to 100,000 cells/mL — no error bar given, stated as a typical definitional threshold rather than a measured statistic.

Table 1 (p.4) EPA 2019 recommended recreational-water cyanotoxin magnitude thresholds: 8 µg/L and 15 µg/L, one value for microcystins and one for total cylindrospermopsin. NOTE: the pre-extracted table's column/row layou
Table 1 duration criterion: evaluated within each 10-day assessment period across the recreational season ("1 in 10-day assessment period").

Table 1 frequency/'excursion' criterion: not more than 3 excursions per recreational season; an excursion = a 10-day assessment period whose observed toxin concentration exceeds the criteria magnitude; the water is flagg

Table 2, WHO (2003) 4-tier guidance — cyanobacteria (cells/mL) | chlorophyll-a (µg/L) | microcystin (µg/L): Low <20,000 | <10 | <10; Moderate 20,000–100,000 | 10–50 | 10–20; High >100,000–10,000,000 | 50–5,000 | 20–2,000

Satellite remote-sensing constraints stated by the source (Section 2.2): spatial resolution ~30 m–1 km (suitable only for moderate-to-large lakes); temporal revisit frequency no more than about once per week.
Utah risk-based prioritization example: 16 'sensitive' water bodies selected for close swim-season monitoring (Section 1.2, p.5).
tensions: This source repeatedly cautions that bloom visibility, cell density, and biomass are only an "interim step" or "weight of evidence" input and not a substitute for direct toxin measurement, since toxin production is "highly variable" and can occur (or not) independent of cell density/visible bloom — this complicates any HAB framing (including remote-sensing- or chlorophyll-based early-warning products) that treats a satellite-derived cyanobacteria/chlorophyll index as a direct proxy for toxin risk rather than a screening signal. title: Recommended Human Health Recreational Ambient Water Quality Criteria or Swimming Advisories for Microcystins and Cylindrospermopsin
what: A May 2019 U.S. EPA Office of Water document (EPA 822-R-19-001) that recommends non-binding human-health recreational ambient water quality criteria / swimming-advisory trigger values for two cyanotoxins, microcystins and cylindrospermopsin, for states to use under Clean Water Act Section 304(a) (optionally adoptable a

claims.c: EPA published these recommended microcystins/cylindrospermopsin values under CWA Section 304(a) specifically for states to use as the basis for recreational swimming advisories, with the separate option that states formally adopt the same values as water quality standards under CWA 303(c).
The document explicitly disclaims binding legal force: it is advisory only, not an enforceable standard, unless and until a state independently adopts it.

The recommended values were not derived from new primary toxicology research; they reuse hazard/dose-response conclusions already published in EPA's 2015 Health Effects Support Documents (HESDs) and Drinking Water Health Advisories for microcystins and cylindrospermopsin, combined with EPA's standard 2000 criteria-derivation methodology and recreation-specific exposure parameters from the 2011 Exposure Factors Handbook (EFH).

Hazard identification treats noncancer and cancer endpoints separately, drawing on animal studies of liver effects for microcystins and kidney/liver effects for cylindrospermopsin compiled from the underlying 2015 HESDs.

The exposure assessment goes beyond incidental ingestion, running separate screening-level comparisons of inhalation exposure and dermal absorption exposure against the ingestion-based dose, using distinct parameter/result tables for each route.

The document performs a dedicated lifestage analysis, differentiating exposure factors (incidental ingestion rate, body weight) for children younger than six years old from children six-to-ten and adults, and compares resulting microcystins protective values across these groups.

A prior EPA national field survey (the 2007 National Lakes Assessment) found multiple U.S. states with in-situ waterbody microcystin concentrations exceeding 10 micrograms per liter, cited by this document as background evidence of the toxin's occurrence/magnitude before the recreational criteria were set.

Before this 2019 EPA recommendation, the World Health Organization (2003) and numerous individual U.S. states already maintained their own, non-uniform recreational guidance/action levels for cyanobacteria, chlorophyll-a, and microcystins.

The document treats livestock and pet cyanotoxin poisoning as a concern separate from the human recreational criteria it derives, compiling existing state-issued animal-specific action levels (e.g., California, Oregon, and a Texas county's dog-specific microcystin guidance) rather than deriving its own animal-protective value.

Beyond dissolved/extracellular toxin concentration, the document separately evaluates cyanobacterial whole-cell density/biomass as a potential hazard indicator and reviews its use in existing guidelines, while its own section title signals this as an area of unresolved science.

June 2 to August 1, 2017 — date window for state-reported HAB notices tallied by EPA region (Figure 3-5, p. 39)
Lifestage cutpoints used to differentiate exposure factors: children younger than six years old vs. children six to ten years old vs. adults (Section 7.3, Tables 7-4 through 7-6)
EPA Contract No. EP-C-16-011 — vehicle for technical support by ICF and Bigelow Laboratory for Ocean Sciences (Acknowledgments, p. 4-5)
tensions: This is a recreational-exposure (swimming) criteria document, explicitly distinct from EPA's parallel 2015 Drinking Water Health Advisories for the same two toxins — different exposure routes/durations are assessed, so any downstream use that treats "the EPA microcystin number" as one universal threshold risks conflating recreational and drinking-water contexts that this source itself keeps separate. title: Recommended Human Health Recreational Ambient Water Quality Criteria or Swimming Advisories for Microcystins and Cylindrospermopsin
what: A June 6, 2019 Federal Register Notice of Availability (84 FR 26413, Docket EPA-HQ-OW-2016-0715) in which EPA announces its final CWA section 304(a) recommended human health recreational ambient water quality criteria / swimming advisories for the cyanotoxins microcystins (8 µg/L) and cylindrospermopsin (15 µg/L), for

claims.c: EPA's recommended recreational criteria/swimming-advisory concentrations are 8 µg/L for microcystins and 15 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin, protective of human health under a primary-contact recreational (swimming) exposure scenario.

The 2019 recreational values supplement (do not replace) EPA's 2012 Recreational Water Quality Criteria, and rest on the same toxicological science base as EPA's 2015 10-Day Drinking Water Health Advisories for the same two cyanotoxins.

For use as a water quality standard, EPA recommends assessing the criteria over 10-day, non-rolling assessment periods across a recreation season; if used instead as a swimming advisory, the same values should not be exceeded on any single day.
The exposure basis for the criteria is recreating children, identified as having higher exposure than other age groups.

Between the December 2016 draft and the June 2019 final notice, the recommended values changed primarily because EPA updated the children's incidental-ingestion-rate input to reflect a study published in 2017.

Documented human health effects motivating the criteria include headaches, sore throat, vomiting, nausea, stomach pain, dry cough, diarrhea, blistering around the mouth, pneumonia, and liver/kidney damage from oral/incidental-ingestion exposure.

These CWA section 304(a) recommended criteria, and any swimming advisories derived from them, are explicitly not legally binding regulations; states/tribes may adopt them as-is, modify them for local conditions, or use other scientifically defensible criteria, and are not required to adopt any criteria for these cyanotoxins because the toxins are not on EPA's 126-pollutant priority list.

Criteria issued under CWA section 304(a) authority must reflect the latest scientific knowledge of health/welfare effects but are explicitly not permitted to factor in economic impact or technological feasibility of achieving the recommended concentrations.
EPA states that cyanotoxin exposure risk can exist even when a bloom is not visibly apparent, i.e., visual bloom presence is not a reliable proxy for absence of toxin risk.

The draft version of this document underwent a 90-day public comment period beginning December 16, 2016; the final notice was signed May 22, 2019 and published in the Federal Register June 6, 2019 (84 FR 26413, Docket EPA-HQ-OW-2016-0715, FRL-9994-58-OW).

title: Recreational Water Quality Criteria and Methods
year: 2012 (base RWQC); 2019 (cyanotoxin criteria); page is a rolling EPA index (footer dated Oct 8, 2025 at time of fetch)
what: An EPA Office of Water landing/index page that catalogs the Agency's recommended Recreational Water Quality Criteria (RWQC) and supporting technical/implementation materials — including the 2012 RWQC for fecal-indicator bacteria (E. coli, enterococci) and the 2019 recommended human-health recreational criteria or swimm

claims.c: EPA issued final (2019) recommended human-health recreational ambient water quality criteria or swimming advisories for two cyanotoxins: microcystins and cylindrospermopsin.

The cyanotoxin (and general RWQC) criteria are non-binding federal recommendations: states/territories/Tribes may adopt them into enforceable water quality standards, or instead use the same values only as informal public swimming-advisory triggers.
The current 2012 RWQC replaced EPA's prior (1986) recreational criteria and were developed from updated science, public comment, and external peer review.

EPA is legally required, under BEACH Act amendments to Clean Water Act section 304(a)(9)(B), to review its recreational water quality criteria at least every five years, considering new science and barriers to state adoption.

EPA has developed a Quantitative Microbial Risk Assessment (QMRA)-based technical-support methodology, including open-source Python/R code, to help jurisdictions derive alternative recreational criteria for waters affected predominantly by non-human (e.g., wildlife) fecal sources rather than human sewage.

EPA provides an 'Alternative Methods Calculator Tool' spreadsheet that statistically compares locally collected indicator-bacteria water quality data (via index-of-agreement and R-squared statistics) against EPA's national epidemiology-based relationships, to support development of site-specific criteria.

A 2016 EPA-associated coliphage literature review found coliphages to be comparably good fecal-contamination indicators to E. coli/enterococci, and better indicators of viruses in treated wastewater than bacterial indicators — but coliphage criteria remain under research/development rather than finalized as of this page.

EPA explicitly acknowledges that some waterbodies have conditions differing from those underlying the 2012 RWQC, which the page gives as the rationale for offering alternative/site-specific criteria development pathways rather than a single national threshold.

This webpage functions as a document index/hub rather than a technical report: it names and links the 2012 RWQC, the 2019 cyanotoxin criteria package, alternative-criteria technical support materials, and coliphage research, but does not itself reproduce the specific numeric bacterial or cyanotoxin threshold concentrations, illness-rate statistics, or safety-factor derivations underlying any of them.

numbers: No fecal-indicator bacteria concentration thresholds (e.g., for E. coli or enterococci) appear with units anywhere in the fetched page text.

No microcystins or cylindrospermopsin recreational-criteria concentration values appear with units in the fetched page text; the page only names the criteria documents (EPA 822-R-19-001 / 822-F-19-001 / 822-R-19-002, May

title: Reregistration Eligibility Decision (RED) for Coppers (EPA 738-R-09-304)
what: A May 2009 U.S. EPA Office of Pesticide Programs document that amends the original July/August 2006 Reregistration Eligibility Decision (RED) for copper-containing pesticides, presenting EPA's human-health and ecological risk assessments for coppers (used as fungicides, algaecides, aquatic herbicides, bactericides, mol

claims.c: This 2009 document is a formal amendment to the original Coppers RED (completed July 2006, published August 2006), issued after a public comment period that drew comments from growers, university extension services, registrants, and publicly-owned treatment works.

EPA conducted a qualitative human-health assessment for copper, not a quantitative dose-response assessment, because available data show copper is efficiently regulated by the human body with no identified systemic toxicity.

EPA's screening-level ecological risk assessment found that copper pesticides can pose acute risk to non-target organisms, with aquatic organisms facing the greatest documented risk, attributed to direct water application and to runoff from adjacent treated fields.

This RED's ecological risk assessment covers only agricultural uses of copper; a separate ecological assessment of antimicrobial uses (e.g., pools, spas, fountains, urban runoff/sewer discharge, anti-fouling coatings, wood preservatives) was still pending at the time of this document.

EPA regulates a direct-water 'Algae Control' use of copper products (Appendix A use site) capped at a maximum concentration of 0.4 parts per million (ppm) per application; the 2009 amendment narrows this limit to apply specifically in aquaculture ponds when fish are present.

Because many current copper product labels lack specified application rates or retreatment intervals, EPA's ecological exposure assessment used conservative benchmark rates well above real label rates: the highest single rate actually allowed on labels is 8.0 lbs Cu2+ (metallic copper) per acre (dormant fruit/nut tree application), versus a highest assessed benchmark of 31.8 lbs Cu2+/acre used in the ecological assessment (applications to filberts).

Several copper compounds (copper hydroxide, basic copper sulfate, copper sulfate pentahydrate) had their worker restricted-entry interval (REI) increased from 12 or 24 hours to 48 hours because of acute (not systemic) toxicity — specifically severe eye, dermal, or inhalation irritation potential identified in acute toxicity studies.

For cuprous oxide specifically, a review of more recent acute toxicity studies found its highest toxicity rating to be Category III (lower severity than I/II), leading EPA to reduce its REI from 48 hours to 12 hours.

Despite California water-district/POTW complaints that copper sulfate pentahydrate sewer-root-killer products elevate influent copper and risk Total Maximum Daily Load (TMDL) exceedances — and despite some California and New York counties already banning these products — EPA declined to impose a nationwide ban, citing insufficient information, and deferred to state/local authority instead.

numbers: 46 public comment submissions received on the original 2006 Coppers RED during the Phase 3 comment period (Feb-Apr 2006, per 71 FR 45550-45551)

Algae Control (direct water use, e.g., aquaculture ponds): maximum concentration 0.4 parts per million (ppm) per application; 2009 amendment restricts this cap to aquaculture ponds when fish are present (no change to the
Highest single agricultural application rate on current copper product labels: 8.0 lbs Cu2+ (metallic copper) per acre, for dormant applications to fruit/nut trees

tensions: This RED both authorizes and flags a trade-off directly relevant to copper-based HAB/algae treatment: the same screening-level ecological assessment that supports continued registration of copper products (including a direct-water 'Algae Control' use capped at 0.4 ppm) also concludes that copper poses acute risk to non-target organisms, with 'the greatest risk to aquatic organisms' coming from exactly the direct-water-application pathway that algaecide treatment relies on — i.e., the source's own data complicate any framing of copper algaecide

title: Status and Progress (GLWQA Annex 4 – Lake Erie Nutrients)
what: An EPA web page reporting "Status and Progress" under Annex 4 (Lake Erie Nutrients) of the Great Lakes Water Quality Agreement (GLWQA) — the binational US-Canada framework (the Agreement lists 10 annexes total) — summarizing findings of the joint "Report of the Parties" progress report (released January 2026, covering

claims.c: In 2016, the U.S. and Canada committed under GLWQA Annex 4 to reduce phosphorus loads to Lake Erie by 40%, and this remains the core benchmark against which all subsequent progress on this page is measured.
NOAA's annual Lake Erie HAB assessment has rated bloom severity as "mild" every year since 2020, and bloom-severity targets were specifically achieved in 2018, 2020, and 2023.

Despite program activity, Lake Erie continues to show eutrophic conditions, major tributaries routinely exceed target phosphorus levels, and basin-wide load reductions are difficult to attribute to management because loads and in-lake responses are dominated by year-to-year precipitation/discharge variability.

Spring soluble phosphorus in the Maumee River (Lake Erie's largest phosphorus-contributing tributary) has declined by up to about 10% more than streamflow changes alone would predict, which the source interprets as a genuine reduction in phosphorus sources, while also noting some of the decline reflects drier conditions.
USGS has tracked total phosphorus loads in 24 U.S. Lake Erie tributaries since 2011 (Great Lakes Tributary Water Quality Dashboard) and finds loads have decreased or remained steady in 21 of the 24.

Ohio's H2Ohio Initiative has committed $250 million since 2019, enrolling more than 2,600 farmers on 1.85 million acres across 24 counties -- about half of the 4 million cropland acres in the western Lake Erie watershed as of 2025 -- and in 2024 enrolled farmers kept an estimated 420,000 pounds of phosphorus out of waterways.

USDA NRCS doubled its Lake Erie basin conservation financial assistance between 2018 and 2023, executing more than 3,000 cost-sharing contracts covering over 742,000 cropland acres, with 30% of contracts for nutrient management, 41% for cover crops, and 20% for animal waste management.

The Great Lakes Water Authority has cut its annual total phosphorus loads by over 55%, and Michigan (EGLE) is pursuing a framework to require a 0.5 mg/L total phosphorus effluent limit at all 25 major publicly owned wastewater treatment facilities in the watershed.

Fort Wayne's Deep Tunnel combined-sewer-overflow storage tunnel (5 miles) became operational in 2024, a year ahead of schedule, and is projected to cut combined sewer overflow events from 72 per year to four or fewer per year.

Research and monitoring investment cited includes federal science support of over $15 million (NOAA, USGS, USACE combined) for 2023-2025, GLRI funding of approximately $22 million per year (about 85% to on-the-ground projects, 15% to science) plus $3.7 million in 2025 technical-assistance grants, and Ohio's HABRI program investing $24 million in 115 HAB-related research projects at 15 Ohio universities from 2015-2025.

A USACE demonstration wetland built in 2020 is reducing phosphorus losses by approximately 50%, and 183 wetland projects have been constructed or enhanced overall as part of watershed-based nutrient-reduction strategy.

NOAA's Great Lakes Environmental Research Laboratory deployed a network of real-time microcystin-measuring instruments in 2023-2024 and uses hyperspectral imagery for Lake Erie HAB forecasts, with an expansion of forecasting to predict bloom toxicity planned for 2026.

Outreach and governance structures cited include a "Farmer Advocates" program (2020-2024) of 26 advocates managing engagement across 28,590 acres and reaching more than 11,680 farmers, and a Western Lake Erie Basin (WLEB) Community Advisory Group of 65 state and local members formed in 2023 under the broader WLEB Partnership (active since 2006, with a 20th-anniversary observance planned for 2026).

numbers: 40% — target reduction in phosphorus loads to Lake Erie, US-Canada commitment established 2016 (Annex 4)
"mild" — NOAA-assessed Lake Erie HAB severity rating every year since 2020
2018, 2020, 2023 — years in which bloom-severity targets were achieved (within the 2017-2024 reporting window)
up to 10% — additional spring soluble-phosphorus decline in the Maumee River beyond what streamflow changes alone would explain
24 — number of U.S. Lake Erie tributaries monitored by USGS since 2011 (Tributary Water Quality Dashboard)
21 of 24 — tributaries where total phosphorus loads have decreased or remained steady
$250 million — H2Ohio Initiative funding committed since 2019
2,600+ — farmers enrolled in H2Ohio
1.85 million acres — cropland enrolled in H2Ohio, across 24 counties
~half of 4 million acres — WLEB cropland enrolled in H2Ohio as of 2025
420,000 lbs — phosphorus kept out of waterways by H2Ohio-enrolled farmers in 2024
183 — wetland projects constructed or enhanced
tensions: The page holds an optimistic top-line narrative (HAB severity "mild" since 2020; bloom-severity targets met in 3 of the reported years; large, well-funded programs) in tension with its own conceded caveat that Lake Erie remains eutrophic, tributaries still exceed targets, and basin-wide load/response trends cannot be cleanly attributed to management versus weather (precipitation/discharge) variability. For a HAB literature review, this is a useful corrective against reading a run of "mild" bloom-severity years as proof that phosphorus-reduction

title: Surveillance for Harmful Algal Bloom Events and Associated Human and Animal Illnesses — One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System, United States, 2016–2018
what: A CDC MMWR Surveillance Summary reporting the first three years (2016–2018) of data from the One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System (OHHABS), the first U.S. national public health surveillance system that links reports of harmful algal bloom (HAB) events with associated human and animal illness cases, voluntarily submit

claims.c: OHHABS is a CDC-launched (2016) national, One Health, multisectoral public health surveillance system designed to link HAB event reports with human and animal illness data to inform prevention efforts.
Over the 2016–2018 period, 18 states reported a combined total of 421 HAB events, 389 human illness cases, and 413 animal illness cases to OHHABS.

Most reported HAB events were laboratory/field-confirmed, but the large majority of human illness cases were only "probable," and the large majority of animal illness cases were only "suspected" — fewer than 5% of illness cases (human or animal) were laboratory-confirmed.
HAB events and associated illnesses were heavily concentrated in freshwater bodies and in the warm-weather months (May–October), peaking in July.

Among events where a toxin was identified, microcystins were by far the most frequently detected toxin; other cyanotoxins (anatoxin-a, saxitoxin, cylindrospermopsin, nodularin) and the marine toxin ciguatoxin were detected much less often.

A single large freshwater HAB event — a July bloom at one large lake that spread to connected waterways over more than three months — accounted for over half of all human illness cases reported system-wide during the three-year period.

No human deaths were reported among the 389 human illness cases, whereas 89% of the 413 reported animal cases died, with wildlife (chiefly birds) driving most of that animal mortality, including one large single-event bird die-off.
Very few ill persons or animals received confirmatory clinical/veterinary testing, and time from a single-point exposure to illness onset was generally short (median hours, not days).

The authors state that reported HAB event/illness counts substantially underrepresent the true national burden because participation and surveillance capacity vary across the reporting jurisdictions, and this was still the initial data-collection period.

numbers: 18 states reported data to OHHABS, 2016–2018
421 total HAB events reported (2016–2018)
370 confirmed events (88% of events)
51 suspected events (12% of events)
377 freshwater events (90%)
14 brackish events (3%)
12 salt-water events (3%)
18 unknown-water-source events (4%)
413 events (98%) occurred May–October
115 events (27%) occurred in July, the peak month
166 event reports (39%) noted visible scum
372 events (88%) had lab testing performed
tensions: This is passive, voluntary surveillance from only 18 of 50 U.S. states, explicitly stated by the authors to undercount true incidence — so its absolute counts (421 events; 389 human and 413 animal cases) should not be read as a national HAB-illness rate or compared directly against remote-sensing-based bloom-extent products (e.g., EPA CyAN, which detects far more cyanobacterial blooms across many more water bodies/states than are captured here), since the gap reflects reporting/ascertainment limits, not necessarily true geographic/temporal diff

title: U.S. Action Plan for Lake Erie 2018-2023 (Commitments and Strategy for Phosphorus Reduction)
what: A binational U.S.–Canada policy action plan (finalized February 2018, covering 2018-2023), developed by USEPA's Great Lakes National Program Office with five U.S. states and multiple federal agencies, that lays out phosphorus-load reduction commitments and targets for Lake Erie under Annex 4 (Nutrients) of the 2012 Gre

claims.c: Lake Erie is a drinking-water source for 12 million people in the U.S. and Canada, and harmful/nuisance algal growth has risen sharply over the past decade, linked to high phosphorus delivery from major rivers during spring storms, threatening a $12.9 billion tourism industry.
During the 2011 Lake Erie bloom, measured microcystin concentrations reached 50x the WHO safe body-contact limit and 1,200x the WHO safe drinking-water limit.
In August 2014, more than 500,000 people lost drinking water for three days when algal toxins forced closure of the Toledo, Ohio drinking-water treatment plant.

Lake Erie's Central Basin hypoxic ('dead zone') area has averaged about 4,500 km2 since the early 2000s, with the largest single event of 8,800 km2 in 2012 (the year after the record 2011 bloom); 2015 is cited as the worst bloom year on record as of this report.
In 2012, hypoxic conditions were linked to a large fish kill — tens of thousands of dead fish washing up along roughly a 40 km stretch of Ontario shoreline.

Phosphorus load is unevenly distributed across Lake Erie's three basins: Western 61%, Central 28%, Eastern 11% of the whole-lake annual total-phosphorus load, correlating with different basin depths and algal problems.
The Western Basin, being shallow, warm, and phosphorus-rich (fed by the Detroit and Maumee Rivers), regularly hosts blooms of the toxin-producing cyanobacterium Microcystis aeruginosa.

The Eastern Basin, despite much lower phosphorus levels than the Western/Central basins, supports excessive growth of the filamentous green alga Cladophora on rocky substrate, causing beach fouling, odor, and clogged intakes rather than cyanotoxin risk.

The plan cites at least three different population figures for Lake Erie's human footprint in different contexts: 12 million (binational drinking-water users), 'more than 10 million' (basin residents), and 'over 11 million' (population served, in a cross-Great-Lakes comparison) — figures that should not be treated as interchangeable.

Since the spread of invasive zebra and quagga mussels (established in Lake Erie in the 1990s), nearshore nutrient recycling has increased via the mussels' filtering/excretion, and greater water clarity has let algae grow at greater depths — factors the plan links to increased nearshore nuisance algal growth, alongside wetland/riparian loss and more intense spring storms.

'Dissolved phosphorus' (also called soluble/dissolved reactive phosphorus) is more readily taken up by algae than particulate phosphorus, and its share of Lake Erie's total P load has grown since the resurgence of blooms in the late 1990s.

The plan's strategy targets phosphorus alone and assumes P-focused actions will also reduce nitrogen, yet it cites Gobler et al. (2016) for the finding that phosphorus reduction without nitrogen reduction would not reduce bloom toxicity (blooms would shrink in extent but could remain toxic, possibly longer).
Under the 2012 GLWQA, the U.S. and Canada adopted six binational 'Lake Ecosystem Objectives,' including explicitly capping cyanobacterial toxin levels judged to threaten human or ecosystem health.

Central-basin hypoxia target: a 40% reduction in total phosphorus (TP) entering the western and central basins (vs. a 2008 baseline) to reach an annual central-basin load of 6,000 metric tons, split as a 3,316 metric-ton reduction from the U.S. and 212 metric tons from Canada.

Nearshore algal-species target: a 40% reduction in spring TP and soluble reactive phosphorus (SRP) loads from named priority watersheds (Canada: Thames River, Leamington tributaries; U.S.: Maumee, River Raisin, Portage, Toussaint Creek, Sandusky, and Huron [Ohio] rivers).

Western-basin cyanotoxin target: a 40% reduction in spring (March-July) TP/SRP loads from the Maumee River specifically, equating (vs. the 2008 baseline) to a spring load target of 860 metric tons TP and 186 metric tons SRP.

The plan states the science is unclear whether phosphorus-load reductions from Eastern-basin sources are warranted for Cladophora control, so it defers firm Eastern-basin targets and commits only to precautionary actions and research, with a re-evaluation planned for 2020.

Most of Lake Erie's annual phosphorus load comes from a handful of tributaries (Maumee, Detroit, Sandusky in the U.S.; Thames, Grand in Ontario); nonpoint-source runoff is estimated to supply about 72% of the total annual P load lake-wide, and up to about 89% of the annual TP load in the western-basin's tributaries.

The plan states Maumee River phosphorus loads are 'the single best predictor' of western-basin bloom severity (attributed to its high P concentration), whereas the higher-flow Detroit River (95% Lake Huron-derived flow) has a much lower TP concentration (0.014 mg/L vs. Maumee's 0.42 mg/L, 2011-2013) that the plan says is 'too low to spur an algal bloom' even though its cumulative load still contributes to downstream algal growth and hypoxia.
