$12.9 billion — Lake Erie's tourism industry value cited as threatened by blooms/dead zones (Purpose, p.ii)
5 U.S. states + 1 Canadian province (Ontario) share the Lake Erie basin
>10,000 square miles of farmland in the Lake Erie basin (Background, p.1)
Basin/service population cited inconsistently: '>10 million' (Background, p.1) vs 'over 11 million' (Need to Control Phosphorus, p.4) vs '12 million' drinking-water users (Purpose, p.ii)
2011 bloom microcystin levels: 50x the WHO safe body-contact limit; 1,200x the WHO safe drinking-water limit (p.1)
August 2014 Toledo, OH crisis: >500,000 people without drinking water for 3 days (p.1)
2015 cited as the worst Lake Erie bloom year on record, as of this Feb-2018 report (p.1)
Central Basin hypoxic area: ~4,500 km2 average since the early 2000s; largest single event 8,800 km2 in 2012 (p.1)
2012 fish kill: 'tens of thousands' of dead fish along ~40 km of Ontario shoreline (p.1-2, sentence truncated in excerpt)
Lake-wide annual total phosphorus load share by basin: Western 61%, Central 28%, Eastern 11% (p.3)
Western Basin depth: mean 7.4 m (24 ft), max 19 m (62 ft) (p.3)
tensions: (1) The plan's chosen management lever is phosphorus-only, and it assumes P-focused action will also lower nitrogen, yet in the same section it cites Gobler et al. (2016) for the finding that phosphorus reduction without nitrogen reduction would not reduce bloom toxicity (blooms would shrink in extent but could remain, or become, toxic longer) — a tension the plan surfaces but resolves only by deferring to future nitrogen research, not through its current targets. (2) Evidence strength is uneven across basins: the Western/Central basin hypoxia

title: USDA Legacy Phosphorus Assessment Project
year: Project ongoing (component project duration March 2020-September 2025); flagship synthesis paper published online September 29, 2024, issue dated July-August 2025
what: A multi-year USDA NRCS-ARS interagency research initiative, run under the Conservation Effects Assessment Project (CEAP), that samples soils and stream/ditch sediments across seven U.S. agricultural watersheds (in the Chesapeake Bay, Lake Champlain, Lake Erie, Mississippi River, and Snake River basins) to characterize

claims.c: The project's premise is that phosphorus accumulated in agricultural soils over decades ('legacy P') continues to threaten water quality long after inputs are reduced, framing the need for management focused on existing soil/sediment P stocks rather than only current fertilizer inputs.
Measured stocks of labile (readily exchangeable) phosphorus in surface soils/sediments were large enough, at all seven study sites, to sustain high dissolved P losses for decades without new inputs.

All seven sites contained surface soils capable of sustaining dissolved P losses above a reference high-loss benchmark (the 75th percentile of measured field dissolved-P loss, 0.5 kg P/ha) for decades or longer, and most surface sediments held more than 1 kg P/ha in labile form.
Topsoil (0-5 cm) is enriched in labile P relative to subsoil (5-15 cm) at most sites, and labile P is the most vertically stratified P pool measured, more so than Mehlich-3 P, Olsen P, or total P.

A single across-site statistical model (a 'quantity-intensity-capacity' Generalized Additive Model predicting EPC0, the equilibrium phosphate concentration at net-zero sorption, from labile P and buffering variables) explained the large majority of variation in EPC0 and generalized better than fitting separate models per site.

Sediments from stream/ditch channels differ systematically from adjacent field soils in P sorption behavior (sediments generally showed higher sorption capacity and lower equilibrium phosphate concentration, EPC0, than soils), so the two matrices are not interchangeable when assessing legacy P risk.

Labile-P concentrations measured at these seven agriculturally-influenced sites are well above levels reported for soils without anthropogenic P enrichment, consistent with (but not proof of) legacy buildup from historical agricultural P inputs.

The study's sampling design was not built to formally test the effects of land use or tillage management on P lability, so associations between management history and measured P properties are descriptive and site-specific rather than statistically confirmed drivers.

A related component of the broader effort, an ARS project focused specifically on the Western Lake Erie Basin, uses an edge-of-field dataset from 46 agricultural fields plus laboratory desorption experiments and phosphate isotope tracing to distinguish legacy P from recently-applied P and to inform P-management recommendations for that basin.

The overarching project pairs edge-of-field monitoring (coordinated by NRCS) with in-stream and watershed-outlet (HUC12) monitoring (coordinated by other partners) across the five basins, to track how legacy phosphorus export behaves over time at multiple spatial scales, feeding into improved P-management recommendations.

numbers: 562 soil samples (0-5 cm and 5-15 cm depths) collected across 274 unique locations, spring 2022
62 sediment samples collected across the same 7 watersheds, spring 2022

7 watersheds/research sites spanning 5 major basins: Lake Champlain (VT), Le Sueur River/Mississippi R. basin (MN), Beasley Lake/Mississippi R. basin (MS), Snake River Basin (ID), Western Lake Erie Basin (IN), Mahantango
31 distinct soil series and 6 soil orders represented across sites
Soil EPC0 (equilibrium phosphate concentration at net-zero sorption): median 0.158 mg P L-1, mean 0.521 mg P L-1, range 0.0057-15.6 mg P L-1, IQR 0.0466-0.53 mg P L-1 (n=562)
Sediment EPC0: median 0.0344 mg P L-1, mean 0.0758 mg P L-1, range 0.00778-1.43 mg P L-1, IQR 0.0192-0.0631 mg P L-1 (n=62)
Soil labile P via 24-h anion-exchange-membrane desorption (PAEM): median 65 mg P kg-1, mean 77.8 mg P kg-1, range 4-325 mg P kg-1, IQR 36.4-108 mg P kg-1
Sediment labile P (PAEM): median 32.6 mg P kg-1, mean 43.7 mg P kg-1, range 2.9-288 mg P kg-1, IQR 16.6-53.8 mg P kg-1

Historical literature comparison ranges cited (not this project's own new data, used for context): 43-56 mg P kg-1 labile P (Sharpley et al. 1984); 26-90 mg P kg-1 (Wolf et al. 1985); up to 750 mg P kg-1 in manured soils
Legacy P stock benchmark: 0.5 kg P ha-1 as the 75th percentile of field dissolved-P loss in the MANAGE reference dataset, used as the 'sustained for decades' threshold
Vertical stratification ratios (0-5 cm : 5-15 cm): labile P 1.34-3.05x; Mehlich-3 P 1.15-2.44x (5 of 7 sites); Olsen P 1.14-1.71x (6 of 7 sites); total P 1.11-1.37x
EPC0 prediction model (quantity-intensity-capacity GAM): 92.4% deviance explained; site-specific models penalized by delta-AIC = -235 relative to the pooled model
tensions: This project's central finding -- that legacy (already-accumulated) labile P stocks alone can sustain high dissolved P losses for decades -- complicates any HAB-management narrative that assumes cutting current-year external nutrient inputs (fertilizer, manure) will produce a fast water-quality or bloom response; it implies a lag/hysteresis between load-reduction actions and observed in-lake improvement that should be considered when interpreting other HAB studies that model bloom risk mainly from current-season loading or weather drivers witho

title: Using satellite imagery and national surveys to identify lakes at risk for toxic cyanobacteria blooms in the US
what: A short EPA ORD conference-presentation record (presented at the AWWA Pacific Northwest Section's "Cascade to Coast" Short School, Feb 27–Mar 2, 2023) describing a statistical approach that relates a satellite-derived cyanobacteria index to field-measured toxin/bloom-threshold exceedance, in order to flag which U.S. la

claims.c: For every 0.01 CI_cyano/km² increase in the satellite-derived lake-level Cyanobacteria Index, the odds of a lake exceeding six bloom thresholds increased by 23–54%.

Applied nationally, the models identified 162 lakes at ≥75% probability of exceeding the lower microcystin threshold (0.2 µg/L) and 70 lakes at ≥75% probability of exceeding the higher microcystin threshold (1.0 µg/L).
The analysis covers a national set of roughly 2,192 satellite-monitored U.S. lakes, combining MERIS satellite imagery with the U.S. National Lakes Assessment field-survey program.
The study's design deliberately leverages spatial variation across many lakes using two national-scale data sources, rather than analyzing temporal (within-lake, over-time) variability.

Algal toxins cannot be directly detected through satellite imagery; the study's premise is that monitoring toxins linked to cyanobacterial blooms is critical for assessing risk, motivating a statistical link between the satellite cyanobacteria signal and field-measured toxin data.

Beyond the microcystin-specific counts, the models identified 335 lakes at ≥75% probability of exceeding (an unspecified set of) "lower" thresholds, and 70 lakes for "higher" thresholds, when applied nationally.

This specific record is a conference presentation (not a journal article), presented Feb 27–Mar 2, 2023 at the AWWA Pacific Northwest Section's Cascade to Coast Short School in Albany, OR, by EPA-affiliated authors, and carries no full-text/PDF link.

Six total bloom/toxin thresholds evaluated (per source phrase 'six bloom thresholds'; the parameter breakdown behind this count is not confirmed in the retrieved text)

title: Vertical distribution of buoyant Microcystis blooms in a Lagrangian particle tracking model for short-term forecasts in Lake Erie
what: A peer-reviewed 2016 journal article (J. Geophys. Res.: Oceans) presenting a 3-D Lagrangian particle-tracking model, coupled to the FVCOM hydrodynamic model, that simulates the vertical distribution of buoyant Microcystis (cyanobacteria) colonies in western Lake Erie by balancing measured colony buoyancy against turbul

claims.c: The paper's own 3-D model (adding vertical turbulent mixing plus Microcystis buoyancy to the particle-tracking simulation) produced improved forecast-skill statistics versus a 2-D advection-only version of the same model, and beat a naive persistence forecast through day 6 of a 10-day simulation window, across a set of 26 hindcasts of the 2011 Lake Erie bloom.

Western Lake Erie, the study system, is a large (~3000 km²), shallow (~8 m mean depth) freshwater system in which CHABs occur July-October under intermittent ('polymictic') stratification driven by wind and surface heating/cooling.

The paper frames the water-supply/health stakes of Lake Erie CHABs: the lake is a drinking-water source for about 11 million people, and a 2014 microcystin contamination event caused Toledo to issue a do-not-drink order affecting roughly half a million residents for two days.

Forecasts of Microcystis vertical distribution matter specifically for drinking-water operators because intakes are usually subsurface, so raw-water toxin risk may be higher during vertical-mixing events than when colonies are concentrated at the surface.

A cited pre-existing seasonal statistical forecast (Obenour et al., 2014), based on cumulative March-July Maumee River phosphorus load, is reported to explain more than 90% of interannual variance in lake-wide bloom severity, and is used operationally for season-ahead phosphorus-reduction planning, distributed to over 1600 subscribers.

The source explicitly distinguishes short-term from seasonal CHAB forecasting by mechanism: short-term bloom variability is explained mainly by physical transport processes, whereas the seasonal forecast rests on nutrient-load/biological response statistics.

A cited prior 2-D forecast model (Wynne et al., 2011) more accurately predicted horizontal movement of the bloom centroid (center of mass) than a persistence forecast, over a ten-day simulation of an August 2003 bloom event.

Quantitative forecast-skill assessment from satellite imagery is not always possible: a cited prior case (August 2008) could not be scored against a persistence benchmark because cloud cover and vertical mixing obscured parts of the bloom in subsequent images, preventing an accurate centroid estimate.

A cited prior analysis (Wynne et al., 2010) found wind speed to be a significant predictor of apparent bloom intensity in satellite imagery, and offered supporting evidence for the hypothesis that changes in the vertical mixing depth of buoyant colonies (not changes in true abundance) drive much of the observed surface-concentration variability.

For the random-walk vertical-mixing numerical scheme, the authors selected the Milstein scheme (with linear diffusivity interpolation, cheaper than cubic splines) over the simpler Visser scheme and the more complex Strong-1.5/Platen-two-step schemes, based on formal orders of accuracy: weak order 1 for Visser/Milstein vs. 2 for Strong-1.5/Platen-two-step; strong order 1 for Milstein, 1.5 for Strong-1.5, undefined for Visser/Platen-two-step.

The random-walk time step was adaptively bounded between 0.01 s and the model's 600 s advection time step at every particle and step, based on local diffusivity-profile curvature, and the 0.01 s floor was tracked and found to be rarely needed.

A satellite-derived cyanobacterial index (CI) value of 10⁻³ sr⁻¹ corresponds to approximately 10⁵ cells/mL, cited as the World Health Organization's threshold for significantly increased human-health risk, and (via the paper's own CI-to-chlorophyll regression) to about 23 mg/L chlorophyll, which the model used as its threshold for defining CHAB presence.

The paper's own empirical CI-to-chlorophyll conversion was calibrated on eutrophic Florida lakes (not Lake Erie), with a reported R²≈0.95, standard error of 7.7 mg/L, and calibration range 16-115 mg/L; when checked against satellite-derived CI it gave a 27% relative RMSE (citing Tomlinson et al., 2016).

Satellite CI imagery required a shoreline exclusion buffer (about 1 km near Stony Point/Catawba Island, 1.5 km elsewhere) because of frequent false-positive CI readings near shorelines, attributed to contamination of the water signal by nearby land or by bottom reflectance; no buffer was applied in Maumee Bay because CHABs were frequently genuinely present there.

Microcystis colonies in Lake Erie are described (citing Vanderploeg et al., 2001) as typically larger than 50 micrometers and buoyant; the model's particle buoyant velocities were assigned from the authors' own colony-size distributions (FlowCam imaging 2014; microscopy 2012-2013) combined with directly measured buoyant-velocity-vs-diameter relationships (video microscopy of July 2015 samples from station WE15).

A general, source-stated weakness of the Lagrangian random-walk approach is that particles can artificially accumulate in low-diffusivity regions when vertical diffusivity is spatially nonuniform, if an inappropriate numerical scheme or time step is used — and such artifacts can be mistaken for genuine bloom features.

The simulations and in-situ observations together indicate that subtle thermal structure (typical of a polymictic lake), together with buoyancy, shapes not only the vertical but also the horizontal distribution of Microcystis.

numbers: ~3000 km² surface area (western Lake Erie)
~8 m mean depth (western Lake Erie)
~11 million people served drinking water from Lake Erie (cited, Lake Erie LaMP 2011)
~500,000 Toledo residents affected by a 2014 do-not-drink order, lasting 2 days (cited, Henry 2014)
>90% of interannual bloom-severity variance explained by a cited seasonal phosphorus-load statistical forecast (Obenour et al. 2014; not this paper's own model)
26 hindcast simulations covering the 2011 bloom season (July-October 2011), each a 10-day run
This paper's 3-D model's skill advantage over a persistence baseline is stated only 'through simulation day 6' of the 10-day hindcasts
Advection time step Δt = 600 s (explicit 4th-order Runge-Kutta integration)
Random-walk mixing time step adaptively bounded 0.01 s ≤ dt ≤ 600 s; 0.01 s floor 'rarely applied'
Signal-to-noise goal minimum SN = 5 in the well-mixed artifact test, based on the Rose (1948) criterion for human visual detection
1000 particles used in 1-D well-mixed condition test simulations (using August 2011 Lake Erie diffusivity profiles)
Weak order of numerical accuracy: 1 (Visser, Milstein) vs. 2 (Strong 1.5, Platen two-step)
tensions: Within its own introduction, the source draws a sharp line between two coexisting Lake Erie forecast paradigms that a literature review should not conflate: a seasonal statistical forecast (cumulative spring Maumee River phosphorus load explaining >90% of interannual bloom-severity variance, per cited Obenour et al. 2014) versus the short-term physical-transport forecast this paper builds, explicitly stating that short-term variability is dominated by "physical transport processes" rather than "biological mechanisms." These are different tools

title: Water Quality Data Download
year: 2026 (page "last updated" May 22, 2026; this is a continuously-maintained EPA web page, not a dated publication)
what: EPA's "Water Quality Data Download" page is the agency's central web hub explaining how to obtain in-situ water quality data (physical, chemical, biological, habitat, and metrics/indexes data) contributed by over 1,500 federal, state, and tribal agencies and other organizations — chiefly via the Water Quality Portal (W

claims.c: EPA's Water Quality Data Download page states that water quality data submitted from over 1,500 federal, state and tribal agencies, watershed organizations, and other groups are available to support water quality analyses, spanning physical, chemical, biological, habitat, and metrics/indexes data.
The Water Quality Portal (WQP) is a cooperative service sponsored by USGS, EPA, and the National Water Quality Monitoring Council, and it serves over 430 million water result records.
The WQP aggregates data from multiple underlying source systems, including EPA's Water Quality eXchange (WQX) and the USGS National Water Information System (NWIS), plus other government agencies.

The Legacy Data Center (LDC) contains historical water quality data dating back to the early 20th century through the end of 1998, is no longer being added to, but remains publicly accessible (including via FTP).

The page enumerates downstream tools built on WQP/WQX data, including How's My Waterway (which integrates over 30 separate data sources), TADA, the WQI screening tool, EDM, CyAN, and Freshwater Explorer (covering all 50 U.S. states, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands), plus a Harmful Algal Toxins StoryMap.

title: Water Quality Portal (WQP)
year: N/A — live, continuously updated web portal, not a dated publication; pages were accessed 2026-07-01, and the fetched text itself references a stated USGS-data cutoff of 2024-03-11 for the default (WQX2.2) interface and a stated data-volume snapshot dated December 2021.
what: The Water Quality Portal (WQP) is a free, public web portal and companion web-service API that describes itself as "the premiere source of discrete water-quality data in the United States and beyond." It is a cooperative service sponsored jointly by USGS and EPA that federates two backend U.S. government water-quality

claims.c: WQP describes itself as the leading U.S. source for discrete (i.e., non-continuous, sample-based) water-quality data, run as a cooperative service integrating publicly available water-quality information from multiple source systems rather than being a single agency's in-house database.

WQP is jointly sponsored by USGS and EPA, each of which supplies one of the two backend databases the portal federates — USGS via the National Water Information System (NWIS), EPA via the Water Quality Exchange (WQX) Data Warehouse.

Beyond USGS and EPA, more than 400 additional state, federal, tribal, and local agencies contribute monitoring data that flows into the portal (largely via the EPA/WQX side, which receives submissions from states, tribes, watershed groups, other federal agencies, volunteer groups, and universities).
The USGS NWIS system underlying WQP alone covers more than 1.5 million monitoring sites nationwide (current and historical), indicating the scale of one of the portal's two backends.

As a portal-wide (not NWIS-only) figure, WQP's FAQ states that as of December 2021, results from more than 2.6 million monitoring locations were accessible through the portal — a dated snapshot statistic rather than a live counter.

The two backend systems refresh on different schedules and the default interface has a hard USGS data-currency cutoff: EPA/WQX data refreshes weekly (Thursday evenings), while USGS/NWIS data feeding the default (WQX2.2) interface stopped updating as of March 11, 2024; more recent USGS records require the separate beta interface serving WQX 3.0 profiles.

Site identifiers are not harmonized between the WQX and NWIS source systems, so the same physical monitoring site can appear under different, non-matching IDs across the two backends; the FAQ instructs users to prepend organization identifiers (e.g., "USGS-") when searching to disambiguate.

The FAQ warns that large or broad queries are slow, and specifically that applying the "Filter Results" option is reported to make the portal process roughly 100 times more data than a query restricted to sites alone, so it recommends narrowing by date range first to shrink the result set.

The portal exposes multiple distinct data profiles beyond a simple site list — organization/site data, project data, sample results with both physical/chemical and biological metadata, sampling activity records, biological habitat metrics, and detection/quantitation-limit data — retrievable either through a web UI (CSV, TSV, or Excel 2007+ downloads) or through machine-readable web services (shareable query URLs, cURL commands, WFS GetFeature requests).

EPA-side WQX data is "refreshed once a week on Thursday evening" (WQP FAQ)

Support contact figures given on the homepage: phone 1-800-424-9067, email WQX@epa.gov (administrative, not a data statistic)
tensions: WQP is a data-access/aggregation layer, not a HAB-specific scientific finding, so it makes no empirical claims that could directly contradict other HAB literature. title: Water Quality Portal (WQP) User Guide
year: 2021 (per the guide's own citation format); page content includes notices referencing updates as recent as March 2024
what: The Water Quality Portal (WQP) is a joint USGS/EPA/National Water Quality Monitoring Council web portal and set of web services that provides unified public access to water-quality monitoring data by merging the USGS NWIS database and the EPA WQX database (formerly STORET). This page is its User Guide, documenting the

claims.c: The WQP unifies data from two underlying systems: NWIS (USGS) and WQX (EPA), the latter formerly called STORET.
NWIS (USGS) covers roughly 1.5 million monitoring sites across the US and territories and is refreshed every 24 hours.
WQX (EPA) data is refreshed on a weekly cadence, Thursday evenings.
USGS-sourced records in WQP may not yet have received Director's approval, are labeled provisional/subject to revision, and USGS/the federal government disclaim liability for their use.
Individual USGS results move through a status workflow from 'provisional' to 'accepted' after hydrologist review, but even 'accepted' status does not guarantee the value will never be revised later.

The standard/default WQP interface only serves WQX2.2-format profiles, which do not include USGS data added after March 11, 2024; that newer USGS data is only reachable through a separate beta WQX3.0 interface.
Downloaded result sets that exceed Excel's row limit will be silently truncated when opened, with only the first 1,048,576 rows displayed.
Many monitoring stations outside the continental US lack latitude/longitude referenced to the NAD83 datum, so they cannot be located via the portal's point-location/radial-distance search.
The portal specifies an official citation format, including a DOI, for anyone using WQP-derived data or query results.

title: Chlorophyll algorithms for ocean color sensors - OC4, OC5 & OC6
what: A methods paper by O'Reilly and Werdell (2019, Remote Sensing of Environment) that develops and documents 65 empirical "maximum band ratio" (MBR) chlorophyll-a algorithms - the OC2/OC3/OC4/OC5/OC6 family - for 25 past, current, and planned global open-ocean color satellite instruments, tuned on a shared in-situ chlorop

claims.c: The paper develops 65 empirical ocean-color (OC) maximum band ratio (MBR) chlorophyll algorithms for 25 satellite instruments, tuned on the largest available globally representative in-situ chlorophyll/reflectance database, to support merging and extending chlorophyll time series across overlapping and future missions (PACE, OLCI, HawkEye, EnMAP, SABIA-MAR).
The new 'Version -7' algorithm set shows high mutual (cross-algorithm) consistency: median R2 = 0.859 and median regression slope = 0.985 across 903 pairwise comparisons of OC-modeled chlorophyll.

In direct satellite-to-in-situ matchups, the new algorithms perform about as well as, and sometimes slightly better than, the current operational heritage algorithm (OCI): OC4_SEAWIFS was closer to in-situ chlorophyll than OCI in 51.4% of SeaWiFS matchup pairs, and OC3_MODIS was closer than OCI in 51.6% of MODIS-Aqua matchup pairs.

OC6 is defined using the same numerator bands as OC5 (max band ratio among violet/blue bands over green) but replaces the single green denominator band with the mean of a green and a red band (e.g., Rrs555 and Rrs670 for SeaWiFS), approximating a band near 613 nm.

The OC/MBR approach rests on an assumption that does not always hold: that optically relevant seawater constituents other than phytoplankton - non-algal particles (NAP) and colored dissolved organic material (CDOM) - co-vary with chlorophyll a.

Any MBR algorithm's accuracy is contingent on accurate radiometric calibration and internal consistency across the 3 to 6 bands it uses; early SeaWiFS retrievals of the violet/blue bands were frequently unusable (negative) in phytoplankton-rich continental shelf waters.

CDOM absorption is well known to strongly influence (confound) the Rrs412 retrieval used by OC5/OC6, yet algorithms including Rrs412 still achieved R2 comparable to the algorithm excluding it (OC5=0.836, OC6=0.851, vs OC4=0.851 for SeaWiFS).

The newer, more bio-optically diverse Version -7 training set yields a lower internal R2 for OC4_SEAWIFS (0.851) than earlier algorithm versions (Version-1: 0.932; Version-4: 0.892), which the authors attribute to the broader diversity of the new dataset rather than to a worse-performing algorithm.

At very low chlorophyll concentrations (CHL <= 0.1 mg m-3), the violet/blue single-band ratios (412:555, 443:555) correlate with in-situ chlorophyll better than the standard blue 490:555 ratio, which the authors present as part of the rationale for adding the 412 nm band in OC5/OC6.

The algorithms are explicitly scoped as intended for global open-ocean application, and the authors frame the broader effort as potentially extending the satellite chlorophyll climate data record to roughly fifty years once combined with heritage sensors (CZCS, 1978) and upcoming ones (PACE, 2022).

Estimating remote-sensing reflectance at wavelengths a given sensor does not directly measure (needed to fit some of the 65 algorithms) is undesirable but was unavoidable for this exercise; the log-linear interpolation method used was accurate to only about 1-8%.

Median regression slope = 0.985 and median R2 = 0.859 across 903 pairwise inter-algorithm comparisons (Version -7 algorithms)
Training/tuning dataset: 2,720 paired in-situ chlorophyll-a and Rrs(lambda) observations (1,341 HPLC-derived + 1,379 fluorometric), CHL range 0.012-77.9 mg m-3
SeaWiFS satellite-to-in-situ matchups: n = 2,278; OC4_SEAWIFS closer to in-situ CHL than heritage OCI in 51.4% of pairs
MODIS-Aqua satellite-to-in-situ matchups: n = 955; OC3_MODIS closer to in-situ CHL than heritage OCI in 51.6% of pairs
OC4_SEAWIFS R2 across algorithm versions: Version-1 = 0.932; Version-4 = 0.892; Version-7 = 0.851
SeaWiFS R2 (Version-7): OC4 = 0.851; OC5 = 0.836; OC6 = 0.851
Oligotrophic-water SeaWiFS matchups (CHL <= 0.2 mg m-3), n = 439: MAE range OCI 58%, OC4 60%, OC5 64%, OC6 67%; bias range 28% (OC5) to 45% (OCI)
Productive-water SeaWiFS matchups (CHL > 0.2 mg m-3) bias: OCI 4.4%, OC4 2.3%, OC5 2.2%, OC6 1%
Version-7 improved bias relative to prior version for >97% of SeaWiFS pairs and >99% of MODIS pairs

MBR dynamic range at CHL = 0.1 mg m-3: OC4_SEAWIFS ~ 5.0; OC5_SEAWIFS ~ 5.9; OC6_SEAWIFS ~ 10.6

title: CyAN File Search / Cyanobacteria Assessment Network
year: Not stated on page
what: CyAN File Search is a NASA Earthdata-catalogued web tool, co-developed by NASA's Ocean Biology DAAC (OB.DAAC), that provides search, discovery, analysis, and visualization access to the multi-agency (NASA/NOAA/USGS/EPA) Cyanobacteria Assessment Network's satellite-derived cyanobacteria-index (CI_cyano) bloom data produ

claims.c: CyAN is a multi-agency initiative among NASA, NOAA, USGS, and EPA to support environmental management and public use of U.S. lakes and estuaries by detecting and quantifying cyanobacteria algal blooms.

The CyAN project generates satellite remote-sensing products using a 'cyanobacteria index (CI)' algorithm to estimate cyanobacteria concentration (CI_cyano) in lakes across the contiguous United States and Alaska.
The CyAN File Search tool itself was co-developed by NASA's Ocean Biology Distributed Active Archive Center (OB.DAAC).
NASA Earthdata classifies the tool under three functional types: Analysis, Search and Discovery, and Visualization.
Reading the tool's description page requires no account, but downloading the underlying satellite data and using full tool functionality requires an Earthdata Login.
NASA Earthdata lists an ARSET training, 'Monitoring Water Quality of Inland Lakes using Remote Sensing,' as a learning resource for this tool, held July 18-25, 2023.
NASA Earthdata also lists an ARSET training, 'Introduction to Remote Sensing of Harmful Algal Blooms,' held Sept. 5-26, 2017.

The tool's actual search interface lets users query by region (Alaska or CONUS), by date range, and by weekly-vs-daily cadence, returning Cyanobacteria Index (weekly or daily) or True Color (daily only) products as GeoTIFF files at 300 m or 1.2 km resolution.
Historical data through the tool is available from 2002 to the present, retrievable via wget, cURL, or Python scripts, with the page noting reported command errors specifically on Windows machines.

As a gap rather than a c

title: Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN) | NASA Earthdata
year: 2025 (current data version 6.0; release notes referenced by filename as Aug 2025 — the page does not give an explicit page-publication date or project-founding year)
what: A NASA Earthdata project/documentation page describing the Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN) — a multi-agency (EPA, USGS, NOAA) effort that produces the satellite-derived Cyanobacteria Index (CI) data product (from MERIS and Sentinel-3 OLCI ocean-color imagery) to detect and quantify cyanobacteria blooms in U.S.

claims.c: CyAN is a multi-agency project (EPA, USGS, NOAA) providing satellite-based detection and quantification of cyanobacteria algal blooms in U.S. lakes and estuaries.

The Cyanobacteria Index (CI) data product is built from three sequential/overlapping ESA ocean-color sensor records — MERIS (2002-2012), OLCI on Sentinel-3A (2016-present), and OLCI on Sentinel-3B (2018-present) — and is issued as daily GeoTIFFs plus 7-day maximum-value composites.
The CI product has a 300 m sensor spatial resolution, with a 50 m land mask for CONUS versus a coarser 500 m land mask for Alaska.
MERIS senses across a 390 nm to 1040 nm spectral range.
Raw pixel Digital Numbers (DN) are converted to a cyanobacteria cell-density estimate via a specified log-linear formula, with valid outputs spanning roughly 10,000 to 7,000,000 cells/mL.

The DN scale reserves specific values for non-quantitative conditions: 0 = below the CI detection limit, 1-253 = valid data, 254 = land, and 255 = no data (e.g., cloud cover) — i.e., the product cannot quantify cyanobacteria under clouds, over land, or below its detection floor on any given pixel/day.
The current CyAN CI dataset is processed to Version 6.0, produced by the NASA Ocean Biology Processing Group (OBPG) and citable via a specific DOI.
Data quality/coverage is explicitly tied to satellite availability: 'best coverage' is only available since 2018, once both Sentinel-3A and Sentinel-3B OLCI sensors were operating together.
CONUS CI data are organized into a discrete column/row tile grid rather than one continuous national raster (Alaska has its own separate tile map).

The page itself provides no inline validation/accuracy statistics and no stated count of monitored lakes or reservoirs; it explicitly defers 'interpretation, limitations and known issues' to a separate Version 6 release-notes document that is linked but not reproduced on this page.

Multiple software tools support working with CyAN CI data: a CyAN File Search API, an EPA CyAN mobile app (via Google Play), SeaDAS, and RS_Toolbox for ArcGIS, with RS_Toolbox versioned separately for ArcGIS 10 (v2.3.1) and ArcGIS Pro (v3.1.4).

numbers: 300 m — sensor spatial resolution of the CyAN Cyanobacteria Index (CI) product
50 m — land mask resolution used for CONUS imagery
500 m — land mask resolution used for Alaska imagery (page describes it as 'less refined' than the CONUS mask)
2002–2012 — MERIS sensor period contributing to the CI record
2016–present — OLCI on Sentinel-3A period
2018–present — OLCI on Sentinel-3B period; page states 'best coverage since 2018' once both Sentinel-3 satellites were contributing
390 nm to 1040 nm — MERIS spectral sensing range stated on the page
DN 0 = below CI detection threshold (grey); DN 1–253 = valid data; DN 254 = land (brown); DN 255 = no data, e.g. a cloudy pixel (black)
CI_cyano = 10^(DN × 0.011714 − 4.1870866) — stated DN-to-cyanobacteria-index conversion formula
~10,000 to 7,000,000 cells/mL — stated valid output range of CI_cyano
Version 6.0 — current CyAN CI data processing version; DOI 10.5067/MERGED-S3/OLCI/L3M/CYAN/CI/6.0
RS_Toolbox v2.3.1 — stated as compatible with ArcGIS 10

However, its own stated constraints imply a general remote-sensing-vs-in-situ tension relevant to this project's satellite+in-situ fusion approach: a 300 m sensor pixel size, a non-zero CI detection floor (DN=0), and complete signal loss under cloud cover (DN=255) or over land (DN=254) mean the CyAN CI product cannot resolve blooms in water bodies or nearshore areas smaller than its footprint, cannot quanti

title: dataretrieval.nwis reference (Python)
what: This is the API reference page for the `dataretrieval.nwis` module of USGS's `dataretrieval` Python package — a set of wrapper functions for programmatically downloading hydrologic and water-quality data (instantaneous values, daily values, site metadata, discharge peaks, rating tables, statistics) from the USGS Nation

claims.c: The dataretrieval.nwis module provides Python functions for downloading data from the USGS National Water Information System (NWIS) web services.
get_iv() retrieves instantaneous-values time series for one or more USGS sites over a start/end date range (YYYY-MM-DD), returning a DataFrame plus an NWIS_Metadata object.
For get_iv(), get_dv(), and get_record(), if no start or end date is supplied, the service returns only the single most recent record rather than a full history.

get_ratings() retrieves the rating table (stage-discharge relationship) for an active USGS streamgage and supports three table formats via the file_type parameter: 'base' (default), 'corr', or 'exsa'.

get_stats() supports statReportType values of 'daily' (default), 'monthly', or 'annual', and statTypeCd values of 'all', 'mean', 'max', 'min', 'median', per USGS's water-services statistics documentation.

get_info() site searches via bounding box (bBox) are constrained so that the product of the latitude range and longitude range cannot exceed 25 degrees, and the function requires at least one major search parameter.

Geographic filters for get_info()/what_sites() include stateCd (a 2-digit state code, limited to one per request) and huc (hydrologic unit codes: one major 2-digit HUC or up to ten minor HUCs), plus countyCd as a 5-digit FIPS-based code.

get_record() is a universal wrapper across NWIS service types, dispatched by a `service` parameter (default 'iv'), with 'iv', 'dv', 'site', 'peaks', 'ratings', and 'stat' currently functional, while 'measurements', 'gwlevels', 'pmcodes', and 'water_use' are marked defunct even within this universal function.

Five legacy retrieval functions — get_discharge_measurements(), get_gwlevels(), get_pmcodes(), get_qwdata(), and get_water_use() — are now defunct and raise errors pointing to replacement functions, mostly in a separate `waterdata` module.

The legacy NWIS water-use web service was retired outright (not just the client-side wrapper), with modeled water-use estimates now served through a different USGS system, the National Water Availability Assessment Data Companion (NWDC).
The documented package build is an in-development, pre-release version identifier rather than a fixed numbered release.

numbers: Date parameters use ISO-8601 format: YYYY-MM-DD
Bounding box (bBox) constraint: product of latitude range × longitude range ≤ 25 degrees
stateCd: 2-digit U.S. postal state code; only 1 per request
huc: 1 major HUC (2 digits) or up to 10 minor HUCs per request
countyCd: 5-digit county code (FIPS State Code + county)
period keyword example: 'P10W' (ISO-8601 duration = 10 weeks)
modifiedSince/query_waterservices duration examples: 'P1D' (1 day), 'P1Y' (1 year)
get_ratings() file_type options: 'base' (default), 'corr', 'exsa'
get_ratings() site parameter: USGS site number 'typically 8 digits'
get_stats() statReportType options: 'daily' (default), 'monthly', 'annual'
get_stats() statTypeCd options: 'all', 'mean', 'max', 'min', 'median'
get_dv() example uses statCd='00003' (mean statistic code)

title: Daymet: Daily Surface Weather and Climatological Summaries
year: 2021 (Thornton et al. methods paper in Scientific Data); Climate Data Guide entry stated as "Last modified 2025-12-11"
what: Daymet is a gridded daily surface-weather and climatology dataset produced by Oak Ridge National Laboratory that statistically interpolates/extrapolates ground-based weather-station observations onto a continuous 1 km x 1 km grid over continental North America, Hawaii, and Puerto Rico. This source itself is not a HAB s

claims.c: Daymet provides long-term, continuous, gridded daily weather and climatology estimates by interpolating and extrapolating ground-based station observations through statistical modeling.
The dataset's spatial resolution is a 1 km x 1 km grid.
Coverage spans continental North America and Hawaii from 1980 onward and Puerto Rico from 1950, with the guide describing data as current through July 2023.
Daymet outputs seven primary variables: daily minimum and maximum temperature, precipitation, vapor pressure, shortwave radiation, snow water equivalent, and day length.

The Daymet V4 algorithm uses three-dimensional spatial regressions with objective weighting to capture vertical and horizontal climate gradients, and sizes its station search radius to capture the average number of input stations based on pre-calculated station-distance arrays.
Daymet's ground-station input data is NCEI's Global Historical Climatology Network-daily (GHCNd) dataset.

Shortwave radiation, vapor pressure, and snow water equivalent are not independently observed but are derived from the primary temperature and precipitation fields via theoretical and empirical relationships; day length is computed from location and time of year.
The published Daymet datasets include, as a standard component, detailed and rigorous cross-validation-based error reporting for temperature and precipitation.

A stated key strength is that the combination of high (1 km) spatial resolution, seven variables, and continuous updates since 1980 is an unusual/unique combination of attributes among gridded weather products.
A stated key limitation is that Daymet's empirical humidity-estimation relationships produce climate-zone-related biases in arid environments.
A stated key limitation is that station network density cannot resolve patchy daily precipitation patterns at high latitudes.
A stated key limitation is that Daymet does not include all variables needed to drive full land-surface models, with the page pointing to NLDAS as a comparison product for that purpose.

Cross-validation error is regionally uneven: it is lowest in the densely-instrumented eastern US, and the central-US Rocky Mountains show relatively low error compared with the more sparsely-instrumented mountain regions of Canada and Alaska.
The dataset's algorithm and uncertainty-quantification approach are documented in a 2021 peer-reviewed paper by Thornton et al. in Scientific Data, volume 8.

Daymet Version 4 R1 data are distributed as multiple separately-DOI'd products: daily surface weather data, annual climate summaries, monthly climate summaries, station-level inputs and cross-validation data, and a monthly-latency daily product.

title: Edge-of-Field Water Quality Monitoring - Data Collection and Evaluation (NRCS CEMA 201, FY23 edition)
year: 2022 (FY23 edition; page-1 header/footer text indexed as "October 2022"; FY22 and FY24 editions of the same CEMA 201 standard also exist)
what: A USDA NRCS Conservation Evaluation and Monitoring Activity (CEMA) technical standard — CEMA 201, "Edge-of-Field Water Quality Monitoring: Data Collection and Evaluation" — that specifies acceptable study designs, sampling methods, required water-quality/flow parameters, and Qualified-Individual (QI) personnel qualific

claims.c: CEMA 201 ("Data Collection and Evaluation") and CEMA 202 ("System Installation") are the two paired NRCS standards that together govern edge-of-field water quality monitoring.
Under CEMA 201, edge-of-field monitoring may be conducted by one of two permitted data-collection modes: continuous monitoring, or discrete (grab) sampling.

CEMA 201 requires water samples to be analyzed for a nine-parameter panel: nitrate-N, nitrite-N, total phosphorus, total Kjeldahl nitrogen, orthophosphate-P, total suspended solids, pH, total dissolved solids, and specific conductance.

To be scientifically defensible, monitoring under CEMA 201 must use either a paired-field design (a control field and a treatment field matched on soil, slope, vegetation, hydrology, initial management, and weather) or an above-and-below-treatment design.

A Qualified Individual (QI) must oversee CEMA 201 activities; the minimum qualifying experience is having successfully managed at least two prior edge-of-field water-quality monitoring projects (covering flow, precipitation, and water-quality sample data collection, including O&M of the monitoring system); QIs self-certify against the standard's criteria, and NRCS's National Water Quality and Quantity Team (NWQQT) maintains the roster of QIs.

The monitoring plan/report must state whether flow and constituent measurements are grab samples or continuous, and, if grab sampling is used, must specify how many samples were collected per year and over how many years of record.

The standard frames edge-of-field monitoring's purpose as measuring field runoff to determine sediment and nutrient losses and to compare the performance of different conservation systems, describing edge-of-field collection of runoff water as the most direct way to evaluate BMP effectiveness on sediment/nutrient runoff.
The FY23 edition of CEMA 201 is dated October 2022.

2 — number of NRCS-permitted edge-of-field data-collection modes (continuous monitoring; discrete/grab sampling)
October 2022 — publication/revision date of the FY23 edition (per page-1 document header fragment and the '2022-10' URL path segment)

title: EPA Drinking Water Health Advisory Documents for Cyanobacterial Toxins
what: An EPA Office of Water webpage/document set that hosts the agency's non-enforceable federal Health Advisories (HAs) for two cyanobacterial toxins — microcystins and cylindrospermopsin — issued in 2015 to give states and drinking-water utilities numeric, age-stratified concentration guidance below which adverse health e

claims.c: EPA's 10-day drinking water Health Advisory for microcystins is 0.3 µg/L for bottle-fed infants and pre-school children (≤6 years) and 1.6 µg/L for school-age children through adults.
EPA's 10-day drinking water Health Advisory for cylindrospermopsin is 0.7 µg/L for bottle-fed infants and pre-school children (≤6 years) and 3.0 µg/L for school-age children through adults.
Both sets of advisory concentrations are explicitly tied to a 10-day exposure duration, i.e., they are short-term/acute guidance values, not chronic or lifetime health-based values.
EPA explicitly frames these Health Advisories as non-enforceable technical guidance, not a legally binding federal drinking-water regulation or standard.

Of the three cyanotoxins for which EPA produced a Health Effects Support Document (anatoxin-a, cylindrospermopsin, microcystins), only two (cylindrospermopsin and microcystins) received a quantitative numeric drinking-water Health Advisory; no numeric HA value for anatoxin-a appears anywhere in the fetched material.

The Health Effects Support Documents (HESDs) underlying the advisories are literature-review compilations — covering chemical/physical properties, toxin synthesis and environmental fate, occurrence and exposure, and health effects — rather than new original experiments conducted by EPA.

EPA characterizes the Health Advisory values as provisional/revisable rather than fixed, stating they may change as new science emerges; the underlying documents nonetheless remain dated 2015 in the fetched material, with no revision evident.
The federal Health Advisory does not preempt states, which may set their own, potentially different, standards or guidelines for cyanotoxins in drinking water.

title: Evaluation of Passive Samplers for Cyanotoxin Detection by Immunoassay and Chromatographic-Mass Spectrometry (USGS Scientific Investigations Report 2025-5046)
what: A 2019 USGS/NYSDEC field study (SIR 2025-5046) that deployed SPATT (solid phase adsorption toxin tracking) passive samplers in three New York Finger Lakes (Seneca, Owasco, Skaneateles) to test their ability to detect four cyanotoxin classes (microcystins, cylindrospermopsins, anatoxins, saxitoxins), comparing results f

claims.c: 110 SPATT passive samplers were deployed across the three lakes for 5-22 days (mean 14 days), and about 86 percent (95 samplers) were recovered intact.
For microcystins, ELISA and LC-MS/MS each detected the toxin in 100 percent of analyzed samples (71 of 71), while LC-MS detected it in only 63 percent of samples (27 of 43).

On the same SPATT-extract samples, mean measured microcystin concentration differed sharply by method - ELISA (11,925.8 micrograms/liter) read roughly 8x higher than LC-MS (1,468.7 micrograms/liter) and about 2x higher than LC-MS/MS (5,069.1 micrograms/liter).
Despite the large mean differences, the three analytical methods correlated strongly for microcystins: ELISA vs. LC-MS r=0.90, ELISA vs. LC-MS/MS r=0.95, and LC-MS vs. LC-MS/MS r=0.95.

The chemical preservative required to make samples ELISA-compatible biased the mass-spectrometry concentration readings upward: preserved samples averaged about 35 percent (LC-MS) and 47 percent (LC-MS/MS) higher than unpreserved samples for microcystins, and about 31 percent higher for anatoxins.

For anatoxins, ELISA detected the toxin in 100 percent of samples (95/95) versus 32 percent for LC-MS/MS (26/81); the two methods still correlated at r=0.90, but the report cautions this correlation rests on a small, skewed sample set.

SPATT extracts registered many cyanotoxin detections absent from paired discrete (grab) water samples: about 40 percent of ELISA-analyzed and about 70 percent of LC-MS-analyzed SPATT samples showed microcystin detections not seen in corresponding discrete samples, and 80 percent of SPATT samples were ELISA-positive for anatoxins while no discrete sample exceeded the anatoxin minimum reporting level.

ELISA registered low-level cylindrospermopsin (below 2.0 micrograms/liter) and saxitoxin (below 0.40 micrograms/liter) readings across samples, but none of the cylindrospermopsin signals were confirmed by LC-MS/MS (and saxitoxin had no LC-MS/MS confirmatory testing at all), with no cylindrospermopsin-synthetase genes found molecularly - the report attributes these ELISA signals to likely false positives from dissolved organic matter / cross-reactivity rather than true toxin presence.

The report states that SPATT-extract concentrations cannot be readily converted into ambient in-lake toxin concentrations, because of variable water flow past the sampler and changing toxin concentration over the multi-day deployment window.

Quality-assurance blanks indicated possible method-level errors: a blank LC-MS/MS run produced a microcystin false positive of about 3.0 micrograms/liter attributed to instrument noise, and blank SPATT samples showed a detectable cylindrospermopsin signal (about 0.05 micrograms/liter), which the report says points to an error somewhere in the analytical method.

tensions: This is a methods/measurement-agreement study, not a bloom-driver or predictive study, so its correlations (r=0.90-0.95 between ELISA and mass spectrometry) describe agreement between two assay techniques on the same physical samples, not a causal or environmental driver-outcome relationship - it should not be read as evidence about what drives toxin production. Its most relevant tension for a broader HAB review is a data-quality caution: ELISA, the cheapest and most widely used cyanotoxin immunoassay in field/monitoring programs, is shown here

title: Evaluation of sensors for continuous monitoring of harmful algal blooms in the Finger Lakes region, New York, 2019 and 2020
what: A USGS Scientific Investigations Report (SIR 2024-5010, posted March 26, 2024), produced in cooperation with the New York State Department of Environmental Conservation, documenting a pilot deployment of high-frequency in-situ sensor platforms (multiparameter sondes, nutrient optical sensors, fluorometers, weather/ligh

claims.c: The continuous monitoring sensors, deployed on open-water platforms at three Finger Lakes, performed reasonably well overall in 2019-2020 despite operational challenges (power issues, biofouling), but statistical agreement with laboratory reference samples varied substantially by lake and by parameter.
Multivariate (stepwise) regression models built from multiple sensor parameters did not explain more variance in chlorophyll-a or biovolume than simple univariate fluorescence-based models.

Sensor-measured chlorophyll fluorescence showed only moderate correlation with laboratory-measured chlorophyll-a, with correlation strength differing by lake (strongest at Seneca, weaker at Owasco and Skaneateles), and was weaker still against total phytoplankton biovolume.

Phycocyanin fluorescence from the dual-channel sonde fluorometers was not significantly related to cyanobacterial biovolume, and turbidity was a more significant indicator of cyanobacterial biovolume variability than phycocyanin.

The fluorescent dissolved organic matter (fDOM) sensor had a weak (and in several cases negative-direction) relationship with laboratory-measured dissolved organic carbon (DOC) at all three study lakes.

Nitrate sensors showed a strong overall monotonic/linear relationship with laboratory nitrate values across lakes combined, but the Seneca Lake sensor underestimated concentrations relative to lab samples (lower R^2, narrower sensor range than the lab range), while the Owasco Lake sensor showed better agreement.

Orthophosphate sensor performance could not be meaningfully evaluated in this study because the overwhelming majority of discrete laboratory samples fell below the analytical detection or reporting limits, leaving almost no paired data for comparison.
Among all sensor-measured parameters, water temperature was one of the strongest correlates of chlorophyll-a, total phytoplankton biovolume, and cyanobacterial biovolume.

The report attributes the limited explanatory power of its chlorophyll-a/biovolume models partly to the absence of cyanoHAB events at the specific open-water monitoring locations during the study, and separately cautions that Finger Lakes cyanoHABs are often spatially isolated, ephemeral, or heterogeneous, such that fixed-point sensor platforms and discrete grab sampling may not reliably capture elevated bloom concentrations occurring elsewhere in a lake.

The stepwise multivariate regression analysis was constrained by substantial missing sensor data, using only 66 of 147 possible paired observations, illustrating how incomplete concurrent sensor records limit model-building even when many instruments are deployed.

Laboratory replicate quality-control samples for chlorophyll-a showed a median relative percent difference (RPD) of 7.9% (within the study's 20% acceptance threshold), but 3 of 13 replicate pairs exceeded 20% RPD (23.7-29.5%), indicating the lab 'ground truth' itself carries measurable sampling/analytical noise against which sensors were judged.

Data loss was substantial in 2019 due to daisy-chained wiring among multiparameter sondes, a problem the study resolved in 2020 by giving each sonde independent cabling; extended 2019 deployment intervals (4-8 weeks between visits) also caused heavy biofouling (dreissenid mussels) that was reduced in 2020 by increasing site-visit frequency to every two weeks.

The report recommends that future CyanoHAB monitoring integrate machine learning to exploit the large multi-sensor datasets these platforms generate, but states that broader data collection capturing more actual cyanoHAB events is needed before such models can be adequately refined.

title: Field and Laboratory Guide to Freshwater Cyanobacteria Harmful Algal Blooms for Native American and Alaska Native Communities (USGS Open-File Report 2015-1164)
what: A 2015 USGS Open-File Report (2015-1164, DOI 10.3133/ofr20151164) by Rosen and St. Amand that serves as a field-and-laboratory identification guide to freshwater cyanobacteria harmful algal blooms, developed specifically to help Native American and Alaska Native subsistence-fishing communities recognize and distinguish

claims.c: The guide's stated rationale is that Native American and Alaska Native communities relying on subsistence fishing face an increased risk of cyanotoxin exposure, motivating a visual field/lab identification guide to distinguish toxic cyanobacterial blooms from non-toxic blooms.

Cyanobacteria are described as producing six major recognized groups of toxins — microcystins, saxitoxins, anatoxins, cylindrospermopsins, nodularins, and dermatoxins — each typically representing a family of structurally related derivative compounds.

Because a field sample is usually a mixture of organisms, attributing a detected toxin to one specific cyanobacteria species is inherently uncertain; the only reliable attribution method (isolating and culturing a single organism) is itself described as difficult and logistically burdensome.
Cyanobacteria are reported (via cited literature) to generally proliferate at water temperatures above roughly 25°C and to be more tolerant of these warm conditions than competing green algae.

Warmer surface waters are described as promoting vertical stratification, which is said to favor buoyancy-regulating cyanobacteria over other (eukaryotic) algae; the report frames global warming as a possible contributor to increased cyanobacterial bloom growth.

An algal bloom is described as forming under a specific combination of environmental conditions — nutrient abundance, water-column stability, ample light, and optimal temperature — with cyanobacteria identified as the algae type posing the greatest HAB problem.

Most bloom-forming cyanobacteria can actively regulate their buoyancy to position themselves in the water column and float to the surface, and wind can further concentrate phytoplankton accumulations along downwind shorelines.

Visual bloom coloration alone is explicitly stated to not indicate which cyanobacteria genus is present; the guide recommends repeated sampling plus microscopic confirmation to improve field identification confidence.
The presence or absence of taste-and-odor compounds (also produced by many cyanobacteria) is explicitly stated to not be predictive of whether algal toxins are present.

Human cyanotoxin exposure through consumption of fish and shellfish is described as poorly documented, resting on only a handful of studies measuring toxin concentrations in specific food organisms (e.g., microcystins in shrimp/catfish/carp, nodularins in mullet, saxitoxins in freshwater mussel).

Bloom coloration observed in the field is tied to specific pigments: phycocyanin (blue) can turn water/shorelines turquoise or chartreuse when released from ruptured cells, while phycoerythrin (red) is associated with brown, red, or pink bloom coloration.

The guide's Table 2 compiles literature-cited toxin associations for 26 illustrated cyanobacteria taxa, noting some species produce multiple toxin types and that exhaustively cataloguing all toxin-producing genera/species in the wider literature is explicitly out of scope.

44 p.: total length of the report per its own suggested-citation block
2015: publication year; Open-File Report 2015-1164; DOI 10.3133/ofr20151164 (bibliographic identifiers, not empirical data)

It does, however, state two caveats that bear on any HAB-risk workflow relying on color or pigment proxies: 'Coloration is not an indicator of the genus of the organism(s)' and 'the presence or absence of taste and odor compounds is not predictive of the presence of algal toxins.' Read alongside its explanation that bloom color is driven by specifi

title: Forecasting microcystin concentrations in Lake Erie using an Eulerian tracer model
what: A 2023 peer-reviewed journal article (Journal of Great Lakes Research 49(5):1029-1044; also archived as a NOAA institutional repository record) that develops and tests a 3-D Eulerian tracer model (ETM) to forecast the short-term (7-day) transport and evolution of microcystin (MC) toxin concentrations across Lake Erie,

claims.c: The study builds weekly, lake-wide microcystin (MC) distribution maps for Lake Erie from an in-situ MC monitoring dataset for the summers of 2018 and 2019, then uses a 3-D Eulerian tracer model (ETM) initialized on those maps to simulate MC transport forward 7 days at a time.

Four ETM configurations were compared, crossing two initial-condition assumptions (MC held within the surface-mixed layer vs. spread through the entire water column) with two process assumptions (hydrodynamic transport alone vs. transport plus an added MC production term).
The hydrodynamics-only ETM (no MC production term) captured transport and predicted observed microcystin concentrations in 69% of simulations -- the paper's baseline transport-only skill figure.
Incorporating the MC production term into the ETM raised forecast accuracy by an additional ~10 percentage points over the hydrodynamics-only baseline.
Model skill was evaluated against five microcystin concentration thresholds: 0.3, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 and 20.0 microg/L.

Per a secondary aggregated summary (not independently verified against original text), production-enabled configurations correctly signaled microcystin concentrations above 5 microg/L in 2019 (described as the 'large bloom, high-microcystin year') but incorrectly forecast exceedances of 5 microg/L in 2018 (a smaller-bloom year) -- i.e., reliability at this threshold was not stable across bloom-severity years.

The authors state that, given limited data available to initialize the ETM, no single one of the four model configurations consistently outperformed the others, and they recommend that users consider the full range of configurations -- not a single 'best' run -- when using model output for management decisions.

The paper's motivating problem, per aggregated secondary description, is that routine Lake Erie cyanobacteria forecasts predict bloom biomass/occurrence but not the microcystin toxin itself, because remote sensing cannot detect microcystin directly and there is no consistent ratio between cyanobacterial biomass and microcystin concentration.

This paper corresponds to one chapter of Xing Zhou's 2023 Michigan Tech PhD dissertation, which separately describes a companion chapter (overlapping co-authors) comparing three transport models -- a Lagrangian particle model, this Eulerian tracer model, and a hybrid 'property-carrying particle' model -- for predicting Lake Erie CHAB biomass (not microcystin) over 2017-2019 and finding comparable accuracy across the three; the dissertation frames the present MC-forecasting work around the point that maintaining sufficient monitoring coverage remains essential for improved predictions.

Bibliographically, the paper appears in the Journal of Great Lakes Research, volume 49, issue 5, pages 1029-1044, published October 2023 (DOI 10.1016/j.jglr.2023.06.006); CrossRef funder metadata lists NOAA's National Centers for Coastal Ocean Science (award NA17NOS4780186) among the funders, alongside Ohio State University and Michigan Technological University.

title: Freshwater Harmful Algal Blooms: Causes, Challenges, and Policy Considerations
what: A U.S. Congressional Research Service (CRS) report (No. R44871, last updated August 20, 2018) that surveys the causes and impacts of freshwater harmful algal blooms (HABs), summarizes the federal/state legislative and regulatory response (HABHRCA, Clean Water Act provisions, agency roles, funding), and lays out the fie

claims.c: CRS states that in recent years the frequency and geographic distribution of freshwater HABs have been increasing nationally and globally, with HABs recorded in every state.
The 2014 Lake Erie cyanobacterial HAB contaminated Toledo, Ohio's drinking water supply, affecting more than 500,000 people, with an estimated $65 million in lost benefits.
Between January 1 and August 12, 2016, U.S. states reported at least 266 notices (cautions, warnings, public health advisories/warnings) for freshwater HABs.

In EPA's 2012 National Lakes Assessment, microcystin was detected in 39% of lakes sampled — a 9.5-percentage-point increase from the 2007 assessment — while median lake total phosphorus rose from 20 µg/L (2007) to 37 µg/L (2012).

EPA's draft (non-binding) water quality criteria recommend numeric values of 4 µg/L for microcystin and 8 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin, while WHO's 2003 recreational-water guidance places microcystin exposure bands from <10 µg/L (low probability of adverse effects) up to >2,000 µg/L (very high probability).

As of November 2015, roughly 30 states had implemented their own cyanobacterial HAB guidelines for recreational waterways, of which 21 set numeric guidelines for microcystin or cylindrospermopsin (ranging 0.8-20 µg/L for microcystin and 4-20 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin) — i.e., no single federally mandated numeric threshold exists and state guidance varies widely.

Nutrient enrichment (nitrogen and phosphorus) is widely considered by scientists to be a key cause of HAB formation, but the Clean Water Act does not give EPA authority to directly regulate the nonpoint (diffuse, e.g., agricultural runoff) nutrient sources that are a major contributor, only point-source discharges.
The report states outright scientific/predictive uncertainty: it is not known precisely what nutrient level triggers a bloom, nor what factors trigger toxin release within a bloom.

Federal legislative authority for HABs (HABHRCA) has been amended three times — 1998 (P.L. 105-383), 2004 (P.L. 108-456), and 2014 (P.L. 113-124) — progressively expanding scope from marine-only to include freshwater HABs, adding CDC to the interagency task force, and authorizing $20,500,000 per year for FY2014-FY2018.

The President's FY2019 budget request proposed eliminating or drastically cutting numerous EPA nutrient/HAB-relevant grant programs (e.g., CWA §319 nonpoint-source grants and several EPA geographic programs cut to $0.0M; Great Lakes Restoration cut from $300.0M enacted to $30.0M requested), which the report says creates uncertainty about whether EPA can still meet its nutrient-reduction goals.

Golden algae (a non-cyanobacterial freshwater HAB taxon) are described as an emerging problem that has caused large fish kills worldwide, including millions of fish in Texas, with most major fish kills occurring since 2000; per two affected states, there is currently no evidence golden algal toxins directly threaten humans, other mammals, or birds.

Under CWA §303(d), 24 states have listed a combined 1,015 waters as impaired for algal blooms, while only 2 states (California and New Hampshire) have listed waters impaired specifically for algal toxins (67 waters total, plus only 2 TMDLs developed for algal toxins) — showing formal impairment listing for toxins specifically lags listing for blooms generally.

claims.c: gridMET is a daily, ~4-km (1/24th degree) resolution surface meteorological dataset covering the contiguous US from 1979 to the present, produced by blending PRISM's spatial attributes with NLDAS-2's temporal attributes, and is updated daily.
The dataset's defining method is a blend of PRISM (spatial climatology) with NLDAS-2 (temporal/reanalysis-informed) data.
gridMET is also known/cited under the alternate name METDATA.

The catalog entry does not provide a point of contact, a license or use-constraints statement, bounding-box coordinates, or a record-level metadata/publication date, and does not enumerate its full variable list on the static page.

title: HAB Forecasts | US EPA
year: 2026 (page states "Last updated on April 14, 2026"; underlying cited model publications are Myer et al. 2020 and Schaeffer et al. 2024)
what: An EPA program web page (part of the EPA HABs "Trends, Monitoring Results & Forecasts" section) that describes the operational HAB forecasting products available for U.S. waters: EPA's own CyAN-satellite-based weekly cyanoHAB probability forecast for the freshwater lakes/reservoirs, plus a directory of NOAA/NCCOS/NANOO

claims.c: EPA scientists built a weekly forecast model that predicts the probability of a cyanobacteria-dominated harmful algal bloom (cyanoHAB), using data from the Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN) satellite-based system, covering over 2,000 of the largest U.S. lakes and reservoirs in the lower 48 states, running seasonally from March/April through November.
The freshwater forecast model's stated overall prediction accuracy is 90 percent.
The model operationally defines a cyanoHAB bloom as a median lake chlorophyll-a concentration of at least 12 µg/L together with cyanobacteria dominance of the phytoplankton community.
The forecast output is a probability from 0 to 100% that a given lake/reservoir will experience a cyanobacteria-dominated bloom within the next 7 days.
EPA explicitly states the forecast is meant to support, not substitute for, on-the-ground monitoring.

The underlying CyAN satellite data cannot resolve shallow near-shore areas, small embayments, or narrow parts of a lake/reservoir, which limits the forecast's ability to detect localized blooms in those zones.

Beyond U.S. freshwater lakes, NOAA and its partners/collaborators separately serve HAB forecasts for several U.S. coastal/marine regions: the Gulf of America and Florida, the Gulf of Maine, the Pacific Northwest, and California, via NCCOS, NANOOS, and SCCOOS partner sites; the Gulf of Maine product is specifically named as an Alexandrium catenella predictive model.

The page cites two underlying peer-reviewed model publications: a 'National Forecasting Model' (Schaeffer et al., 2024) and a 'State Forecasting Model' (Myer et al., 2020), as the scientific basis for the freshwater CyAN-based forecasts.

title: Harmful Algal Bloom Monitoring System

NOAA/NCCOS issues two tiers of HAB forecasts: short-term forecasts, issued once or twice per week, that locate and size a bloom and project where it is headed; and longer-term seasonal forecasts that predict overall bloom severity for a region's season.

The page frames HABs generally as caused by a small subset of naturally occurring algal species capable of producing toxins, and asserts (without citation on this page) that HABs now occur in every U.S. state and that new HAB problems have emerged in recent years.

title: Introduction to Remote Sensing of Harmful Algal Blooms
year: 2017 (training delivered Sept. 5–26, 2017; landing page hosted on the current NASA Earthdata site)
what: A four-part (one hour per session), introductory, instructor-led NASA ARSET (Applied Remote Sensing Training Program) webinar course delivered September 5–26, 2017, that teaches practitioners how satellite remote sensing — ocean-color sensors plus coupled model/remote-sensing decision-support systems — can be used to d

claims.c: The course is a four-part, one-hour-per-session, introductory, online instructor-led NASA ARSET training on satellite remote sensing of HABs, delivered September 5–26, 2017.

The course's central premise is that satellite remote sensing can detect impaired water quality from HABs because it samples frequently and over large spatial areas, positioning it as complementary to in-situ monitoring (which is covered in Part 1).

The stated learning objectives are narrowly practitioner-facing: identify NASA remote-sensing data products for HAB identification/monitoring, describe how coupled remote-sensing-and-modeling approaches feed decision-support tools, and use a selection of NASA Earth Science data tools to monitor HABs.

The course requires 'Fundamentals of Aquatic Remote Sensing' as a prerequisite and targets management/practitioner organizations (local/regional/state/federal/international bodies using satellite imagery for coastal and ocean applications) rather than research scientists.

Session 2 (Sept. 12) is specifically about the satellite platforms/sensors and processing pipeline used for HAB ocean-color remote sensing: MODIS, VIIRS, Sentinel-2A, Sentinel-3, and Landsat 8, plus NASA data-access tools and the SeaDAS processing tool.

Session 3 (Sept. 19) covers coupled remote-sensing/modeling decision-support systems and accuracy assessment for marine HABs, including a live demonstration of California's C-HARM (California Harmful Algae Risk Mapping) system, taught with guest instructor Clarissa Anderson of the Southern California Coastal Ocean Observing System.

Session 4 (Sept. 26) shifts focus to freshwater cyanobacterial HABs specifically, covering tools named 'HAB Tracker' and a 'Great Lakes HAB bulletin,' plus citizen-science monitoring approaches, taught with guest instructor Wilson Salls of EPA's CyAN Project.
The training page itself explicitly warns that its technical content is time-bound and may already be superseded by more current methods.

claims.c: The page's own definition frames HABs as a rapid overgrowth of algae that causes toxic/harmful effects on people, wildlife, and local economies.

The Lake Erie HAB forecast is produced jointly by four NOAA components: National Ocean Service, Office of Atmospheric Research/GLERL, National Weather Service, and the National Centers for Coastal Ocean Science (NCCOS).
NCCOS only issues the daily forecast during the bloom season (typically July–October) once field/in-situ sampling has confirmed the HAB species Microcystis is present.
The forecast is updated daily and reports the bloom's current extent and trajectory as it forms.

The bloom position forecast projects the bloom's modeled location for a minimum of 96 hours from the date of publication by advecting the satellite-observed bloom using 3-D hydrodynamic lake-current output from the Lake Erie Operational Forecast System (LEOFS).
The product explicitly disclaims quantitative reliability: predicted bloom concentrations are guidance only and may not reflect the true, on-the-ground bloom condition.
The model visualization has stated representational gaps: cloud cover is not depicted, and the color legend distinguishing 'no chlorophyll' from 'no data' could be misread if not understood.

The weather.gov/cle page itself functions mainly as a navigation portal to the authoritative NOAA/NCCOS materials (a seasonal bulletin PDF and the NCCOS Lake Erie HAB forecast website) rather than hosting the underlying bloom imagery/data directly.
