title: Lake Erie Harmful Algal Bloom FAQs
what: An FAQ webpage published by NOAA's National Centers for Coastal Ocean Science (NCCOS) explaining the operational Lake Erie Harmful Algal Bloom Forecast System (part of NOAA's Harmful Algal Bloom Operational Forecast System, HAB-OFS): what forecast products are issued and when, what satellite/in-situ/model data feed the

claims.c: NOAA's HAB-OFS is the official U.S. forecast source for Karenia brevis (red tide) blooms in the Gulf of Mexico and for cyanobacteria (chiefly Microcystis aeruginosa) blooms in Lake Erie, meant to help the public and response personnel make informed decisions.

NOAA issues four distinct Lake Erie HAB products across a season: weekly early-season severity projections starting mid-May (based on Maumee River phosphorus loading), a final seasonal forecast at end of June, near-daily bulletins through the July–October bloom season, and a late-October retrospective seasonal assessment.
The source states that cyanobacteria blooms are driven primarily by nutrient (phosphorus) loading occurring between March and July, which is the basis for NOAA's early-season severity projections.
Bloom growth is temperature-gated: Microcystis blooms essentially stop growing once water temperature drops below 65°F.

Forecast bulletins begin the first Monday in July and continue until the Microcystis bloom dissipates; during an active bloom they are issued twice weekly (Monday and Thursday); because they depend on clear satellite imagery, they may be delayed up to one day after overcast weather or after federal holidays.

Each bulletin combines satellite bloom imagery with a minimum 96-hour forecast of bloom position (via modeled surface currents) and a separate minimum 96-hour mixing/surface-scum forecast produced by a 3-dimensional circulation model driven by predicted winds.

The forecast system fuses satellite remote sensing with several in-situ and model data streams: Copernicus Sentinel-3 OLCI imagery (processed by NOAA NCCOS), Lake Erie Operational Forecast System (LEOFS) surface currents/winds, GLERL water sampling, state-agency/academic Microcystis and toxin sampling, National Weather Service water temperature observations/forecasts, and NOAA National Data Buoy Center wind data.
The forecast horizon is capped at 3 to 4 days because bloom transport, mixing, and decline cannot be reliably modeled further out, given how much the underlying environmental conditions vary.

Visible surface scums form specifically after a period of calm winds, which lets buoyant Microcystis cells rise and accumulate at the surface — i.e., wind/mixing state, not cell abundance alone, governs whether a bloom is visible at the surface (and thus detectable by a surface-reflectance satellite sensor).

The source states two explicit caveats about the reliability/completeness of bloom observation: bloom intensity and areal extent vary significantly year to year, and vertical mixing of cyanobacterial cells through the water column reduces the bloom's visibility at the surface.

numbers: 96 hours (minimum) — forecast horizon for bloom position (via modeled surface currents)
96 hours (minimum) — forecast horizon for mixing / surface-scum formation (via 3-D model + predicted winds)
3 to 4 days — maximum forecast lead time stated for bloom location/transport/mixing/decline overall
65°F — water temperature threshold below which Microcystis blooms 'essentially stop growing'
March to July — nutrient (phosphorus) loading season used for early-season bloom severity projections (Maumee River)
Mid-May — start of weekly early-season projections
End of June — issuance of the final seasonal forecast
First Monday in July — start of near-daily bulletin issuance, continuing until bloom dissipates
July–October — active bloom / bulletin season
2x per week (Monday and Thursday) — bulletin frequency during an active bloom
Up to 1 day — bulletin delay tolerance after overcast weather (imagery quality) or federal holidays
Late October — timing of the seasonal retrospective assessment
tensions: This source explicitly states that "vertical mixing of cyanobacterial cells through the water column reduces the visibility of the bloom at the surface" — a direct, source-acknowledged blind spot for any bloom-detection approach that relies on surface ocean-color/reflectance satellite imagery (e.g., the Sentinel-3 OLCI imagery this same system uses, and by extension other satellite-based HAB products such as EPA CyAN). This complicates any downstream finding elsewhere in the literature that treats satellite-derived surface chlorophyll/phycocyan

title: Learn about Harmful Algae, Cyanobacteria and Cyanotoxins
what: An official U.S. EPA educational/reference webpage (part of EPA's HABs program) that introduces and defines harmful algal blooms, cyanobacteria, and the major cyanotoxin classes (microcystins, cylindrospermopsins, anatoxins/guanitoxins, saxitoxins, nodularins), their general health/ecological effects, and the federal r

claims.c: EPA defines Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) as algal growths that harm human health and the environment, arising from excessive biomass, toxin production, or environmental degradation such as hypoxia/anoxia or acidification.
Cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) are microscopic, photosynthetic organisms common to freshwater, estuarine, and marine waters.

Cyanotoxins are produced under specific environmental conditions (light intensity, nutrients, temperature, salinity) and are released mainly when cyanobacterial cells die and rupture (lysis), not necessarily continuously during growth.

Microcystins are described as the most common cyanobacterial toxins, with over 200 known congeners (microcystin-LR most studied); they are primarily hepatotoxic, with some evidence linking microcystin-LR to liver and colorectal cancers, though EPA states current evidence is inadequate to assess carcinogenic potential.

Cylindrospermopsins have three known environmental congeners and act primarily on the liver and kidney, with potential to increase cellular stress, affect development, and cause DNA damage; EPA again states insufficient evidence to assess carcinogenicity.

Anatoxins/guanitoxins are neurotoxins that bind neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, with effects ranging from loss of coordination to death by respiratory failure; EPA states there is insufficient information on their carcinogenicity.

Saxitoxins are potent neurotoxins representative of the Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP) toxin family, with health effects ranging from minor tingling/numbness to death from respiratory failure; no carcinogenicity information is available for this class per EPA.
Nodularins are produced by cyanobacteria of the genus Nodularia, which primarily thrive in salty or brackish coastal or inland waters (rather than typical freshwater lakes).

Beyond direct toxicity, HABs cause ecological and economic harm through hypoxia/anoxia or acidification, unsightly water conditions, taste-and-odor problems affecting recreation/tourism/drinking water, and (for marine taxa such as K. brevis) large die-offs of fish, birds, and marine mammals including dolphins and manatees.

Regulatory responsibility for HABs in the U.S. is split by statute: the Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Act (HABHRCA) assigns NOAA responsibility for marine and Great Lakes HABs, while EPA holds responsibility for other freshwaters and separately has regulatory authority over marine waters affected by HABs under the Clean Water Act.

The page itself is an introductory/educational reference, not a technical or primary-data source: it names toxin classes and general contributing conditions but supplies no quantitative exposure thresholds, advisory concentrations, temperature/nutrient ranges, or monitoring data, instead pointing to separate EPA Health Effects Support Documents, Drinking Water Health Advisory documents, and state/tribal program links for that information.

numbers: More than 200 forms/congeners of microcystins have been identified, with microcystin-LR the most studied (no unit; a count of chemical congeners, not a concentration)
3 congeners of cylindrospermopsin found in the environment: cylindrospermopsin, 7-epicylindrospermopsin, and 7-deoxycylindrospermopsin (count, not a concentration)

No concentration-based thresholds, health-advisory levels (e.g., µg/L or ppb), temperature ranges, or nutrient (N/P) concentration limits are stated anywhere on this page — the page explicitly defers to separate EPA Heal
tensions: This page is background/glossary material, not a data or methods source, so it cannot itself be fused with satellite (e.g., EPA CyAN) or in-situ (Water Quality Portal, NWIS) signals - it supplies no thresholds, concentrations, or model inputs to feed a risk model. It does, however, complicate any simple "toxin present = health risk" framing used elsewhere in a HAB literature base: EPA explicitly says evidence for microcystin-LR carcinogenicity is 'inadequate' and for the other three toxin classes ranges from 'insufficient' to 'no information,'

title: National Aeronautics and Space Administration - ENVISAT MERIS Global Binned Cyanobacteria Index (CI) Data, version 5.0

claims.c: The specific catalog.data.gov landing page for this dataset (version 5.0, "Global Binned" CI) is no longer live; direct fetches of the page, its resource sub-page, and the CKAN API all returned HTTP 404, and NASA's current CyAN documentation confirms the product line has since moved to Version 6.0.
NASA's "Version 5" (V5) reprocessing of the CyAN Cyanobacteria Index data (the version this dataset entry names) was released on May 22, 2023.
Version 5 introduced an improved turbid-water exclusion filter, a clear-water correction for invalid retrieved Kd, and an OLCI-to-MERIS inter-calibration step for the Cyanobacteria Index.
Version 5 CI_cyano values are on average 15-20% higher than Version 4 values for the same imagery, an artifact of recalibration rather than a real change in cyanobacteria abundance.
NASA explicitly prohibits comparing or trending Cyanobacteria Index values across different processing versions of this product.
NASA itself describes the CI_cyano algorithm/product as still under active evaluation with known unresolved issues, i.e., not presented as a finalized, fully validated product.

The underlying CyAN sensors and coverage windows are: ENVISAT MERIS 2002-2012, OLCI on Sentinel-3A from 2016-present, and OLCI on Sentinel-3B from 2018-present, with satellite sensor spatial resolution of 300 m and CI products distributed as GeoTIFF daily and 7-day maximum-value composite files over CONUS and Alaska.

A live, structurally-analogous successor product in the same NASA OB.DAAC/CyAN family — "ENVISAT MERIS Regional Mapped Cyanobacteria Index (CI) Data, version 6.0" — comprises 248,675 netCDF-4 granules covering 2002-03-21 to 2012-05-09 (the full MERIS mission), distributed openly under EOSDIS data-use terms; this is offered as family context, not as a direct description of the version-5.0 "Global Binned" dataset itself.

A separate, still-live EPA ScienceHub catalog record, "Cyanobacteria Index (MERIS)" (Ohio and Florida scope, a distinct dataset from FED-066), documents the underlying MERIS instrument as having a 68.5-degree nadir-pointing field of view, 15 spectral bands, and a 300x300 m grid resolution, and cites a peer-reviewed method paper for using this CI data to track HAB spatial extent over time.

numbers: 300 m — CyAN sensor (MERIS/OLCI) spatial resolution, per current CyAN project page
50 m — CONUS land mask resolution used in CyAN CI products
500 m — Alaska land mask resolution used in CyAN CI products
2002–2012 — ENVISAT MERIS operational coverage period feeding CyAN CI
2016–present — Sentinel-3A OLCI coverage period feeding CyAN CI (as stated on source page)
2018–present — Sentinel-3B OLCI coverage period feeding CyAN CI (as stated on source page)
May 22, 2023 — release date of CyAN "Version 5" (V5) reprocessing
15–20% — average increase in CI_cyano values in V5 vs V4, attributed to updated calibration gains, not a real bloom change
10 to 16 months — stated CyAN reprocessing cadence
~10,000 to 7,000,000 cells/mL — approximate output range of the CI-to-cyanobacteria-cell-count conversion, per current (v6-era) formula CIcyano=10^(DN×0.011714−4.1870866)
248,675 — granule/file count for the related v6.0 "Regional Mapped" CI netCDF-4 product (NOT the v5.0 Global Binned dataset itself)
2002-03-21 to 2012-05-09 — temporal extent of the v6.0 Regional Mapped CI product (full MERIS mission span)
tensions: This source is itself a built-in warning against a common HAB remote-sensing pitfall: because the CyAN CI_cyano algorithm has been reprocessed multiple times — with V5 alone producing a stated 15-20% average value shift from calibration changes alone — and NASA explicitly disallows cross-version trend analysis, any HAB study, dashboard, or product claim (including within this project) that stitches together CyAN CI values spanning a reprocessing boundary (e.g., pre- vs. post-May-2023, or the 2025 move to V6) risks mistaking a processing artifac

title: NLDAS: North American Land Data Assimilation System
year: 2025 (Climate Data Guide page last modified 2025-12-11); primary NLDAS-2 methodology paper cited on the page is Xia et al. 2012
what: NLDAS (North American Land Data Assimilation System) is a system, described on NCAR/UCAR's Climate Data Guide, that fuses observation-based and model-reanalysis data into a long-term (1979-present), hourly, 1/8-degree-resolution gridded atmospheric forcing dataset over the continental U.S., southern Canada, and norther

claims.c: NLDAS integrates observation-based data and model reanalysis data to produce long-term hourly atmospheric forcing data, which is then used to drive offline (uncoupled) land-surface and/or hydrological models.
The NLDAS forcing grid has 1/8th-degree (~0.125°) spatial resolution and covers the continental United States, southern Canada, and northern Mexico between 25 and 53 degrees North latitude.
The dataset spans 1979 to the present at hourly time steps and is delivered operationally with roughly a 4-day latency behind real time.
NLDAS-2 forcing data is used to drive four offline land-surface/hydrological models: NASA's Mosaic, NOAA's Noah, the NWS SAC hydrological model, and the Community VIC model run in energy mode.

The NLDAS forcing dataset provides seven surface meteorological variables: precipitation, 2-m air temperature, 2-m air humidity, downward shortwave radiation, downward longwave radiation, 10-m wind speed, and surface pressure.
The source asserts, as a stated strength, that NLDAS data products have been evaluated and validated against various observations and are widely used in the meteorology and hydrology community.

The source documents several specific, named precipitation-forcing problems: spurious precipitation along the Canadian side of the U.S.-Canada border in many years, occasional artificial 'dry spots' from gauge quality-control issues that dry out modeled soil moisture, a discontinuity in precipitation behavior after 1 January 2012 caused by switching input precipitation datasets, and an anomalously high, localized precipitation artifact in Texas and the southeastern U.S. in July-August 2008.

The source explicitly cautions that, because of its operational nature, not all known issues in NLDAS-2 can be resolved and the datasets re-generated, and that NLDAS is not intended to be used as an independent dataset for monitoring climate variability and change.
The source contrasts NLDAS with PRISM and Daymet, noting NLDAS is an hourly product whereas PRISM/Daymet are monthly or daily products.

The primary methodological reference for NLDAS-2 is a 2012 paper describing continental-scale water and energy flux analysis and validation, including intercomparison and application of the model products.

numbers: Spatial resolution: 1/8th-degree (~0.125°) grid spacing
Spatial domain: continental United States, southern Canada, and northern Mexico, 25-53° North latitude
Temporal resolution: hourly
Temporal coverage: 1979 to present, with an example time series shown from 13Z 01 Jan 1979 to 12Z May 2023
Operational latency: ~4-day latency behind real time
7 surface meteorological forcing variables: precipitation, 2-m air temperature, 2-m air humidity, downward shortwave radiation, downward longwave radiation, 10-m wind speed, surface pressure
4 land-surface/hydrological models driven by the forcing data: NASA Mosaic, NOAA Noah, NWS SAC, and Community VIC (in energy mode)
Precipitation annual climatology example period: 1981-2020 (Figure 1 caption)
Example grid point in Figure 6: 33.9375° North, -86.9375° West
Soil moisture example depth: top 1-meter (Mosaic model, Figure 3, 00Z 15 Aug 2002)
Precipitation input dataset change date: 01 January 2012 (switch from US-Mexico precipitation dataset to a global precipitation dataset)
Documented anomalous precipitation artifact: Texas and southeastern U.S., July-August 2008

title: NOAA Lake Erie Harmful Algal Bloom Forecast Products
year: n.d. (continuously updated NOAA operational webpage; content reflects the 2025 and 2026 forecast seasons as fetched on 2026-07-01)
what: A NOAA GLERL webpage describing NOAA's operational suite of Lake Erie harmful-algal-bloom (HAB) forecast products for coastal managers and drinking-water facility operators: an early-season projection (from May), an early-July seasonal severity forecast, an in-season (roughly June/July–October) short-term bloom extent/

claims.c: NOAA/GLERL issues three distinct Lake Erie HAB forecast products across the year: an early-season projection (from May), a seasonal severity forecast (early July), and a seasonal retrospective (November, after the bloom season ends).

The early-season projection and seasonal forecast estimate how severe the upcoming bloom season is likely to be primarily from measured phosphorus loading in the Maumee River, combined with long-range weather/hydrological forecasts and historical bloom records.

During the bloom season, NOAA issues an in-season operational bulletin giving current bloom extent plus a 5-day outlook of where the bloom will move and what algae concentrations are likely, updated twice weekly, to support managers' decisions on preventative action.

The companion NOAA/NCCOS Lake Erie HAB forecast portal, linked from the GLERL bulletin page as the main forecast portal, describes a shorter, 96-hour minimum horizon for its bloom-position and vertical-mixing forecasts, generated from hydrodynamic modeled currents combined with the most recent satellite imagery.
Current bloom location/extent for the online tool is derived from satellite true-color imagery from the Ocean Land Color Imager (OLCI) sensor, and the bloom-position layer is updated daily.

The forecast's Severity Index (SI) is a unitless seasonal metric anchored to the lowest observed bloom-biomass season (2005) at one end and the highest observed bloom-biomass season (2011) at the other, computed over a 30-day assessment window.

For summer 2026, NOAA and partners forecast a moderate bloom with a Severity Index of 3.5 and a potential range of 3 to 4.5; the source defines 3-5 as the moderate band, above 5 as more severe, and above 7 as particularly severe with extensive scum formation.

NOAA attributes the width of the forecast's severity range (e.g., 3-4.5 for 2026) explicitly to two unresolved factors: nutrients already resident in the lake from prior seasons, and competition from other (non-target) algal species.
NOAA states that bloom duration is additionally gated by a factor with no advance predictability at seasonal-forecast lead time: the frequency of high wind events in September.

The forecast system is a multi-institution operational partnership: nutrient-loading data from Heidelberg University's National Center for Water Quality Research; forecast models run by NOAA NCCOS, the University of Michigan, and North Carolina State University; field observations from the NWS Ohio River Forecast Center, NOAA GLERL, CIGLR, Ohio Sea Grant, Ohio State University's Stone Laboratory, the University of Toledo, and Ohio EPA.

numbers: Severity Index (SI, unitless) forecast for 2026: 3.5 point value, potential range 3-4.5, classified "moderate"

SI interpretation bands per NCCOS: "Moderate blooms have an index of 3-5", "an index above 5 indicates more severe HABs", "Blooms over 7 are particularly severe, with extensive scum formation and bloom coverage"
SI scale anchors: minimum observed bloom biomass = 2005; maximum observed bloom biomass = 2011

Early-season projections issued weekly from May until the seasonal forecast is announced
Seasonal forecast generally issued "in early July"; the actual 2025 seasonal forecast was issued June 25, 2025 ("June 25th")
Bloom/forecast season window given as "June until October" (GLERL bulletin page) vs. "July to October" (NCCOS forecast portal)
2025 forecast season ended 11/19/2025 ("final day of the 2025 Lake Erie HAB forecast season")

Severity assessment period described as a "30 day period" (NCCOS portal)
Historical comparison bar graph in the 2026 news release spans years "dating back to 2002"
tensions: No direct contradiction with other HAB literature is stated in the source itself, but two internal inconsistencies across NOAA's own linked pages describing this one forecast system are worth flagging: (a) the bloom/forecast season window is "June until October" on the GLERL bulletin.html landing page but "July to October" on the directly-linked NCCOS forecast portal; (b) the in-season outlook horizon is "5-day outlooks" on bulletin.html but a "96 hours" (4-day) minimum on the NCCOS portal. These likely describe two layers of the same system (a

title: Overview of the Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN) satellite monitoring and the future of expanding to >270,000 lakes
what: An EPA Science Inventory record for the abstract of a 2023 NALMS conference presentation by EPA scientists (Schaeffer, Guenzel, Salls) giving a program-level overview of the Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN) satellite HAB monitoring effort, its current validation basis and public data products, and EPA's plans to

claims.c: CyAN uses satellite data to detect cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms in support of environmental management and public use of U.S. lakes, reservoirs, and estuaries.

The CyAN satellite algorithm's validation basis consists of three types of reference data: Water Quality Portal chlorophyll-a measurements, state-reported HAB advisories, and state-reported cyanobacteria cell counts and toxin levels.

CyAN currently quantifies cyanobacterial metrics (spatial extent, temporal frequency, magnitude, and lake occurrence) across more than 2,000 of the largest lake systems in the contiguous United States.

CyAN outputs are made publicly available through multiple channels: the CyAN mobile and web apps, EPA's How's My Waterway, quantified metrics on EnviroAtlas and EPA's Report on Environment, and GeoTIFF files via direct download.
Unspecified case studies are asserted to show that CyAN satellite data helps save resources and protect human health, but no case-study details, metrics, or citations are given in this abstract.
EPA's stated future plans for CyAN include adding direct chlorophyll-a detection, expanding coverage to over 270,000 lakes, and addressing a need for discrete field validation information.

numbers: >2,000 of the largest lake systems in the contiguous United States — current CyAN monitoring coverage
>270,000 lakes — targeted future CyAN expansion coverage (aspirational, not yet achieved per this abstract)
Presentation dates: October 22–26, 2023 (NALMS 2023 conference, Erie, PA)
EPA Science Inventory record published: 10/26/2023; last revised: 03/08/2024
EPA Science Inventory Record ID: 360661
tensions: This is a program overview/roadmap abstract rather than a peer-reviewed validation study: it asserts that "case studies demonstrate CyAN satellite data helps save resources and protect human health" with no supporting data in this record, immediately followed by an acknowledgment that further "discrete field validation information" is still needed for the planned expansion — i.e., a confident outcome claim and an admitted validation gap appear side by side in the same short abstract. It should be treated as a scope/roadmap citation (current ~2,

title: Water Treatment Optimization for Cyanotoxins (Version 1.0)
what: A U.S. EPA Office of Water technical guidance document (EPA 810-B-16-007, Version 1.0, October 2016) that gives drinking-water treatment plant managers and operators a framework — source-water monitoring indicators, cyanotoxin measurement methods, and a decision-tree distinguishing intracellular vs. extracellular cyano

claims.c: Warmer water temperatures (typically ≥25°C/77°F) favor growth of some cyanobacteria, while Microcystis growth is usually limited below 15°C (59°F); other genera such as Planktothrix and Cylindrospermopsis can survive in colder water, even under ice.

Turbidity, a common jar-testing optimization parameter for particulate removal, may not be a reliable indicator of cyanobacteria cell or toxin removal under certain conditions, particularly when raw water turbidity is below 10 NTU.

Wind can concentrate cyanobacteria cells into surface scums representing a thousand-fold to million-fold increase in cell concentration, which is especially concerning if this occurs near a public water system intake; strong winds can also mix surface blooms downward toward intake depths.

The presence or abundance of cyanobacteria cells does not reliably indicate whether toxins are present at a given time: toxins can occur even at low cell concentrations, and elevated toxin concentrations can persist after cells/blooms are no longer detectable in the source water.

Cyanotoxins can occur intracellularly (cell-bound) or extracellularly (dissolved); applying certain oxidants/disinfectants can lyse cells and release toxin, increasing the extracellular (dissolved) toxin concentration, which makes the choice and location of oxidant application a treatment challenge.

In June 2015 EPA issued drinking-water Health Advisories for only two cyanotoxins (total microcystins and cylindrospermopsin) and Health Effects Support Documents for three (adding anatoxin-a), because at that time there was insufficient information to set a Health Advisory specifically for anatoxin-a.

As of this document's writing, no specific, generally accepted in-plant treatment targets/goals existed for cyanobacteria or cyanotoxins; the document presents itself as only a step toward establishing such optimization goals.

The document defines 'treatment optimization' as achieving the best possible performance from each water-treatment unit process through process control and problem-solving (priority setting, tailored studies, data trending), continuously assessed against pre-established goals, in line with EPA's Composite Correction Program (CCP) 'multiple barrier' concept.

At the time of writing, there was no published EPA analytical method for phycocyanin (unlike chlorophyll-a, which has EPA Method 445.0), so phycocyanin measurement relied on commercial in-vivo fluorescence probes/instruments rather than a standardized EPA method.

Indirect indicators such as visual inspection, turbidity, Secchi depth, and diurnal pH shifts can flag a possible cyanobacteria problem, but the document states these indicators do not substitute for quantitative, confirmatory cyanobacteria/cyanotoxin measurements.

The document organizes extracellular cyanotoxin treatment guidance around four technology categories — carbon adsorption, membranes, biofiltration, and oxidation — and supplies comparative tables (general oxidant effectiveness, and CT [concentration x time] tables for microcystin-LR and cylindrospermopsin) plus technology-specific process-evaluation appendices for both intracellular and extracellular toxin treatment.

numbers: 25°C (77°F) — threshold above which warmer water temperatures favor growth of some cyanobacteria species (Section 2.1/Table 2-1); qualitative literature synthesis, no CI/uncertainty given

15°C (59°F) — threshold below which Microcystis growth is usually limited, citing Robarts and Zohary 1987 (Section 2.1); other genera (Planktothrix, Cylindrospermopsis) can persist colder, even under ice
10 NTU — raw-water turbidity level below which turbidity is flagged as a poor/limited indicator of cyanobacteria cell concentration, citing Newcombe et al. 2015 (Table 2-2)

Thousand-fold to million-fold — factor by which wind-driven surface scums can concentrate cyanobacteria cell populations relative to ambient water, citing Chorus & Bartram 1999 (Section 2.1); no error bound given

2 cyanotoxins (total microcystins, cylindrospermopsin) received EPA drinking-water Health Advisories in June 2015; 3 cyanotoxins (adds anatoxin-a) received Health Effects Support Documents, but anatoxin-a lacked sufficie
Document identifier: EPA 810-B-16-007, Version 1.0, October 2016 (Office of Water, MS-140)
tensions: The document's statement that cyanobacteria cell abundance does not reliably track toxin presence — toxins can occur at low cell counts and can persist after cells/blooms are no longer detectable — complicates any approach (including satellite/remote-sensing chlorophyll-a or cyanobacteria-cell-count products) that treats biomass or cell-count signals as a direct proxy for cyanotoxin risk; the source indicates biomass and toxin concentration can decouple. Its explicit caution that turbidity is an unreliable cyanobacteria/toxin indicator under so

title: Smaller-than-average harmful algal bloom predicted for western Lake Erie
what: A NOAA/NCCOS news release, issued June 29, 2023, announcing the pre-season (seasonal) severity forecast for the 2023 western Lake Erie cyanobacterial harmful algal bloom: a smaller-than-average bloom (severity index expected at 3, range 2–4.5) compared with 2022 (index 6.8), produced by combining spring nutrient-loadin

claims.c: NOAA and research partners forecast a smaller-than-average 2023 western Lake Erie HAB, expected to measure 3 on the severity index (potential range 2–4.5), versus the 2022 bloom which measured 6.8.
The bloom severity index is based on the bloom's biomass (amount of algae) over a sustained/peak period, and index values above 5 are described as indicating more severe blooms.

Blooms with an index over 7 are described as 'particularly severe' with extensive scum formation; the largest blooms on record occurred in 2011 (index 10) and 2015 (index 10.5), and the 2023 forecast (2–4.5) is characterized as among the smaller blooms since 2011.
NOAA expected the visible bloom to begin in mid-to-late July 2023.

The width of the forecast range (2–4.5) reflects uncertainty in forecasting precipitation, particularly for July; larger summer rain events could push the severity index higher via increased river/nutrient flow.
Bloom duration is a separate, unpredictable factor tied to the frequency of wind-mixing events in September, which cannot be forecast this far in advance.

Bloom size/severity is explicitly not a proxy for toxicity: a large bloom's toxins may be less concentrated than a smaller bloom's, and as of this release NOAA was still developing tools to detect and predict bloom toxicity.
Western Lake Erie HABs consist of cyanobacteria capable of producing microcystin, a liver toxin posing risk to human and wildlife health.
Blooms can force cities/local governments to increase drinking-water treatment, close beaches, and harm local recreation/tourism economies (fishing, swimming, boating, shoreline visits).

The forecast is operationally relevant to water-treatment-plant operators because intake structures are typically located below the surface, where sinking cyanobacterial cells can raise source-water toxin risk.

The 2023 forecast combined spring nutrient (phosphorus) load data from Heidelberg University with forecast models run by NCCOS, the University of Michigan, North Carolina State University, LimnoTech, Stanford University, and the Carnegie Institution for Science.
NOAA Chief Scientist Sarah Kapnick described the forecast's purpose as translating science into actionable information for partners and the public.
NCCOS lead scientist Richard Stumpf attributed the expectation of at least a mild 2023 bloom to a large March nutrient load despite an otherwise dry spring.
NOAA highlighted a newly updated Lake Erie HAB Forecast website providing more accurate predictions/visualizations, alongside in-season 5-day nowcast outlooks.

2022 actual bloom severity index: 6.8 (comparison year cited in the release)
2011 bloom severity index: 10 (cited among the largest on record)
2015 bloom severity index: 10.5 (cited as the largest on record)
Severity index threshold >5 = described as 'more severe' blooms
Severity index threshold >7 = described as 'particularly severe' with extensive scum formation
Expected visible bloom onset: mid-to-late July 2023

Great Lakes Water Quality Agreement (GLWQA) target severity index cited as <3 in structured summaries of the release from both fetched mirrors — flagged as lower-confidence since neither fetch returned it as a directly q
Seasonal update cadence referenced: an 'early August' update, plus 5-day nowcast outlooks once the bloom becomes visible (no exact calendar dates given in the release)
tensions: This is an ex-ante (pre-season) forecast, not an observed outcome — any other source in this review reporting the actual/observed 2023 western Lake Erie bloom severity is a distinct, later data point to compare against, not merge with, this forecast; this document alone cannot establish forecast accuracy. It also explicitly warns that bloom size/biomass (its severity index) is not a proxy for toxin concentration or human-health risk ("the size of a bloom isn't necessarily an indication of how toxic it is"), which complicates any other HAB sourc

title: State & Tribal Toxin Thresholds and HAB Advisory Decision Methods | US EPA
year: 2026 (living/maintained webpage; page states "Last updated on June 25, 2026," no fixed publication year)
what: An EPA webpage that compiles, state by state and tribe by tribe, the numeric cyanotoxin thresholds (for anatoxin-a, microcystins, cylindrospermopsin, and saxitoxins, all in µg/L) that U.S. state and tribal programs actually use to trigger drinking-water and recreational-water HAB advisories, with a link to each jurisdi

claims.c: The page's own stated purpose is to compile the cyanotoxin thresholds that state and tribal programs use to make advisory decisions for drinking water and recreational uses.
The compilation is explicitly incomplete by design: it only lists thresholds for which EPA found publicly accessible documentation, with a source link given for each entry.

A large share of 'state' thresholds are not independently derived — many states simply adopted EPA's own national Health Advisory (HA) values for drinking water or Ambient Water Quality Criteria (AWQC) for recreational water as their action levels.

EPA's own baseline drinking-water Health Advisory values, which numerous states reference directly, are 0.3 µg/L microcystin for children under 6 and 1.6 µg/L for school-age children through adults, and 0.7 µg/L cylindrospermopsin for children under 6 and 3 µg/L for the general population.
EPA's recommended recreational-water criteria (AWQC), used as the default reference by many states, are 8 µg/L for microcystins and 15 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin.

Where states do set independent recreational microcystin advisory levels, the numeric trigger points vary roughly an order of magnitude around EPA's 8 µg/L reference, and the number/structure of tiers also differs — e.g., California starts a 'caution' tier at 1 µg/L and reaches 'danger' at 6 µg/L, while Nevada and Kansas keep a single wide 'warning' tier spanning roughly 8 µg/L up to 2,000 µg/L before a top-tier 'danger/hazard' designation.

Several states set materially lower cyanotoxin action levels for dogs than for humans at the same recreational waters — e.g., Oregon's dog-specific anatoxin-a level (0.4 µg/L) versus its 15 µg/L human recreational level, and Indiana's dog advisory/prohibited microcystin levels (0.4/0.8 µg/L) versus its human advisory/prohibited levels (8/20 µg/L).
The compiled framework covers all 50 U.S. states plus six tribal nations, each entry linking out to that jurisdiction's own underlying guidance document.

Across jurisdictions, advisory frameworks use inconsistent tier vocabularies and criteria types — plain detect/non-detect, caution/advisory, warning, danger/prohibition — and inconsistent stratification (age band, animal species, or shoreline vs. open water), rather than one harmonized national decision schema.

The page itself provides no on-page definition of EPA HA/AWQC methodology and no explicit caveat about the currency, verification, or accuracy of the compiled state values — a user must follow the outbound links to each state's or EPA's own source document for that detail.

numbers: EPA Health Advisory, microcystins, children <6 years, drinking water: 0.3 µg/L
EPA Health Advisory, microcystins, school-age children through adults, drinking water: 1.6 µg/L
EPA Health Advisory, cylindrospermopsin, children <6 years, drinking water: 0.7 µg/L
EPA Health Advisory, cylindrospermopsin, general population, drinking water: 3 µg/L
EPA recommended Ambient Water Quality Criteria, microcystins, recreational: 8 µg/L
EPA recommended Ambient Water Quality Criteria, cylindrospermopsin, recreational: 15 µg/L
California recreational microcystin tiers: <1 µg/L (no advisory), 1 µg/L (caution), 4 µg/L (warning), 6 µg/L (danger)
Nevada recreational microcystin tiers: 4-8 µg/L (watch), 8-2000 µg/L (warning), >2000 µg/L (danger)
Kansas recreational microcystin tiers: >4 to <8 µg/L (watch), >8 to <2000 µg/L (warning), >2000 µg/L (hazard)
New York recreational microcystin tiers: <4 µg/L (no bloom), 4-20 µg/L (confirmed bloom), >20 µg/L (high toxins, shoreline), >10 µg/L (high toxins, open water)
Utah recreational saxitoxin: >8 µg/L (warning), >75 µg/L (danger)
Washington recreational saxitoxin danger level: 75 µg/L
tensions: This source documents, rather than resolves, wide cross-jurisdiction inconsistency: for the identical toxin and use case (e.g., recreational microcystin exposure), action thresholds range from as low as a 1 µg/L "caution" trigger in California to single "warning" bands stretching from about 8 µg/L up to 2,000 µg/L in Nevada/Kansas/Utah, and some states have no numeric threshold at all. Any HAB analytics or risk-communication tool that reports a single "advisory" or "action" level therefore cannot claim one universal, nationally validated numeri

title: Toxins and Harmful Algal Blooms Science Team | U.S. Geological Survey
year: Undated program page (content cites findings spanning 2013–2019+)
what: A USGS Environmental Health Program science-team page describing the Toxins and Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) Science Team, whose mission is to "develop advanced methods to study factors driving algal toxin production, how and where wildlife or humans are exposed to toxins, and ecotoxicology" so as to build decision tool

claims.c: The team's mission is to develop advanced methods to study factors driving algal toxin production, exposure pathways for wildlife and humans, and ecotoxicology, in order to build decision tools assessing whether exposure causes adverse health effects.

The actual health threats posed to the public, pets, livestock, and wildlife by algal toxins in waters used for recreation and drinking water remain poorly understood, despite blooms occurring frequently in U.S. water resources.

A 2016 USGS assessment found microcystins (a cyanotoxin) in 39 percent of small streams assessed across the southeastern United States; the page headlines this same finding as toxins detected in roughly 'one-third' of streams assessed.

In a 2013-15 Lake Erie study, cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins were not associated with mussel mortality at the concentrations observed, but mussel growth was lower at sites with higher microcystin concentrations.

At Kabetogama Lake, toxin-producing cyanobacteria (or genes indicating toxin-synthesis capability) and cyanotoxins were detected both before and after the formation of a visible algal bloom — toxin presence was not confined to the period when a bloom was visually apparent.
An objective scientific review concluded that the hypothesis linking BMAA (a cyanobacterial toxin) to neurodegenerative diseases such as ALS and Parkinsonism is not supported by existing data.
The team's affiliated Algal and Environmental Toxins Research Laboratory (Organic Geochemistry Research Laboratory, Lawrence, Kansas) operates from a 2,500-square-foot facility built in 2019.
A phytoplankton data release documented on the page covers sampling at eleven large river sites across the United States, conducted June through September 2017.
The team states that a single bloom-detection metric (e.g., a satellite/remote-sensing bloom-extent measure) cannot by itself adequately represent how severe a bloom is in terms of toxicity.
Data releases associated with the team's work explicitly state that values are reported raw and not rounded to standard USGS significant-figure conventions.
Decisions to restrict public access to water bodies due to algal blooms are often driven largely by perceived risk rather than measured/actual risk.

numbers: 39 percent of small streams assessed in the southeastern U.S. tested positive for microcystins (2016 USGS finding; the page's own headline separately rounds this to 'one-third')
2,500 square foot laboratory facility (Organic Geochemistry Research Laboratory / Algal and Environmental Toxins Research Laboratory, Lawrence, KS; constructed 2019)
Eleven large river sites sampled across the U.S., June–September 2017 (phytoplankton data release)
Lake Erie mussel/cyanotoxin study period: 2013–15
Historical bloom-magnitude dataset period: 2003–2011
Lake Okeechobee salinity-tolerance assessment window: July 9–17, 2017
tensions: (a) The page's own headline ('...One-Third of Streams...') and body text ('39 percent') are not numerically consistent — 39% is closer to two-fifths than one-third — illustrating how public-facing headlines can round or simplify the underlying statistic. (b) The Kabetogama Lake finding that toxin-producing cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins can occur before and after a visible bloom directly complicates the premise — used by satellite/remote-sensing bloom-detection approaches such as CyAN, which this same page discusses — that a visible or spectral

title: U.S. Action Plan for Lake Erie: Commitments and Strategy for Phosphorus Reduction (August 2017 Draft)
what: A 2017 draft joint U.S. federal-state action plan (EPA's Great Lakes National Program Office with five Great Lakes states and multiple federal agencies) presenting phosphorus load-reduction commitments and management strategies for Lake Erie. It documents state- and federal-led actions implementing the binational nutri

claims.c: Lake Erie HABs pose acute drinking-water and public-health risk, illustrated by the August 2014 Toledo, Ohio water crisis and by extreme microcystin levels during the 2011 bloom relative to WHO safety limits.

Binational GLWQA Annex 4 phosphorus targets (adopted Feb. 2016, 2008 baseline) call for a 40% cut in total phosphorus loading to the western and central basins, equating to an annual central-basin load target of 6,000 metric tons, split as a 3,316-metric-ton reduction from the U.S. and a 212-metric-ton reduction from Canada.

A stricter, separate target applies to the Maumee River specifically: a 40% cut in spring (March–July) total and dissolved reactive phosphorus (DRP), equal to a 2008-baseline spring load of 860 metric tons TP and 186 metric tons DRP, intended to keep western-basin cyanobacteria below toxin-threat levels.

Lake Erie's three basins differ sharply in depth and phosphorus loading share, which the plan links to different bloom types: the shallow Western Basin gets ~61% of the lake's total phosphorus load and hosts toxin-producing Microcystis aeruginosa; the Central Basin gets 28% and experiences bottom-water hypoxia; the deep Eastern Basin gets only 11% but supports nuisance benthic Cladophora growth.

Central-basin hypoxia has grown since the early 2000s, averaging about 4,500 km² with a peak of 8,800 km² in 2012 (the year after the record 2011 bloom), and a 2012 low-oxygen event coincided with a large fish kill along 40 km of Ontario shoreline.

The plan's strategy targets phosphorus specifically (treated as the growth-limiting nutrient) and explicitly flags, via a cited study, that phosphorus reduction alone may not reduce bloom toxicity even if it shrinks bloom extent.

Whether phosphorus-load reductions are warranted in Lake Erie's Eastern Basin (where the main nuisance is benthic Cladophora rather than planktonic cyanobacteria) is explicitly unresolved; the U.S. and Canada deferred setting Eastern Basin targets and committed only to re-evaluate the question in 2020.

The plan attributes the post-1990s bloom resurgence to several concurrent, non-quantified factors: a shift toward more bioavailable dissolved (vs. particulate) phosphorus, invasive zebra/quagga mussels recycling nutrients nearshore, greater water clarity extending algal growth to depth, wetland/riparian loss, warming (longer growing seasons), and more intense spring storms delivering nutrients at critical times.

The plan's practical management levers are organized around a 10-point joint action list (Great Lakes Commission LENT working group, endorsed 2015), spanning agricultural nutrient-application restrictions, 4R nutrient stewardship certification, municipal discharger phosphorus cuts, green infrastructure, a 5-year phase-out of residential phosphorus fertilizer, and an explicit adaptive-management commitment to revisit the targets themselves.

title: USGS National Water Quality Network (NWQN)
year: 2025 (undated, continuously-updated program page; cites data/reports through 2024)
what: The National Water Quality Network (NWQN) is a USGS national monitoring program, established in 2013, that collects standardized water-quality data from surface-water (streams/rivers) and groundwater sites across the U.S. to answer "What is the water quality of our Nation's streams, rivers, and groundwater?" and "How a

claims.c: The NWQN was established in 2013 to provide standardized data on the status of, and trends in, the Nation's stream, river, and groundwater quality, supporting management and policy at multiple levels of government.
The network currently comprises 102 surface-water monitoring sites and 258 groundwater sampling locations.

The groundwater network is structured into 80 regional trend groundwater networks and 22 public-supply groundwater networks, and the 22 public-supply networks cover the large majority of public-supply groundwater use nationally.
In 2024 the groundwater trends network contained 2,114 total monitoring sites, of which 228 were actually sampled that year, plus 30 supplemental wells sampled for PFAS characterization.

Surface-water sites are typically sampled 12–20 times per year depending on site type, with continuous (high-frequency) water-quality monitoring at 12 river sites and continuous temperature monitoring at most minimally disturbed reference sites.

Groundwater sites are resampled on roughly a 10-year cycle rather than annually, on the stated rationale that groundwater quality changes more slowly than surface water, with about 8 of the regional networks sampled in any given year on a rotating basis.

Field methods are standardized network-wide via the USGS National Field Manual, on-site sensors record temperature, specific conductance, dissolved oxygen, pH, and turbidity, and laboratory panels (nutrients, major ions, pesticides, PFAS, tritium, radionuclides, suspended sediment, microbiological contaminants, VOCs) are each applied only to specific site subsets rather than network-wide.
The current network's site history traces back to the early 1990s and, after NAWQA ended in 2022, absorbed sites from four predecessor programs: NAWQA, NASQAN, NMN, and HBN.

NWQN's federal authorization explicitly includes the Harmful Algal Blooms and Hypoxia Research and Control Act of 1998, under which USGS produced a national HAB/hypoxia strategy and a separate targeted Great Lakes strategy, but the page's own list of measured constituents (nutrients, sediment, general chemistry, contaminants) does not name any bloom-specific parameter such as chlorophyll-a, cyanobacterial cell counts, or cyanotoxins (e.g., microcystin).

NWQN data and two purpose-built trend tools are publicly available: "Water-Quality Changes in the Nation's Streams and Rivers" (annual surface-water nutrient/sediment trend summaries) and "Decadal Change in Groundwater Quality" (decadal groundwater trends in pesticides, nutrients, metals, and organic contaminants), both accessed via USGS Water Data for the Nation.

258 groundwater sampling locations

2,114 total groundwater monitoring sites in the trends network (2024)
228 of those sites actually sampled in 2024

Continuous water-quality monitoring at 12 river sites
Groundwater sites resampled once every 10 years

Although the page invokes the Harmful Algal Blooms and Hypoxia Research and Control Act of 1998 as a legal authority and describes USGS-authored national/Great Lakes HAB strategies, the constituent list given for NWQN itself contains no bloom-specific analyte (no chlorophyll-a, no cyanobacterial cell counts, no microcystin/cyanotoxin p

title: Water Quality Portal | U.S. Geological Survey
what: A short USGS "tool" catalog/landing page (dated March 4, 2019) that describes the Water Quality Portal (WQP) — a unified web search interface integrating publicly available water-quality data from USGS NWIS/BioData, EPA STORET, and USDA-ARS STEWARDS — and points users to the actual portal at waterqualitydata.us; it is

claims.c: The Water Quality Portal integrates and provides access to publicly available water-quality data from multiple federal/state source databases (USGS NWIS and BioData, EPA STORET, USDA-ARS STEWARDS) through one single search interface.
Data in the portal is contributed by USGS, EPA, and a large additional set of other agencies, described as over 400 state, federal, tribal, and local agencies.
The actual query/download interface for the integrated data lives at a separate site, www.waterqualitydata.us, not on this USGS page itself.

This specific overview page is administratively tied to USGS's Water Resources Mission Area and was published March 4, 2019, filed under Web Tools > Data Access Tools, with topical association to the Idaho, New Jersey, New York, and Upper Midwest Water Science Centers.

numbers: over 400 (state, federal, tribal, and local agencies contributing data to the portal — approximate/lower-bound count, no uncertainty or exact figure given)
Page date: March 4, 2019 (page publication/last-noted date, not a data-currency date)
tensions: None directly, since the page makes no scientific or HAB-related claims. The main tension for our use is methodological/traceability: this page alone cannot support any claim about WQP's data quality, coverage, or limitations (e.g., provisional-data flags, chlorophyll-a/nutrient parameter availability, record counts) — those would require fetching the actual portal or its API docs; citing only this landing page for anything beyond "WQP exists, is run by USGS/EPA/USDA-ARS, and aggregates 400+ agencies" would overstate what this source supports.

title: Watershed Protection: Clean Lakes Case Study: Phosphorus Inactivation and Wetland Manipulation Improve Kezar Lake, NH
what: A U.S. EPA Clean Lakes Program case study (EPA 841-F-95-002, EPA Office of Water, published circa 1995) describing a New Hampshire Department of Environmental Services-led restoration of Kezar Lake, NH, that combined an in-lake aluminum-salt (alum) phosphorus-inactivation treatment with upstream Chadwick Meadows wetlan

claims.c: Eliminating most of the external (point-source) phosphorus load did not stop Kezar Lake's algal blooms, implicating internal (sediment) phosphorus loading as the sustaining cause and the rationale for an alum-based, sediment-targeting treatment.
Kezar Lake is a small, shallow, dimictic, phosphorus-limited lake with a well-defined morphometry used to frame the whole restoration analysis.

The main 1984 alum (aluminum sulfate + sodium aluminate) treatment covered 48 hectares at 40 g Al/m3, cost $65,604 total ($1,367/ha; 0.50 person-days/ha), applied by a custom-modified weed harvester rather than a barge, and was markedly cheaper per hectare and per labor-day than the barge-based Annabessacook Lake, ME comparator.
A separate, smaller pilot-scale aluminum application treated only 10 hectares of Kezar Lake, at a dose recorded in the source text as far lower/differently-united than the main 48-ha treatment.

Copper sulfate algicide was applied before the aluminum treatment specifically to release phosphorus that was bound up in phytoplankton, and a weed harvester was mechanically modified to apply the aluminum salts instead of using a barge.

Raising water levels at the 20-hectare Chadwick Meadows wetland removed more than 80% of incoming phosphorus during the summer growing season, but the wetland's overall, year-round effectiveness at removing phosphorus from Lion Brook was poor, and it could act as a phosphorus source rather than a sink during high-flow fall/spring periods.

Water-quality improvements from the 1984 treatment were not permanent: most monitored parameters showed their strongest response for roughly 2-4 years before drifting back toward pre-treatment conditions, though total phosphorus and transparency remained somewhat improved beyond that window and dissolved aluminum showed no impact at all.

The source proposes four specific, hedged (not confirmed) hypotheses for why treated water-quality parameters drifted back toward pre-treatment conditions: saturation of aluminum phosphorus-binding sites, downward migration of the aluminum floc through the sediment, continued tributary phosphorus inputs, and hypolimnetic anoxia re-releasing sediment phosphorus.
No water-column impact from dissolved aluminum was detected as a result of the treatment.

The treatment period coincided with a shift in zooplankton community composition (fewer cladocerans, loss of Daphnia as co-dominant, more Keratella, fewer Polyartha, more ciliates) that the source associates with altered food-web structure following the phytoplankton shift, and this altered community had not reverted as of 1988.

Later monitoring (through 1994) suggests year-to-year water-quality variability at Kezar Lake tracked annual precipitation, with the wet years 1988 and 1993 showing degraded conditions and the 1995 drought showing the best recorded transparency and lowest productivity - a confound the source uses to qualify how much of the water-quality trend can be attributed to the treatment itself.
Recreational use of the lake increased after restoration, with attendance at the adjoining Wadleigh State Park rising by roughly 2,000 visitors per summer in 1984 and 1986.

The project was executed and funded under the EPA Clean Lakes Program (Section 314 of the Clean Water Act), structured as a Phase I diagnostic/feasibility study beginning in 1980 followed by a Phase II restoration/protection project beginning in 1984.

Lake surface area: 73.5 ha (verbatim: 'Lake area 73.5 ha')
Lake volume: 1,975,500 m3
Shoreline length: 3,400 m
Mean depth: 2.7 m; Maximum depth: 8.2 m
Elevation: 276 m above sea level

Chadwick Meadows wetland area: 20 hectares (verbatim: 'the 20-hectare Chadwick Meadows')

External (point-source) phosphorus load eliminated by wastewater facility closure: 71 percent ('eliminating 71 percent of the external phosphorus load')
tensions: This case study complicates any HAB framing that treats current external nutrient loading (e.g., watershed land-use or point-source proxies, including those derivable from remote sensing/land-cover data) as sufficient to explain bloom risk: here a 71% cut in external phosphorus load did not stop blooms because of internal/legacy sediment phosphorus, implying models built only on contemporaneous external-loading signals could miss a dominant driver in P-legacy lakes. It also complicates a "treatment solves the problem" narrative: the alum treatm

title: What Are Probability Surveys?
year: Page footer states "Last updated on April 15, 2026"; no original publication date is given on the page.
what: A short EPA "National Aquatic Resource Surveys" (NARS) program webpage that defines "probability surveys" (random-site-selection sample/statistical surveys) as the general survey-design concept NARS uses to assess the condition of the nation's waters, with a map of the 2013-2014 National Rivers and Streams Assessment s

claims.c: Probability (a.k.a. sample- or statistical-) surveys select sampling sites randomly, and each site represents a specific portion of the total resource/population of interest (e.g., all river and stream length in the nation).

Because site selection is random/statistical, results from the sampled sites can be extrapolated to the entire population of interest, which the page says makes probability surveys well suited for unbiased assessment of a whole resource across large areas without monitoring every waterbody.
EPA's National Aquatic Resource Surveys (NARS) use probability-based survey designs specifically to meet the objective of assessing the condition of the nation's waters.

Probability surveys are a general statistical method used across many fields (e.g., medical studies, political opinion polls) when a population of interest is too large to sample every individual/unit.

As a worked example of the design, the 2013-2014 National Rivers and Streams Assessment randomly selected its original sampling sites so that they were distributed across the entire conterminous United States.

numbers: No sample sizes, percentages, confidence levels, margins of error, or other quantitative statistics are stated anywhere on this page.
Only date/identifier present: '2013-2014' — the survey cycle name of the National Rivers and Streams Assessment, shown in the map image caption; not attached to any numeric statistic.
Footer date: page 'Last updated on April 15, 2026' (site metadata, not a study finding).
tensions: This page makes a general design-based statistics claim — that random site selection yields "unbiased assessments" extrapolatable to a whole population — but this is a claim about representativeness of the sampled *population/frame*, not a claim about temporal sensitivity. It does not address (and a reader should not assume it addresses) whether infrequent, multi-year probability-survey snapshots (e.g., NARS/National Lakes Assessment index-period visits) can characterize fast-changing, time-variant phenomena such as harmful algal blooms, which

title: Copper Algaecides — HCB-1 (Strategies for Preventing and Managing Harmful Cyanobacterial Blooms)
what: A section of ITRC's web-based guidance document HCB-1 ("Strategies for Preventing and Managing Harmful Cyanobacterial Blooms"), published 2021, that describes copper-based algaecides (copper sulfate, acidified copper products, and chelated products such as copper ethanolamine, copper citrate, and copper gluconate) as a

claims.c: Copper algaecides have a long history of use against algae and cyanobacteria and have been extensively studied in the peer-reviewed literature covering efficacy, environmental fate of copper, and off-target aquatic life impacts.

Copper algaecides are typically deployed as a reactive/intervention measure once cyanobacteria reach high cell densities, begin producing cyanotoxins, or produce taste-and-odor compounds that interfere with water use.

Successful, low-risk use of copper algaecides depends on treatment timing, and sites with recurring HCB problems benefit from a documented management plan with explicit monitoring triggers (cell density, cyanotoxin concentration, or taste/odor compound concentration) rather than ad hoc treatment decisions.

Copper algaecide products (copper sulfate, acidified copper, and chelated forms including copper ethanolamine, copper citrate, and copper gluconate) are registered with the U.S. EPA for algae/cyanobacteria control, and specific application methods for lakes, reservoirs, ponds, and irrigation canals are governed by the individual product's EPA-approved label.

The degree and outcome of cyanobacterial response to copper algaecide treatment is not uniform — it varies with the target cyanobacteria's innate copper sensitivity and abundance, exposure duration, and site-specific water characteristics including hardness, alkalinity, conductivity, and pH.

Killing cyanobacterial cells with copper algaecide (or any algaecide) can lyse the cells and release previously intracellular cyanotoxins into the water column as dissolved toxin, creating a potential exposure risk distinct from the risk of the intact bloom.

Copper treatments can cause dissolved oxygen to decline as a secondary effect of decomposition of the algae/cyanobacteria killed by treatment, and this is a distinct operational risk from the toxin-release risk.

The guidance recommends staging copper algaecide applications (i.e., not treating an entire bloom/waterbody in a single pass) to avoid or manage the risk of significant post-treatment dissolved-oxygen decline.

General best-practice guidance in the HCB-1 document (applicable to algaecides and coagulation compounds generally, including copper products) is to use the minimum effective dose and to treat preferably during early stages of bloom development rather than after a bloom is already dense.

Dissolved organic carbon (DOC), pH, alkalinity, and hardness are treated as toxicity-modifying factors (TMFs) that govern how much free/bioavailable copper is present, and therefore how toxic a given nominal copper dose is to target and non-target organisms in a given waterbody.

tensions: This ITRC guidance treats copper algaecide cell-lysis and dissolved-oxygen decline as manageable, staging-mitigable operational risks within a broader "monitor, trigger, treat" management framework — a more permissive, use-it-carefully stance than sources that emphasize copper accumulation in sediment or non-target ecotoxicity as reasons to avoid or minimize copper treatment altogether. It also implicitly frames copper algaecide as a late-stage reactive intervention (triggered once cyanotoxins, cell density, or taste/odor already exceed a thres

title: Cyanobacterial toxins: microcystins — Background document for development of WHO Guidelines for drinking-water quality and Guidelines for safe recreational water environments (WHO/HEP/ECH/WSH/2020.6)
what: A 2020 WHO background document that reviews toxicokinetic, human, and animal toxicological evidence on microcystins (a family of cyanobacterial cyclic-peptide hepatotoxins) to derive provisional health-based guideline values for microcystins in drinking-water and recreational water, replacing WHO's 2003 microcystin-LR-

claims.c: WHO's provisional guideline values (GVs) for microcystins are 1 µg/L (lifetime drinking-water), 12 µg/L (short-term drinking-water) and 24 µg/L (recreational water), derived from a mouse study of MC-LR corroborated by a similar rat study, with the liver identified as the most sensitive organ.

The short-term drinking-water GV is meant only to bound temporary exceedances (up to 2 weeks) while treatment is upgraded, not repeated seasonal exceedance of the lifetime GV, and is derived for adults; bottle-fed infants and small children are recommended an alternative water source if concentrations exceed 3 µg/L, even briefly.

Microcystins are described as the most commonly reported cyanobacterial toxins worldwide, produced mainly by freshwater cyanobacteria with wide geographic/ecological distribution; drinking-water is identified as the most likely human exposure route, with recreational contact during blooms as another potentially high-concentration pathway, and limited data suggesting some food accumulation.

The document attributes high cyanobacterial biomass to nutrient loading from anthropogenic sources (agricultural runoff, wastewater) and, on that basis, recommends nutrient-source control as the primary long-term bloom-management lever.

Conventional drinking-water treatment (coagulation, flocculation, filtration, chlorination) is generally sufficient to bring microcystins to acceptable levels; where it is not, ozonation and activated carbon (filtration or powdered addition) can be effective supplementary steps.

Mechanistically, microcystins are actively imported into cells via organic anion transport proteins (OATPs); because the liver highly expresses OATPs, microcystins act primarily as hepatotoxins, though other OATP-expressing tissues can be affected. Intracellularly they bind protein phosphatases with high affinity, disrupting cytoskeletal, cell-replication, stress-response and DNA-repair pathways, producing acute intrahepatic haemorrhage and, chronically, impaired tumour-proliferation control.

Because the GVs are derived from the single congener MC-LR while environmental exposure is generally to mixtures of MC variants, the document states that comparing total MC concentration (gravimetric or molar) to the GV is 'likely' to be protective in most circumstances — a hedged, not absolute, extrapolation.

The document notes an open scientific disagreement: some recent studies suggest microcystin effects on reproductive organs at lower concentrations than those underlying the liver-based GVs, but these findings are contradicted by earlier studies and are described as requiring corroboration.

Microcystins are cyclic heptapeptides first characterized in the early 1980s (named for Microcystis aeruginosa); more than 250 distinct variants have been identified, with molecular weights of 882–1101 Da; most are hydrophilic and highly water-soluble, with saturation concentrations several orders of magnitude above the WHO guideline values; microcystins and the related nodularins are also described as extremely heat-stable.

numbers: Lifetime drinking-water guideline value (provisional): 1 µg/L microcystin
Short-term drinking-water guideline value (provisional): 12 µg/L
Recreational water guideline value (provisional): 24 µg/L
Precautionary threshold for bottle-fed infants/small children: 3 µg/L (alternative water source recommended above this even for short periods)
Short-term GV validity window: up to 2 weeks (temporary, until treatment augmented; not for repeated seasonal exceedance)
>250 distinct microcystin variants identified to date
Microcystin molecular weight range: 882–1101 Da

No explicit numeric uncertainty factors, confidence intervals, or point-of-departure/TDI values are given in the extracted excerpt itself (that quantitative derivation is in Section 8.1, outside the provided window); GVs
tensions: The source explicitly flags an unresolved internal tension: some recent studies report microcystin effects on reproductive organs at concentrations below those used to derive the liver-based guideline values, but these are "contradicted by earlier studies" and unconfirmed — a live discrepancy in the toxicology literature as of 2020, relevant to any HAB source that treats the WHO liver-based GVs as the definitive human-health threshold. Separately, the statement that "the main driver of high amounts of cyanobacterial biomass is nutrients from an

title: Cyanobacterial toxins: Microcystins — chemical fact sheet supporting WHO Guidelines for drinking-water quality and safe recreational water environments
year: 2022 (fact sheet compiled as part of WHO Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality, 4th ed. incorporating the 1st and 2nd addenda, per WHO's publications page, confirmed via WebSearch); the in-text guideline-value assessment date for microcystin-LR itself is given as 2003, with an added note that IARC classified microcystin-LR in 2006
what: A WHO chemical fact sheet (Chapter 12 of the Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality) that lays out the health-based provisional drinking-water guideline value, tolerable daily intake, analytical detection limits/methods, monitoring approach, and prevention/treatment options for microcystin-LR, the best-studied cyanobact

claims.c: Microcystins are presented as the best-researched cyanobacterial toxin group and are said to probably occur most frequently in fresh waters among cyanotoxins.

Microcystins are usually cell-bound (intracellular) and are released into surrounding water mainly upon cell rupture/lysis, whereas other cyanotoxins (cylindrospermopsins, saxitoxins, anatoxin-a, anatoxin-a(s)) tend to occur to a larger extent already dissolved — a distinction the source flags as relevant to drinking-water treatment efficacy.

Human exposure to microcystins is largely via drinking-water or recreational contact with cyanobacterial blooms, though microcystins may also occur in fish, molluscs and shellfish from affected water bodies.

More than 80 microcystin variants have been identified, but only a few occur frequently or at high concentration; microcystin-LR is among the most frequent and most toxic congeners and is the only one with a toxicological database sufficient to derive a (provisional) guideline value.
Cyanobacterial genera frequently reported to contain microcystins are Microcystis, Planktothrix and Anabaena.
WHO's provisional drinking-water guideline value for total microcystin-LR (free plus cell-bound) is 0.001 mg/l (1 µg/l).

The guideline value is explicitly labeled provisional because it covers only microcystin-LR, the underlying toxicological database is limited, and new cyanotoxin toxicity data continue to be generated.

The tolerable daily intake (TDI) underpinning the guideline value is 0.04 µg/kg body weight, derived from liver pathology observed in a 13-week oral mouse study, with a 1000-fold uncertainty factor applied because of database gaps, notably the absence of chronic-toxicity and carcinogenicity data.

Three analytical methods for microcystins are compared: HPLC (limit of detection 0.1-1 µg/l), ELISA immunoassay (0.1-0.5 µg/l), and protein phosphatase assay (0.5-1.5 µg/l); HPLC is described as most precise and able to differentiate congeners, while the immunoassay and phosphatase assay are less precise but useful for screening.

WHO's preferred cyanotoxin monitoring approach is visual/microscopic surveillance of source water for rising cyanobacterial cell density or bloom-forming potential, with heightened vigilance once such events occur, rather than routine measurement of finished-water toxin concentration, which the source calls unsatisfactory for safety determination.

Failing to extract the cell-bound (intracellular) toxin fraction during analysis causes a large underestimate of true cyanotoxin concentration, even though this extraction step is described as easy to perform for microcystins.

Mechanistically, microcystin-LR is a potent inhibitor of eukaryotic protein serine/threonine phosphatases 1 and 2A; the liver is the primary toxicological target because microcystins cross cell membranes chiefly via the bile acid transporter.

The guideline-value derivation drew on an oral 13-week mouse study supported by an oral 44-day pig study; the source also notes recorded livestock/wildlife poisonings and published evidence of tumour promotion, and states that in 2006 IARC classified microcystin-LR as a possible human carcinogen (Group 2B).

Blooms are described as occurring when phosphorus and nitrogen concentrations are elevated, particularly in stagnant or very slowly flowing water bodies; blooms recur in the same water bodies, can form surface scums or accumulate at the thermocline of stratified reservoirs, can develop rapidly, and vary in duration from hours to weeks, often on a seasonal pattern.

The most sustainable and effective bloom-prevention measure identified is reducing nutrient loading — particularly phosphorus — into the water body, via control of sewage effluent and catchment sources (erosion, manure, fertilizer); hydrological management such as mixing and flushing can additionally shift the plankton community away from cyanobacteria toward less health-relevant taxa such as diatoms.

For treatment, particle-removal processes (soil/riverbank filtration, flocculation and filtration, dissolved air filtration) are described as effectively controlling the cell-bound microcystin fraction when optimized and operated to avoid cell rupture; free (dissolved) microcystins are said to be well removed by most activated carbon and by chlorination/ozonation at sufficient dose and contact time; potassium permanganate is effective for microcystins specifically (with limited/no data for other cyanotoxins); oxidation is 'not very effective' for saxitoxins; and chlorine dioxide and chloramine are stated to be ineffective for removing cyanotoxins generally.

numbers: Provisional drinking-water guideline value, total microcystin-LR (free + cell-bound): 0.001 mg/l (1 µg/l) — explicitly labeled provisional/uncertain by the source
Tolerable daily intake (TDI): 0.04 µg/kg body weight — derived using a 1000-fold uncertainty factor applied to a 13-week oral mouse study (liver pathology as critical effect)
Limit of detection, HPLC: 0.1–1 µg/l (after methanol extraction or C-18 concentration)
Limit of detection, ELISA immunoassay: 0.1–0.5 µg/l (less precise than HPLC)
Limit of detection, protein phosphatase assay: 0.5–1.5 µg/l (less precise than HPLC)
Guideline-value derivation defaults: 80% of TDI allocated to drinking-water; 60 kg default adult body weight; 2 litres/day default water consumption
More than 80 distinct microcystin congeners identified to date (only a few reported to occur frequently or at high concentration)
Supporting toxicology: 13-week oral mouse study (primary basis) plus a 44-day oral pig study (supporting)
IARC carcinogen classification: Group 2B (possible human carcinogen), assigned in 2006
Assessment date given for the microcystin-LR guideline value: 2003
tensions: Nothing in this excerpt directly contradicts other HAB sources, but it complicates two things a remote-sensing/in-situ HAB tool might want to claim. First, the source explicitly calls finished-water toxin-concentration monitoring "unsatisfactory" and instead endorses source-water surveillance for bloom/cell-density evidence — this supports using optical/cell-density bloom proxies (e.g., satellite cyanobacteria or chlorophyll signals) as an early-warning input, but the source frames such surveillance as a proxy for bloom presence/potential, not

title: Global Lake Ecological Observatory Network (GLEON) — Working Groups
year: n.d. (undated directory page; individual working-group founding years cited on the page range from 2010–2023)
what: A directory/landing page on GLEON's website listing the network's ten active thematic working groups (e.g., Cyanobacteria, Remote Sensing, Lake Modelling, Community Science, Winter Limnology), each with a short description of its founding meeting and research focus; it is an organizational overview, not a research pape

claims.c: GLEON's active thematic working groups convene during the network's 'All Hands' Meetings.

The Cyanobacteria working group, which first convened at the GLEON 8 meeting in Hamilton, New Zealand, is investigating whether repeatable patterns in physical buoy (in-situ sensor) variables occur before or after cyanobacterial blooms, across the four major cyanobacterial groups.

The Community Science working group began as an ad hoc citizen-science group at GLEON 17 in Korea in 2015 and was formally established at GLEON 18 in Austria in 2016, partnering with the sister network NETLAKE to launch lake-based citizen science on a global scale.
The Lake Modelling working group was established in 2010 to use the GLEON network for developing and applying lake ecosystem models.
The Remote Sensing working group first met in person at the GLEON 2022 meeting in New York and focuses on using remote sensing to examine broad-scale, climate-driven changes in lakes.

The Reservoirs & Lake Management working group was formed at the GLEON 14 meeting in Ireland and addresses research and management of waterbodies used for water supply, power generation, and recreation.
The Freshwater Biological Interactions working group was reorganized out of the former 'Theory Group (TTG)' at the GLEON 2023 meeting in Poland.
The Lake Metabolism & Carbon Cycling working group focuses on lake gross primary production (GPP) and ecosystem respiration (R), as well as the broader carbon cycle.
The Synthesis working group was established at the GLEON 2022 meeting in New York to review GLEON's collective contributions and coordinate cross-working-group initiatives.
The Winter Limnology working group first convened at the GLEON 19 meeting in New York and has already completed several projects.

As listed on this page, GLEON currently organizes exactly ten named active working groups: Climate & Lake Dynamics, Community Science, Cyanobacteria, Freshwater Biological Interactions, Lake Metabolism & Carbon Cycling, Lake Modelling, Remote Sensing, Reservoirs & Lake Management, Synthesis, and Winter Limnology.
GLEON's working-group program is supported in part by an anonymous donor and the Cary Institute of Ecosystem Studies.
