tensions: This page reports no empirical findings of its own, so there is nothing here to directly contradict other HAB studies. The one notable interpretive tension is epistemic rather than factual: the Cyanobacteria working group frames pre/post-bloom patterns in physical buoy variables as an open, actively-investigated hypothesis ('examines whether... exist... in repeatable ways for the four major cyanobacterial groups'), not a settled or generalizable signal. That framing cautions against treating 'in-situ high-frequency sensors reliably detect bloom

title: Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water, 2nd Edition — Chapter 4: Understanding the occurrence of cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins
year: 2021 (confirmed via WebSearch of WHO and Taylor & Francis/Routledge listings for "Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water" 2nd edition, Chorus & Welker eds.; not stated within the provided chapter excerpt itself)
what: Chapter 4 ("Understanding the occurrence of cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins") of the WHO reference book "Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water," 2nd edition — a review chapter synthesizing the physiological traits (nutrient storage, nitrogen fixation, buoyancy regulation, light harvesting, carbon concentration, grazing and viral

claims.c: The chapter explicitly cautions that presence of cyanobacteria does not automatically mean a health risk, and that assuming a tight, general link between eutrophication and cyanobacterial/toxin risk is too simplistic: some taxa (e.g., Planktothrix rubescens) tend to decrease under eutrophic conditions, while others (e.g., Microcystis) can reach hazardous levels at much reduced nutrient concentrations if a relatively low biomass concentrates into shoreline scums.

Cyanobacteria store surplus phosphorus intracellularly as polyphosphate in amounts exceeding immediate demand, letting a cell undergo multiple divisions after external P supply is exhausted below detectable levels.

Only a subset of cyanobacterial genera (e.g., Aphanizomenon, Dolichospermum) can fix atmospheric N2 ("diazotrophy") via specialized oxygen-free cells (heterocytes), giving a competitive edge over eukaryotic microalgae when other nitrogen sources are scarce; non-fixing genera such as Microcystis and Planktothrix can still benefit indirectly from nitrogen leaking from co-occurring N-fixing taxa.
Nitrogen fixation is energetically costly (heterocytes must maintain anoxic internal conditions while surrounded by oxygenated water), and the chapter states this process is limited in turbid water.
Thermal stratification, a key physical precondition for buoyant cyanobacteria to concentrate near the surface, is given a specific quantitative definition by the chapter.

Buoyancy in gas-vesiculate cyanobacteria is described as regulated most importantly by light (more gas vesicles produced under low irradiance; vesicles diluted by growth or collapsed by high turgor pressure under high irradiance), producing diel vertical migration with populations most buoyant at night and losing buoyancy during the day.

Different bloom-forming taxa show characteristically different vertical distribution patterns in stratified waterbodies: surface-bloom/scum formers (Microcystis, Dolichospermum, Aphanizomenon), near-homogeneous water-column distributions with only exceptional scum formation (Planktothrix agardhii), and deep metalimnetic/deep-chlorophyll-maximum layer formers (red-pigmented Planktothrix rubescens in deep stratified lakes; green-pigmented Aphanizomenon flosaquae and Dolichospermum reported to form metalimnetic layers lasting months; Raphidiopsis/Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii in deep, low-light layers of stratified subtropical reservoirs).

The chapter's own schematic (Figure 4.2) illustrates that, for a hypothetical buoyant Microcystis-type population, wind-driven physical concentration alone (independent of any biomass growth) could in principle span roughly five orders of magnitude in cell density and toxin concentration between a fully-mixed "low risk" state and a wind-concentrated shoreline scum "very high risk" state — but the chapter explicitly flags these numbers as illustrative only, not measured or reference values.

Resistance of cyanobacteria to zooplankton grazing is inconsistent across studies and remains scientifically debated: some grazers (e.g., Daphnia carinata) have been observed feeding on toxic Microcystis, while other experiments found daphnids preferentially avoided toxic Microcystis in favor of green algae/diatoms; outcomes appear to depend on the grazer population's prior exposure/co-evolutionary history with cyanobacteria.

Cyanophage (virus) infection is documented as one possible mechanism of Microcystis bloom termination and toxin release, but the same host genus showed the opposite outcome (lysogeny, prolonging bloom persistence) in a different lake in the same bloom year, so the chapter does not present viral lysis as a universal or reliably predictable driver.

numbers: One cyanobacterial cell can complete 8–16 cell divisions using stored intracellular polyphosphate without any further external phosphorus uptake (dimensionless count; Reynolds, 2006, as cited).
Thermal stratification (limnological definition used by the chapter): water-temperature difference of more than 1 °C per 1 m of depth.

Figure 4.2 illustrative wind-concentration schematic, explicitly labeled 'crude estimates...must not be taken as reference values': (a) fully-mixed/'low' risk ≈ 10^7 cells/L, chlorophyll-a 2 µg/L, microcystin (MC) 1 µg/L
Assumed average toxin cell quota underlying the Figure 4.2 estimates: 100 fg microcystin (MC) per cell (chapter's Box 4.8).
Raw-water microcystin concentration spike at Toledo, Ohio's drinking-water intake, associated with a 2014 Lake Erie Microcystis cyanophage lytic event: >5 µg/L (Steffen et al., 2017, as cited).

Approximately 1 cyanophage marker transcript per host (Microcystis) marker transcript detected at bloom termination in the 2014 Lake Erie metatranscriptomic study (Steffen et al., 2017, as cited), described as implying a
tensions: The chapter's central framing caveat — that a tight link between eutrophication/nutrient loading and cyanobacterial or toxin risk is "too restrictive" because taxa and physical concentration mechanisms (e.g., wind-driven scum formation) can decouple toxin risk from nutrient status — is in tension with simpler HAB screening approaches that use nutrient concentration or trophic state alone as a proxy for bloom/toxin risk; this source suggests such proxies could under- or over-state risk depending on taxa and physical concentration dynamics. title: Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water: A Guide to Their Public Health Consequences, Monitoring and Management
what: A WHO-commissioned edited reference guide (1st edition, 1999; eds. Ingrid Chorus and Jamie Bartram) that synthesizes expert consensus on cyanobacterial ecology, cyanotoxins, and human/animal health effects into operational guidance for protecting drinking-water and recreational water bodies. Per its own Table of Conten

claims.c: The only known human fatalities associated with cyanobacteria and their toxins occurred via renal dialysis exposure in Caruaru, Brazil, killing over 50 patients.
Non-fatal human poisoning outbreaks attributed to toxic cyanobacteria have been reported in Australia (contaminated drinking water) and the UK (army recruits exposed while swimming and canoeing).

Severe acute human-health effects from cyanotoxins appear rare, but the scale and nature of long-term effects (e.g., tumour promotion, liver damage) and milder short-term effects (e.g., contact irritation) remain poorly understood.
The guide's content was produced via a defined sequence of WHO-convened international expert meetings and reviews rather than a single original study.
Total phosphorus concentrations below 0.1 mg/L are described as sufficient to induce a cyanobacterial bloom.
A regional lake survey found that 54% of Asia-Pacific lakes, 53% of European lakes, 28% of African lakes, 48% of North American lakes, and 41% of South American lakes were classified as eutrophic.

Decline in cyanobacterial blooms is described as lagging behind reductions in phosphorus inputs, because phytoplankton growth only becomes nutrient-controlled once concentrations fall below a threshold.
Bloom formation is framed as jointly dependent on temperature, light, and nutrient status together, not on nutrient level alone.

Temperate waters typically show a seasonal phytoplankton succession (diatoms/small flagellates in winter-spring, then green algae, then grazing-resistant taxa such as dinoflagellates, desmids and large yellow-green algae in late summer-autumn, with cyanobacteria often dominating summer phytoplankton in eutrophic/hypertrophic waters), whereas in the tropics seasonal environmental differences are often insufficient to displace cyanobacteria the way they are displaced in temperate systems.

The guide's full scope (per its own Table of Contents) spans cyanobacterial ecology and toxin classification, human/animal poisoning and toxicology, safe exposure levels, a risk-management framework including an "Alert Levels Framework," policy/legislation implementation, preventative measures (including phosphorus target values), remedial measures (including algicides and drinking-water treatment efficiency for cyanotoxin removal), and monitoring-programme design plus laboratory analytical methods for cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins.

numbers: Total phosphorus < 0.1 mg/L (mg P L⁻¹): stated as sufficient to induce a cyanobacterial bloom (Ch. 1.1); no confidence interval, sample size, or supporting citation given in the excerpt for this specific figure.

Eutrophic lake proportion by region — Asia Pacific 54%, Europe 53%, Africa 28%, North America 48%, South America 41% — from an ILEC/Lake Biwa Research Institute survey conducted 1988–1993 (Ch. 1.2); single historical sna
>50 patients died (Caruaru, Brazil) from cyanotoxin exposure via renal dialysis (Foreword); no incident date, toxin identity, total number exposed, or dose reported in the excerpt.

title: Toxic cyanobacteria in water: a guide to their public health consequences, monitoring and management — Second edition
what: An 859-page, 15-chapter WHO-edited (Chorus & Welker, 2021) technical guide that synthesizes the scientific and practical basis for identifying, assessing, monitoring, and managing public-health risk from cyanobacteria and their toxins in drinking-water and recreational-water settings. It is explicitly the technical bac

claims.c: The 2nd edition (published 28 Feb 2021, ISBN 978-1-003-08144-9, 859 pages) is organized into 15 chapters spanning the full water-use chain — cyanobacteria/cyanotoxin science, exposure, risk assessment, catchment nutrient control, waterbody condition management, drinking-water offtake and treatment, monitoring-programme design, field/laboratory methods, and public health surveillance/communication — and covers four priority cyanotoxin groups: anatoxin-a and analogues, cylindrospermopsins, microcystins, and saxitoxins.
This guide is explicitly the technical background document that WHO used to develop its 2020 drinking-water-quality guidelines and recreational-water-environment guidelines.

A chapter co-authored by Chorus & Zessner (2021) states that hazardous levels of cyanobacterial biomass are unlikely below a total phosphorus (TP) concentration of about 20 µg/L in general water bodies, or about 50 µg/L in shallow water bodies, with the corresponding total-nitrogen (TN) limitation threshold roughly 7 times higher.

A chapter attributed to Welker et al. (2021) identifies remote sensing as one method used to identify and track cyanobacterial blooms, but states this and related approaches still need further development before they are affordable and practical for routine use.

A chapter attributed to Ibelings et al. (2021) argues that climate-change effects on cyanobacterial bloom incidence are more complex than a simple direct-temperature growth-rate effect, because climate change alters hydrophysical conditions and nutrient concentrations in ways that can outweigh the direct temperature effect.

A chapter attributed to Burch, Brookes & Chorus (2021) states that deep, strong water-column mixing can suppress the proliferation of scum-forming cyanobacteria, even though many cyanobacteria otherwise tolerate mixing well.

A chapter attributed to Ibelings et al. (2021) describes a buoyancy-regulation mechanism in which cyanobacteria accumulate carbon (from photosynthesis near the surface) as ballast, causing them to sink and thereby determine their own vertical position in the water column.

A chapter attributed to Padisák et al. (2021) covers microscopy-based estimation of cyanobacterial biomass — quantifying both cell numbers and biovolume — as a laboratory method distinct from chlorophyll-a or phycocyanin pigment-based measurement.

A chapter attributed to Welker et al. (2021) frames monitoring-programme planning as requiring established routines with analytical laboratories, staff training, pilot-phase testing of the sampling scheme, and systematic data storage/documentation.

numbers: 859 pages (total length of the 2nd edition book)
15 chapters plus front matter

title: Welcome to the IOC Harmful Algal Bloom Website (GlobalHAB Programme)
year: n.d. (footer copyright © 2021; page content continuously updated, includes events/courses dated through 2027)
what: The official website of the IOC-UNESCO Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) Programme - an institutional portal (not a research paper) that describes the Programme's mission, its governing body IPHAB (IOC-FAO Intergovernmental Panel on Harmful Algal Blooms), the IOC-SCOR GlobalHAB research initiative, the HAIS/HAEDAT information

claims.c: The Programme's own framing statement asserts HABs as a worldwide coastal problem: every coastal country is potentially affected.
HABs are described as capable of killing marine life and causing human death.
The Programme's stated mandate is to help IOC member states build capacity to understand HAB causes, predict occurrence, and mitigate effects.
Concretely, this capacity-building takes the form of training in species identification, toxicity testing, and monitoring/management strategies.

The Harmful Algal Information System (HAIS) is the Programme's central data system: taxonomic-name data, HAB event/biogeographic information, an expert directory, and (added in 2021) an algal-toxin database.
The Global HAB Status Report (launched June 8, 2021) is described as the first-ever compiled overview of HAB events and their societal impacts worldwide.
Harmful Algae News (HAN) has been published as an IOC newsletter since 1992, covering toxic/harmful algal blooms, socio-economic impacts, research, and management activities.

IPHAB is characterized as "the only global intergovernmental effort" to understand, manage and mitigate HAB effects, and became jointly cosponsored with FAO (as IOC-FAO IPHAB) effective November 1, 2021.
IPHAB's 17th session (IPHAB-XVII) was held March 18-20, 2025 at UNESCO Headquarters in Paris; its 18th session (IPHAB-XVIII) is scheduled for March 16-18, 2027 at FAO Headquarters in Rome.

GlobalHAB (IOC-SCOR) is presented as the Programme's international collaborative-research arm, intended to advance international collaboration, improve predictive capability, and inform science and policy, organized around three focus areas (training; the international research programme; manuals & guides) with a Scientific Steering Committee and sub-committees.

As an applied, country-level example, UNESCO/IOC and Morocco's National Institute of Fisheries Research (INRH) began a joint project (workshop December 5-8, 2022) to develop a national HAB early-warning system.

The Programme's regional structure spans multiple ocean basins through named regional HAB networks: HANA (North Africa), IOC/WESTPAC-HAB, IOCAFRICA/HAB, ANCA (under IOCARIBE, Caribbean), and FANSA (South America).

The Publications page lists at least 31 distinct HAB-related technical publications spanning 1997-2026, including numbered institutional series such as IOC Manuals and Guides No. 88 and No. 59, IOC Technical Series No. 59 and No. 41, IOCCG Report Series No. 20, PICES Scientific Report No. 59, and FAO Fisheries and Aquaculture Technical Paper No. 551.

The Programme is hosted by the UNESCO/IOC Project Office for IODE in Oostende, Belgium (contact: hab.ioc@unesco.org), with a footer copyright dated 2021 even though the live site displays events and courses scheduled through 2027.

title: World Health Organization (WHO) 1999 Guideline Values for Cyanobacteria in Freshwater
year: 1999 (per page title); the WHO document EPA cites on the page as the source is dated 2003 and described as based on the 1999 guidelines
what: A U.S. EPA summary webpage (part of EPA's Harmful Algal Blooms / HABs program) that presents WHO-developed guidance values for the relative probability of adverse human health effects from recreational (contact/ingestion/inhalation) exposure to cyanobacteria in freshwater, expressed as a three-tier alert framework keye

claims.c: EPA's page summarizes WHO-developed guidance values expressing the relative probability of acute health effects from recreational exposure to cyanobacteria in freshwater, organized as three tiered 'Guidance Levels.'

Guidance Level 1 ('relatively low probability of adverse health effects') is triggered at 20,000 cyanobacterial cells/mL or, alternatively, 10 µg chlorophyll-a/litre; it is derived from a human bathing epidemiological study and is linked to short-term outcomes such as skin irritation and gastrointestinal illness.

Guidance Level 2 ('moderate probability of adverse health effects') is triggered at 100,000 cyanobacterial cells/mL or 50 µg chlorophyll-a/litre; it is derived from WHO's provisional drinking-water guideline value for microcystin-LR together with data on other cyanotoxins, and carries potential for long-term illness with some cyanobacterial species.

Guidance Level 3 ('high probability of adverse health effects') is defined qualitatively by cyanobacterial scum formation in whole-body-contact areas, not by a specific cell-count or chlorophyll-a number, and its basis is inference from oral animal lethal-poisoning data plus documented human poisoning case histories, with acute poisoning risk noted.
The guidance values are scoped specifically to recreational exposure pathways — contact, ingestion, and inhalation — during recreational use of freshwater, not to general environmental exposure.

EPA's page attributes the numeric guidance values to the WHO 2003 publication 'Guidelines for safe recreational water environments. Volume 1, Coastal and fresh waters,' which it describes as based on the WHO 1999 guidelines — i.e., the '1999 Guideline Values' in this source's title are documented on the page via a 2003 WHO publication rather than a document literally titled '1999 guideline values.'

Recommended management actions escalate by guidance level, from posting advisory signage and notifying authorities at Level 1, to watching for scum development at Level 2, up to immediate control of body contact (potentially prohibiting swimming) and a public health investigation at Level 3.

numbers: 20,000 (WHO/EPA render as "20 000") cyanobacterial cells/mL — Guidance Level 1 ("relatively low probability of adverse health effects") threshold
10 µg chlorophyll-a/litre — Guidance Level 1 alternative threshold (triggers same tier as the cell-count value; source uses "OR" between the two metrics)
100,000 (rendered "100 000") cyanobacterial cells/mL — Guidance Level 2 ("moderate probability of adverse health effects") threshold
50 µg chlorophyll-a/litre — Guidance Level 2 alternative threshold

Guidance Level 3 ("high probability of adverse health effects") — NO numeric cell-count or chlorophyll-a threshold given; defined only qualitatively as cyanobacterial scum formation in whole-body-contact areas

1999 = year of the underlying WHO guideline values per the page's own title; 2003 = publication year of the WHO document ('Guidelines for safe recreational water environments. Volume 1, Coastal and fresh waters') that th
No baseline, sample size, confidence interval, or error bar is given anywhere on the page for any of these threshold numbers
tensions: (1) Title/citation mismatch: the source's own title frames these as "1999 Guideline Values," but the document EPA actually cites on the page is the WHO 2003 publication "Guidelines for safe recreational water environments. Volume 1, Coastal and fresh waters," described as merely "based on" the 1999 guidelines — a reviewer should not treat this EPA page as a direct restatement of a document literally titled/dated 1999 without checking WHO's original texts. title: Global Lake Ecological Observatory Network (GLEON) — Cary Institute research project page
year: 2005 (GLEON's stated founding year); the page itself is undated and lists items through a "GLEON 2025 All Hands' Meeting"
what: A Cary Institute of Ecosystem Studies project-profile page describing the Global Lake Ecological Observatory Network (GLEON), a grassroots international research network (established 2005) that shares and synthesizes high-frequency, sensor-based (e.g., buoy) data from lakes worldwide to understand, predict, and communi

claims.c: GLEON was established in 2005 as a grassroots research network built around sharing and interpreting high-resolution/high-frequency lake sensor data.
GLEON operates as a global network-science effort in which high-frequency sensor data function as a shared 'common language' linking scientists, students, and community members across countries.
Dr. Kathleen Weathers led GLEON for close to a decade, from its early stage to a mature network, and two cited synthesis papers document the network's evolution over its first ten years.
GLEON's physical infrastructure includes environmental monitoring buoys deployed on member lakes (e.g., Lake Sunapee) that record environmental conditions as part of the global network.
GLEON runs Fellowship/Lake Expedition training programs that teach graduate-student cohorts to synthesize and analyze lake sensor data and to practice team science and leadership.
A GLEON-affiliated publication (Hamilton et al. 2015) explicitly frames the network's purpose as synthesizing high-frequency sensor data for validating deterministic ecological (lake) models.
GLEON-affiliated research has examined cyanobacteria as biological drivers of nitrogen and phosphorus cycling in lakes.
Content associated with Cary Institute/GLEON links Landsat 8 satellite imagery to algal bloom monitoring.
GLEON-affiliated research (Dugan et al. 2017, PNAS) found that North American freshwater lakes are experiencing increasing salinization, a lake stressor distinct from nutrient loading.
GLEON-linked research spans continental-scale ("macrosystems ecology") questions and cross-site drivers of water quality across the contiguous United States.

title: Harmful Algal Blooms: A Scientific Summary for Policy Makers
what: A 2015 policy-facing scientific synthesis on harmful algal blooms (HABs), published by UNESCO's Intergovernmental Oceanographic Commission (report IOC/INF-1320) and prepared by the SCOR-IOC Scientific Steering Committee of the GEOHAB programme (Global Ecology and Oceanography of Harmful Algal Blooms, 1998-2014). Per it

claims.c: HABs occur in nearly all aquatic environments and can harm aquatic ecosystems (plants and animals) and humans, either via direct exposure to water-borne toxins or via consumption of contaminated ('toxic') seafood.

The report's scope is organized around three primary areas — the causes of HABs, their impacts on ecosystems and society, and mitigation strategies — plus management issues associated with harmful algal events.

The report is an official IOC/UNESCO technical information document (series number IOC/INF-1320), prepared under the auspices of the GEOHAB programme's SCOR-IOC Scientific Steering Committee, giving it standing as an intergovernmental expert-consensus summary rather than a primary research study.

title: Stopping Algae Blooms With Bacteria-Busting Buoys
what: A press release (American Chemical Society "PressPac," syndicated on EurekAlert, phys.org, Mirage News, and Water Online) summarizing a peer-reviewed paper in ACS ES&T Water (DOI 10.1021/acsestwater.5c01257) in which University of Toledo engineers describe and lab-test a passive, "set it and forget it" floating buoy th

claims.c: Researchers built a passive, slow-release algaecide-delivery buoy, explicitly framed as a "set it and forget it" system, intended to be less labor-intensive and cheaper than existing algaecide-application methods that require frequent reapplication.
In laboratory testing with real cyanobacteria-containing Lake Erie water, the buoys eliminated nearly all cyanobacteria within about one week while non-target microbes were largely unaffected.

The buoys come in three sizes (small, medium, large), built from PVC pipe in a "T" or cross shape, with hydrogel disks inserted into the pipe openings to control diffusion of a commercial hydrogen-peroxide-based algaecide into the surrounding water; the buoy visibly tips onto its side when the algaecide is depleted, signaling a refill is needed.
The buoys reliably completed at least four consecutive algaecide-release cycles, each lasting 35 days, before depletion.

Small buoys were lab-tested in approximately 1 liter of cyanobacteria-containing water, monitored over about two weeks, with the water replaced daily using fresh lake water to simulate continuous cyanobacteria exposure.

Beyond beaker-scale lab tests, the research group also ran a pond-scale experiment under ambient/real weather conditions and, per the University of Toledo's own article, was planning a seasonal field deployment with water-treatment operators in northwest Ohio.

The research was funded by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers; the hydrogen-peroxide-based algaecide used in testing was supplied by SePRO Corporation; University of Toledo researchers Lapitsky, Kober, and Seo have filed a U.S. patent application on the buoy concept, and licensing discussions were underway as of the UToledo News article.
Lead researcher Yakov Lapitsky (Professor and Chair, Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Toledo) frames the practical payoff as conditional on further engineering work.

Co-researcher Youngwoo Seo (Professor, Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Toledo) frames the motivation as shifting bloom control from reactive/labor-intensive manual dosing toward earlier-stage intervention.

The source itself states further research is still needed, specifically to prevent microbial growth ('fouling') on the buoy's surface, and frames the whole concept as requiring successful scale-up before practical deployment; UToledo News separately describes the work as being at an 'early prototype stage' and reports researchers proposing larger buoys to cut the number of units needed for big water bodies from hundreds down to about a dozen.

The press coverage frames the research as motivated by the 2014 Lake Erie cyanobacterial bloom near Toledo, Ohio, which triggered a 'do not drink' water advisory affecting a large regional population.

numbers: 2014 — year of the Lake Erie cyanobacterial bloom near Toledo, Ohio that motivated the research (contextual/background, not a study result)

"Hundreds of thousands" of Toledo-area residents affected by the 2014 bloom per ACS-syndicated press release copies; University of Toledo News gives a more specific figure of 400,000 residents — order-of-magnitude consis
1 liter — volume of cyanobacteria-containing (Lake Erie) water used per lab beaker test
2 weeks — monitoring duration for small buoys in laboratory testing, with daily replacement of the water with fresh lake water
~1 week — time within which cyanobacteria were 'almost entirely eliminated' by the algaecide-releasing buoys in lab tests (no baseline die-off rate or control-buoy comparison reported)
4 — number of consecutive algaecide-release cycles the buoys reliably completed before depletion
35 days — duration of each release cycle
3 buoy sizes tested — small, medium, and large (PVC-pipe, 'T' or cross-shaped)
DOI 10.1021/acsestwater.5c01257 — journal ACS ES&T Water (DOI landing page itself returned HTTP 403 and could not be directly verified)

Secondary-outlet posting dates found for the same press release differ across mirrors: March 4, 2026 (per one fetch) vs. March 31, 2026 (per another) — not resolved because the primary ACS page and the DOI landing page b

~1 day — approximate timeframe in which cyanobacteria populations were reported (per a single fetch's paraphrase, not seen in a direct quotation) to rebound without sustained algaecide concentrations; lower-confidence fi
tensions: This is a small-scale (beaker/pond) engineering-intervention study, not a detection, forecasting, or driver-analysis study, so it does not directly bear on remote-sensing/in-situ HAB-prediction findings elsewhere in the literature; it is complementary (post-detection treatment) rather than contradictory to them. One general tension worth flagging for the wider review: the efficacy claim ("almost entirely eliminated within a week") comes from a controlled regime with a small, replenished cyanobacteria supply and a fixed algaecide-release rate in

title: BlueGreen Water Technologies Introduces BGi Water Health Intelligence Platform to Power Proactive, Scalable Water Management
what: A corporate press release (dated November 4, 2025) announcing BlueGreen Insights (BGi), a next-generation AI-powered remote-sensing analytics platform from BlueGreen Water Technologies that fuses satellite imagery with in-situ monitoring data to detect harmful algal blooms (HABs) and guide the company's own Lake Guard

claims.c: BGi is an evolution of the company's prior 'Lake Guard View' analytics platform into a next-generation AI-powered remote sensing platform.
BGi integrates satellite data with in-situ monitoring data to identify the first signs of HAB events and to guide precision application of the company's own Lake Guard treatments.
The platform's AI/model component was trained on more than five years of environmental and weather data.

The company's Lake Guard products are registered with the U.S. EPA and other regulatory bodies.

It also conflates a commercial monitoring product with it

title: Captain® Liquid Copper Algaecide — EPA-Registered Product Label (EPA Reg. No. 67690-9)
what: An EPA-registered aquatic pesticide product label (EPA Reg. No. 67690-9) for Captain®, a chelated (ethanolamine-complexed) copper algaecide/herbicide manufactured and distributed by SePRO Corporation. It specifies the product's composition, approved use sites, application methods and dose rates, safety precautions, and

claims.c: Captain is marketed as effective against a broad spectrum of green and blue-green (cyanobacteria) algae — planktonic, filamentous, and macrophytic forms — plus copper-susceptible submersed weeds.

The product's ethanolamine chelation is stated to keep copper from precipitating out of solution with carbonates/bicarbonates, which the label presents as the functional rationale for the chelated formulation.
The label states copper does not degrade in the environment and accumulates with repeated applications, distinguishing it from organic pesticides.
Copper treatment alone does not render water potable; drinking-water use requires additional, separate treatment.
Killing treated biomass can deplete dissolved oxygen enough to suffocate fish and invertebrates, so the label mandates partial-waterbody treatment and a minimum retreatment interval as mitigation.

Applying algaecide to a high-density cyanobacteria bloom risks lysing cells and releasing hepatotoxic and neurotoxic intracellular compounds into the water, so the label instructs avoiding treatment of already-dense toxic blooms.
The label's dose rates are only stated to be appropriate above specific water-chemistry thresholds; below them, non-target acute toxicity risk (including to sensitive fish) increases.
A hard single-application ceiling of 1.0 ppm metallic copper applies to general aquatic weed/algae control.

For whole-waterbody treatment (staggering half the waterbody every 14 days), the label sets an annual cap of 21.9 lbs metallic copper per acre-foot (8 applications/year at up to 1 ppm) across an 8-month/244-day season.

For partial-waterbody treatment (staggered discrete sections summing to ≤ half the waterbody, still within the 14-day interval), the label sets a higher annual cap of 46.6 lbs metallic copper per acre-foot per year (17 applications/year at up to 1 ppm).
Aquaculture ponds with fish present are restricted to a lower single-application cap (0.4 ppm) than the general 1.0 ppm ceiling, specifically for nuisance-algae targeting.
The label states its own efficacy can fail under specific conditions: retreating too soon after a prior application, or heavy infestation combined with low flow.
The label explicitly supports combining Captain with other aquatic herbicides (endothall-based products, diquat) rather than presenting copper as a stand-alone treatment.

numbers: Active ingredient: Copper Ethanolamine Complex = 28.2% of product (mixed CAS 82027-59-6 & 14215-52-2); Other Ingredients = 71.8%; metallic copper equivalent = 9.1% of product
Single-application cap: do not exceed 1.0 ppm copper in any waters per application (= 3 gal product, or 2.74 lbs metallic copper, per acre-foot)
Aquaculture ponds with fish present: do not exceed 0.4 ppm copper per single application when targeting nuisance algae (stricter than the general 1.0 ppm cap)
Effective surface-spray/injection algaecide range: 0.2-1.0 ppm copper (static or minimal-flow water)
Whole-waterbody strategy: max 21.9 lbs metallic copper/acre-foot/year (8 applications/year at up to 1 ppm), based on treating each half of the waterbody every 14 days across an 8-month/244-day season

Partial-waterbody strategy (staggered ≤half-body sections): max 46.6 lbs metallic copper/acre-foot/year (17 applications/year at up to 1 ppm) over the same 244-day season, 14-day minimum retreatment interval
Retreatment/oxygen-depletion mitigation: treat no more than 1/2 of a waterbody at a time; wait at least 14 days between treatments

Label-validated water chemistry window: pH > 6.5, DOC > 3.0 mg/L, alkalinity > 50 mg/L. Below these (pH ≤ 6.5; DOC ≤ 3.0 mg/L; alkalinity < 50 mg/L, i.e. soft/acidic water) the label states increased acute toxicity risk

Flowing-water dosing examples (Table 2): at 1 cfs (450 gal/min), 0.2-1.0 ppm copper = 0.2-1.0 quart product/hr = 3.2-15.7 mL/min; at 100 cfs (45,000 gal/min), 0.2-1.0 ppm copper = 20-100 quart/hr = 315-1,577 mL/min
Flow-rate estimation formula given in the label: cfs = average width (ft) x average depth (ft) x average velocity (ft/s) x 0.9
Flowing-water maintenance retreatment frequency: every 2-6 weeks

Pulse application (irrigation conveyance systems only): max 13 lbs metallic copper/year per 5 miles of conveyance per cfs; up to 0.5 lbs metallic copper (0.55 gal product) per cfs per 5-30 mile treatment segment
tensions: This is a manufacturer/registrant-issued regulatory label (SePRO Corporation is the registrant and is repeatedly named as the source of implementation advice), not an independent peer-reviewed efficacy study; its dosing and "effective control" language is regulatory/operational guidance rather than trial-validated efficacy evidence in the excerpted text, so it should be weighted as such alongside independent HAB-treatment research. More substantively for HAB decision-support framing: the label's own toxin-release warning complicates any simple

title: CyanoLakes — Manage Cyanobacterial Blooms Using Satellite Imagery
year: n.d. (live commercial website; most recent guideline it cites is dated 2023; company traces to a 2015 award and a 2014 PhD)
what: CyanoLakes is a commercial satellite-remote-sensing product (enterprise web app + iOS/Android mobile app) built by Mark Matthews (PhD, bio-optical remote sensing, U. Cape Town, 2014) that maps and forecasts cyanobacterial (harmful algal) blooms in lakes/reservoirs from EU Copernicus and NASA satellite imagery, with no

claims.c: CyanoLakes markets itself as the first product able to distinguish harmful cyanobacteria from non-toxic algae using satellite imagery.
The service claims to give up to three weeks of advance warning of cyanobacteria blooms compared with routine in-situ (field) monitoring.
The underlying satellite data comes from the EU Copernicus Program and NASA (no specific sensor/mission named on the fetched pages).
Premium imagery is delivered at 10 m spatial resolution (high-resolution tier); a medium-resolution tier is 300 m.
Update cadence is generally 5-6 days/week, while the high-definition imagery product updates 2-3 times/week; both are reduced by cloud, ice, or snow cover and vary seasonally.

The per-waterbody cyanobacteria risk level is computed from the median (50th percentile) chlorophyll-a of pixels classified as cyanobacteria, following WHO (2021) recreational-water guidance, and only counts if cyanobacterial bloom coverage reaches a minimum area threshold (10% of the waterbody for larger lakes, 50% for small lakes).

The site tabulates specific WHO (2021), Australian NHMRC (2008), California Water Board (2023), and US EPA (2019) numeric thresholds for chlorophyll-a, cyanobacterial biovolume, cell counts, and microcystin that it uses to classify risk.
The site states a fixed conversion of 1 µg/L chlorophyll-a to approximately 2,000 cyanobacteria cells/mL, attributed to WHO, and a companion conversion of 0.4 µg/L microcystin per µg/L chlorophyll-a.
The vendor states its high-risk bloom forecasts are 'up to 80% accurate' at a 1-week lead time and drop to '70% accurate' at a 2-week lead time.
A separate '3 weeks' figure is used for a different purpose: new lakes can take up to three weeks of processing-queue time before forecasts become available at all.

The product explicitly disclaims coverage of any water-quality hazard other than cyanobacteria/algal blooms (no pathogens, heavy metals, or chemical contaminants), so its recreational-safety guidance is stated to be non-comprehensive.
The 'Low Risk' classification does not guarantee cyanobacteria are absent, because cyanobacteria can be present below the satellite's detection limit.

CyanoLakes received R1 million (South African Rand) from the South African Water Research Commission's Water Technologies Demonstration Programme (WADER) to pilot the technology with three operational water utilities: City of Cape Town, Rand Water, and Umgeni Water.
The company states its underlying detection algorithms are open, peer-reviewed, and cited more than 500 times on Google Scholar.
CyanoLakes won the Copernicus Masters Ideas Challenge (a global Earth-observation business-idea competition), which the homepage's 'Approvals' section dates to 2015.

The platform guarantees 99% uptime and offers unlimited waterbodies per subscription (priced by waterbody count), with a standard 6 months of included historical data and up to 6 years purchasable, plus API data download.

numbers: 10 m — pixel size of the premium/high-resolution satellite imagery product (FAQ; Features; Plans-Pricing pages)
300 m — pixel size of the medium-resolution imagery tier (Plans-Pricing page)
5-6 days/week — general data update frequency, reduced by cloud/ice/snow cover (FAQ page)
2-3 times/week — update frequency specifically for the 'high definition' imagery product (FAQ page)

up to 3 weeks — (a) marketed 'advance warning' lead time vs. routine in-situ monitoring (homepage/How-It-Works), and (b) separately, the onboarding delay before forecasts become available for a new lake due to processing
80% — stated forecast accuracy for high-risk blooms at 1-week lead time (FAQ page; no metric definition, n, or study period given)
70% — stated forecast accuracy for high-risk blooms at 2-week lead time (FAQ page; same caveats as above)
1-1000 µg/L — stated measurement range of the chlorophyll-a satellite data product (FAQ page)

WHO (2021) recreational-water thresholds: Vigilance 3 µg/L chl-a or 1 mm3/L biovolume (microcystin ~1.2 µg/L); Alert 1: 12 µg/L or 3 mm3/L (microcystin ~4.8 µg/L); Alert 2: 24 µg/L or 8 mm3/L (microcystin ~10 µg/L) (FAQ

Australian NHMRC (2008) thresholds: Green 0.04 mm3/L or 500 cells/mL (chl-a ~0.25 µg/L); Amber 0.4 mm3/L or 5,000 cells/mL (chl-a ~2.5 µg/L); Red 4 mm3/L or 50,000 cells/mL (chl-a ~25 µg/L, microcystin ~10 µg/L) (FAQ pag

California Water Board (2023) thresholds: Tier 1 <0.8 µg/L microcystin or <4000 cells/mL toxin producers (chl-a ~2 µg/L); Tier 2 >6 µg/L microcystin (chl-a ~15 µg/L); Tier 3 >20 µg/L microcystin (chl-a ~50 µg/L) (FAQ pag
US EPA (2019) recreational guideline: microcystin ≥ 8 µg/L (chl-a ~20 µg/L) (FAQ page)
tensions: Two internal readings of the same '3 weeks' figure should not be conflated: a marketing claim of 3-weeks earlier warning than in-situ sampling versus a separate operational caveat that new-lake forecasts can take up to 3 weeks to activate due to processing queues. More substantively, the headline claims most useful to a literature review — the '3-week early warning' value proposition and the '80% / 70%' forecast-accuracy figures — are presented on marketing/FAQ pages with no visible methodology, validation sample, study period, or comparison ba

title: PAK 27 — SePRO Corporation Product Page / SDS
year: Undated as a live web page; most recent associated EPA label revision found is dated 2021-12-23
what: PAK 27 is a granular, non-copper algaecide sold by SePRO Corporation (EPA Reg. No. 68660-9; active ingredient sodium carbonate peroxyhydrate, reported at 85.0% by weight on SePRO-affiliated/distributor pages, not on the primary page itself), marketed for selective control of cyanobacteria plus broader-spectrum control

claims.c: PAK 27 is an EPA-registered, non-copper granular algaecide (EPA Reg. No. 68660-9) whose active ingredient is sodium carbonate peroxyhydrate.
The active ingredient is present at 85.0% by weight, with 15.0% other/inert ingredients.
The product's mechanism of action is an oxidation reaction, releasing hydrogen peroxide, that destroys algal cell membranes and chlorophyll for rapid ('immediate') algae control.

The product is positioned for selective control of cyanobacteria plus broader-spectrum control of filamentous/planktonic green algae, diatoms, and Chara, across lakes, ponds, drinking-water reservoirs, irrigation canals, aquaculture, and wastewater systems.

Dosing guidance (per a distributor's application-instructions page) directs users to base application rate on actual cyanobacterial cell counts, within a labeled range from 3 lbs/acre-foot (preventive/'algaestatic') up to 100 lbs/acre-foot (curative/'algaecidal' in severely compromised water).
SePRO's own retail and canal-use pages instead state an application rate of 30-100 lbs per surface acre, with roughly two weeks recommended between repeat applications.

The product carries no fishing, drinking, swimming, or irrigation restrictions after treatment; SePRO states treated canal water can be used immediately for irrigation or livestock consumption, with no NPDES discharge/monitoring requirement for irrigation-canal use.
The product is marketed as safe for use in waters containing sensitive fish species such as Koi and trout.
PAK 27 is OMRI-listed for organic production use and NSF/ANSI Standard 60 certified for use in drinking-water supplies at maximum labeled rates.

A distributor page states the signal word as 'DANGER / PELIGRO' and classifies the product as a DOT hazardous material requiring adult signature on delivery; this could not be cross-checked against the actual EPA label or SDS text.

A distributor states the product 'has been discontinued by the manufacturer,' which is inconsistent with its continued live marketing on SePRO's own primary, retail, and DIY-storefront domains as of this fetch.

title: SeClear Algaecide and Water Quality Enhancer — Product Label
what: This is the EPA-registered pesticide product label (EPA Reg. No. 67690-55, EPA Est. No. 067690-NC-002; label date 2023-05-15) for SeClear, a copper sulfate pentahydrate-based algaecide and water quality enhancer for ponds, lakes, reservoirs, and irrigation/drainage conveyances, including potable water sources subject t

claims.c: SeClear's active ingredient is copper sulfate pentahydrate at 16.2% of the formulation, equivalent to 4.1% metallic copper, delivering 0.417 lbs of metallic copper per gallon of product.
The product is EPA-registered and labeled for use across a range of aquatic sites, explicitly including potable water sources (subject to restrictions elsewhere in the label).

The product's stated mechanism is rapid disruption of algal cell processes, and it is labeled effective against a broad range of algae growth forms, explicitly including blue-green algae (cyanobacteria), not just green algae.

Beyond killing algae, the label claims the product improves water quality by offsetting eutrophication, lowering water-column phosphorus via binding/precipitation, and reducing turbidity by binding/precipitating suspended solids.

The label states the product is toxic to fish and aquatic organisms, and that unlike organic pesticides, its copper active ingredient does not degrade and accumulates in the environment with repeated applications.

Treating algae/weeds can deplete dissolved oxygen as biomass decomposes, risking fish and invertebrate suffocation; to mitigate this the label restricts treatment to no more than half a water body at a time with at least 14 days between treatments (with an exception process for public-health emergencies).

Applying the algaecide to high-density cyanobacteria blooms can rupture cells and release intracellular contents into the water, some of which are known mammalian hepatotoxins and neurotoxins; the label advises against treating toxin-producing cyanobacteria at high density for this reason.

Low pH (≤6.5), low dissolved organic carbon (≤3.0 mg/L), and soft water (alkalinity <50 mg/L) are stated to increase acute toxicity of the product to non-target aquatic organisms (e.g., koi, trout); labeled rates assume pH >6.5, DOC >3.0 mg/L, and alkalinity >50 mg/L.

The product does not itself make water potable; for water destined for drinking use, no more than 1.0 ppm metallic copper may be applied in a single treatment, and additional separate potable-water treatment is required.

Table 1 sets application rates by algae growth form: 0.15-1.0 ppm metallic copper (1.0-6.5 gal product/acre-foot) for planktonic and filamentous algae, and 0.4-1.0 ppm (2.5-6.5 gal/acre-foot) for macrophytic algae (Chara/Nitella/Starry Stonewort), with a footnote allowing up to 1.0 ppm for difficult species.

Annual copper loading caps differ by treatment strategy: whole waterbodies are capped at 21.9 lbs metallic copper/acre-foot/year (8 applications/year at up to 1 ppm, staggering half the waterbody every 14 days over an 8-month/244-day season), while discrete 'water management units' within a larger waterbody are capped higher, at 46.6 lbs/acre-foot/year (17 applications/year at up to 1 ppm) over the same 244-day season.
In aquaculture ponds where fish are present, the single-application cap for targeting nuisance algae is lowered to 0.4 ppm metallic copper, versus the general 1.0 ppm single-application maximum.

For flowing water (canals, ditches, irrigation/drainage systems, potable water conveyances), control requires maintaining a minimum exposure of 1-3 hours at 0.15-1.0 ppm; a separate 'Pulse Application' method for irrigation conveyances only caps annual load at 13 lbs metallic copper/year per 5 miles of conveyance per CFS, applied at up to 0.5 lbs copper (1.22 gal product) per CFS per 5-30 mile segment.

The label includes a Resistance Management section advising pre- and post-application scouting, listing indicators of suspected resistance, and recommending rotation to a different mechanism of action if resistance is suspected.

For aerial application, the label restricts release height, droplet size, and wind speed to manage spray drift: no release above 10 ft over canopy/water (absent pilot-safety need), medium-or-coarser droplet size (ASABE S572.1), no application above 15 mph wind, and reduced boom length (≤65% of wingspan) for fixed-wing aircraft above 10 mph.

A front-panel notice appears to state 'A Maximum Use Level ... of 24 mg/L is required ... for the product,' but this sentence is interleaved (via PDF column extraction) with an unrelated warranty-disclaimer notice, so the parameter this 24 mg/L limit applies to cannot be confirmed from the extracted text.

Product contains 0.417 lbs metallic copper per gallon
Front-panel notice (extraction-jumbled): 'Maximum Use Level ... of 24 mg/L ... for the product' — parameter it applies to is unclear from the excerpt
Non-target toxicity risk thresholds: pH ≤6.5, DOC ≤3.0 mg/L, alkalinity <50 mg/L (label rates presume pH >6.5, DOC >3.0 mg/L, alkalinity >50 mg/L)
Retreatment restriction: ≥14 days between treatments; ≤1/2 of water body treated at a time
Drinking-water use cap: ≤1.0 ppm metallic copper per single treatment (product does not itself potabilize water)
Aquaculture ponds with fish present: ≤0.4 ppm metallic copper per single application (vs. 1.0 ppm general single-application max)

Table 1 rates: Planktonic (suspended) algae 0.15-1.0 ppm copper, 1.0-6.5 gal product/acre-foot; Filamentous (mat-forming) algae 0.15-1.0 ppm, 1.0-6.5 gal/acre-foot; Macrophytic algae (Chara/Nitella/Starry Stonewort) 0.4-
Single-application max: 1.0 ppm copper = 6.57 gal product or 2.74 lbs metallic copper per acre-foot
Whole-waterbody annual max: 21.9 lbs metallic copper/acre-foot/year (8 applications/year at up to 1 ppm), based on treating half the waterbody every 14 days across a 244-day (8-month) season
Water-management-unit annual max: 46.6 lbs metallic copper/acre-foot/year (17 applications/year at up to 1 ppm) over the same 244-day season
Flowing water: minimum exposure 1-3 hours at 0.15-1.0 ppm metallic copper
tensions: The label's own hazard language complicates a simple "detect-then-treat" story: it warns that applying this algaecide to high-density cyanobacteria blooms can rupture cells and release intracellular hepato- and nervous-system toxins into the water, and separately restricts retreatment to no more than half a waterbody every 14 days specifically to avoid oxygen-depletion fish kills from decaying biomass. Read together, these manufacturer-stated cautions suggest that chemical treatment of an already-dense, toxin-producing bloom is itself a constra

title: SePRO Corporation — Aquatics Products / SeClear (SeClear® Algaecide and Water Quality Enhancer)

SeClear's stated mechanism is that copper ions inhibit photosynthesis in algal cells (giving algae control) while simultaneously removing phosphorus from the water column.
The page states visible algae control occurs 3 to 7 days after application, with retreatment recommended once regrowth appears.

Treated water is claimed to be safe for turfgrass/ornamental plants and usable for irrigation immediately after treatment.
SeClear is stated to be certified to NSF/ANSI/CAN 60, the standard for drinking-water treatment additives.
The page claims control of a 'broad-range of algae species' and 'nuisance algae' without naming specific target genera or species.
SeClear Algaecide is a federally registered pesticide (EPA Reg. No. 67690-55) manufactured by SePRO Corporation, with copper sulfate pentahydrate as its labeled active ingredient.

A secondary, WebSearch-aggregated reading of the SeClear label reports the active ingredient at 16.2% copper sulfate pentahydrate by weight, equivalent to 4.1% metallic copper, with signal word 'Caution.'
The product page links out to a formal Label and a Safety Data Sheet (SDS) as separate documents rather than embedding full regulatory/safety detail inline.

claims.c: Captain XTR is marketed as a broad-spectrum, copper-based algaecide containing a chelating agent and a proprietary surfactant package, positioned as SePRO's go-to product for fast knockdown of severe/established algae infestations.
Komeen Crystal is a highly concentrated, chelated-copper formulation marketed specifically for bottom-dwelling (benthic) algae, including pithophora, chara, and nitella.

PAK 27 is positioned as a non-copper algaecide alternative that is OMRI-listed and NSF-certified for use in drinking water, recommended where copper-sensitive fish (trout, koi) are present, and described as selectively controlling blue-green algae (cyanobacteria).

SeClear and SeClear G are described as the only EPA-approved combination algaecide-plus-water-quality-enhancer product line, claimed to remove phosphorus nutrients in addition to controlling algae, and recommended as the first-line, routine/preventive product before escalating to stronger algaecides.

The page recommends a tiered escalation strategy for algae management: continuous monitoring and early/routine SeClear use for prevention, escalating to Captain XTR for established infestations, Komeen Crystal for benthic macroalgae, and PAK 27 for sensitive-habitat situations.

The page classifies problem algae into three categories used to route product selection: filamentous mat formers (green/brown/black surface or bottom mats), planktonic forms (water discoloration in brown/green/red/blue, surface scums, potentially toxic), and macroalgae (bottom-dwelling forms such as chara and nitella that lack true roots and instead anchor via rhizoids).
The page attributes several harms to unmanaged algae in general: toxin production affecting fish, wildlife, and humans; clogging of irrigation intakes; and interference with swimming and fishing.

The site's "Algae Corner" content hub features a video series on specific named nuisance organisms with colorful common names and associated harms, including Lyngbya (called "Godzilla algae," described as toxin-producing with unpleasant odor), Cladophora (a filamentous green alga stated to be linked to botulism in birds), Chara/muskgrass (garlic/skunk-like odor), Pithophora ("horsehair" algae), and Bryozoan organisms (a non-algal organism included in the same content hub).
Beyond a generic label-compliance disclaimer, the page provides no application rates, dosages, concentrations, treatment timeframes, or cost figures anywhere in its content.

title: EOMAP Water Quality Deep Dive (Remote Sensing Methodology) — a.k.a. "Water Quality – Technical Deep Dive"
what: A commercial technical whitepaper ("Water Quality – Technical Deep Dive," dated May 2025) in which EOMAP GmbH, a satellite Earth-observation service provider, describes its proprietary remote-sensing methodology for deriving inland- and coastal-water quality parameters (chlorophyll, turbidity, Secchi depth, CDOM, surfa

claims.c: EOMAP defines Chlorophyll (CHL) as a pigment in phytoplankton cells used as a proxy for algae abundance, with stated concentrations spanning about four orders of magnitude: roughly 0.01–10 µg/l in marine waters/clear lakes versus up to 100 µg/l (or more) in eutrophic lakes.
Turbidity (TUR) is retrieved from backward light scattering measured in the 450–800 nm spectral range.

EOMAP's Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) indicator is explicitly a qualitative proxy for cyanobacteria, not a calibrated concentration, derived from the reflectance/absorption discrepancy between the 550 nm and 650 nm bands and sensitive to phycocyanin/phycoerythrin pigments.

Secchi Disk Depth (SDD) is not measured directly but calculated from the water's optical attenuation coefficient, ranging from under 1 metre in very turbid water to over 20 metres in very clear water.

EOMAP states its satellite water-quality products can be delivered at revisit frequencies of 1–8 days, within 3–12 hours of image acquisition or archive availability, and can draw on satellite archives extending back up to 40 years.

A minimum water-body size is required for retrieval, tied to sensor resolution: at least 5×5 water pixels, e.g. at least 50×50 metres when using 10 m resolution Sentinel-2 imagery, with a 1–2 pixel shoreline buffer excluded from analysis.

The methodology explicitly reduces available scenes due to clouds, cloud shadow, atmospheric aerosols and sun glint, and separately flags out floating-vegetation (macrophyte) pixels rather than quantifying them; shallow-water bottom reflectance can also disturb the retrieved signal.

Retrieved parameter values represent a depth-integrated quantity from the surface down to the light-penetration depth (approximately 1.5 times the visible/Secchi depth, i.e., the euphotic zone), not a single surface-point sample.

In an applied case study (WasMon-CT, for German state agency LUBW in Baden-Württemberg), EOMAP combined Landsat 7, Landsat 8 and Sentinel-2 (high resolution) with Sentinel-3 and MODIS Aqua/Terra (medium resolution, for Lake Constance) to derive turbidity, Secchi disc depth, total/organic absorption, chlorophyll-a and harmful algae blooms for more than 20 lakes over roughly 2011–2017, in a state EOMAP describes as having 'no fewer than 1300 lakes bigger than 1 hectare.'
EOMAP groups waterbodies into three trophic-state categories: oligotrophic (nutrient-poor), mesotrophic (moderately productive), and eutrophic (nutrient-rich and highly productive).

numbers: Chlorophyll (CHL): ~0.01–10 µg/l typical for marine waters/clear lakes; up to 100 µg/l or more in eutrophic lakes (spans ~4 orders of magnitude)
Turbidity (TUR) retrieval spectral window: 450–800 nm (backward light scattering)
HAB/cyanobacteria proxy indicator spectral bands compared: 550 nm vs. 650 nm
Secchi Disk Depth (SDD) range: <1 m (very turbid) to >20 m (very clear)
Effective measurement/integration depth ≈ 1.5 × visible (Secchi) depth (approx. euphotic zone)
Revisit frequency: 1–8 days
Product delivery latency: 3–12 hours after acquisition or archive availability
Historical satellite archive depth referenced: up to 40 years
Minimum mappable water body: ≥5×5 water pixels; e.g., ≥50×50 m when using 10 m resolution Sentinel-2 imagery
Shoreline exclusion buffer: 1–2 pixels
Sentinel-2 resolution cited: 10 m
Document version date: May 2025 (per indexed PDF title 'Version: May 2025')

title: Harmful Algal Bloom Recreational Health Advisory Guidance
year: 2023 (page states "last updated in 2023"); a related benthic-cyanobacteria supplement is dated 2025
what: A Utah state guidance page (DWQ, with DHHS, last updated 2023, "based largely on guidance from the...EPA") that defines two recreational-exposure advisory tiers — Warning and Danger — for cyanobacteria/cyanotoxin-affected waterbodies. It gives local health departments (LHDs, who hold legal authority to post advisories/

claims.c: The guidance is issued by Utah DWQ in coordination with DHHS, last updated in 2023, and is based largely on EPA guidance updated with newer science on cell-density health impacts.

Legal authority to post recreational health advisories or close a waterbody rests with local health departments (LHDs), not with DWQ/DHHS, which instead provide monitoring, sampling, and interpretation support.

The guidance uses two advisory tiers (Warning, Danger); either tier is triggered if any one of several independent indicators (cyanobacteria cell density or any of four cyanotoxins) is exceeded, and Danger can also be triggered directly by a plausible human/animal illness report regardless of lab results.

Warning Advisory (recommendation to avoid primary contact recreation such as swimming, waterskiing, wading) is tied to specific lab thresholds: cyanobacteria cell density, and concentrations of microcystins, cylindrospermopsin, anatoxin-a, and saxitoxin.

Danger Advisory (recommendation to stay away from the waterbody entirely, reflecting risk of short-term effects, long-term illness, or death) uses substantially higher lab thresholds for three of the four toxins: microcystins, anatoxin-a, and saxitoxin.

A separate, lower set of numeric values (cyanobacteria >20,000 cells/mL; microcystin >4 µg/L; cylindrospermopsin >4 µg/L; anatoxin-a "detected at any level") also appears on the page, described by the fetch tool as coming from a decision flowchart/diagram distinct from the main thresholds table.

An advisory is not lifted until two consecutive water samples, collected at least one week apart, both show cyanotoxin/cell-density levels back below the relevant threshold -- i.e., a minimum multi-week confirmation window.

As of 2025, Utah issued a separate benthic-cyanobacteria ("harmful algae mat") advisory guidance because the existing planktonic-bloom HAB guidance and its cell-density/toxin thresholds do not apply to mat-forming benthic cyanobacteria; the new guidance instead uses percent areal mat coverage as its primary metric.

Advisory determinations draw on visual bloom confirmation plus laboratory water sampling for cell density and the four named cyanotoxins, supplemented by documented Standard Operating Procedures for phytoplankton sample collection, SPATT (Solid Phase Adsorption Toxin Testing) passive toxin samplers, and fish/wildlife carcass sampling during mortality events.

The page's own descriptive text refers to "four independent indicators" underlying the advisory decision, but enumerates cell density, microcystin, cylindrospermopsin, and anatoxin-a in that description while saxitoxin thresholds are also given in the tables -- an internal count/labeling inconsistency (four named vs. five thresholds actually tabulated) that this dossier flags rather than resolves.

20,000 cells/mL — cyanobacteria cell-density value shown in a separate flowchart element on the page (tier attribution unresolved)

2,000 µg/L — microcystin Danger-tier threshold (independently corroborated)
90 µg/L — anatoxin-a Danger-tier threshold (independently corroborated)
75 µg/L — saxitoxin Danger-tier threshold (independently corroborated)
1 week — minimum interval required between the two consecutive confirmatory samples before an advisory can be lifted
2 weeks (approx.) — overall minimum duration of below-threshold measurements described before an advisory is removed
tensions: This guidance's own design (requiring exceedance of ANY ONE of five largely independent indicators -- cell density or one of four distinct cyanotoxins -- to trigger an advisory, rather than inferring toxin risk from cell density/biomass alone) is consistent with, rather than contradictory to, the broader HAB literature finding that cyanobacterial cell density and toxin concentration are only loosely coupled (toxin quota per cell varies by strain, species, and conditions). This has a direct implication -- stated here as an inference, not as some

title: NYHABS: New York Harmful Algal Bloom System — Notifications
year: 2019 (system launch); ongoing
what: NYHABS (New York Harmful Algal Bloom System) is a New York State DEC operational notification service, combining an online public/agency reporting form with a public interactive map, that compiles and displays DEC-verified reports of confirmed freshwater harmful algal bloom (cyanobacteria) locations across New York Sta

claims.c: NYHABS is a two-part system — an online reporting system plus a public interactive map — through which DEC compiles and publishes notifications of documented HAB locations reported by government personnel, trained monitors, and the public.

A report only earns "Confirmed Bloom" status after DEC staff determine it meets HAB criteria using visual observation, digital photographs, and/or water sampling results — i.e., confirmation is a human/agency verification step, not an automated or purely citizen-reported label.
The live interactive map is restricted to a rolling 2-week window of current reports, with icon styling distinguishing recently-reported points from points archived earlier in the same season.
Reports are classified into four standardized bloom-extent categories: Small Localized, Large Localized, Widespread or Lakewide, and Open Water.

Each point on the map carries a defined metadata schema: observation date, HAB status, extent category, submitter identity, county, and any submitted photographs; users can filter the map by county and waterbody, and the print function only outputs the currently filtered/displayed records.

Reports feeding NYHABS originate from four streams: the general public, DEC's Lake Classification and Inventory Program, Citizen Statewide Lake Assessment Program (CSLAP) volunteers, and other partner monitoring programs.

NYHABS explicitly scopes itself to freshwater cyanobacteria HABs and states it does not cover marine HABs (red tide, brown tide), filamentous green algae blooms, most Lake Champlain HAB reports (NY-side beach reports only; the Vermont Department of Health runs a separate cyanobacteria notification system for the rest of that lake), or beach-closure/drinking-water safety information (referred instead to DEC's swimming page or local health departments).
Historical (prior-season) HAB notification summaries are not kept on the live map but are archived separately on Open Data NY, with coverage going back to 2012.
DEC explicitly states that the notification map is not a complete, exhaustive inventory of all HABs statewide, since it depends on reports actually being submitted to DEC.

A HAB reported on part of a large lake or river does not imply the entire waterbody is affected; other portions may remain clear and fully usable for recreation, and DEC's assurance of recreational safety applies only to "designated swimming areas," not to the waterbody as a whole.

numbers: 2 weeks — rolling window for which the interactive map displays current HAB reports ("reported in the past 2 weeks")

518-402-8179 — contact phone number, DOW Bureau of Water Assessment and Management
625 Broadway, Albany, NY 12233 — DEC Division of Water office address (contact info, not a data statistic)

title: Public Water System Harmful Algal Bloom Response Strategy
what: An October 2025 Ohio EPA (Division of Drinking and Ground Waters) regulatory guidance document that sets numerical cyanotoxin "do-not-drink" thresholds for finished drinking water and defines HAB/cyanotoxin monitoring, sampling, and analytical-method requirements for surface-water public water systems (PWSs) in Ohio. I

claims.c: For children under 6 (including bottle-fed infants) and other sensitive populations, Ohio's finished-drinking-water 'Do Not Drink' thresholds are 0.3 μg/L microcystins, 0.3 μg/L anatoxin-a, 0.7 μg/L cylindrospermopsin, and 0.3 μg/L saxitoxins.
For children 6 and older and adults, the corresponding thresholds are higher: 1.6 μg/L microcystins, 1.6 μg/L anatoxin-a, 3.0 μg/L cylindrospermopsin, and 1.6 μg/L saxitoxins.

The microcystins and saxitoxins thresholds are applied to the total of all reported congeners/variants (not a single congener reading), and Ohio EPA additionally applies established saxitoxin toxicity-equivalency factors when judging whether a threshold is exceeded.
The child and adult drinking-water thresholds for all four cyanotoxins are unchanged from Ohio's prior (2022) version of this strategy.

Ohio's 'sensitive populations' category (held to the children-under-6 threshold tier for all four cyanotoxins) includes pregnant women, nursing mothers, and dialysis patients; people with pre-existing liver conditions are added to that category only for microcystins and cylindrospermopsin, because those two toxins are hepatotoxins, while elderly and immunocompromised individuals receive only a non-binding recommendation to consider following the children's guidance.

Ohio's regulatory definition of a HAB is either visible water discoloration from cyanobacteria, or a cyanobacteria cell count above 4,000 cells/mL for genera capable of producing cyanotoxins, citing Shambaugh and Brines (2003).
A public water system must notify Ohio EPA of any finished-water cyanotoxin detection within 24 hours, using a dedicated after-hours spill hotline if needed.

For saxitoxins, cylindrospermopsin, and anatoxin-a specifically (unlike microcystins, which has its own dedicated notification rule, OAC 3745-90-06), Ohio EPA's 24-hour public-notification timeline after a confirmed resample/repeat exceedance is a recommendation rather than a codified rule requirement, with Ohio EPA evaluating results case-by-case with the PWS.
Among the four regulated cyanotoxins, microcystins and saxitoxins are described as the most commonly detected in Ohio waters, while cylindrospermopsin and anatoxin-a are detected far less often.

The document frames its precautionary posture around the fact that no antidote exists for cyanotoxins, and notes that Ohio's health and agriculture departments have received reports of probable human illness and dog deaths from cyanotoxin exposure, with the CDC's OHHABS system tracking cases nationwide.

Beyond direct toxicity, cyanobacteria increase organic material in source water, which drives production of disinfection byproducts described as carcinogenic, plus taste-and-odor compounds that affect drinking-water palatability.

The document's background section associates cyanobacterial blooms with eutrophic/hypereutrophic conditions (excess nitrogen and phosphorus) and states bloom formation is favored by nutrient availability, sufficient light, and warmer temperatures, with spatial distribution shaped by weather, hydrology, morphology, currents, and wind.

Every public water system with a surface-water source in Ohio must maintain a Treatment Optimization Protocol (TOP) describing how it will optimize its existing treatment specifically for microcystins removal.

Document version date: October 2025; the four cyanotoxin threshold values are stated as unchanged since the '2022 PWS HAB Response Strategy' (Executive Summary, p.1).

title: State of Ohio Harmful Algal Bloom Response Strategy for Recreational Waters
what: A 2020 State of Ohio interagency policy and operations document (Ohio EPA, Ohio Department of Natural Resources, and Ohio Department of Health) that lays out a unified statewide strategy for detecting, reporting on, and posting public-health advisories for cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (HABs) at Ohio's recreation

claims.c: The Strategy's stated purpose is to give Ohio a unified, statewide, multi-agency approach to addressing HABs in recreational waters and to protect people from cyanotoxins, operationalized through numeric thresholds used to make advisory decisions.

Ohio deliberately concentrates HAB sampling and advisory effort on public beaches rather than open-water recreation (boating, jet skiing), reasoning that beach-goers face substantially greater cyanotoxin exposure potential (full-body immersion, more children) than open-water users, who instead get caution signage and outreach.

Responsibility for observing/sampling blooms and posting advisories is divided by water-body type and ownership: ODNR is primary contact for state-park beaches; local beach managers/local health districts handle other public beaches; property owners are responsible for private beaches; USACE coordinates with ODNR on jointly managed lakes; and Ohio River responsibilities split between ORSANCO, local government, and Ohio EPA depending on access type. ODH separately runs illness surveillance via the One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System (OHHABS) and hosts the public BeachGuard advisory-posting website.

The document frames cyanobacterial blooms as generally occurring in eutrophic/hypereutrophic water bodies, and states (citing external literature) that eutrophication is most often the result of elevated nitrogen and phosphorus supply enhancing phytoplankton/aquatic-plant production.

Among cyanotoxins the state screens for, microcystins and saxitoxin are described as the more commonly detected in Ohio waters, while cylindrospermopsin and anatoxin-a are detected much less frequently.

Cyanotoxins are colorless, odorless, and tasteless, can persist in the water after a visible bloom has disappeared, and toxin production is strain-specific (a given cyanobacterium may produce one, several, or no toxin types) — meaning visible bloom presence does not reliably indicate toxin presence or absence.

Because cyanotoxins are potent and no antidote is known, Ohio explicitly adopted a conservative (health-protective) policy stance when setting its recreational-water thresholds, rather than thresholds calibrated to a precise toxicological safety margin.

Ohio's Recreational Public Health Advisory thresholds are 8 µg/L for microcystins, 8 µg/L for anatoxin-a, 15 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin, and 0.8 µg/L for saxitoxins, with the microcystins and saxitoxin values applied to the total of all reported congeners/variants/analogs.

The microcystins and cylindrospermopsin thresholds in this document are not Ohio-original values — they were issued by U.S. EPA as final recommended recreational-swimming advisories in June 2019 and were then formally adopted by Ohio.

The stated thresholds are described as protective of human health via incidental ingestion based on current information, but the source explicitly states they may not be protective of animals such as dogs or livestock.

ODH has built a clinician-facing diagnostic aid called 'Screen for Green,' a flowchart to help physicians assess a patient's probable cyanotoxin exposure and treatment options, with an analogous resource for veterinarians.

The state uses NOAA/NASA-processed satellite remote-sensing imagery (under the multi-agency Cyanobacteria Assessment Network, CyAN) to help locate HABs in Lake Erie, inland state-park lakes, and the Ohio River, but the source frames this imagery's role explicitly as a human visual-confirmation aid for recreation managers, not an automated or stand-alone detection/forecasting output.

The state's satellite remote-sensing tools are stated to work only on lakes/rivers at least 300 meters wide, and can detect blooms approximately 1-2 feet below the surface (beyond human visual detection) and in turbid water where visual identification is difficult.

NOAA has produced a bi-weekly Lake Erie satellite bulletin (bloom imagery, predicted densities, wind directions) for some time, but a comparable inland-lake satellite product for Ohio has only existed since May 2017, reviewed near-daily by Ohio EPA.

The source explicitly warns that the absence of a visible surface bloom does not mean a bloom is absent, since blooms can move to another part of a lake or mix through the water column instead of dying off, and some cyanobacteria never form surface scums at all.

Color is explicitly described as an unreliable way to distinguish cyanobacteria from green algae or suspended sediment; the source states satellite imagery, microscopic examination, or dedicated screening tools (molecular methods, cyanotoxin field test kits) are needed to confirm cyanobacteria presence.

The advisory thresholds and overall guidelines are not presented as fixed/permanent: they were set by an interagency committee, are reviewed and updated annually, supersede prior versions, and are explicitly stated as likely to require future revision as HAB toxicity science evolves.

Saxitoxins: Recreational Public Health Advisory threshold = 0.8 µg/L (Table 2, Section 2.3, p.5; no uncertainty stated; applies to total of all reported congeners/variants/analogs)

Ohio inland-lake satellite product operational since: May 2017 (Section 3.1, p.6); Lake Erie satellite bulletin issued bi-weekly by NOAA (Section 3.1, p.6)
U.S. EPA final recommended recreational-swimming advisory values for microcystins and cylindrospermopsin: issued June 2019, adopted verbatim by Ohio in this August 2020 document (Section 2.3, p.5)
