title: Voluntary Guidance for Agencies When Making Bloom and Water Contact Recreation Public Health Decisions (HABs Response Guidance)
year: 2016 (planktonic guidance adopted); benthic mat guidance reviewed/adopted 2020, updated March 2025
what: A California state voluntary (non-regulatory) guidance document, adopted by the CCHAB Network in 2016, that lays out an eight-step interagency response plan and a tiered (No Advisory / Caution / Warning / Danger) system of numeric cyanotoxin and cell-density trigger levels that state and local agencies use to decide wh

claims.c: The planktonic-bloom guidance is explicitly voluntary and non-regulatory, developed by state agencies and adopted by the CCHAB Network in 2016.

The tiered advisory system (Caution/Warning/Danger) is based on a combination of concentrations of three cyanotoxins, cell counts of potential toxin-producing cyanobacteria, and site-specific visual indicators, not any single metric.

Total microcystins trigger levels are: No Advisory below 0.8 micrograms/liter, Caution (Tier 1) at/above 0.8 micrograms/liter, Warning (Tier 2) at/above 6 micrograms/liter, and Danger (Tier 3) at/above 20 micrograms/liter.

Anatoxin-a trigger levels are: No Advisory = non-detect, Caution (Tier 1) = detected, Warning (Tier 2) at/above 20 micrograms/liter, Danger (Tier 3) at/above 90 micrograms/liter; assigning the Caution tier requires an analytical method sensitive enough to detect anatoxin-a at or below 1 microgram/liter.

Cylindrospermopsin trigger levels are: No Advisory below 1 microgram/liter, Caution (Tier 1) at/above 1 microgram/liter, Warning (Tier 2) at/above 4 micrograms/liter, Danger (Tier 3) at/above 17 micrograms/liter.

Cell density of potential toxin-producing cyanobacteria has a No Advisory/Caution threshold at 4,000 cells/mL, but the table gives no distinct Warning or Danger cell-density values (those cells are blank/dashed).

A site is escalated to a given advisory tier if any one of the criteria (a single cyanotoxin or the cell-density criterion) reaches that tier's threshold -- not all criteria need to be met simultaneously.
Removing ("de-posting") an advisory sign requires that all 'No Advisory' criteria be met for a minimum of two consecutive weeks before the sign comes down.
The guidance explicitly does not cover cyanotoxins in drinking water; that is handled by a separate program (the State Water Board's Division of Drinking Water).

A separate benthic (bottom-attached/mat-forming) cyanobacteria guidance exists alongside the planktonic guidance, most recently updated in March 2025, and instructs responders to choose the more conservative (protective) sign when a benthic mat and a planktonic bloom co-occur at the same site.

The overall response process is organized as an eight-step interagency plan running from surveillance/general awareness through initial discovery and reporting (via a toll-free hotline, online form, or email), interagency coordination, field screening, laboratory analysis, posting of advisory signs, continued (e.g., bi-weekly) monitoring, and eventual de-posting.

Laboratory sample results are referenced as needing to be reported within a short turnaround, stated in the fetched text as 'less than 5 days,' though it is not fully clear from the extracted text whether this refers to a sample holding time or a results-reporting turnaround time.

numbers: Total microcystins - No Advisory: < 0.8 µg/L
Total microcystins - Caution (Tier 1): ≥ 0.8 µg/L
Total microcystins - Warning (Tier 2): ≥ 6 µg/L
Total microcystins - Danger (Tier 3): ≥ 20 µg/L
Anatoxin-a - No Advisory: non-detect
Anatoxin-a - Caution (Tier 1): detected (requires analytical method with ≤ 1 µg/L detection limit)
Anatoxin-a - Warning (Tier 2): ≥ 20 µg/L
Anatoxin-a - Danger (Tier 3): ≥ 90 µg/L
Cylindrospermopsin - No Advisory: < 1 µg/L
Cylindrospermopsin - Caution (Tier 1): ≥ 1 µg/L
Cylindrospermopsin - Warning (Tier 2): ≥ 4 µg/L
Cylindrospermopsin - Danger (Tier 3): ≥ 17 µg/L
tensions: This source is a risk-communication/management policy document rather than a predictive or causal scientific study, so its numbers are consensus-based regulatory trigger levels rather than outputs of a dose-response or forecasting model validated in this text. Its own 'action level met when one or more criteria are met' rule means an 'advisory' label conflates several distinct, co-varying signals (specific toxin concentration OR cell density OR visual appearance) into one ordinal tier; any downstream HAB model that treats 'advisory posted' as a

title: Blue-Green Algae / Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) — Water Quality Dashboard (Protecting Florida Together, "Resources: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)" page)
year: Not stated on the page (undated public-information page); the only date-anchored content is a 2018 FWC red-tide aerial-survey example
what: A public-facing informational/resources page on Florida's state "Protecting Florida Together" water-quality website that explains blue-green algae (cyanobacteria) and Red Tide harmful algal blooms (HABs), names the state agencies and monitoring apparatus feeding the associated statewide Water Quality Dashboard, and giv

claims.c: The associated Water Quality Dashboard is described as delivering statewide water-quality information -- blue-green algae, red tide, and nutrient monitoring data -- for public transparency, fed by DEP and FWC data.
The most common HABs in Florida are cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) in freshwater and Red Tide (Karenia brevis) in coastal waters.

HABs are described as triggered by warm weather and increased nutrients, can occur in saltwater, freshwater, or brackish water bodies, and some blooms release toxins harmful to people and the environment.
A documented blue-green algal bloom example in Lake Okeechobee was small in scale, roughly 12 by 15 meters.

A 2018 FWC aerial survey found Red Tide-related water discoloration visible from Pinellas County to Sarasota County within 3 miles of the coast, with the bloom extending at least 15 miles offshore in some areas.
Multiple Florida agencies are named as part of the state's HAB monitoring apparatus: DEP, FWC, St. Johns River Water Management District (SJRWMD), and South Florida Water Management District (SFWMD).
FWC conducts aerial surveys specifically for Red Tide detection, with 2018 examples cited as a monitoring method.
Two cyanobacteria species are specifically associated with Florida blue-green algae blooms: Microcystis aeruginosa in freshwater, and Aphanizomenon flos-aquae in brackish and freshwater.
The public can report suspected blue-green algae blooms 24/7 online via ReportAlgalBloom.com or by phone toll-free, Monday-Friday 8 a.m.-5 p.m.
The page issues a direct public-health caution against contact with algae blooms or red tide water, alongside separate hotlines for fish kills, wildlife alerts, and human illness/poison control.

title: Current Cyanobacteria Advisories (Oregon Health Authority)
what: The Oregon Health Authority's live public portal listing current recreational-use health advisories for cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) harmful blooms in Oregon waterbodies, updated through the monitoring season (2026 season began May 4, 2026). It communicates advisory status and confirmed cyanotoxin measurements to t

claims.c: Oregon operates a three-tier recreational advisory system: **Advisory (red)** = cyanotoxin levels confirmed by testing (or conditions indicating potential) to be unsafe for swimming/boating and unsafe for pets; **Precaution (yellow)** = bloom confirmed or cyanotoxins present but at levels safe for human recreation, though possibly still unsafe for pets; **Lifted (blue)** = testing shows cyanotoxins absent or the bloom visibly gone.

Advisories are grounded in confirmed cyanotoxin testing, with specific measured microcystin concentrations reported per waterbody (e.g., Agency Lake 16.9 µg/L; Detroit Reservoir 0.73 µg/L microcystin at time of access).
Coverage is explicitly partial: only a fraction of Oregon waterbodies are monitored or sampled due to limited resources, and the absence of an advisory does not mean a bloom is absent.
The program advises a precautionary default for unmonitored or visibly bloom-affected waters.

numbers: 2026 monitoring season start: May 4, 2026
Example confirmed microcystin measurements at access: Agency Lake 16.9 µg/L; Detroit Reservoir 0.73 µg/L
Advisory tiers: 3 (Advisory / Precaution / Lifted)
tensions: Consistent with other state programs (e.g., [[SLG-005]] Ohio, [[SLG-006]] California) in using tiered, toxin-confirmed recreational advisories, and with the broader monitoring-coverage gap documented across the literature: sparse, resource-limited in-situ advisory networks leave most waterbodies unmonitored — the coverage problem that satellite cyanobacteria detection (e.g., EPA CyAN) is positioned to help address, subject to its own small-waterbody resolution limits.

title: Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) Program Guide (Version 3)
year: Not stated as a clear cover-page date in the extracted text. Internal tables/figures (Table 3.1, Figure 3.1) are explicitly captioned "updated in 2020," which is the only date evidence available from the source text itself; WebSearch could not independently confirm a distinct publication date for "Version 3."
what: A New York State Department of Environmental Conservation (NYSDEC) program guide (Version 3) documenting how the state's DEC HABs Program defines and classifies cyanobacteria bloom status, coordinates monitoring/reporting/outreach across state agencies and partners, and summarizes approaches to HAB prevention and in-la

claims.c: Freshwater HABs generally consist of cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) that are naturally present at low background abundance in most marine and freshwater systems, and can multiply rapidly into blooms under particular conditions, notably high nutrients and warm temperatures.

The DEC HABs Program assigns each bloom report one of four standardized status levels (No Bloom, Suspicious Bloom, Confirmed Bloom, Confirmed with High Toxins Bloom) using a documented decision-tree that combines visual/photographic evidence, blue-green (BG) chlorophyll concentration, and total microcystins concentration.

"No Bloom" is assigned when, among other allowed criteria, BG chlorophyll is ≤25 µg/L or (absent other criteria) total microcystins is ≤4 µg/L; "Confirmed Bloom" is assigned when BG chlorophyll is ≥25 µg/L, or (absent that) total microcystins is ≥4 µg/L but <20 µg/L together with a photo or field report.

The program's highest alert tier, "Confirmed with High Toxins Bloom," is triggered (in addition to meeting Confirmed Bloom criteria) by total microcystins ≥20 µg/L for shoreline samples, but by a numerically lower threshold of ≥10 µg/L for open-water samples, or by staff judgment of potential exposure risk to anatoxin or another cyanotoxin.

DEC states it adopted chlorophyll/microcystin surrogate criteria, rather than requiring microscopy or taxonomic identification on every report, specifically because of practical program constraints: need for rapid turnaround, high volume of reports/waterbodies, limited microscopy/taxonomy capacity, and lags in toxin-lab turnaround.

New York is estimated to contain between 7,500 and 16,000 ponded waterbodies, though the source notes there is no formal legal definition of "lake"; HABs have been documented in several hundred of these waterbodies since 2012, but DEC states the true extent of occurrence is likely greater than what has been reported.

More than 200 lakes per year are sampled through two DEC ambient lake-monitoring programs — CSLAP (Citizens Statewide Lake Assessment Program) and LCI (Lake Classification and Inventory Program) — both of which have expanded in recent years to include HABs-specific monitoring components.

The guide states that cyanobacterial bloom exposure can cause health effects in people and animals whether or not toxins are confirmed to be present, via skin contact, ingestion, or inhalation of airborne droplets.

Following a 2018 state initiative, four regional summits brought together national, state, and local experts, which led to the creation of Action Plans for 12 priority lakes and unspecified "advanced technology pilots," intended to generate knowledge applicable to other waterbodies statewide.

BG chlorophyll ≥ 25 µg/L → "Confirmed Bloom" status criterion
Total microcystins ≤ 4 µg/L → "No Bloom" criterion, used only if the other (visual/chlorophyll/microscopy) criteria are not met
Total microcystins ≥ 4 µg/L and < 20 µg/L (plus a photo or field report) → "Confirmed Bloom" criterion, used only if other criteria are not met
Total microcystins ≥ 20 µg/L in shoreline samples → "Confirmed with High Toxins Bloom"
Total microcystins ≥ 10 µg/L in open-water samples → "Confirmed with High Toxins Bloom" (a lower numeric bar than the shoreline criterion)
New York estimated to contain 7,500–16,000 ponded waterbodies (range only; source notes no formal legal definition of "lake" exists, so this is an approximation, not a precise count)
>200 lakes/year sampled via two DEC ambient monitoring programs (CSLAP and LCI)

HABs documented in "several hundred" NY waterbodies since 2012 (qualitative, non-precise figure; source explicitly states the true extent is "likely...substantially greater," i.e., a stated known undercount with no numer
2018: 4 regional summits held; Action Plans produced for 12 priority lakes

title: Oregon DEQ Freshwater Cyanobacteria Harmful Algal Blooms (CyanoHABs) Strategy
what: A 2023 Oregon DEQ operational strategy document (Version 2.1, October 2023) that lays out the agency's decision points, workflows, and regulatory thresholds for detecting, monitoring, and managing freshwater cyanobacteria harmful algal blooms (CyanoHABs) across three tracks — drinking-water protection, recreational-use

claims.c: Between 2007 and 2023, Oregon Health Authority issued 189 recreational advisories statewide for elevated cyanotoxins in waterbodies.
A 2018 CyanoHAB in Detroit Reservoir led the City of Salem to issue a 'Do Not Drink' advisory for vulnerable populations due to elevated cyanotoxins in finished (treated) drinking water.

As of the 2022 EPA-approved Integrated Report, 53 Oregon waterbodies are listed as water-quality impaired due to CyanoHABs under CWA Section 303(d); 7 of these have an established TMDL (Category 4A) and 1 has a Category 4B Alternative Plan.

OHA has adopted EPA-established numeric health-advisory thresholds for cyanotoxins in finished drinking water, differentiated by population: total microcystins 0.3 µg/L (vulnerable people) / 1.6 µg/L (everyone); cylindrospermopsin 0.7 µg/L (vulnerable people) / 3 µg/L (everyone).

A raw-water 'action level' of total microcystins ≥ 0.20 µg/L or cylindrospermopsin ≥ 0.30 µg/L triggers escalated monitoring: weekly raw-water sampling, finished-water sampling within one business day and then weekly, and daily finished-water sampling once cyanotoxins are detected there.

Public water systems (PWSs) deemed susceptible to CyanoHABs must sample raw water for cyanotoxins biweekly from May 1 to October 31 each year (the baseline monitoring season) unless/until action-level exceedances escalate the schedule.

DEQ/OHA determine a drinking-water source's cyanotoxin 'susceptibility' via a qualitative criteria checklist that explicitly includes satellite imagery alongside in-situ/physical indicators (temperature, nutrient loading, slow-moving or stagnant water, water quality data) and molecular indicators (presence of microcystin- or cylindrospermopsin-producing genes) — not a quantified or validated predictive model.

If a confirmed cyanotoxin concentration in finished drinking water exceeds the Table 1 advisory levels, the water system and any purchasing distributor must issue a 'Do-Not-Drink' order within 24 hours; lifting the order requires two consecutive samples at or below the vulnerable-population thresholds.
The 2023 strategy updates and continues a 2011 DEQ HABs strategy, and some of its recommended actions/subprograms may require additional funding not currently secured.

The document states, as general background (not a tested or quantified finding), that CyanoHAB frequency, extent, and magnitude can be associated with water temperature, nutrient loading, and hydrologic conditions.

numbers: 189 recreational health advisories issued by OHA statewide for elevated cyanotoxins, 2007-2023 (cumulative count over ~16 years; no per-year rate or uncertainty stated)
53 waterbodies listed as CyanoHAB-impaired in Oregon's 2022 EPA-approved Integrated Report (count; no uncertainty stated)
7 of 53 impaired waterbodies have an established TMDL (Category 4A); 1 of 53 has a Category 4B Alternative Plan
Finished drinking water health advisory (EPA levels adopted by OHA): total microcystins 0.3 µg/L (vulnerable people) / 1.6 µg/L (everyone)
Finished drinking water health advisory (EPA levels adopted by OHA): cylindrospermopsin 0.7 µg/L (vulnerable people) / 3 µg/L (everyone)
Raw-water action level triggering escalated monitoring: total microcystins ≥ 0.20 µg/L or cylindrospermopsin ≥ 0.30 µg/L

Baseline susceptible-PWS raw-water sampling: biweekly, May 1-Oct 31; escalates to weekly raw-water sampling + finished-water sample within 1 business day then weekly; daily finished-water sampling if cyanotoxins detected
'Do-Not-Drink' order must be issued within 24 hours of a confirmed Table 1 exceedance; lifted after 2 consecutive samples at/below vulnerable-people thresholds

None of the above figures are reported with an error bar, confidence interval, or explicit uncertainty estimate in the extracted text — they are administrative counts and fixed regulatory/statutory thresholds, not model

title: Pinto Lake catchment TMDL addressing cyanobacterial blooms
year: 2020 (adopted); approval chain completed 2021
what: The Central Coast Regional Water Quality Control Board's program page and document docket for the Pinto Lake (Santa Cruz County, CA) Total Maximum Daily Load (TMDL) for phosphorus - a Clean Water Act Section 303(d) regulatory pollution-budget and implementation-strategy program, adopted July 16, 2020, whose explicit pu

claims.c: The Central Coast Regional Water Quality Control Board adopted a phosphorus TMDL and implementation strategy for Pinto Lake on July 16, 2020, which then moved through a four-agency approval chain finishing with US EPA approval on November 21, 2021.
The Regional Board's adopting action was Resolution No. R3-2020-0034 (July 16, 2020).
The TMDL's stated rationale is that reducing phosphorus loading is the primary strategy for reducing the frequency and severity of cyanobacteria blooms in the lake.
The program sets a target of reducing the sediment-bound phosphorus load delivered from the watershed by 50%, over the useful life of the selected sediment-control practices.
The program also targets an 80% reduction in internal (in-lake sediment) phosphorus loading, to be achieved by dosing phosphorus-binding compounds across 95% of the lake's area.

Roughly 85% of the nutrient load driving cyanobacteria/cyanotoxin production in Pinto Lake is attributed to internal recycling of phosphorus from lake sediments, versus roughly 15% from seasonal watershed runoff (agriculture, septic systems, erosion).

Pinto Lake is hypereutrophic and is listed on the CWA Section 303(d) list of impaired waters because of harmful-algal-bloom-linked parameters: microcystins, ammonia, pH, scum/floating material, chlorophyll a, and low dissolved oxygen.
Pinto Lake is a shallow, roughly 100-acre, up to 27-foot-deep, approximately 8,000-year-old lake, fed by a 1,470-acre catchment drained by four creeks (Amesti, CCC, Pinto, and Todos Santos).
During 2007-2011, blooms at Pinto Lake produced microcystin at average concentrations around 183 ppb, with a 2007 peak reported at 2.9 million ppb.

Restoration work preceding the formal TMDL was funded by a $125,000 CWA Section 319(h) planning/assessment grant (2010) and a 2013 California Proposition 84 Agricultural Water Quality Grant that funded a sediment retention basin; interventions included a 2013 carp-removal effort and a 2017 phosphorus-binding (alum-type) treatment reported to have sealed away more than 60% of the lake's phosphorus.
Following these restoration actions, Pinto Lake recorded only one 3-week-long HAB closure in 2017 and no closures from 2018 through 2020.
A harmful algal bloom recurred at Pinto Lake in 2022, with microcystin reported at levels up to 10,000-fold an FDA recommended exposure limit.

Beyond numeric load targets, the program's implementation strategy includes non-regulatory outreach: septic-system maintenance guidelines, blue-green algae public-health guidance (updated 2016), livestock-owner outreach, and residential runoff-reduction guidance; the TMDL package also underwent a CEQA environmental review and an independent scientific peer review with public comments and staff responses posted.

A secondary journalistic account attributes at least 21 sea otter deaths in Monterey Bay to cyanobacterial toxins linked to this watershed system, and reports that 2011 lake toxin levels reached 750 times California's safe recreational-exposure limit.

numbers: Lake surface area: ~100 acres (described as a 'shallow 100-acre lake')
Lake maximum depth: 27 feet
Lake age: ~8,000 years old
Watershed/catchment area: 1,470 acres, drained by 4 creeks (Amesti, CCC, Pinto, Todos Santos)
TMDL adopted by Central Coast RWQCB: July 16, 2020 (Resolution No. R3-2020-0034)
TMDL approved by State Water Resources Control Board: April 20, 2021 (Resolution No. 2021-0014)
TMDL approved/effective via CA Office of Administrative Law: September 9, 2021
TMDL approved by US EPA: November 21, 2021
Target (per WebSearch synthesis of source): 50% reduction in sediment-bound phosphorus load from the watershed, over the useful life of sediment-control practices
Target (per WebSearch synthesis of source): 80% reduction in internal phosphorus loading via phosphorus-binding compound dosing, with 95% lake-area coverage
Source split (per WebSearch synthesis of source): ~85% of nutrient load from internal lake-sediment recycling vs. ~15% from watershed runoff
Microcystin, 2007-2011 average bloom concentration: ~183 ppb (parts per billion); 2007 peak: 2.9 million ppb

title: SWAMP Freshwater CyanoHABs Program (Blue-Green Algae)
year: n/a (living/continuously-updated program webpage; content spans historical references from 1925 to a most-recently-referenced document dated March 2025)
what: A California State Water Resources Control Board (SWAMP) webpage describing the state's Freshwater and Estuarine Harmful Algal Bloom (FHAB) Program — the regulatory, monitoring, and public-communication infrastructure for cyanobacterial HABs (citizen bloom reporting, a satellite-imagery screening tool, interactive inci

claims.c: California FHABs have been documented since at least 1925, with the earliest cited incident being a dog's illness after exposure to contaminated water at Clear Lake.
FHABs continue to occur roughly a century later across lakes, reservoirs, and rivers throughout California, in all months of the year (not just summer).
The program asserts FHABs are increasing statewide in incidence, duration, and toxicity, with increasingly prevalent health impacts to humans, domestic animals (dogs, livestock), and wildlife.
The program states that FHAB seasonality is shifting in some areas from predominantly summer blooms toward year-round occurrence.
The program frames California's at-risk freshwater scope as more than 3,000 lakes and 190,000 river miles, affecting over 40 million inhabitants.

The program's monitoring/assessment toolkit combines a voluntary citizen "Bloom Report Form," a public interactive map of bloom location and magnitude, a satellite imagery tool that remotely screens larger waterbodies for potential HABs, and a HAB Incident Web Map.

California Assembly Bill 834, signed in September 2019, is the legislative driver requiring expanded FHAB monitoring, research, and risk assessment, including the 2021 Monitoring Strategy developed with SCCWRP.
The state approved 5 permanent staff positions for the FHAB program via a Budget Change Proposal in 2020.

The 2021 Monitoring Strategy was explicitly written as a conceptual document surveying all possible monitoring options before the actual resources available for FHAB monitoring under AB 834 had been established.
The program's original coordinating stakeholder body, the "Blue Green Algae Work Group" (formed 2006), was later renamed the "California Cyanobacteria Harmful Algal Bloom Network (CCHAB)."

A dedicated technical guidance document specifically on satellite remote sensing best practices for freshwater HABs in California inland waters was published in March 2025, separate from this general program page.

title: Total Maximum Daily Load (TMDL) for Phosphorus in Chautauqua Lake, Chautauqua County, New York
what: A November 2012 Clean Water Act Section 303(d) Total Maximum Daily Load (TMDL) technical report for Chautauqua Lake, NY — a two-basin lake listed as phosphorus-impaired since 2004 that experienced a toxic blue-green algal bloom in 2009 — quantifying phosphorus loading by source (wastewater treatment plants, septic syst

claims.c: Chautauqua Lake's North and South basins were added to NYS DEC's Clean Water Act Section 303(d) list of impaired water bodies in 2004 specifically for phosphorus impairment, which triggered this TMDL's development as a 'high priority' water.

Chautauqua Lake experienced a toxic blue-green algal (cyanobacteria) bloom in 2009, and the South segment has been qualitatively described (per a 2007 NYS DEC characterization) as having 'definite algae greenness' versus clearer water in the North segment.

New York's numeric TMDL target for both Chautauqua Lake basins is a summer mean epilimnetic total phosphorus concentration not exceeding 20 µg/L (0.02 mg/L), based on biweekly sampling from June 1 to September 30, derived from NYS DEC guidance (TOGS 1.1.1) for 'ponded' waters (lakes/reservoirs/ponds excluding Lakes Erie, Ontario, Champlain).
Combined (external plus internal) total phosphorus loading to Chautauqua Lake was estimated at 80,828.7 lbs/yr as a long-term (1990-2007) mean annual figure.

Wastewater treatment plants are a far larger relative phosphorus source in the North basin (13 facilities; 5,487.4 lbs/yr, 19.6% of that basin's total load) than in the South basin (9 facilities; 1,008.0 lbs/yr, 1.9% of that basin's total load).

Septic systems within 250 feet of the lake are treated as being at risk of 'short-circuiting' (limited soil contact/adsorption before discharge reaches the lake), a distance-based criterion used in apportioning septic-system phosphorus loads (975.4 lbs/yr / 3.5% of North basin load; 719.4 lbs/yr / 1.4% of South basin load).

Groundwater seepage was modeled as a major nonpoint pathway, transporting an estimated 36.7% of the North basin's total phosphorus load and 20.9% of the South basin's total load; the model-estimated groundwater phosphorus concentrations (0.054 mg/L North, 0.055 mg/L South) were compared against a literature background value of 0.006 mg/L (GWLF manual, for ≥90% forested eastern U.S. land) to attribute roughly 11% of the groundwater load to 'natural' sources and the remainder to developed/agricultural land.

Internal (bottom-sediment) phosphorus loading is estimated to supply 25.1% (7,014.7 lbs/yr) of the North basin's total load and 55.1% (29,147.8 lbs/yr) of the South basin's total load — making it the single largest modeled source category in the South basin — but this figure is not an independent measurement; it is the residual load needed to reconcile modeled external sources with observed lake phosphorus concentrations.

The TMDL's lake-response modeling fed AVGWLF-simulated external loads plus the calculated internal load into the BATHTUB model (U.S. Army Corps of Engineers; Walker, 1987), and the report characterizes the resulting simulated summer mean epilimnetic phosphorus concentrations as fitting the observed 1990-2007 lake monitoring data well, both as a long-term average and year-by-year.

Drainage from the North basin into the South basin (the two segments are connected by a narrow channel) is itself modeled as a phosphorus source to the South basin, estimated at 8,094 lbs/yr, or 15.4% of the South basin's total load.

numbers: TMDL numeric target: 20 µg/L (0.020 mg/L) mean summer epilimnetic total phosphorus, biweekly sampling June 1-Sept 30 (NYS DEC TOGS 1.1.1 guidance value)
Total modeled phosphorus load entering Chautauqua Lake: 80,828.7 lbs/yr (long-term mean, 1990-2007, AVGWLF-based estimate; no confidence interval reported in extracted text)
WWTP loading: North basin 5,487.4 lbs/yr (19.6% of North total, 13 facilities); South basin 1,008.0 lbs/yr (1.9% of South total, 9 facilities)
Septic system loading: North basin 975.4 lbs/yr (3.5%); South basin 719.4 lbs/yr (1.4%); short-circuiting risk threshold = within 250 ft of lake
Wetland loading: North basin 20.6 lbs/yr; South basin 7.9 lbs/yr (each <0.1% of total load); wetlands cover 2,081 acres (4%) of North basin and 1,469 acres (3%) of South basin drainage area
Stream bank erosion: North basin 36.0 lbs/yr (0.1%); South basin 47.8 lbs/yr (0.1%)

Groundwater seepage load: North basin 10,243.0 lbs/yr (36.7%); South basin 11,038.7 lbs/yr (20.9%); modeled groundwater P concentration 0.054 mg/L (North) / 0.055 mg/L (South) vs. 0.006 mg/L forested background (GWLF man
Internal (residual) sediment loading: North basin 7,014.7 lbs/yr (25.1% of total load); South basin 29,147.8 lbs/yr (55.1% of total load)
North-to-South basin drainage transfer: 8,094 lbs/yr (15.4% of South basin's total load)
Direct drainage basin area: 101,943 acres; elevation range 1,308-1,863.5 ft AMSL
Land use (similar in both basins): Forest ~62.1% (N) / 63.3% (S); Agriculture [hay & pasture + cropland] ~28.8% (N) / 27.4% (S); Developed land ~3.5% (N) / 5.8% (S); Wetland ~4.2% (N) / 2.8% (S)
tensions: This TMDL's own source apportionment shows internal (legacy sediment) phosphorus loading as the largest single modeled category in the South basin (55.1% of total load) and a substantial share in the North basin (25.1%). Because this internal-load figure is a calculated residual rather than a direct measurement, and because it is large, this source complicates — without directly contradicting — simpler HAB-management framings that treat watershed nutrient-source control (wastewater, septic, agricultural/groundwater pathways) as sufficient on it
